source.title: Tenant Handback and Cost Stewardship Runbook
source.author: Monica Alvarez
source.content:
Purpose
======

This runbook standardizes the transition (handback) of an enterprise tenant from the onboarding project team to steady-state Customer Success (CS) and Platform Operations. Its primary goals are to:  
- Ensure operational ownership and alert routing are correctly assigned.  
- Lock down cost controls and validate billing telemetry.  
- Confirm SLO coverage and QBR data availability.  
- Provide clear escalation and rollback procedures for the first 90 days after handback.

Scope
-----

Applies to: Dedicated and Private tenants (reserved capacity and VPC/on-prem) that completed provisioning and initial canaries. Excludes short-term PoCs and internal developer sandboxes.

Key Definitions
---------------

- Handback: formal transfer of operational responsibility from Onboarding Squad -> Customer Success + Platform Ops.
- Cost Stewardship: ongoing controls and alerts to prevent runaway spend.
- Steady-state Owner: CS contact and Platform owner listed on the tenant card.

Roles & Responsibilities
------------------------

- Onboarding Lead (OL): completes the handback checklist, produces final runbook artifacts, and remains on-call for the 72-hour stabilization window.
- Customer Success Manager (CSM): primary customer-facing owner for contractual SLAs and QBR preparation.
- Platform Ops (POps): infrastructure and telemetry owner; configures capacity controls and SLO alerts.
- Billing Ops: validates quota metering and custom billing hooks.
- Security Reviewer: validates post-deployment controls (KMS, network ACLs).

Handback Gate Checklist (must be green to hand back)
---------------------------------------------------

| Gate | Required Artifact | Owner | Pass Criteria |
|---|---:|---|---|
| 1. Canary verification | Canary test report (id + runs) | OL | 3/3 stable runs under 95th percentile latency SLO |
| 2. Telemetry wiring | Metric dashboard + trace links | POps | All SLOs reporting for last 2 hours; alerts firing test OK |
| 3. Cost guardrails | Quotas, soft caps, and quota-protector webhook | Billing Ops | Soft cap at 1.2x expected baseline; quota webhook validated |
| 4. Security posture | KMS integration, egress rules | Security Reviewer | Proof of key rotation policy and allowed CIDR lists |
| 5. Docs & runbooks | Tenant runbook in Confluence + runbook card | OL | Step-by-step runbook and contact matrix present |

Operational Runbook: First 72 hours after handback
-------------------------------------------------

1. Pre-handback meeting (T-2 days)
   - Participants: OL, CSM, POps, Billing Ops, Security Reviewer, Customer contacts.
   - Agenda: review gate artifacts, confirm monitoring endpoints, set 72-hour stabilization calendar.

2. Final configuration freeze (T-1 day)
   - OL runs `redwoodctl tenant export-config --tenant-id $TENANT` and stores output in secured artifact store.
   - POps applies cost soft caps: `rb set-cap --tenant $TENANT --budget 1.2x` (example).

3. Formal handback (T0)
   - OL creates the confluence handback page and marks the tenant status flag to "steady-state".
   - POps runs the alert smoke test: trigger synthetic error and confirm oncall routing.

4. Stabilization window (T0 -> T+72h)
   - OL remains available for technical deep dives during business hours.
   - POps observes SLOs and sets tickets for any degraded metrics.
   - CSM schedules first 14-day check-in with customer and confirms QBR artifact access.

5. Post-stabilization audit (T+72h)
   - Audit owners: POps and Billing Ops.
   - Deliverables: audit report with observations and action items to be incorporated into the tenant card.

Cost Stewardship Controls (required by Billing Ops)
--------------------------------------------------

Minimum configuration applied at handback:  
- Budget soft cap at 1.2x forecast baseline (email warning at 0.8x, pager at 1.0x).  
- Quota-protector webhook enabled for token/embedding volume spikes.  
- Model quantization profile pinned for predictable CPU/GPU utilization for the first 30 days.

Recommended alerts and thresholds:
- Cost burn rate increase > 30% over 6 hours -> Page to Billing Ops.
- Sustained batch size increase causing API 95th latency breach -> Page to POps.

Cost Controls Table
-------------------

| Control | Example Config | Owner | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Soft budget cap | 1.2 x baseline | Billing Ops | Non-blocking; triggers email and Slack notification |
| Hard quota | tokens/day = baseline * 3 | Billing Ops | Blocks additional tokens; use only in emergencies |
| Quota-protector webhook | /hooks/quota-protector/{tenant} | POps | Should return 200 within 250ms |

Telemetry & SLO Gating
----------------------

Required SLOs (examples):
- Inference latency P95 < 300ms for standard prompt (dedicated).
- Successful response rate > 99.5% (5m window).
- KV-cache hit rate >= 60% for eligible workloads.

Key dashboards/queries to validate during handback:
- `inference.latency.p95{tenant:$TENANT}` (last 2h)
- `responses.success_rate{tenant:$TENANT}` (5m sliding window)
- `billing.tokens.24h{tenant:$TENANT}` and `billing.burn_rate.6h{tenant:$TENANT}`

Alerting matrix (minimum):
- SLO warning (deviation > 10% for 15m) -> Slack #tenant-alerts (POps + CSM).
- SLO critical (deviation > 20% for 5m) -> Pager to POps primary and CSM.
- Cost critical (burn rate spike > 30% for 1h) -> Pager to Billing Ops primary.

Canary, Rollback and Safe-Fallback Patterns
------------------------------------------

- Canary scope: 1% traffic on a matched synthetic profile + 5% of real customer traffic (if safe).
- If canary latency or error rate breaches SLO: automatic rollback to prior model variant within 2 minutes.
- Safe-fallback: traffic is routed to a smaller model variant to preserve availability when capacity depleted.

Escalation Matrix (contact in order)
-----------------------------------

1. Platform Ops primary: p.ops@redwood.ai, pager: +1-555-101-0101  
2. Billing Ops primary: billing-ops@redwood.ai, pager: +1-555-101-0202  
3. Customer Success (CSM): csm-team@redwood.ai (matches tenant owner)  
4. Engineering on-call (runtime): oncall-runtime@redwood.ai, pager as rostered  

Escalation guidance:
- For availability impacting incidents (SLO critical): follow oncall-runtime escalation immediately.
- For cost incidents without availability impact: page Billing Ops and notify CSM.
- For security events: notify Security Reviewer and follow the incident-response-security runbook.

QBR Handoff Artifacts (what CSM needs for the first QBR)
-------------------------------------------------------

- 30/60/90-day adoption metrics (tokens, active users, revenue attribution).
- SLO history and significant incidents with root cause summaries.
- Cost trend and any cost-mitigation actions taken.
- Action items log from post-stabilization audit.

Appendix A: Example CLI snippets and telemetry JSON
--------------------------------------------------

CLI: export tenant config (example)
```
redwoodctl tenant export-config --tenant-id $TENANT --out /secrets/$TENANT-config.json
```

CLI: set a budget soft cap (example)
```
rb set-cap --tenant $TENANT --type soft --value 1.2x --notify-email billing-ops@redwood.ai
```

Telemetry sample (filter)
```
{ "filter": "tenant:$TENANT AND environment:prod AND metric:inference.latency.p95", "window": "2h" }
```

Appendix B: Common failure modes and quick mitigations
-----------------------------------------------------

- Symptom: sudden token burn without latency increase.
  - Mitigation: enable hard quota temporarily, run `rb audit-requests --tenant $TENANT --since 1h` to identify spike source.
- Symptom: KV-cache miss storm causing latency increase.
  - Mitigation: switch traffic to model variant with smaller context window or warm caches using synthetic prefix requests.
- Symptom: quota-protector webhook timing out.
  - Mitigation: fail-open to soft-cap for 10 minutes while webhook is fixed; page POps.

Change Control & Documentation Updates
------------------------------------

- Any persistent changes to budget, quota, or model pinning must be recorded on the tenant card and linked to a change ticket.
- Runbook ownership transfers must be approved by the CSM and POps lead.

Review cadence
--------------

- 14-day check-in: CSM + OL to review stabilization notes.
- 30-day audit: POps + Billing Ops to confirm telemetry and costs.
- 90-day QBR readiness: CSM prepares QBR artifacts and confirms roadmap alignment.

Document maintenance
--------------------

Author: Monica Alvarez (Customer Success). Please tag reviewers for substantive changes. Major revisions should be accompanied by a short post-change readout and updated audit checklist.

End of document

source.title: Customer Model Economics Deep Dive Template
source.author: Marina Lopez
source.content:
Summary:

This template is a standardized, customer-facing deep-dive used during QBRs and technical review sessions to explain the economics of a customer's inference footprint on Redwood. It focuses on translating telemetry and billing signals into actionable recommendations: model mix, batching and cache strategy, quantization/variant choices, and reserved capacity sizing.

Audience:
- Customer Success Manager (CSM)
- Solutions Engineer (SE)
- Customer SRE / Platform lead
- Finance contact for the customer (optional)

When to use:
- Quarterly business reviews (QBR) where cost/perf is a top objective
- Post-cutover stabilization if spend or latency deviates from expectations
- Prior to pricing or dedicated capacity negotiations

Prerequisites and data sources:
1) Time window: last 28 / 90 / 365 days (choose based on volatility).
2) Telemetry sources:
   - Redwood inference telemetry (per-tenant token/embedding counts, model name/version, route tags)
   - Observability: latency P50/P95/P99, error rates (per-route), batch size distribution
   - Cost signals: per-token billing extracts, reserved capacity charges, GPU hours by tenant (for Dedicated)
   - Cache stats: prefix/KV cache hit rate and hit latency
   - Infrastructure: GPU utilization, queue depth, autoscaling events
3) Representative prompts/promotions sets and any recent model upgrades or prompt engineering changes.

Key metrics to present (suggested table):

KPI | Description | Aggregation | Example target 
----|-------------|-------------|----------------
Effective unit cost (USD per 1k tokens) | All-in cost combining per-token billing + allocated infra | 28-day median | $0.85 (customer target)
Model mix by spend (%) | Percent of spend per model family (e.g., redwood-v1-small, llama-2-70B) | 28-day | 4 models, top 2 = 78% spend
Latency P95 (ms) | Tail latency for customer routes | 28-day P95 | <= 350ms
Cache hit rate (prefix + KV) | Percent of requests served at cache layer | 28-day | >= 45%
Batching effectiveness | Median tokens/request per batch | 7-day rolling | >= 12 tokens/batch
Reserved capacity utilization | Average shard utilization (Dedicated) | 24h avg | 60–85%
Cold start frequency | Requests that miss warm KV cache/state | 28-day rate | < 0.5%
Model-induced error rate | Failure/invalid response rate per 1k requests | 28-day | < 0.3%

Core sections of the deep-dive (structure to follow during the QBR):

1) Executive summary (1 slide / 1 paragraph)
- One-sentence summary of the customer's cost vs. target, plus one recommended high-level action (e.g., "Move 30% of traffic from redwood-v2-large to quantized redwood-v2-medium to reduce unit cost by ~37% with minimal latency change").

2) Observed telemetry and spend breakdown (2–3 slides)
- Model-by-model spend table and charts (last 28/90d)
- Route-level latency and cost per 1k tokens
- Time-of-day and georegional variance (if applicable)

3) Root-cause analysis for deviations (2 slides)
- Example triggers: sudden model upgrade, increased prompt length, agricultural traffic spike, disabled caching, batch size regression, or dedicated pool oversubscription.
- For each issue: evidence (query/metric), likely cause, confidence, and immediate mitigation.

4) Actionable recommendations (3–4 items)
- Low friction (customer-facing): adjust default model on non-critical routes, enable prefix caching for high-cardinality prompts, shorten default max tokens on ephemeral endpoints.
- Medium friction (needs engineering): rollout of quantized variant + canary, small model distillation trial, adjust batch-windowing to favour throughput on non-latency-critical routes.
- High friction (contract/ops): purchase X reserved GPUs for peak savings, migrate heavy routes to Dedicated with fair-share slots.
- Each recommendation should include estimated cost delta (USD/mo), expected latency impact, implementation owner, and risk.

5) Simulation and sensitivity scenarios
- Show 2–3 what-if scenarios with simple math: e.g., "If 40% traffic shifts to quantized model -> unit cost -36%; if batching improves by 25% -> token cost -18%." Include assumptions used (model throughput, batching gain, cache hit uplift).

6) Short-term runbook (30/60/90-day plan)
- 0–30 days: enable prefix caching on top 3 routes, shorten max_tokens on ingest pipeline, adjust rate-limit burst window.
- 30–60 days: deploy quantized model canary for 10% traffic, measure latency/QA regressions, instrument golden prompts.
- 60–90 days: review reserved capacity sizing, finalize new per-route model defaults, schedule cost-sharing / pricing discussion.

7) Ownership and OLA (who does what)
- CSM: schedule QBR, prioritize customer sign-off for low-friction actions.
- SE: prepare canary build + telemetry dashboards, run A/B test.
- Customer SRE: validate cache keys and integrate sampling of golden prompts.
- Finance: review forecasting model and margin sensitivities.

8) Escalation triggers (for post-QBR monitoring)
- Daily unit cost > 30% above baseline for 3 consecutive days -> SE + CSM alert.
- P95 latency increase > 50% vs baseline for 2 windows -> schedule incident review.
- Model error rate spike (>=1% absolute increase) -> immediate rollback canary.

9) Appendix: suggested queries & dashboard panels (examples)
- Example PromQL: "sum by (tenant, model) (rate(tokens_consumed_total[1d]))"
- Billing extract SQL (BigQuery) example:
  SELECT tenant_id, model_name, SUM(tokens) as tokens, SUM(cost) as cost
  FROM `billing.tokens_by_request`
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 28 DAY) AND CURRENT_DATE()
  GROUP BY tenant_id, model_name
  ORDER BY cost DESC;
- Suggested dashboard panels: cost-by-model, token-rate heatmap, batch-size histogram, cache-hit-rate timeseries, reserved-utilization gauge.

Presentation tips and anti-patterns:
- Use representative "golden prompts" to demonstrate quality trade-offs for any model/quantization suggestion.
- Don't surface raw billing numbers without normalizing for prompt length and model mix.
- Avoid recommending under-provisioning reserved capacity to hit a short-term cost target; instead present tiered scenarios with safety margins.

Deliverables to attach to the QBR packet:
- Exported dashboards (PNG/PDF) for last 28 / 90 days
- CSV with model-by-model spend and top 20 routes by tokens
- Runbook checklist for short-term actions and owners
- A one-page executive summary with suggested next steps and estimated savings

Template checklist (pre-QBR):
- [ ] Confirm time window and obtain billing extract
- [ ] Validate telemetry mapping (tenant tags, route naming)
- [ ] Identify golden prompts and QA test suite
- [ ] Run baseline cost simulation and prepare 2 alternative scenarios
- [ ] Draft CSM talking points and action owners

Common pitfalls and mitigations (real examples):
- Pitfall: Large ephemeral prompts from a background job inflate avg tokens. Mitigation: Add a separate route, cap tokens, and apply batch-friendly settings.
- Pitfall: Cache keys contain transient IDs so cache hit rate is low. Mitigation: Normalize prompt templates and add pre-processing to strip unique IDs.
- Pitfall: Quantized variant introduces subtle hallucination rate increase. Mitigation: Canary on 5% of traffic and expand golden prompt set for automatic regression detection.

Contact and ownership logging:
- Document author: Marina Lopez (CSM)
- Standard reviewers: Ethan Park (SE), Riya Shah (Finance), Liam O'Connor (SRE)
- Next review cadence: every 90 days or after any model migration affecting >10% of customer traffic.

Related templates and playbooks:
- QBR inference economics overview (link in related_pages)
- Tenant synthetic health suite onboarding playbook (for automated monitoring after changes)

Change log:
- 2026-03-15: Created initial template.
- 2026-11-10: Added sensitivity scenario examples and reserved capacity guidance.

title: Customer Model Economics Deep Dive Template
preview:
Summary:

This template is a standardized, customer-facing deep-dive used during QBRs and technical review sessions to explain the economics of a customer's inference footprint on Redwood. It focuses on translating telemetry and billing signals into actionable recommendations: model mix, batching and cache strategy, quantization/variant choices, and reserved capacity sizing.

Audience:
- Customer Success Manager (CSM)
- Solutions Engineer (SE)
- Customer SRE / Platform lead
- Finance contact for the customer (optional)

When to use:
- Quarterly business reviews (QBR) where cost/perf is a top objective
- Post-cutover stabilization if spend or latency deviates from expectations
- Prior to pricing or dedicated capacity negotiations

Prerequisites and data sources:
1) Time window: last 28 / 90 / 365 days (choose based on volatility).
2) Telemetry sources:
   - Redwood inference telemetry (per-tenant token/embedding counts, model name/version, route tags)
   - Observability: latency P50/P95/P99, error rates (per-route), batch size distribution
   - Cost signals: per-token billing extracts, reserved capacity charges, GPU hours by tenant (for Dedicated)
   - Cache stats: prefix/KV cache hit rate and hit latency
   - Infrastructure: GPU utilization, queue depth, autoscaling events
3) Representative prompts/promotions sets and any recent model upgrades or prompt engineering changes.

Key metrics to present (suggested table):

KPI | Description | Aggregation | Example target 
----|-------------|-------------|----------------
Effective unit cost (USD per 1k tokens) | All-in cost combining per-token billing + allocated infra | 28-day median | $0.85 (customer target)
Model mix by spend (%) | Percent of spend per model family (e.g., redwood-v1-small, llama-2-70B) | 28-day | 4 models, top 2 = 78% spend
Latency P95 (ms) | Tail latency for customer routes | 28-day P95 | <= 350ms
Cache hit rate (prefix + KV) | Percent of requests served at cache layer | 28-day | >= 45%
Batching effectiveness | Median tokens/request per batch | 7-day rolling | >= 12 tokens/batch
Reserved capacity utilization | Average shard utilization (Dedicated) | 24h avg | 60–85%
Cold start frequency | Requests that miss warm KV cache/state | 28-day rate | < 0.5%
Model-induced error rate | Failure/invalid response rate per 1k requests | 28-day | < 0.3%

Core sections of the deep-dive (structure to follow during the QBR):

1) Executive summary (1 slide / 1 paragraph)
- One-sentence summary of the customer's cost vs. target, plus one recommended high-level action (e.g., "Move 30% of traffic from redwood-v2-large to quantized redwood-v2-medium to reduce unit cost by ~37% with minimal latency change").

2) Observed telemetry and spend breakdown (2–3 slides)
- Model-by-model spend table and charts (last 28/90d)
- Route-level latency and cost per 1k tokens
- Time-of-day and georegional variance (if applicable)

3) Root-cause analysis for deviations (2 slides)
- Example triggers: sudden model upgrade, increased prompt length, agricultural traffic spike, disabled caching, batch size regression, or dedicated pool oversubscription.
- For each issue: evidence (query/metric), likely cause, confidence, and immediate mitigation.

4) Actionable recommendations (3–4 items)
- Low friction (customer-facing): adjust default model on non-critical routes, enable prefix caching for high-cardinality prompts, shorten default max tokens on ephemeral endpoints.
- Medium friction (needs engineering): rollout of quantized variant + canary, small model distillation trial, adjust batch-windowing to favour throughput on non-latency-critical routes.
- High friction (contract/ops): purchase X reserved GPUs for peak savings, migrate heavy routes to Dedicated with fair-share slots.
- Each recommendation should include estimated cost delta (USD/mo), expected latency impact, implementation owner, and risk.

5) Simulation and sensitivity scenarios
- Show 2–3 what-if scenarios with simple math: e.g., "If 40% traffic shifts to quantized model -> unit cost -36%; if batching improves by 25% -> token cost -18%." Include assumptions used (model throughput, batching gain, cache hit uplift).

6) Short-term runbook (30/60/90-day plan)
- 0–30 days: enable prefix caching on top 3 routes, shorten max_tokens on ingest pipeline, adjust rate-limit burst window.
- 30–60 days: deploy quantized model canary for 10% traffic, measure latency/QA regressions, instrument golden prompts.
- 60–90 days: review reserved capacity sizing, finalize new per-route model defaults, schedule cost-sharing / pricing discussion.

7) Ownership and OLA (who does what)
- CSM: schedule QBR, prioritize customer sign-off for low-friction actions.
- SE: prepare canary build + telemetry dashboards, run A/B test.
- Customer SRE: validate cache keys and integrate sampling of golden prompts.
- Finance: review forecasting model and margin sensitivities.

8) Escalation triggers (for post-QBR monitoring)
- Daily unit cost > 30% above baseline for 3 consecutive days -> SE + CSM alert.
- P95 latency increase > 50% vs baseline for 2 windows -> schedule incident review.
- Model error rate spike (>=1% absolute increase) -> immediate rollback canary.

9) Appendix: suggested queries & dashboard panels (examples)
- Example PromQL: "sum by (tenant, model) (rate(tokens_consumed_total[1d]))"
- Billing extract SQL (BigQuery) example:
  SELECT tenant_id, model_name, SUM(tokens) as tokens, SUM(cost) as cost
  FROM `billing.tokens_by_request`
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 28 DAY) AND CURRENT_DATE()
  GROUP BY tenant_id, model_name
  ORDER BY cost DESC;
- Suggested dashboard panels: cost-by-model, token-rate heatmap, batch-size histogram, cache-hit-rate timeseries, reserved-utilization gauge.

Presentation tips and anti-patterns:
- Use representative "golden prompts" to demonstrate quality trade-offs for any model/quantization suggestion.
- Don't surface raw billing numbers without normalizing for prompt length and model mix.
- Avoid recommending under-provisioning reserved capacity to hit a short-term cost target; instead present tiered scenarios with safety margins.

Deliverables to attach to the QBR packet:
- Exported dashboards (PNG/PDF) for last 28 / 90 days
- CSV with model-by-model spend and top 20 routes by tokens
- Runbook checklist for short-term actions and owners
- A one-page executive summary with suggested next steps and estimated savings

Template checklist (pre-QBR):
- [ ] Confirm time window and obtain billing extract
- [ ] Validate telemetry mapping (tenant tags, route naming)
- [ ] Identify golden prompts and QA test suite
- [ ] Run baseline cost simulation and prepare 2 alternative scenarios
- [ ] Draft CSM talking points and action owners

Common pitfalls and mitigations (real examples):
- Pitfall: Large ephemeral prompts from a background job inflate avg tokens. Mitigation: Add a separate route, cap tokens, and apply batch-friendly settings.
- Pitfall: Cache keys contain transient IDs so cache hit rate is low. Mitigation: Normalize prompt templates and add pre-processing to strip unique IDs.
- Pitfall: Quantized variant introduces subtle hallucination rate increase. Mitigation: Canary on 5% of traffic and expand golden prompt set for automatic regression detection.

Contact and ownership logging:
- Document author: Marina Lopez (CSM)
- Standard reviewers: Ethan Park (SE), Riya Shah (Finance), Liam O'Connor (SRE)
- Next review cadence: every 90 days or after any model migration affecting >10% of customer traffic.

- 2026-03-15: Created initial template.
- 2026-11-10: Added sensitivity scenario examples and reserved capacity guidance.

source.title: Tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling playbook
source.author: Amira Patel
source.body:
Summary

This playbook describes the operational procedures, validation tests, and escalation steps for tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling for Dedicated and Private tenants. It targets customer-success, SRE, and platform engineers responsible for onboarding enterprise customers with unpredictable burst traffic or constrained egress capacity.

Goals

- Ensure predictability for tenant traffic when outbound (egress) capacity is constrained.
- Maintain latency SLOs for high-priority tenants while applying fair-share policies to noisy neighbors.
- Provide a deterministic automated fallback that preserves availability and preserves customer data guarantees.

When to use this playbook

Use this playbook during:
1) Pre-production validation for Dedicated/Private tenant cutovers.
2) Go-live where tenant traffic patterns are unknown or have burst risk.
3) Incidents where egress link saturation, pod/instance ejects, or third-party API throttles cause increased latency or errors.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: Customer Success (Onboarding team) — Amira Patel
- Operational owner: Eng SRE — Diego Morales
- Runtime/Platform owner: Eng Platform — Priya Natarajan
- Pager: #sre-oncall (primary), #cs-help (secondary)
- Escalation: If pager not acknowledged in 15m, escalate to SRE L2 and Customer Success Manager.

Scope and constraints

- Applies to tenants on Dedicated capacity and Private VPC deployments.
- Not for Hosted (shared) tenants unless explicitly configured with fair-share proxies.
- Does not modify customer data retention or encryption policies.

Prerequisites and config artifacts

- Tenant capacity profile created in Capacity Service (capacity-service v2): tenant_id, target_rps, burst_rps, expected_seq_len.
- Ingress and egress proxies must expose the following metrics to metrics-ingest: ingress.rps, egress.bandwidth.bytes, proxy.queue_length, request.latency.p50/p95/p99.
- Fair-share controller enabled in tenant_proxy.conf with the following knobs: max_tokens_per_second, priority_weight, weight_decay_window_ms.
- Sufficient observability: synthetic journey for tenant with end-to-end p50/p95 thresholds.

Validation tests (pre-go-live)

Execute the validation harness below in a staging environment that mirrors production network topology. Document all results in the onboarding ticket.

1) Baseline characterization
- Inject steady traffic at target_rps for 10 minutes. Verify p95 latency < tenant_p95_slo and error rate < 0.1%.
- Required metrics: ingress.rps, egress.bytes/s, kvcache.hit_rate.

2) Burst resilience check
- Ramp all tenant test clients from target_rps to burst_rps over 60s. Observe fallback behavior. Expected outcome: lower-priority tenants receive graceful rate-limiting; high-priority tenants retain >= 85% of baseline throughput.

3) Egress saturation simulation
- Throttle external egress (eg simulate 40% link loss). Verify that fallback to local cache and degraded-mode responses occur: response codes 200 (degraded ok) or 503 (if hard fail) per policy. Verify circuit-breaker triggers at configured thresholds.

4) Fair-share fairness validation
- Run 10 tenants simultaneously with varied priority_weight values. Measure delivered RPS per tenant and verify proportional share within +/- 10% of configured weights.

Pre-Go-Live checklist (must pass all items)

- [ ] Capacity profile uploaded and approved.
- [ ] Tenant proxy config reviewed and committed to config repo (reviewer: Eng Platform).
- [ ] Synthetic journey created and scheduled for tenant.
- [ ] Alerting rules created: high egress utilization (>85%), proxy queue_length > 50, fair-share-imbalance > 15%.
- [ ] Backstop fallback policy documented in the customer runbook (customer-facing).

Go-Live steps (recommended timebox: 60–90 minutes)

1) Golden path smoke (0–10m)
- Verify ingress/routing is pointing to Dedicated/Private capacity.
- Run 2 quick synthetic requests and validate correlation IDs flow through logs.

2) Canary ramp (10–30m)
- Start with 5% of traffic routed to new tenant environment. Monitor p50/p95, error rate, and proxy queue.
- If p95 increases > 25% baseline or error rate > 0.5%, abort canary and roll back routing.

3) Gradual ramp (30–60m)
- Increase traffic by 20% every 10 minutes, monitoring fairness and egress usage.
- Confirm fair-share controller is distributing capacity proportionally.

4) Full cutover and verification (60–90m)
- Update routing to 100%. Keep enhanced monitoring for 6 hours. Document any anomalies.

Degraded-mode behavior and automatic fallback

- Soft-fallback: When proxy queue_length > 75 or egress_bandwidth >= 90% for > 30s, the system applies fair-share throttling and enables degraded responses for non-critical endpoints (e.g., background embeddings refresh).
- Hard-fallback: If requests.latency.p99 > 3x SLO for > 2 minutes, or error rate > 2% for > 5 minutes, fail open to read-only cached responses and notify SRE.
- Customer notification: For hard-fallbacks, Customer Success must send a templated notification within 30 minutes describing impact and mitigation steps.

Automated remediation actions (ordered)

1) Activate fair-share throttling profile (fast path).
2) Evict low-priority background requests for 60s.
3) Route new tenant traffic to lower-cost region if cross-region fallback policy exists and region latency delta < 50ms.
4) Provision burst capacity (manual escalation to capacity team if autoscale cannot allocate).

Telemetry, alerts, and SLOs to configure

- Metrics to send to metrics-ingest: tenant.{id}.ingress_rps, tenant.{id}.egress_bps, tenant.{id}.delivered_rps, tenant.{id}.throttle_rate, tenant.{id}.priority_share_pct.
- Core alerts (severity mapping):
  - P0: egress_bandwidth >= 95% for > 3m (pager)
  - P1: proxy.queue_length >= 75 for > 2m (page + slack)
  - P2: fair-share-imbalance > 20% for > 10m (slack only)
- Suggested SLOs (tenant-level):
  - Availability: 99.95% (excluding scheduled maintenance)
  - Latency p95: tenant-specific (recommended default 300ms)
  - Throttling transparency: any throttled request must return `X-Redwood-Throttle: true` header and include an advisory in response body where applicable.

Escalation matrix and playbook actions

1) First responder (SRE oncall) — Triage, perform automated remediation steps 1–3.
2) If unresolved after 15 minutes — SRE L2 and Customer Success notified; open incident channel and begin stakeholder comms.
3) If unresolved after 60 minutes — Eng Platform and Capacity team pager.
4) If unresolved after 3 hours — Executive escalation and formal incident postmortem.

Incident comms template (short)

Subject: [Incident] Tenant {tenant_id} - Egress congestion detected\\nBody:
- Impact: Elevated latency and partial degradation for tenant {tenant_id}.
- Timeline: {start_time} UTC
- Mitigation: Applied fair-share throttling and evicted background requests; routing adjusted.
- Next steps: Capacity team provisioning in progress; monitoring in place.

Post-incident items and QBR inputs

- Document the root cause, timeline, and mitigation in the incident ticket.
- Recommend capacity or architectural changes: e.g., increase egress pool by N GPUs or enable region-fallback by default.
- Add the incident to the next QBR with the following artifacts: synthetic journey traces, request/response samples with correlation IDs, and fairness delta graphs.

Testing scenarios matrix (examples)

| Scenario | Goal | Expected outcome | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Single-tenant burst | Verify high-priority preserved | High-priority tenant keeps >=85% throughput | Run for 30m
| Multi-tenant simultaneous burst | Validate fair-share | Proportional throughput within +/-10% | Use 10 tenants
| Egress link failure | Validate degraded-mode | Cached responses + advisory header | Simulate packet loss at edge

Appendix: sample tenant_proxy.conf snippet

# Example TOML snippet for fair-share controller
[fair_share]
max_tokens_per_second = 1200
priority_weights = { gold = 10, standard = 5, trial = 1 }
weight_decay_window_ms = 60000
eviction_policy = "background-first"

Appendix: basic CLI checks for oncall

# Verify current queue lengths and throttle rates (run on proxy-host)
curl -sS http://proxy-host.local/metrics | grep tenant.{tenant_id}.throttle_rate

# Force a synthetic request with correlation id
curl -H "X-Correlation-ID: onboarding-123" -X POST https://tenant-ingress.example/v1/generate -d '{"prompt": "smoke test"}' -v

Recovery and rollback guidance

- Immediate rollback: Re-route traffic back to incumbent environment via edge-router config if p95 > 2x SLO during canary.
- Long-term remediation: Schedule capacity expansion, add egress caching layer for large-response endpoints, or adjust tenant priority weights for steady-state fairness.

Document history and change log

- 2026-01-15: Created by Amira Patel (initial draft).
- 2026-03-02: Added automated remediation ordering and telemetry section (reviewer: Diego Morales).
- 2026-08-02: Published with expanded testing matrix and CLI snippets (reviewer: Priya Natarajan).

Related pages and references

- Dedicated tenant cutover safety-net playbook — see related_pages for link.
- Synthetic health suite implementation guide (health-and-qbr).
- Eng SRE capacity profiling guide for tenant planning.

End of playbook

- Ingress and egress proxies must expose the following metrics to metrics-ingest: ingress.rps, egress.bandwidth.bytes, proxy.queue_length, request.latency.p50/p95/p99.
- Fair-share controller enabled in tenant_proxy.conf with the following knobs: max_tokens_per_second, priority_weight, weight_decay_window_ms.
- Sufficient observability: synthetic journey for tenant with end-to-end p50/p95 thresholds.

Validation tests (pre-go-live)

Execute the validation harness below in a staging environment that mirrors production network topology. Document all results in the onboarding ticket.

1) Baseline characterization
- Inject steady traffic at target_rps for 10 minutes. Verify p95 latency < tenant_p95_slo and error rate < 0.1%.
- Required metrics: ingress.rps, egress.bytes/s, kvcache.hit_rate.

2) Burst resilience check
- Ramp all tenant test clients from target_rps to burst_rps over 60s. Observe fallback behavior. Expected outcome: lower-priority tenants receive graceful rate-limiting; high-priority tenants retain >= 85% of baseline throughput.

3) Egress saturation simulation
- Throttle external egress (eg simulate 40% link loss). Verify that fallback to local cache and degraded-mode responses occur: response codes 200 (degraded ok) or 503 (if hard fail) per policy. Verify circuit-breaker triggers at configured thresholds.

4) Fair-share fairness validation
- Run 10 tenants simultaneously with varied priority_weight values. Measure delivered RPS per tenant and verify proportional share within +/- 10% of configured weights.

Pre-Go-Live checklist (must pass all items)

- [ ] Capacity profile uploaded and approved.
- [ ] Tenant proxy config reviewed and committed to config repo (reviewer: Eng Platform).
- [ ] Synthetic journey created and scheduled for tenant.
- [ ] Alerting rules created: high egress utilization (>85%), proxy queue_length > 50, fair-share-imbalance > 15%.
- [ ] Backstop fallback policy documented in the customer runbook (customer-facing).

Go-Live steps (recommended timebox: 60–90 minutes)

1) Golden path smoke (0–10m)
- Verify ingress/routing is pointing to Dedicated/Private capacity.
- Run 2 quick synthetic requests and validate correlation IDs flow through logs.

2) Canary ramp (10–30m)
- Start with 5% of traffic routed to new tenant environment. Monitor p50/p95, error rate, and proxy queue.
- If p95 increases > 25% baseline or error rate > 0.5%, abort canary and roll back routing.

3) Gradual ramp (30–60m)
- Increase traffic by 20% every 10 minutes, monitoring fairness and egress usage.
- Confirm fair-share controller is distributing capacity proportionally.

4) Full cutover and verification (60–90m)
- Update routing to 100%. Keep enhanced monitoring for 6 hours. Document any anomalies.

Degraded-mode behavior and automatic fallback

- Soft-fallback: When proxy queue_length > 75 or egress_bandwidth >= 90% for > 30s, the system applies fair-share throttling and enables degraded responses for non-critical endpoints (e.g., background embeddings refresh).
- Hard-fallback: If requests.latency.p99 > 3x SLO for > 2 minutes, or error rate > 2% for > 5 minutes, fail open to read-only cached responses and notify SRE.
- Customer notification: For hard-fallbacks, Customer Success must send a templated notification within 30 minutes describing impact and mitigation steps.

Automated remediation actions (ordered)

1) Activate fair-share throttling profile (fast path).
2) Evict low-priority background requests for 60s.
3) Route new tenant traffic to lower-cost region if cross-region fallback policy exists and region latency delta < 50ms.
4) Provision burst capacity (manual escalation to capacity team if autoscale cannot allocate).

Telemetry, alerts, and SLOs to configure

- Metrics to send to metrics-ingest: tenant.{id}.ingress_rps, tenant.{id}.egress_bps, tenant.{id}.delivered_rps, tenant.{id}.throttle_rate, tenant.{id}.priority_share_pct.
- Core alerts (severity mapping):
  - P0: egress_bandwidth >= 95% for > 3m (pager)
  - P1: proxy.queue_length >= 75 for > 2m (page + slack)
  - P2: fair-share-imbalance > 20% for > 10m (slack only)
- Suggested SLOs (tenant-level):
  - Availability: 99.95% (excluding scheduled maintenance)
  - Latency p95: tenant-specific (recommended default 300ms)
  - Throttling transparency: any throttled request must return `X-Redwood-Throttle: true` header and include an advisory in response body where applicable.

Escalation matrix and playbook actions

1) First responder (SRE oncall) — Triage, perform automated remediation steps 1–3.
2) If unresolved after 15 minutes — SRE L2 and Customer Success notified; open incident channel and begin stakeholder comms.
3) If unresolved after 60 minutes — Eng Platform and Capacity team pager.
4) If unresolved after 3 hours — Executive escalation and formal incident postmortem.

Incident comms template (short)

Subject: [Incident] Tenant {tenant_id} - Egress congestion detected\
Body:
- Impact: Elevated latency and partial degradation for tenant {tenant_id}.
- Timeline: {start_time} UTC
- Mitigation: Applied fair-share throttling and evicted background requests; routing adjusted.
- Next steps: Capacity team provisioning in progress; monitoring in place.

Post-incident items and QBR inputs

- Document the root cause, timeline, and mitigation in the incident ticket.
- Recommend capacity or architectural changes: e.g., increase egress pool by N GPUs or enable region-fallback by default.
- Add the incident to the next QBR with the following artifacts: synthetic journey traces, request/response samples with correlation IDs, and fairness delta graphs.

Testing scenarios matrix (examples)

| Scenario | Goal | Expected outcome | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Single-tenant burst | Verify high-priority preserved | High-priority tenant keeps >=85% throughput | Run for 30m
| Multi-tenant simultaneous burst | Validate fair-share | Proportional throughput within +/-10% | Use 10 tenants
| Egress link failure | Validate degraded-mode | Cached responses + advisory header | Simulate packet loss at edge

Appendix: sample tenant_proxy.conf snippet

# Example TOML snippet for fair-share controller
[fair_share]
max_tokens_per_second = 1200
priority_weights = { gold = 10, standard = 5, trial = 1 }
weight_decay_window_ms = 60000
eviction_policy = "background-first"

Appendix: basic CLI checks for oncall

# Verify current queue lengths and throttle rates (run on proxy-host)
curl -sS http://proxy-host.local/metrics | grep tenant.{tenant_id}.throttle_rate

# Force a synthetic request with correlation id
curl -H "X-Correlation-ID: onboarding-123" -X POST https://tenant-ingress.example/v1/generate -d '{"prompt": "smoke test"}' -v

Recovery and rollback guidance

- Immediate rollback: Re-route traffic back to incumbent environment via edge-router config if p95 > 2x SLO during canary.
- Long-term remediation: Schedule capacity expansion, add egress caching layer for large-response endpoints, or adjust tenant priority weights for steady-state fairness.

Document history and change log

- 2026-01-15: Created by Amira Patel (initial draft).
- 2026-03-02: Added automated remediation ordering and telemetry section (reviewer: Diego Morales).
- 2026-08-02: Published with expanded testing matrix and CLI snippets (reviewer: Priya Natarajan).

Related pages and references

- Dedicated tenant cutover safety-net playbook — see related_pages for link.
- Synthetic health suite implementation guide (health-and-qbr).
- Eng SRE capacity profiling guide for tenant planning.

End of playbook

This playbook describes the operational procedures, validation tests, and escalation steps for tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling for Dedicated and Private tenants. It targets customer-success, SRE, and platform engineers responsible for onboarding enterprise customers with unpredictable burst traffic or constrained egress capacity.

Goals

- Ensure predictability for tenant traffic when outbound (egress) capacity is constrained.
- Maintain latency SLOs for high-priority tenants while applying fair-share policies to noisy neighbors.
- Provide a deterministic automated fallback that preserves availability and preserves customer data guarantees.

When to use this playbook

source.title: Client-proxy Shaping & Adaptive Cost-cap Handoff Playbook
source.author: Lina Morales
source.content:
Overview
This playbook documents a proven integration pattern for enterprise customers that run client-side proxies, API gateways, or feature-specific SDKs and want to negotiate an explicit cost and quality handoff with Redwood's ingress fairshare system. The pattern reduces noisy-neighbor impact, enforces per-feature cost caps before requests reach Redwood, and preserves billing fidelity for reconciliation.

Audience
- Customer Success Engineers (CSEs) and Solutions Engineers
- Customer Platform / SRE teams
- Finance and Cost Ops contacts involved in usage reconciliation

Objectives
- Enforce feature-level hard cost caps at the edge to prevent runaway spend.
- Maintain observable request provenance so Redwood billing attributes usage correctly.
- Reduce server-side throttles and fallbacks by 50% for shaped tenants.
- Provide graceful degradation semantics (priority lanes + adaptive backoff) that preserve user experience when ingress is constrained.

When to use this playbook
- Customer experiences unpredictable spikes tied to one product feature (e.g., mass ingestion, realtime search).
- Customer operates a gateway, service mesh, or sidecar and can add shaping logic.
- Customer requires pre-commit budget enforcement for compliance or fiscal controls.

Conceptual flow
1) Feature policy defined in customer policy repo (JSON/YAML).
2) Client proxy enforces policy locally (hard caps, soft quotas, and priority tags).
3) Proxy forwards requests with provenance headers and a numeric budget token.
4) Redwood ingress maps those headers to tenant and feature metrics, honoring X-Client-Priority and X-Cost-Cap markers as advisory controls where supported.
5) When ingress applies backpressure, Redwood emits X-Redwood-Backpressure and a Retry-After; proxy must honor and adapt traffic.

Key terms
- Feature budget: an hourly or daily token allotment mapped to a logical feature (e.g., indexer, summarizer).
- Hard cap: an enforceable limit that rejects requests when exceeded at the client proxy.
- Soft quota: a throttling level that attempts to smooth traffic with queued leaky-bucket logic.
- Priority lane: a label indicating importance; mapped from client 0..3 -> Redwood lanes e.g., background, standard, interactive, critical.

Required headers (observability & billing)
- X-Redwood-Tenant: <tenant-id>  (mandatory)
- X-Feature-Name: <feature>  (recommended)
- X-Client-Priority: <0|1|2|3>  (recommended)
- X-Client-CostCap: <numeric-units>  (optional advisory)
- traceparent / tracestate  (mandatory for distributed tracing)

Sample client policy (YAML)
---
policies:
  - feature: conversation-summarizer
    window: 1h
    hard_cap_tokens: 200000
    soft_quota_tokens: 50000
    priority: 2
    retry_policy:
      type: exponential
      base_ms: 250
      cap_ms: 4000
      max_attempts: 4

Edge enforcement behavior
- On request: proxy checks feature budget. If below soft_quota, proceed. If below hard_cap but above 0, allow with X-Client-Priority downgraded and mark telemetry. If hard_cap exhausted, return HTTP 402 / 429 to caller with structured body explaining cost cap reason.
- On Redwood X-Backpressure response: proxy reduces egress and demotes non-critical priorities.
- Telemetry: each forwarded request must include tokens_requested and estimated_tokens in the trace span so Redwood can match billing signals.

Integration checklist (pre-onboard to go-live)
1) Policy workshop (owner: CSE)
   - Determine per-feature windows, caps, and contact list.
   - Agree on header contract and where secrets/token mapping occurs.
2) Implement policy in staging proxy (owner: customer infra)
   - Deploy features with policy toggles and enable detailed debug tracing for first canary.
3) Instrument request telemetry (owner: customer infra + Redwoods telemetry team)
   - Ensure tokens_requested and tokens_consumed fields propagate to Redwood billing pipeline.
4) Synthetic surge test (owner: joint)
   - Simulate feature storm at 3x expected peak for 15 minutes. Validate ingress backpressure and that proxy enforces hard caps.
5) Billing reconciliation test (owner: finance + platform)
   - Create sample runs and match recorded usage to expected consumption within 3% tolerance.
6) Gradual rollout (owner: CSE + SRE)
   - Phased percentages: 0% -> 20% -> 50% -> 100% with 1-hour stability gates.

Prometheus examples (validation queries)
- increase(redwood_ingress_throttles_total{tenant="TENANT_ID",feature="conversation-summarizer"}[15m])
- histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_token_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, tenant, feature))
- increase(client_proxy_costcap_denials_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[1h])

Rollout gating policy
- Canary -> 20%: no increase in ingress_throttles > 10% and 95p latency <= +12%
- 20% -> 50%: billing reconciliation for sample traffic within 3% and error rate < 0.5%
- 50% -> 100%: stable for 24 hours and no escalations in oncall channel

Runbook: common failure modes and steps
- Problem: Missing X-Redwood-Tenant header
  - Action: Confirm proxy header map; check upstream load balancer that may strip headers; redeploy fix.
- Problem: Billing mismatch after shaping
  - Action: Collect trace IDs for the period; correlate tokens_requested in proxy traces vs tokens_consumed in Redwood logs; open billing ticket with platform team.
- Problem: Proxy not honoring Retry-After and causing 429 loops
  - Action: Engage customer infra to enforce Retry-After. Temporarily reduce client concurrency on the proxy while investigating.

Escalation matrix (contacts)
- Tier 1 (CSE): Lina Morales (lina.morales@redwood.ai)
- Tier 2 (SRE liaison): Ethan Park (ethan.park@redwood.ai)
- Billing owner: Priya Singh (priya.singh@redwood.ai)
- Security contact (if header tampering suspected): Anisha Rao (anisha.rao@redwood.ai)

When to open an incident
- Uncontrolled spend where proxy denied requests but billing shows continued usage (>500 USD unexpected within 1h)
- Persistent 429/502 loops after proxy deployed for >5 minutes for majority of shaped traffic
- Discrepancy between proxy telemetry and Redwood billing > 5% for a production window

Testing & verification snippets
- Curl example (shows passing headers):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" 
    -H "X-Redwood-Tenant: tenant-corp-99" 
    -H "X-Feature-Name: conversation-summarizer" 
    -H "X-Client-Priority: 2" 
    -H "X-Client-CostCap: 200000" 
    -d '{"model":"xlarge","prompt":"summarize: ..."}'

- Local test: run the proxy with policy in dry-run mode for 1 hour and compare local denials to redwood_ingress_throttles_total. Expect denials to match or exceed ingress throttles when proxy is active.

Telemetry fields required for downstream reconciliation
- trace_id (string)
- tenant_id (string)
- feature_name (string)
- tokens_requested (int)
- tokens_consumed (int)
- client_priority (int)

Operational notes and best practices
- Prefer conservative hard caps at first; increase soft quotas after 72-hour stable run.
- Keep the feature name stable across code changes; renaming features creates split billing artifacts.
- If customers use multiple proxies/CDNs, run the policy in the earliest hop that has visibility into user intent to avoid double-enforcement.

Appendix A: Example backoff configuration (JSON encoded string inside proxy)
{
  "type": "exp",
  "base_ms": 250,
  "cap_ms": 4000,
  "max_attempts": 4
}

Appendix B: Short troubleshooting checklist (for oncall)
- Confirm headers in Redwood ingress logs for the tenant and feature
- Pull traces for 5 representative requests that were billed during the period
- Verify proxy's denial counter vs Redwood throttle metrics
- If mismatch persists, escalate to Billing owner with trace ids and sample logs

Document maintenance
- Owner: Lina Morales (Customer Success)
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any billing pipeline change
- Last review: 2026-07-18 (updates: clarified telemetry keys and added gating thresholds)

Runbook: common failure modes and steps
- Problem: Missing X-Redwood-Tenant header
  - Action: Confirm proxy header map; check upstream load balancer that may strip headers; redeploy fix.
- Problem: Billing mismatch after shaping
  - Action: Collect trace IDs for the period; correlate tokens_requested in proxy traces vs tokens_consumed in Redwood logs; open billing ticket with platform team.
- Problem: Proxy not honoring Retry-After and causing 429 loops
  - Action: Engage customer infra to enforce Retry-After. Temporarily reduce client concurrency on the proxy while investigating.

Escalation matrix (contacts)
- Tier 1 (CSE): Lina Morales (lina.morales@redwood.ai)
- Tier 2 (SRE liaison): Ethan Park (ethan.park@redwood.ai)
- Billing owner: Priya Singh (priya.singh@redwood.ai)
- Security contact (if header tampering suspected): Anisha Rao (anisha.rao@redwood.ai)

When to open an incident
- Uncontrolled spend where proxy denied requests but billing shows continued usage (>500 USD unexpected within 1h)
- Persistent 429/502 loops after proxy deployed for >5 minutes for majority of shaped traffic
- Discrepancy between proxy telemetry and Redwood billing > 5% for a production window

Testing & verification snippets
- Curl example (shows passing headers):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" 
    -H "X-Redwood-Tenant: tenant-corp-99" 
    -H "X-Feature-Name: conversation-summarizer" 
    -H "X-Client-Priority: 2" 
    -H "X-Client-CostCap: 200000" 
    -d '{"model":"xlarge","prompt":"summarize: ..."}'

- Local test: run the proxy with policy in dry-run mode for 1 hour and compare local denials to redwood_ingress_throttles_total. Expect denials to match or exceed ingress throttles when proxy is active.

Telemetry fields required for downstream reconciliation
- trace_id (string)
- tenant_id (string)
- feature_name (string)
- tokens_requested (int)
- tokens_consumed (int)
- client_priority (int)

Operational notes and best practices
- Prefer conservative hard caps at first; increase soft quotas after 72-hour stable run.
- Keep the feature name stable across code changes; renaming features creates split billing artifacts.
- If customers use multiple proxies/CDNs, run the policy in the earliest hop that has visibility into user intent to avoid double-enforcement.

Appendix A: Example backoff configuration (JSON encoded string inside proxy)
{
  "type": "exp",
  "base_ms": 250,
  "cap_ms": 4000,
  "max_attempts": 4
}

Appendix B: Short troubleshooting checklist (for oncall)
- Confirm headers in Redwood ingress logs for the tenant and feature
- Pull traces for 5 representative requests that were billed during the period
- Verify proxy's denial counter vs Redwood throttle metrics
- If mismatch persists, escalate to Billing owner with trace ids and sample logs

Document maintenance
- Owner: Lina Morales (Customer Success)
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any billing pipeline change
- Last review: 2026-07-18 (updates: clarified telemetry keys and added gating thresholds)
text:
Overview
This playbook documents a proven integration pattern for enterprise customers that run client-side proxies, API gateways, or feature-specific SDKs and want to negotiate an explicit cost and quality handoff with Redwood's ingress fairshare system. The pattern reduces noisy-neighbor impact, enforces per-feature cost caps before requests reach Redwood, and preserves billing fidelity for reconciliation.

Audience
- Customer Success Engineers (CSEs) and Solutions Engineers
- Customer Platform / SRE teams
- Finance and Cost Ops contacts involved in usage reconciliation

Objectives
- Enforce feature-level hard cost caps at the edge to prevent runaway spend.
- Maintain observable request provenance so Redwood billing attributes usage correctly.
- Reduce server-side throttles and fallbacks by 50% for shaped tenants.
- Provide graceful degradation semantics (priority lanes + adaptive backoff) that preserve user experience when ingress is constrained.

When to use this playbook
- Customer experiences unpredictable spikes tied to one product feature (e.g., mass ingestion, realtime search).
- Customer operates a gateway, service mesh, or sidecar and can add shaping logic.
- Customer requires pre-commit budget enforcement for compliance or fiscal controls.

Conceptual flow
1) Feature policy defined in customer policy repo (JSON/YAML).
2) Client proxy enforces policy locally (hard caps, soft quotas, and priority tags).
3) Proxy forwards requests with provenance headers and a numeric budget token.
4) Redwood ingress maps those headers to tenant and feature metrics, honoring X-Client-Priority and X-Cost-Cap markers as advisory controls where supported.
5) When ingress applies backpressure, Redwood emits X-Redwood-Backpressure and a Retry-After; proxy must honor and adapt traffic.

Key terms
- Feature budget: an hourly or daily token allotment mapped to a logical feature (e.g., indexer, summarizer).
- Hard cap: an enforceable limit that rejects requests when exceeded at the client proxy.
- Soft quota: a throttling level that attempts to smooth traffic with queued leaky-bucket logic.
- Priority lane: a label indicating importance; mapped from client 0..3 -> Redwood lanes e.g., background, standard, interactive, critical.

Required headers (observability & billing)
- X-Redwood-Tenant: <tenant-id>  (mandatory)
- X-Feature-Name: <feature>  (recommended)
- X-Client-Priority: <0|1|2|3>  (recommended)
- X-Client-CostCap: <numeric-units>  (optional advisory)
- traceparent / tracestate  (mandatory for distributed tracing)

Sample client policy (YAML)
---
policies:
  - feature: conversation-summarizer
    window: 1h
    hard_cap_tokens: 200000
    soft_quota_tokens: 50000
    priority: 2
    retry_policy:
      type: exponential
      base_ms: 250
      cap_ms: 4000
      max_attempts: 4

Edge enforcement behavior
- On request: proxy checks feature budget. If below soft_quota, proceed. If below hard_cap but above 0, allow with X-Client-Priority downgraded and mark telemetry. If hard_cap exhausted, return HTTP 402 / 429 to caller with structured body explaining cost cap reason.
- On Redwood X-Backpressure response: proxy reduces egress and demotes non-critical priorities.
- Telemetry: each forwarded request must include tokens_requested and estimated_tokens in the trace span so Redwood can match billing signals.

Integration checklist (pre-onboard to go-live)
1) Policy workshop (owner: CSE)
   - Determine per-feature windows, caps, and contact list.
   - Agree on header contract and where secrets/token mapping occurs.
2) Implement policy in staging proxy (owner: customer infra)
   - Deploy features with policy toggles and enable detailed debug tracing for first canary.
3) Instrument request telemetry (owner: customer infra + Redwoods telemetry team)
   - Ensure tokens_requested and tokens_consumed fields propagate to Redwood billing pipeline.
4) Synthetic surge test (owner: joint)
   - Simulate feature storm at 3x expected peak for 15 minutes. Validate ingress backpressure and that proxy enforces hard caps.
5) Billing reconciliation test (owner: finance + platform)
   - Create sample runs and match recorded usage to expected consumption within 3% tolerance.
6) Gradual rollout (owner: CSE + SRE)
   - Phased percentages: 0% -> 20% -> 50% -> 100% with 1-hour stability gates.

Prometheus examples (validation queries)
- increase(redwood_ingress_throttles_total{tenant="TENANT_ID",feature="conversation-summarizer"}[15m])
- histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_token_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, tenant, feature))
- increase(client_proxy_costcap_denials_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[1h])

Rollout gating policy
- Canary -> 20%: no increase in ingress_throttles > 10% and 95p latency <= +12%
- 20% -> 50%: billing reconciliation for sample traffic within 3% and error rate < 0.5%
- 50% -> 100%: stable for 24 hours and no escalations in oncall channel

Runbook: common failure modes and steps
- Problem: Missing X-Redwood-Tenant header
  - Action: Confirm proxy header map; check upstream load balancer that may strip headers; redeploy fix.
- Problem: Billing mismatch after shaping
  - Action: Collect trace IDs for the period; correlate tokens_requested in proxy traces vs tokens_consumed in Redwood logs; open billing ticket with platform team.
- Problem: Proxy not honoring Retry-After and causing 429 loops
  - Action: Engage customer infra to enforce Retry-After. Temporarily reduce client concurrency on the proxy while investigating.

Escalation matrix (contacts)
- Tier 1 (CSE): Lina Morales (lina.morales@redwood.ai)
- Tier 2 (SRE liaison): Ethan Park (ethan.park@redwood.ai)
- Billing owner: Priya Singh (priya.singh@redwood.ai)
- Security contact (if header tampering suspected): Anisha Rao (anisha.rao@redwood.ai)

When to open an incident
- Uncontrolled spend where proxy denied requests but billing shows continued usage (>500 USD unexpected within 1h)
- Persistent 429/502 loops after proxy deployed for >5 minutes for majority of shaped traffic
- Discrepancy between proxy telemetry and Redwood billing > 5% for a production window

Testing & verification snippets
- Curl example (shows passing headers):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" 
    -H "X-Redwood-Tenant: tenant-corp-99" 
    -H "X-Feature-Name: conversation-summarizer" 
    -H "X-Client-Priority: 2" 
    -H "X-Client-CostCap: 200000" 
    -d '{"model":"xlarge","prompt":"summarize: ..."}'

- Local test: run the proxy with policy in dry-run mode for 1 hour and compare local denials to redwood_ingress_throttles_total. Expect denials to match or exceed ingress throttles when proxy is active.

Telemetry fields required for downstream reconciliation
- trace_id (string)
- tenant_id (string)
- feature_name (string)
- tokens_requested (int)
- tokens_consumed (int)
- client_priority (int)

Operational notes and best practices
- Prefer conservative hard caps at first; increase soft quotas after 72-hour stable run.
- Keep the feature name stable across code changes; renaming features creates split billing artifacts.
- If customers use multiple proxies/CDNs, run the policy in the earliest hop that has visibility into user intent to avoid double-enforcement.

Appendix A: Example backoff configuration (JSON encoded string inside proxy)
{
  "type": "exp",
  "base_ms": 250,
  "cap_ms": 4000,
  "max_attempts": 4
}

Appendix B: Short troubleshooting checklist (for oncall)
- Confirm headers in Redwood ingress logs for the tenant and feature
- Pull traces for 5 representative requests that were billed during the period
- Verify proxy's denial counter vs Redwood throttle metrics
- If mismatch persists, escalate to Billing owner with trace ids and sample logs

Document maintenance
- Owner: Lina Morales (Customer Success)
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any billing pipeline change
- Last review: 2026-07-18 (updates: clarified telemetry keys and added gating thresholds)

source.title: Automated Degradation Classifier and Customer Failover Runbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemetry mappings, alert routing, escalation windows, and step-by-step procedures for automated and manual remediation.

Goals

- Detect real customer-impacting degradation (latency, error-rate, model-quality drift) with <80% false-positive rate in steady-state traffic.
- Automate non-destructive mitigations (routing to fallback model variants, traffic shaping) within 5 minutes of classification.
- Provide clear, auditable customer communications and playbook for escalation to engineering/ops.

Scope

- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants and high-volume Hosted API customers with SLOs.
- Covers: inference latency P95, error rate, model-generation-quality regression (eval score deltas), KV cache hit-rate, and telemetry signal health (ingest gaps).
- Out of scope: customer billing disputes, hardware-only incidents covered by cloud-provider runbooks.

Telemetry and Signals

Key signals the ADC consumes (priority order):
- p95_inference_latency_ms (per-tenant, 1m/5m windows)
- request_error_rate_pct (HTTP 5xx + model-serve-specific rejects)
- model_eval_delta_pct (per-customer prompt-set baseline vs rolling 24h)
- kvcache_hit_rate_pct (prefix/KV reuse)
- telemetry_ingest_lag_sec (agent/sidecar -> collector)
- synthetic_user_synthetic_score (synthetic journeys score: 0-1)

Signal mapping and derived metrics (table):
| Metric | Window | Threshold (class = degraded) | Notes |
| p95_inference_latency_ms | 5m | >= 1200 ms and delta >= 2x baseline | Baselines stored per-tenant in telemetry-store |
| request_error_rate_pct | 5m | >= 1.5% absolute or 3x baseline | Exclude client-side timeouts |
| model_eval_delta_pct | 24h | >= 7% absolute drop on evaluation suite | Use tenant-specific eval prompt-set |
| kvcache_hit_rate_pct | 10m | drop >= 30% from baseline | Indicates cache invalidation/regression |
| telemetry_ingest_lag_sec | 2m | > 60s | May indicate sidecar/drop or pipeline issue |

Classification logic (simplified):
1) Ingest real-time signals into ADC engine (stream).
2) Compute z-score of each metric vs baseline; apply weights: latency 0.35, error-rate 0.3, model-eval 0.25, kvcache 0.05, ingest-lag 0.05.
3) If weighted score >= 0.7 AND at least two signals exceed thresholds, label tenant_state = "degraded".
4) Run fast deterministic checks (synthetic journey drop, region outage markers).

False-positive protection and cooldowns

- Require sustained classification for 2 consecutive 1-minute intervals before any automated customer-impacting action.
- If classification flips back to healthy within 10 minutes, cancel automated mitigation and create an advisory event only.
- Max automated mitigation frequency per tenant: 3/hr.

Automated Mitigation Options (ordered by least-intrusive first)

1) Lightweight routing fallback: switch small percentage (10-25%) of traffic to a lower-capacity but stable model variant for 3–10 minutes.
2) Dynamic batching adjustment: temporarily increase batching window for background, non-low-latency routes.
3) KV cache warmup prefetch for affected model shards (if kvcache hit-rate drop detected).
4) Regional failover: redirect traffic to secondary region if regional capacity or infra flags are present.
5) Tenant soft-throttle: shed best-effort/lowest-priority routes (e.g., embeddings bulk jobs) while preserving interactive tokens.

Each mitigation must be reversible via the ADC control plane and is subject to TTL and canary rollback.

Escalation Matrix and Ownership

- ADC Alert level 1 (detect only): Customer Success on-call paged by PagerDuty; SRE watch notified (Slack #sre-observe) — 15 minute SLAs for initial triage.
- ADC Alert level 2 (automated mitigation triggered): SRE & Serving Runtime on-call paged; CS notified for customer comms — 10 minute SLA for mitigation validation.
- ADC Alert level 3 (automated mitigation failed or model-quality regression persisted >30m): Incident Commander activated, full incident playbook (see related_pages for incident process).

Routing to escalation teams (table):
| Condition | Primary Team | Secondary Team | Escalation Channel | PagerDuty Policy |
| latency-only | eng-serving-runtime | eng-infra | #serving-runtime | PD-policy-serving |
| error-rate | eng-sre | eng-serving-runtime | #sre-oncall | PD-policy-sre |
| model-eval-delta | applied-ml-and-evals | eng-serving-runtime | #applied-ml | PD-policy-ml |
| telemetry-ingest | eng-infra | customer-success | #telemetry | PD-policy-infra |

Customer Communications (templates)

- Advisory (detect only): "We are monitoring an elevated latency signal affecting X% of your requests and are investigating. No action has been taken. We will follow up within 15 minutes."
- Mitigation notice (automated action): "We detected a service degradation and routed a small percentage of traffic to a stable model variant to preserve availability. We will continue to monitor and notify you of material changes."
- Escalation/update: "Mitigation attempted succeeded/rolled back. Next step: engineering investigation underway; ETA 60 minutes for next update."

All outbound customer messages are logged to the customer-notification-audit stream with template_id and PD-incident id.

Operational Runbook: Step-by-step

1) Detection (automatic)
  a) ADC marks tenant_state = degraded.
  b) ADC posts event to incident-bridge and creates internal ticket (prefix ADC-<tenant>-<ts>).
  c) PagerDuty route triggers based on classification.
2) Initial human triage (CS or SRE) — within 15 minutes
  a) Validate telemetry (run provided queries below).
  b) Check for known issues: maintenance, provider alerts, model rollout windows.
  c) If false positive (e.g., client-side spike), tag ticket and resolve; annotate ADC baseline if needed.
3) If mitigation allowed (policy check) and classification sustained -> ADC triggers mitigation.
4) Post-mitigation verification (5–10 minutes)
  a) Verify p95 latency and error-rate returned to within 90% of baseline.
  b) If improved, send Mitigation notice to customer; schedule 1-hour monitoring window.
  c) If not improved, escalate to Level 3.
5) Full incident (if escalated)
  a) Run full incident template (see oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process).
  b) Capture RCA inputs: model binary, kernel config, KV cache eviction logs, batch window changes.

Observability Queries and Debug Helpers

- Example Prometheus-like queries:
  p95_inference_latency_ms{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m]
  sum(rate(request_errors_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) * 100
  kvcache_hit_rate_pct{tenant="TENANT_ID"}
- Synthetic journey probe: synthetic_probe_status{tenant="TENANT_ID",journey="interactive-check"}
- Model-eval delta: fetch latest eval-run and compare to tenant-baseline stored in eval-store: eval_store.query(tenant_id=..., window=24h)

Diagnostic artifacts to collect for RCA

- ADC classification payload (raw signals and weights)
- Serving runtime shard logs for affected model version (last 60 minutes)
- Kernel scheduling trace (if latency spike)
- KV cache eviction and rewarm traces
- Customer request-id traces (correlated via request-correlation-id header)

Policy and Access Controls

- Only SRE and CS on-call roles can approve automated mitigations for regulated tenants.
- ADC control plane actions are auditable and logged to the CIAM audit stream for 13 months.
- Make/Model variant list that ADC may route to is controlled by Applied ML (whitelist per-tenant).

Testing and Validation

- Quarterly game-days: induce synthetic degradations for 10 tenants across Dedicated and Private modes to validate ADC detection and mitigation runbook.
- Canary tests: ADC changes must pass a canary window of 48h in staging with monitored false-positive rate < 8%.

Appendix: Example alert payload (JSON)

{
  "tenant_id": "tenant_acme_inc",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-15T10:12:34Z",
  "signals": {
    "p95_inference_latency_ms": 1850,
    "request_error_rate_pct": 2.1,
    "model_eval_delta_pct": 8.4
  },
  "weighted_score": 0.78,
  "classification": "degraded",
  "recommended_action": "lightweight-routing-fallback",
  "ticket_id": "ADC-tenant_acme_inc-20260715T101234Z"
}

Appendix: Quick lookup checklist (one-page)

- Is ADC classification sustained for 2 consecutive minutes?
- Does telemetry show at least two signals above threshold?
- Has the tenant reached automated mitigation cap (3/hr)?
- Is tenant regulated (manual approval required)?
- If mitigation: monitor 5–10 minutes, then notify customer.

Related pages and references

- Incident response: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process
- Telemetry schema and baselines: /confluence/eng-infra/observability-standards/telemetry-schema-v2
- Model rollback playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/model-rollback-guidelines

Owners and contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Customer Success)
- ADC engineering owner: Miguel Sanchez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead for escalations: Daniel Park (eng-sre)

Change log

- 2026-03-10: Created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-05-02: Added KV cache mitigation and updated thresholds (review: Ravi Menon).
- 2026-07-15: Published stabilization SLA clarifications and updated sample payload example (review: Daniel Park, Sofia Patel).
