  "ticket_id": "ADC-tenant_acme_inc-20260715T101234Z"
}

Appendix: Quick lookup checklist (one-page)

- Is ADC classification sustained for 2 consecutive minutes?
- Does telemetry show at least two signals above threshold?
- Has the tenant reached automated mitigation cap (3/hr)?
- Is tenant regulated (manual approval required)?
- If mitigation: monitor 5–10 minutes, then notify customer.

Related pages and references

- Incident response: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process
- Telemetry schema and baselines: /confluence/eng-infra/observability-standards/telemetry-schema-v2
- Model rollback playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/model-rollback-guidelines

Owners and contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Customer Success)
- ADC engineering owner: Miguel Sanchez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead for escalations: Daniel Park (eng-sre)

Change log

- 2026-03-10: Created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-05-02: Added KV cache mitigation and updated thresholds (review: Ravi Menon).
- 2026-07-15: Published stabilization SLA clarifications and updated sample payload example (review: Daniel Park, Sofia Patel).
text:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemetry mappings, alert routing, escalation windows, and step-by-step procedures for automated and manual remediation.

Goals

- Detect real customer-impacting degradation (latency, error-rate, model-quality drift) with <80% false-positive rate in steady-state traffic.
- Automate non-destructive mitigations (routing to fallback model variants, traffic shaping) within 5 minutes of classification.
- Provide clear, auditable customer communications and playbook for escalation to engineering/ops.

Scope

- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants and high-volume Hosted API customers with SLOs.
- Covers: inference latency P95, error rate, model-generation-quality regression (eval score deltas), KV cache hit-rate, and telemetry signal health (ingest gaps).
- Out of scope: customer billing disputes, hardware-only incidents covered by cloud-provider runbooks.

Telemetry and Signals

Key signals the ADC consumes (priority order):
- p95_inference_latency_ms (per-tenant, 1m/5m windows)
- request_error_rate_pct (HTTP 5xx + model-serve-specific rejects)
- model_eval_delta_pct (per-customer prompt-set baseline vs rolling 24h)
- kvcache_hit_rate_pct (prefix/KV reuse)
- telemetry_ingest_lag_sec (agent/sidecar -> collector)
- synthetic_user_synthetic_score (synthetic journeys score: 0-1)

Signal mapping and derived metrics (table):
| Metric | Window | Threshold (class = degraded) | Notes |
| p95_inference_latency_ms | 5m | >= 1200 ms and delta >= 2x baseline | Baselines stored per-tenant in telemetry-store |
| request_error_rate_pct | 5m | >= 1.5% absolute or 3x baseline | Exclude client-side timeouts |
| model_eval_delta_pct | 24h | >= 7% absolute drop on evaluation suite | Use tenant-specific eval prompt-set |
| kvcache_hit_rate_pct | 10m | drop >= 30% from baseline | Indicates cache invalidation/regression |
| telemetry_ingest_lag_sec | 2m | > 60s | May indicate sidecar/drop or pipeline issue |

Classification logic (simplified):
1) Ingest real-time signals into ADC engine (stream).
2) Compute z-score of each metric vs baseline; apply weights: latency 0.35, error-rate 0.3, model-eval 0.25, kvcache 0.05, ingest-lag 0.05.
3) If weighted score >= 0.7 AND at least two signals exceed thresholds, label tenant_state = "degraded".
4) Run fast deterministic checks (synthetic journey drop, region outage markers).

False-positive protection and cooldowns

- Require sustained classification for 2 consecutive 1-minute intervals before any automated customer-impacting action.
- If classification flips back to healthy within 10 minutes, cancel automated mitigation and create an advisory event only.
- Max automated mitigation frequency per tenant: 3/hr.

Automated Mitigation Options (ordered by least-intrusive first)

1) Lightweight routing fallback: switch small percentage (10-25%) of traffic to a lower-capacity but stable model variant for 3–10 minutes.
2) Dynamic batching adjustment: temporarily increase batching window for background, non-low-latency routes.
3) KV cache warmup prefetch for affected model shards (if kvcache hit-rate drop detected).
4) Regional failover: redirect traffic to secondary region if regional capacity or infra flags are present.
5) Tenant soft-throttle: shed best-effort/lowest-priority routes (e.g., embeddings bulk jobs) while preserving interactive tokens.

Each mitigation must be reversible via the ADC control plane and is subject to TTL and canary rollback.

Escalation Matrix and Ownership

- ADC Alert level 1 (detect only): Customer Success on-call paged by PagerDuty; SRE watch notified (Slack #sre-observe) — 15 minute SLAs for initial triage.
- ADC Alert level 2 (automated mitigation triggered): SRE & Serving Runtime on-call paged; CS notified for customer comms — 10 minute SLA for mitigation validation.
- ADC Alert level 3 (automated mitigation failed or model-quality regression persisted >30m): Incident Commander activated, full incident playbook (see related_pages for incident process).

Routing to escalation teams (table):
| Condition | Primary Team | Secondary Team | Escalation Channel | PagerDuty Policy |
| latency-only | eng-serving-runtime | eng-infra | #serving-runtime | PD-policy-serving |
| error-rate | eng-sre | eng-serving-runtime | #sre-oncall | PD-policy-sre |
| model-eval-delta | applied-ml-and-evals | eng-serving-runtime | #applied-ml | PD-policy-ml |
| telemetry-ingest | eng-infra | customer-success | #telemetry | PD-policy-infra |

Customer Communications (templates)

- Advisory (detect only): "We are monitoring an elevated latency signal affecting X% of your requests and are investigating. No action has been taken. We will follow up within 15 minutes."
- Mitigation notice (automated action): "We detected a service degradation and routed a small percentage of traffic to a stable model variant to preserve availability. We will continue to monitor and notify you of material changes."
- Escalation/update: "Mitigation attempted succeeded/rolled back. Next step: engineering investigation underway; ETA 60 minutes for next update."

All outbound customer messages are logged to the customer-notification-audit stream with template_id and PD-incident id.

Operational Runbook: Step-by-step

1) Detection (automatic)
  a) ADC marks tenant_state = degraded.
  b) ADC posts event to incident-bridge and creates internal ticket (prefix ADC-<tenant>-<ts>).
  c) PagerDuty route triggers based on classification.
2) Initial human triage (CS or SRE) — within 15 minutes
  a) Validate telemetry (run provided queries below).
  b) Check for known issues: maintenance, provider alerts, model rollout windows.
  c) If false positive (e.g., client-side spike), tag ticket and resolve; annotate ADC baseline if needed.
3) If mitigation allowed (policy check) and classification sustained -> ADC triggers mitigation.
4) Post-mitigation verification (5–10 minutes)
  a) Verify p95 latency and error-rate returned to within 90% of baseline.
  b) If improved, send Mitigation notice to customer; schedule 1-hour monitoring window.
  c) If not improved, escalate to Level 3.
5) Full incident (if escalated)
  a) Run full incident template (see oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process).
  b) Capture RCA inputs: model binary, kernel config, KV cache eviction logs, batch window changes.

Observability Queries and Debug Helpers

- Example Prometheus-like queries:
  p95_inference_latency_ms{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m]
  sum(rate(request_errors_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) * 100
  kvcache_hit_rate_pct{tenant="TENANT_ID"}
- Synthetic journey probe: synthetic_probe_status{tenant="TENANT_ID",journey="interactive-check"}
- Model-eval delta: fetch latest eval-run and compare to tenant-baseline stored in eval-store: eval_store.query(tenant_id=..., window=24h)

Diagnostic artifacts to collect for RCA

- ADC classification payload (raw signals and weights)
- Serving runtime shard logs for affected model version (last 60 minutes)
- Kernel scheduling trace (if latency spike)
- KV cache eviction and rewarm traces
- Customer request-id traces (correlated via request-correlation-id header)

Policy and Access Controls

- Only SRE and CS on-call roles can approve automated mitigations for regulated tenants.
- ADC control plane actions are auditable and logged to the CIAM audit stream for 13 months.
- Make/Model variant list that ADC may route to is controlled by Applied ML (whitelist per-tenant).

Testing and Validation

- Quarterly game-days: induce synthetic degradations for 10 tenants across Dedicated and Private modes to validate ADC detection and mitigation runbook.
- Canary tests: ADC changes must pass a canary window of 48h in staging with monitored false-positive rate < 8%.

Appendix: Example alert payload (JSON)

{
  "tenant_id": "tenant_acme_inc",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-15T10:12:34Z",
  "signals": {
    "p95_inference_latency_ms": 1850,
    "request_error_rate_pct": 2.1,
    "model_eval_delta_pct": 8.4
  },
  "weighted_score": 0.78,
  "classification": "degraded",
  "recommended_action": "lightweight-routing-fallback",
  "ticket_id": "ADC-tenant_acme_inc-20260715T101234Z"
}

Appendix: Quick lookup checklist (one-page)

- Is ADC classification sustained for 2 consecutive minutes?
- Does telemetry show at least two signals above threshold?
- Has the tenant reached automated mitigation cap (3/hr)?
- Is tenant regulated (manual approval required)?
- If mitigation: monitor 5–10 minutes, then notify customer.

Related pages and references

- Incident response: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process
- Telemetry schema and baselines: /confluence/eng-infra/observability-standards/telemetry-schema-v2
- Model rollback playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/model-rollback-guidelines

Owners and contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Customer Success)
- ADC engineering owner: Miguel Sanchez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead for escalations: Daniel Park (eng-sre)

Change log

- 2026-03-10: Created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-05-02: Added KV cache mitigation and updated thresholds (review: Ravi Menon).
- 2026-07-15: Published stabilization SLA clarifications and updated sample payload example (review: Daniel Park, Sofia Patel).

source.title: Enterprise LLM Behavior Delta Detection and Customer Triage Playbook
source.author: Priya Nair
source.body:
Summary:
This playbook documents a repeatable, measurable process for detecting and triaging behavioral deltas (quality regressions or shifts) in LLM outputs that materially affect enterprise customers. It focuses on automated detection signals, deterministic first-line triage, evidence packaging for root-cause analysis (RCA), and customer-safe communications. Use this playbook when telemetry or customers indicate unexpected changes in output behavior ( hallucinations, repeated refusal patterns, persona drift, or factual regressions ).

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted and Dedicated tenants (hosted API and dedicated capacity).
- Covers model serving changes, quantization/rewrites, prompt-layer changes, routing/fanout, and inference runtime rollouts.
- Does NOT replace safety escalation for clear safety violations; refer to the LLM Safety Escalation Matrix for safety-first incidents.

Goals and success criteria:
- Detect behavior deltas within 5 business minutes for enterprise customers with active synthetic monitors.
- First-line response (triage started) within 30 minutes of alert.
- Deliver a reproducible evidence bundle to eng/research within 2 business hours.
- Provide customer-facing summary or mitigation within 4 business hours for P1/P2 severity.

Signal sources (ranked):
1) Synthetic customer-specific probes (Customer Synthetic Suite) — highest fidelity.
2) EvalHub regression alerts (score delta > 3 std dev on key prompts).
3) Production telemetry anomalies: embedding drift, decrease in token-efficiency, unexpected inference latency-correlated quality drops.
4) Customer-reported examples via support ticket or CSE Slack.
5) Observability-sourced NLP-based diff alerts (ModelGuard behavioral clustering).

Key monitoring metrics and thresholds (examples):
- DeltaRate.alert: fraction of probe prompts with changed top-1 intent (threshold 0.2 => 20% of probes changed)
- Answer-Confidence-drop: moving average of model log-prob changes (threshold -1.5 nats per token)
- BLEU/ROUGE/PolicyScore degradation on gold prompts (threshold: 15% relative drop)
- Embedding-similarity mean shift (Cosine drop > 0.08)

Alerting and severity mapping:
- P1 (Customer-impacting production degradation): >50% probe failures OR direct revenue loss signal. Pager to oncall, #cust-incident, immediate exec notification.
- P2 (Material degradation): 20–50% probe failures or consistent customer reports; page oncall, slack channel, triage meeting within 30 minutes.
- P3 (Degradation but limited scope): investigate during business hours; ticket and owner assigned.

Runbook: First-line triage (ordered steps)
1) Ingest alert and classify: note source (synthetic/telemetry/customer) and affected tenant(s).
2) Snapshot evidence: run the customer’s last-known-good prompt set through current and rollback model variants (use EvalHub quick-run).
3) Confirm reproducibility: reproduce with customer prompt and with 3 controlled paraphrases. If behavior is inconsistent, mark as "non-deterministic — sample variance."
4) Capture traces: collect request_id(s), model version hash, kernel config, quantization profile, routing path, and telemetry window (+/- 15 mins).
5) Check recent deploys and infra events: query Deployment Audit (last 24 hours) and infra incidents (GPU preemption, autoscaler events).
6) Short mitigation: consider fallbacks per mitigation matrix (see Mitigation guidance).
7) If P1/P2, assemble evidence bundle and notify Eng SRE + Applied ML with severity tags.

Mitigation guidance (quick options):
- Automatic rollback to pinned model version for the tenant (if configured).
- Route customer traffic to higher-fidelity variant (e.g.,.fp16 -> int8? prefer higher-precision model).
- Apply prompt-layer shim: inject customer-specific guard prompt or primer to correct drift while RCA proceeds.
- Temporary rate-limit or sampling policy to reduce exposure window for hallucinations.

Evidence bundle requirements (must be attached to ticket):
- 3x failing customer examples with timestamps + paraphrases
- Model metadata: model_name, model_version, model_hash, quant_profile_id
- Inference runtime snapshot: kernel selection, batch size, GPU_id, node_id, kv-cache-size
- Telemetry traces: request_id, trace_id, relevant logs (stderr/stdout) clipped to 2000 lines total
- EvalHub run summary CSV and diff image (if applicable)

Customer communication templates (short, editable):
- Initial acknowledgement (within 1hr for P1/P2):
  "We’ve received your report and are actively investigating. We have identified a behavioral regression impacting your inference results and are prioritizing a mitigation. Initial mitigation options are under evaluation; next update in 60–120 minutes. — Customer Success Ops"
- Post-mitigation summary (post RCA):
  "Root cause: [brief summary]. Actions taken: [rollback/patch/deployment tweak]. Impacted timeframe: [t0..t1]. Recommended customer action: [if any]. We will follow up with formal RCA within X business days."

Escalation matrix (who to contact and when):
1) Customer Success Ops (owner) — immediate on triage start
2) Applied ML model-owner — if model delta suspected or sample-based regression confirmed
3) Eng SRE — if infrastructure/capacity signals present or rollback required
4) Product/Account Exec — for high-value customers and contractual SLA implications
5) Legal/Security — if customer data or privacy impact is detected

Operational checklist for engineers (fast reference):
- Confirm model hash: curl https://api.redwood.example/v1/models/{tenant}/versions | jq .current_hash
- Run controlled eval: evalhub run --tenant TID --suite customer-probes --variants baseline,current --output tmp/diff.csv
- Apply temporary routing: rctl set-route --tenant TID --route override --model baseline --duration 2h

Table: Suggested mitigations by root cause
| Root cause category | Short mitigation | Owner |
|---------------------|------------------|-------|
| Model rollout regression | Rollback to previous pinned model, enable canary gating | Applied ML
| Quantization artifact | Re-route to non-quantized variant, escalate to runtime team | Eng Serving Runtime
| Inference kernel mismatch | Force kernel compatibility profile, schedule hotfix | Eng Serving Runtime
| Prompt-layer change | Revert primer change, inject customer primer override | Customer Success / DevEx
| Routing/fanout bug | Apply safe fanout limit, route to single region | Eng Platform / SRE

Retention and evidence policy:
- Keep evidence bundles for 90 days on S3 bucket: s3://redwood-incident-evidence/YYYY/MM/tenantID/incidentID
- Attach EvalHub runs to the ticket and tag with incidentID.

Automation and tooling integration (operational notes):
- Telemetry Gatekeeper rules: create rule with type=behavioral_delta, window=24h, sensitivity=customer_high. Example JSON rule used by Telemetry Gatekeeper included below.
- EvalHub periodic rebaseline: schedule weekly rebaselines for enterprise tenants with heavy customization.

Example Telemetry Gatekeeper rule (JSON):
{
  "rule_id": "cust_behavior_delta_v1",
  "scope": "tenant:{{TENANT_ID}}",
  "metric": "probe_top1_changed_fraction",
  "window_minutes": 60,
  "threshold": 0.20,
  "consecutive_windows": 2,
  "actions": ["ticket:create:CS-TRIAGE", "page:oncall:cs_oncall", "run:evalhub_quickrun"]
}

Operational handoff to Eng (RCA expectations):
- Deliver the evidence bundle within 2 hours for P1 incidents.
- Include a short reproduction script and the exact EvalHub command used.
- Provide hypothesis list prioritized by likelihood with data pointers.

Post-incident steps and QBR inputs:
1) Postmortem: publish within 72 hours (summary + timeline + RCA owner).
2) Follow-up action items tracked in the incident board and closed by owner.
3) Add any new customer probes to the Customer Synthetic Suite if gap discovered.
4) Feed regression into the monthly QBR: include severity, uptime impact, and mitigation cost.

Appendix A — Runbook checklist (copyable):
- [ ] Timestamp and ticket created
- [ ] Evidence bundle attached
- [ ] Repro script included
- [ ] Notified Applied ML & Eng SRE
- [ ] Customer informed (template used)
- [ ] RCA draft in board

Appendix B — Links and references:
- Customer Synthetic Suite docs: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/customer-synthetic-suite
- EvalHub quick-run guide: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/quick-run-guide
- LLM Safety Escalation Matrix: /confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/llm-safety-escalation-matrix

Appendix C — Example paraphrase strategy for reproducibility tests:
1) Exact prompt (customer-submitted)
2) P1: collapse numerals to words
3) P2: shorten each sentence to 75% length
4) P3: replace named entities with placeholders

Ownership and contact list:
- Customer Success Ops: cs-ops@redwood.example (owner for triage)
- Applied ML oncall: applied-ml-oncall@redwood.example (escalation for model-owner)
- Eng SRE: sre-oncall@redwood.example

Change log:
- 2026-02-18: Created by Priya Nair
- 2026-07-10: Updated mitigation matrix and Telemetry Gatekeeper example (reviewers: Ethan Park, Maya Chen, Diego Alvarez)

End of document.

This playbook documents a repeatable, measurable process for detecting and triaging behavioral deltas (quality regressions or shifts) in LLM outputs that materially affect enterprise customers. It focuses on automated detection signals, deterministic first-line triage, evidence packaging for root-cause analysis (RCA), and customer-safe communications. Use this playbook when telemetry or customers indicate unexpected changes in output behavior ( hallucinations, repeated refusal patterns, persona drift, or factual regressions ).

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted and Dedicated tenants (hosted API and dedicated capacity).
- Covers model serving changes, quantization/rewrites, prompt-layer changes, routing/fanout, and inference runtime rollouts.
- Does NOT replace safety escalation for clear safety violations; refer to the LLM Safety Escalation Matrix for safety-first incidents.

Goals and success criteria:
- Detect behavior deltas within 5 business minutes for enterprise customers with active synthetic monitors.
- First-line response (triage started) within 30 minutes of alert.
- Deliver a reproducible evidence bundle to eng/research within 2 business hours.
- Provide customer-facing summary or mitigation within 4 business hours for P1/P2 severity.

Signal sources (ranked):
1) Synthetic customer-specific probes (Customer Synthetic Suite) — highest fidelity.
2) EvalHub regression alerts (score delta > 3 std dev on key prompts).
3) Production telemetry anomalies: embedding drift, decrease in token-efficiency, unexpected inference latency-correlated quality drops.
4) Customer-reported examples via support ticket or CSE Slack.
5) Observability-sourced NLP-based diff alerts (ModelGuard behavioral clustering).

Key monitoring metrics and thresholds (examples):
- DeltaRate.alert: fraction of probe prompts with changed top-1 intent (threshold 0.2 => 20% of probes changed)
- Answer-Confidence-drop: moving average of model log-prob changes (threshold -1.5 nats per token)
- BLEU/ROUGE/PolicyScore degradation on gold prompts (threshold: 15% relative drop)
- Embedding-similarity mean shift (Cosine drop > 0.08)

Alerting and severity mapping:
- P1 (Customer-impacting production degradation): >50% probe failures OR direct revenue loss signal. Pager to oncall, #cust-incident, immediate exec notification.
- P2 (Material degradation): 20–50% probe failures or consistent customer reports; page oncall, slack channel, triage meeting within 30 minutes.
- P3 (Degradation but limited scope): investigate during business hours; ticket and owner assigned.

Runbook: First-line triage (ordered steps)
1) Ingest alert and classify: note source (synthetic/telemetry/customer) and affected tenant(s).
2) Snapshot evidence: run the customer’s last-known-good prompt set through current and rollback model variants (use EvalHub quick-run).
3) Confirm reproducibility: reproduce with customer prompt and with 3 controlled paraphrases. If behavior is inconsistent, mark as \"non-deterministic — sample variance.\"
4) Capture traces: collect request_id(s), model version hash, kernel config, quantization profile, routing path, and telemetry window (+/- 15 mins).
5) Check recent deploys and infra events: query Deployment Audit (last 24 hours) and infra incidents (GPU preemption, autoscaler events).
6) Short mitigation: consider fallbacks per mitigation matrix (see Mitigation guidance).
7) If P1/P2, assemble evidence bundle and notify Eng SRE + Applied ML with severity tags.

Mitigation guidance (quick options):
- Automatic rollback to pinned model version for the tenant (if configured).
- Route customer traffic to higher-fidelity variant (e.g.,.fp16 -> int8? prefer higher-precision model).
- Apply prompt-layer shim: inject customer-specific guard prompt or primer to correct drift while RCA proceeds.
- Temporary rate-limit or sampling policy to reduce exposure window for hallucinations.

Evidence bundle requirements (must be attached to ticket):
- 3x failing customer examples with timestamps + paraphrases
- Model metadata: model_name, model_version, model_hash, quant_profile_id
- Inference runtime snapshot: kernel selection, batch size, GPU_id, node_id, kv-cache-size
- Telemetry traces: request_id, trace_id, relevant logs (stderr/stdout) clipped to 2000 lines total
- EvalHub run summary CSV and diff image (if applicable)

Customer communication templates (short, editable):
- Initial acknowledgement (within 1hr for P1/P2):
  \"We’ve received your report and are actively investigating. We have identified a behavioral regression impacting your inference results and are prioritizing a mitigation. Initial mitigation options are under evaluation; next update in 60–120 minutes. — Customer Success Ops\"
- Post-mitigation summary (post RCA):
  \"Root cause: [brief summary]. Actions taken: [rollback/patch/deployment tweak]. Impacted timeframe: [t0..t1]. Recommended customer action: [if any]. We will follow up with formal RCA within X business days.\"

Escalation matrix (who to contact and when):
1) Customer Success Ops (owner) — immediate on triage start
2) Applied ML model-owner — if model delta suspected or sample-based regression confirmed
3) Eng SRE — if infrastructure/capacity signals present or rollback required
4) Product/Account Exec — for high-value customers and contractual SLA implications
5) Legal/Security — if customer data or privacy impact is detected

Operational checklist for engineers (fast reference):
- Confirm model hash: curl https://api.redwood.example/v1/models/{tenant}/versions | jq .current_hash
- Run controlled eval: evalhub run --tenant TID --suite customer-probes --variants baseline,current --output tmp/diff.csv
- Apply temporary routing: rctl set-route --tenant TID --route override --model baseline --duration 2h

Table: Suggested mitigations by root cause
| Root cause category | Short mitigation | Owner |
|---------------------|------------------|-------|
| Model rollout regression | Rollback to previous pinned model, enable canary gating | Applied ML
| Quantization artifact | Re-route to non-quantized variant, escalate to runtime team | Eng Serving Runtime
| Inference kernel mismatch | Force kernel compatibility profile, schedule hotfix | Eng Serving Runtime
| Prompt-layer change | Revert primer change, inject customer primer override | Customer Success / DevEx
| Routing/fanout bug | Apply safe fanout limit, route to single region | Eng Platform / SRE

Retention and evidence policy:
- Keep evidence bundles for 90 days on S3 bucket: s3://redwood-incident-evidence/YYYY/MM/tenantID/incidentID
- Attach EvalHub runs to the ticket and tag with incidentID.

Automation and tooling integration (operational notes):
- Telemetry Gatekeeper rules: create rule with type=behavioral_delta, window=24h, sensitivity=customer_high. Example JSON rule used by Telemetry Gatekeeper included below.
- EvalHub periodic rebaseline: schedule weekly rebaselines for enterprise tenants with heavy customization.

Example Telemetry Gatekeeper rule (JSON):
{
  \"rule_id\": \"cust_behavior_delta_v1\",
  \"scope\": \"tenant:{{TENANT_ID}}\",
  \"metric\": \"probe_top1_changed_fraction\",
  \"window_minutes\": 60,
  \"threshold\": 0.20,
  \"consecutive_windows\": 2,
  \"actions\": [\"ticket:create:CS-TRIAGE\", \"page:oncall:cs_oncall\", \"run:evalhub_quickrun\"]
}

Operational handoff to Eng (RCA expectations):
- Deliver the evidence bundle within 2 hours for P1 incidents.
- Include a short reproduction script and the exact EvalHub command used.
- Provide hypothesis list prioritized by likelihood with data pointers.

Post-incident steps and QBR inputs:
1) Postmortem: publish within 72 hours (summary + timeline + RCA owner).
2) Follow-up action items tracked in the incident board and closed by owner.
3) Add any new customer probes to the Customer Synthetic Suite if gap discovered.
4) Feed regression into the monthly QBR: include severity, uptime impact, and mitigation cost.

Appendix A — Runbook checklist (copyable):
- [ ] Timestamp and ticket created
- [ ] Evidence bundle attached
- [ ] Repro script included
- [ ] Notified Applied ML & Eng SRE
- [ ] Customer informed (template used)
- [ ] RCA draft in board

Appendix B — Links and references:
- Customer Synthetic Suite docs: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/customer-synthetic-suite
- EvalHub quick-run guide: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/quick-run-guide
- LLM Safety Escalation Matrix: /confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/llm-safety-escalation-matrix

Appendix C — Example paraphrase strategy for reproducibility tests:
1) Exact prompt (customer-submitted)
2) P1: collapse numerals to words
3) P2: shorten each sentence to 75% length
4) P3: replace named entities with placeholders

Ownership and contact list:
- Customer Success Ops: cs-ops@redwood.example (owner for triage)
- Applied ML oncall: applied-ml-oncall@redwood.example (escalation for model-owner)
- Eng SRE: sre-oncall@redwood.example

Change log:
- 2026-02-18: Created by Priya Nair
- 2026-07-10: Updated mitigation matrix and Telemetry Gatekeeper example (reviewers: Ethan Park, Maya Chen, Diego Alvarez)

End of document.

source.title: Ingress proxy connection entropy: intermittent 50x errors and mitigation runbook
source.author: Priya Kapoor
source.content:
Summary:
This document describes a recurring class of production incidents we term 'ingress proxy connection entropy' that manifest as intermittent 502/503/504 errors and short spikes of failed model fanout requests. It provides:

- clear symptom definitions and diagnostic queries
- an immediate mitigation runbook for oncall and customer success engineers
- long‑term engineering fixes and rollout guidance
- test and verification checklists for customer-facing cutovers

Scope:
Applies to: Redwood-hosted ingress proxies and customer-managed sidecar proxies that perform request fanout to model serving pools (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). Not intended for client SDK bugs or pure model runtime failures (see separate model runtime runbooks).

Background and root-cause patterns observed:
We have observed several distinct contributing factors across incidents:

1) Ephemeral port churn + NAT table exhaustion on stateful edge proxies, causing new connections to fail with RST or be reset after TLS handshake.
2) Imbalanced keepalive and health-check interactions where load balancer probes evict live backend sessions during short surges.
3) TLS session cache miss patterns combined with aggressively small client TLS timeouts that increase backend CPU and cause transient overload.
4) Fanout amplification when a single client request fans out to multiple model variants and an upstream slow backend causes head-of-line timeouts.

Symptoms (how customers and SREs will notice this):
- Short, periodic spikes of 502/503/504 errors concentrated to specific ingress proxies (not whole region).
- Error rates correlated to increase in ephemeral port allocation (netstat / ss shows many TIME_WAIT).
- Backend model nodes show normal CPU but elevated accept queue length or connection backlog.
- Trace IDs show upstream TLS handshake succeeded but the first application read returns EOF or ECONNRESET.
- Synthetic journey checks show success for HEAD requests but POST/generation endpoints intermittently fail.

Immediate mitigation runbook (for oncall/customer success):
Priority: restore traffic to customers with minimal impact; preserve telemetry for root-cause analysis.

1) Triage and scope (5–10 minutes)
   - Identify impacted tenants via the error-metrics dashboard (Route: Ingress > proxy-errors by route).
   - Narrow to proxy host(s) using service host labels and trace sampling: search traces for error=502 and group by proxy_hostname.
   - If >50% of requests to a given proxy host are failing, treat as full node degradation.

2) Short-term mitigations (10–30 minutes)
   a) Drain and replace affected proxy instance(s):
      - Mark the proxy node as draining in the autoscaler (CLI):
        kubectl annotate node <proxy-pod> redwood.run/drain=true --overwrite
      - Wait for healthy hosts to absorb connections (~30s–2m).
   b) Enable aggressive TCP keepalive tuning for edge proxies (hotfix):
      - For Linux hosts, set: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1; sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_limit=50; sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=2048
   c) Temporary fanout throttling: switch the fanout policy to sequential or limit to N variants for the affected tenant: update route override in routing-config: 
      - routing-cli set-fanout --route <route> --mode sequential --max-variants 2
   d) If TLS session cache is disabled on the proxy, re-enable with a conservative size (e.g., 10k entries) to reduce handshake load.

3) Customer communication (parallel)
   - Notify affected customers with current scope and ETA: use template 'ingress-interruption-brief' and include mitigations applied and expected next update in 30 minutes.

4) Preserve for RCA (ongoing)
   - Increase trace sampling rate to 25% for the impacted route for the next 2 hours.
   - Capture tcpdump (90s) on affected proxy: sudo tcpdump -w /tmp/proxy-eth0-<ts>.pcap host <client-ip> and upload to incident artifact storage.

Diagnostic steps (deeper root cause analysis):
1) Metrics to query (example PromQL):
   - ingress_proxy_connection_errors_total{code=~"5.."}
   - node_tcp_time_wait_total{instance=~"<host>"}
   - proxy_tls_handshake_duration_seconds_bucket
   - proxy_accept_queue_length{instance=~"<host>"}

2) Log patterns to search (example):
   - "accept: too many open files" in proxy logs
   - "EOF while reading from socket" or "unexpected EOF" in backend service logs
   - Repeated TLS session cache misses (log key: tls_cache_miss=true)

3) Trace analysis approach:
   - Correlate client-side span timestamps to server accept/dispatch spans. Look for large delta between socket accept and first application read.
   - If multiple simultaneous fanout children have increasing tail latency but normal CPU, suspect accept/backlog starvation.

Permanent fixes and rollout plan (engineering recommendations):
Short-term (within 2–4 weeks):
- Default ingress proxy config: increase listen backlog and tcp_max_syn_backlog; enable TW reuse by default on edge OS images.
- Introduce soft limit on concurrent fanout children per request (default 4) and a queue with priority for low-latency paths.
- Add an adaptive TLS session caching strategy that increases cache size under high connection churn.

Medium-term (1–3 months):
- Implement connection pooling and multiplexing for upstream model nodes where protocol allows (HTTP/2 or custom multiplexing).
- Enhance autoscaler to consider accept-queue and ephemeral-port metrics when making scaling decisions (avoid scaling by CPU alone).

Long-term (3–6 months):
- Replace single-threaded accept loops in legacy proxies with multi-acceptor designs and migrate critical paths to the new performant proxy (project: proxy-v2).
- Provide SDK changes to reduce ephemeral port churn (keepalive-friendly defaults, connection reuse).

Rollout guidance for tenant impact minimization:
- Canary deploy proxy config changes to 2% of tenants for 48 hours with synthetic journeys and increased sampling.
- If errors rise less than 0.1% and p95 latency improves, expand to 25% then 100% over 7–14 days.

Testing checklist (for release validation):
1) Preflight (staging):
   - Run 10k concurrent synthetic POST generation requests with fanout multiplier 3.
   - Assert: error rate < 0.2% and no increase in TIME_WAIT > 5x baseline.
2) Canary (production):
   - Monitor: ingress proxy 5xx rate, tcp_time_wait, accept_queue_length, p99 tail latency.
   - Rollback criteria: 5xx rate increase > 0.5% for canary tenants sustained over 10 minutes.

Owner and escalation matrix:
- Primary owner: eng-sre (rotation): oncall-eng-sre@redwood.ai
- Secondary owner: customer-success (Priya Kapoor) — coordinate customer comms
- Escalation to eng-platform lead (Rafael Martins) if mitigation not actionable within 30 minutes.

SLO impact and customer QBR notes:
- Typical SLOs affected: 99.9% availability on generation endpoints and p99 latency targets.
- During incidents, capture affected tenant list and minutes-down per tenant for credit assessment and QBR discussion.
- For QBRs, present: incident timeline, mitigations applied, root cause, and planned preventive actions. Use the template 'slo-incident-summary' and attach trace artifacts.

Appendix A — example CLI and config snippets:

1) Quick sysctl hotfix (Linux):
```
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_limit=50
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=2048
```

2) Routing override to reduce fanout (routing-cli sample):
```
routing-cli set-fanout --route tenant:acme/chat --mode sequential --max-variants 2 --reason "mitigating connection entropy"
```

3) Nginx/TCP proxy snippet (increase backlog / keepalive):
```
worker_processes auto;
events { worker_connections  65536; }
stream {
  server {
    listen 443 backlog=2048 reuseport;
    proxy_pass backend_pool;
    proxy_timeout 65s;
    proxy_connect_timeout 10s;
  }
}
```

Appendix B — incident capture checklist (artifact list):
- pcap capture from affected proxy (90s)
- higher-sample traces (25%) for 2 hours
- metrics window: 30m before, during, 2h after incident
- list of tenants with exact error counts and timestamps

Related pages and references:
- See routing and fanout design doc: /confluence/eng-platform/roadmap/fanout-routing-architecture (for design rationale)
- See SRE backlog: ticket PROXY-452 for proxy-v2 replacement plan

Notes and open questions for follow-up:
- Should we treat ephemeral port exhaustion as a first-class signal in PagerDuty rules? (proposal: yes, add alert when net.ipv4.tcp_time_wait_total increases by 3x)
- Evaluate whether default SDK keepalive should be enabled for all languages.

Contributors:
Priya Kapoor (author), Marco Diaz (SRE reviewer), Ava Chen (integration reviewer), Rafael Martins (platform reviewer)

Document history:
- 2026-04-02: Draft created
- 2026-05-10: Initial mitigations validated in staging
- 2026-06-15: Added rollout guidance and expanded test checklist

   - \"accept: too many open files\" in proxy logs
   - \"EOF while reading from socket\" or \"unexpected EOF\" in backend service logs
   - Repeated TLS session cache misses (log key: tls_cache_miss=true)

3) Trace analysis approach:
   - Correlate client-side span timestamps to server accept/dispatch spans. Look for large delta between socket accept and first application read.
   - If multiple simultaneous fanout children have increasing tail latency but normal CPU, suspect accept/backlog starvation.

Permanent fixes and rollout plan (engineering recommendations):
Short-term (within 2–4 weeks):
- Default ingress proxy config: increase listen backlog and tcp_max_syn_backlog; enable TW reuse by default on edge OS images.
- Introduce soft limit on concurrent fanout children per request (default 4) and a queue with priority for low-latency paths.
- Add an adaptive TLS session caching strategy that increases cache size under high connection churn.

Medium-term (1–3 months):
- Implement connection pooling and multiplexing for upstream model nodes where protocol allows (HTTP/2 or custom multiplexing).
- Enhance autoscaler to consider accept-queue and ephemeral-port metrics when making scaling decisions (avoid scaling by CPU alone).

Long-term (3–6 months):
- Replace single-threaded accept loops in legacy proxies with multi-acceptor designs and migrate critical paths to the new performant proxy (project: proxy-v2).
- Provide SDK changes to reduce ephemeral port churn (keepalive-friendly defaults, connection reuse).

Rollout guidance for tenant impact minimization:
- Canary deploy proxy config changes to 2% of tenants for 48 hours with synthetic journeys and increased sampling.
- If errors rise less than 0.1% and p95 latency improves, expand to 25% then 100% over 7–14 days.

Testing checklist (for release validation):
1) Preflight (staging):
   - Run 10k concurrent synthetic POST generation requests with fanout multiplier 3.
   - Assert: error rate < 0.2% and no increase in TIME_WAIT > 5x baseline.
2) Canary (production):
   - Monitor: ingress proxy 5xx rate, tcp_time_wait, accept_queue_length, p99 tail latency.
   - Rollback criteria: 5xx rate increase > 0.5% for canary tenants sustained over 10 minutes.

Owner and escalation matrix:
- Primary owner: eng-sre (rotation): oncall-eng-sre@redwood.ai
- Secondary owner: customer-success (Priya Kapoor) — coordinate customer comms
- Escalation to eng-platform lead (Rafael Martins) if mitigation not actionable within 30 minutes.

SLO impact and customer QBR notes:
- Typical SLOs affected: 99.9% availability on generation endpoints and p99 latency targets.
- During incidents, capture affected tenant list and minutes-down per tenant for credit assessment and QBR discussion.
- For QBRs, present: incident timeline, mitigations applied, root cause, and planned preventive actions. Use the template 'slo-incident-summary' and attach trace artifacts.

Appendix A — example CLI and config snippets:

1) Quick sysctl hotfix (Linux):
```
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse=1
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_tw_limit=50
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=2048
```

2) Routing override to reduce fanout (routing-cli sample):
```
routing-cli set-fanout --route tenant:acme/chat --mode sequential --max-variants 2 --reason \"mitigating connection entropy\"
```

3) Nginx/TCP proxy snippet (increase backlog / keepalive):
```
worker_processes auto;
events { worker_connections  65536; }
stream {
  server {
    listen 443 backlog=2048 reuseport;
    proxy_pass backend_pool;
    proxy_timeout 65s;
    proxy_connect_timeout 10s;
  }
}
```

Appendix B — incident capture checklist (artifact list):
- pcap capture from affected proxy (90s)
- higher-sample traces (25%) for 2 hours
- metrics window: 30m before, during, 2h after incident
- list of tenants with exact error counts and timestamps

Related pages and references:
- See routing and fanout design doc: /confluence/eng-platform/roadmap/fanout-routing-architecture (for design rationale)
- See SRE backlog: ticket PROXY-452 for proxy-v2 replacement plan

Notes and open questions for follow-up:
- Should we treat ephemeral port exhaustion as a first-class signal in PagerDuty rules? (proposal: yes, add alert when net.ipv4.tcp_time_wait_total increases by 3x)
- Evaluate whether default SDK keepalive should be enabled for all languages.

Contributors:
Priya Kapoor (author), Marco Diaz (SRE reviewer), Ava Chen (integration reviewer), Rafael Martins (platform reviewer)

Document history:
- 2026-04-02: Draft created
- 2026-05-10: Initial mitigations validated in staging
- 2026-06-15: Added rollout guidance and expanded test checklist

source.title: Observability Privacy Filter and Keyed Telemetry Onboarding Playbook
source.author: Leila Moreno
source.content:
Purpose:
This playbook describes the recommended process for onboarding enterprise customers to Redwood's keyed telemetry pipeline with a privacy-filtering layer ("Observability Privacy Filter") between customer data sources and Redwood observability systems. It is intended to ensure compliance with customer privacy constraints, minimize PII leakage, and preserve the fidelity required for SLO/QBR reporting and cost accounting.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private customers integrating their application telemetry (request traces, token-level cost attributions, custom metrics) into Redwood observability.
- Covers contract schema negotiation, filter configuration, implementation patterns, validation, rollout, and post-onboarding handoff to customer ops and Redwood CS.
- Does not replace legal data processing agreements; coordinate with Security & Legal for restricted data.

Audience:
- Customer Success Engineers (CSEs) onboarding telemetry
- Integration engineers on the customer side
- Platform/SRE teams validating ingestion and SLOs
- Account teams preparing QBR telemetry bundles

Definitions:
- Keyed telemetry: telemetry records that include a tenant identifier (key) for attribution and routing.
- Observability Privacy Filter (OPF): a configurable filter service deployed at the customer edge or as a Redwood-managed middleware that redacts/replaces sensitive attributes while preserving routing keys and aggregates.
- Telemetry Contract: a schema agreement between customer and Redwood that defines field names, types, retention hints, and sensitivity labels.

Prerequisites (customer & Redwood):
1) Signed Data Processing Addendum (DPA) and any sector-specific addenda (finance, healthcare).
2) Approved telemetry contract draft and agreed retention windows.
3) Network ingress configured: IP allowlist, TLS certificates, and, if Private, VPC peering details.
4) Access to a staging project/tenant in Redwood with synthetic traffic capability.
5) Contact list: CSE, customer integration lead, customer security lead, Redwood SRE reviewer.

Roles and Ownership:
- Customer Success (owner): run onboarding, validate acceptance tests, and deliver QBR reporting templates.
- Customer Integration Lead (owner): implement client-side emitters and local filters.
- Security & Legal (reviewer): sign-off on privacy filter rules and DPA terms.
- Platform/SRE (reviewer): confirm ingestion capacity and SLO measurement hooks.

Step-by-step onboarding checklist (recommended timeline: 4–8 business days):
1) Kickoff (Day 0):
   - Introductions and objectives; share this playbook and telemetry contract template.
   - Confirm DPA status and desired retention/region constraints.
2) Contract negotiation (Day 0–2):
   - Exchange telemetry contract JSON schema (example below).
   - Mark sensitive fields with sensitivity labels: PUBLIC | CONFIDENTIAL | PII.
3) Filtering strategy workshop (Day 1–3):
   - Decide where OPF will run: customer-side agent (recommended for Private) or Redwood-managed ingress.
   - Choose operators: redact, hash (HMAC-SHA256 with tenant salt), truncate, pseudonymize.
4) Staging implementation (Day 2–4):
   - Customer deploys emitters to staging; OPF applied to traffic.
   - CSE runs acceptance suite including token-counting, latency sampling, and synthetic loads.
5) Validation and telemetry QA (Day 3–5):
   - Verify key preservation for routing and aggregation.
   - Assert no PII in fields labeled PII via automated scans (see validation commands).
6) SLO mapping and QBR dataset selection (Day 4–6):
   - Map telemetry fields to SLOs (latency, error-rate, token-cost).
   - Define QBR metrics and retention windows for executive dashboards.
7) Production go/no-go and rollout plan (Day 5–7):
   - Agree on canary % pattern, throttling/queue limits, and fallback to raw ingest for debugging windows.
8) Production rollout (Day 6–8):
   - Apply OPF to production canary; monitor for missing keys or metric gaps.
   - Gradual ramp with checkpoints at 10%, 50%, 100%.
9) Post-go monitoring and tuning (Week 1):
   - Monitor telemetry completeness, SLO drift, and cost attribution accuracy.
   - Tune filter rules to restore necessary fields if over-redaction occurs.
10) Handoff and QBR preparation (Week 1–2):
   - Deliver a QBR telemetry bundle and runbook for customer ops.

Telemetry contract (example snippet):
| field_name | type | example | sensitivity | retention_hint | notes |
| request_id | string | "req_01a7..." | PUBLIC | 7d | routing key for traces |
| tenant_id | string | "tenant:acme" | CONFIDENTIAL | 30d | must be preserved (hashed if required) |
| user_email | string | "alice@acme.com" | PII | 30d | must be redacted or pseudonymized unless DPA allows |
| prompt_tokens | integer | 12 | PUBLIC | 90d | used for cost attribution |
| response_tokens | integer | 64 | PUBLIC | 90d | used for cost attribution |
| model_id | string | "redwood/gptx-4b-q" | PUBLIC | 365d | model attribution for cost and debugging |

Telemetry contract JSON (recommended format):
{
  "fields": [
    {"name":"request_id", "type":"string", "sensitivity":"PUBLIC", "retention":"7d"},
    {"name":"tenant_id", "type":"string", "sensitivity":"CONFIDENTIAL", "retention":"30d"},
    {"name":"user_email", "type":"string", "sensitivity":"PII", "retention":"30d"},
    {"name":"prompt_tokens", "type":"integer", "sensitivity":"PUBLIC", "retention":"90d"}
  ]
}

Filtering operators (recommended patterns):
- redact: remove the field entirely from emitted telemetry. Use when customer policy prohibits storage.
- hash: HMAC-SHA256 with tenant-specific salt; preserves grouping but hides original value.
- pseudonymize: generate stable pseudo-id via keyed PRF; can be reversible only under strict legal control.
- aggregate: replace detailed values with buckets (e.g., token counts -> 0-32,33-64,...).

Validation commands and test harness (staging):
- Run the Redwood telemetry linter: rwd-telemetry-lint --schema telemetry_contract.json --sample sample_payloads/
- PII scan (simple grep approach for email): grep -E "[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\\.[A-Za-z]{2,}" logs/ingested/ || echo "PII-free"
- Request count verification: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://staging-redwood/api/telemetry/tenant/<tenant_id>/counts

SLO and QBR mapping (example):
- Latency p50/p95 per model_id -> SLO 99.5% p95 < 350ms
- Token-cost attribution: daily delta per tenant -> used in finops QBR
- Ingest completeness: 99% of requests must include request_id and tenant_id

Troubleshooting and common failure modes:
1) Missing tenant_id after filter applied:
   - Symptom: Billing and routing gaps, alerts for missing-key ingestion spikes.
   - Cause: Filter misconfiguration replaced rather than hashed/preserved.
   - Remediation: Re-deploy OPF with tenant-preserve rule; run re-ingestion for the window if allowed.
2) Over-redaction causing SLO regression:
   - Symptom: Missing model_id or token counts; SLO dashboards show gaps.
   - Cause: Aggressive redact rules or wildcard match in OPF.
   - Remediation: Narrow rule scope, enable aggregated fallback (buckets) to preserve SLO signals.
3) Unexpected PII in ingestion:
   - Symptom: Automatic DLP scan flags stored records.
   - Cause: New emitters added fields that bypass the agreed contract.
   - Remediation: Freeze producer schema, run full scan, and engage Security & Legal per DPA.

Known issues and workarounds (current as of last_updated):
- Issue: HMAC salted hashing drift when tenant salt rotated causes broken grouping. Workaround: use versioned salt identifiers in emitted record and schedule a rehash job for recent windows.
- Issue: High-cardinality keys (full user_email hashed) inflates cardinality of metrics and increases cost. Workaround: pseudonymize to coarse buckets for analytics and preserve hashed emails only for reverse-lookup windows under strict access control.
- Issue: Edge OPF running on CPU-constrained appliances causes 200ms spike during peak ingest. Workaround: enable per-route sampling (1%) for detailed traces and send aggregated summaries for high-volume routes.

Acceptance criteria (must pass before handoff):
- No PII present in fields labeled PII in the agreed contract for production data (automated DLP scan).
- Ingest completeness >= 99% for required keys (tenant_id, request_id) across canary period.
- Token-cost attribution within 2% of staging baseline.
- SRE sign-off on ingestion capacity and fallback plan.

Handoff artifacts to provide to customer after onboarding:
- Telemetry contract JSON and final OPF rule-set
- Staging and production verification runbooks (commands and dashboards)
- QBR telemetry bundle and dashboard snapshot with sample queries
- Short operational runbook for rehash/rotation and emergency debug window steps

Appendix A: Example OPF rule (yaml)
- name: redact_user_email
  match: "event.user_email"
  action: "redact"
  reason: "Customer requested no email storage"
- name: hash_tenant_id
  match: "event.tenant_id"
  action: "hash:hmac-sha256:tenant-salt-v1"
  reason: "Preserve grouping without storing raw id"

Appendix B: Escalation path for data policy incidents:
1) CSE opens internal incident in PagerDuty with tag "telemetry-data-policy".
2) Notify Security & Legal immediately and place a temporary ingest halt if data exposure suspected.
3) Execute the containment checklist (snapshot, freeze schema changes, begin audit log collection).
4) If confirmed, follow DPA breach notification timelines; coordinate customer communications via Account team.

Contact and support matrix:
- Primary CSE: Leila Moreno (leila@redwood.example)
- Platform/SRE reviewer: Ethan Park (ethan@redwood.example)
- Security & Legal contact: Rima Gupta (rima@redwood.example)

Document maintenance:
- Owner: Customer Success (telemetry onboarding squad)
- Review cadence: Quarterly or on major stream changes

End of document.

}

Filtering operators (recommended patterns):
- redact: remove the field entirely from emitted telemetry. Use when customer policy prohibits storage.
- hash: HMAC-SHA256 with tenant-specific salt; preserves grouping but hides original value.
- pseudonymize: generate stable pseudo-id via keyed PRF; can be reversible only under strict legal control.
- aggregate: replace detailed values with buckets (e.g., token counts -> 0-32,33-64,...).

Validation commands and test harness (staging):
- Run the Redwood telemetry linter: rwd-telemetry-lint --schema telemetry_contract.json --sample sample_payloads/
- PII scan (simple grep approach for email): grep -E "[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\\.[A-Za-z]{2,}" logs/ingested/ || echo "PII-free"
- Request count verification: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://staging-redwood/api/telemetry/tenant/<tenant_id>/counts

SLO and QBR mapping (example):
- Latency p50/p95 per model_id -> SLO 99.5% p95 < 350ms
- Token-cost attribution: daily delta per tenant -> used in finops QBR
- Ingest completeness: 99% of requests must include request_id and tenant_id

Troubleshooting and common failure modes:
1) Missing tenant_id after filter applied:
   - Symptom: Billing and routing gaps, alerts for missing-key ingestion spikes.
   - Cause: Filter misconfiguration replaced rather than hashed/preserved.
   - Remediation: Re-deploy OPF with tenant-preserve rule; run re-ingestion for the window if allowed.
2) Over-redaction causing SLO regression:
   - Symptom: Missing model_id or token counts; SLO dashboards show gaps.
   - Cause: Aggressive redact rules or wildcard match in OPF.
   - Remediation: Narrow rule scope, enable aggregated fallback (buckets) to preserve SLO signals.
3) Unexpected PII in ingestion:
   - Symptom: Automatic DLP scan flags stored records.
   - Cause: New emitters added fields that bypass the agreed contract.
   - Remediation: Freeze producer schema, run full scan, and engage Security & Legal per DPA.

Known issues and workarounds (current as of last_updated):
- Issue: HMAC salted hashing drift when tenant salt rotated causes broken grouping. Workaround: use versioned salt identifiers in emitted record and schedule a rehash job for recent windows.
- Issue: High-cardinality keys (full user_email hashed) inflates cardinality of metrics and increases cost. Workaround: pseudonymize to coarse buckets for analytics and preserve hashed emails only for reverse-lookup windows under strict access control.
- Issue: Edge OPF running on CPU-constrained appliances causes 200ms spike during peak ingest. Workaround: enable per-route sampling (1%) for detailed traces and send aggregated summaries for high-volume routes.

Acceptance criteria (must pass before handoff):
- No PII present in fields labeled PII in the agreed contract for production data (automated DLP scan).
- Ingest completeness >= 99% for required keys (tenant_id, request_id) across canary period.
- Token-cost attribution within 2% of staging baseline.
- SRE sign-off on ingestion capacity and fallback plan.

Handoff artifacts to provide to customer after onboarding:
- Telemetry contract JSON and final OPF rule-set
- Staging and production verification runbooks (commands and dashboards)
- QBR telemetry bundle and dashboard snapshot with sample queries
- Short operational runbook for rehash/rotation and emergency debug window steps

Appendix A: Example OPF rule (yaml)
- name: redact_user_email
  match: "event.user_email"
  action: "redact"
  reason: "Customer requested no email storage"
- name: hash_tenant_id
  match: "event.tenant_id"
  action: "hash:hmac-sha256:tenant-salt-v1"
  reason: "Preserve grouping without storing raw id"

Appendix B: Escalation path for data policy incidents:
1) CSE opens internal incident in PagerDuty with tag "telemetry-data-policy".
2) Notify Security & Legal immediately and place a temporary ingest halt if data exposure suspected.
3) Execute the containment checklist (snapshot, freeze schema changes, begin audit log collection).
4) If confirmed, follow DPA breach notification timelines; coordinate customer communications via Account team.

Contact and support matrix:
- Primary CSE: Leila Moreno (leila@redwood.example)
- Platform/SRE reviewer: Ethan Park (ethan@redwood.example)
- Security & Legal contact: Rima Gupta (rima@redwood.example)

Document maintenance:
- Owner: Customer Success (telemetry onboarding squad)
- Review cadence: Quarterly or on major stream changes

End of document.

source.title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding
source.author: Aisha Gupta
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook defines steps, telemetry gate criteria, and cost-gating policies for cross-regional enterprise tenant cutovers. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, SRE on-call, and the customer’s cloud/networking contact. The goal is a safe, observable cross-region activation with automatic failback triggers and cost mitigation if unexpected billing or token-rate anomalies occur.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to enterprise customers migrating from single-region hosted or Dedicated deployments to multi-region (active-primary + passive-secondary) configurations during go-live windows.
- Covers pre-cutover verification, telemetry gate definitions, automated gate enforcement, manual and automatic failback, cost gating thresholds, and post-cutover QBR metrics.
- Does NOT replace tenant-specific security acceptance tests (see security gates playbook).

Roles and ownership:
- Customer Success (CS): runbook owner, coordinates stakeholders and business signoffs.
- Solutions Engineering (SE): pre-cutover config and ingress verification.
- Eng-SRE: on-call for capacity and traffic shaping; owns automated failback tooling.
- Customer (networking/security): provide firewall, routing, and cert rotations.
- Escalation: follow standard escalation matrix; primary CS owns customer comms.

Pre-cutover checklist (must be completed no earlier than 72 hours before scheduled cutover):
1) Capacity & model pinning
   - Verify dedicated pools or reserved capacity are provisioned in target region(s) with 20% buffer over expected steady-state TPS.
   - Confirm model versions and quantization profiles are pinned in tenant config (model_id + version).
2) Network & certs
   - Confirm peering or Transit Gateway is established; test 3-way TCP handshake to each inference proxy IP.
   - Ensure client certs and ingress certs rotate with no downtime (test cert chain).
3) Telemetry gating assertions deployed in gatekeeper service
   - Install telemetry assertions bundle (JSON spec) to customer-facing gatekeeper namespace.
4) Cost guard setup
   - Define billing thresholds and emergency cost caps in Tenant FinOps panel; add CS and finance contacts.
5) Synthetic workload
   - Create tenant-specific synthetic journeys that emulate common request shapes (chat, streaming, embeddings).

Telemetry Gate Criteria (these must be green to proceed):
- Latency SLOs (per-route and per-model): 99th percentile latency for short-chat (<=128 tokens) must be <= 350ms.
- Token success rate: >= 99.5% for tokenized responses (no stream resets, 4xx/5xx).
- Error budget burn rate: must be < 2x baseline for the 30-minute rolling window.
- Cost per 1k tokens deviation: <= +25% vs expected unit cost baseline for the first 60 minutes.
- Synthetic fidelity: synthetic journeys must achieve >= 95% success (passes semantic assertions).

Telemetry Gate Table (quick reference - assertions enforced by Gatekeeper):
- "latency_p99_short_chat": threshold 350ms, evaluation_window 30m, action "hold"
- "token_success_rate": threshold 99.5%, evaluation_window 15m, action "alert"
- "error_budget_burn": threshold 2x, evaluation_window 30m, action "failback"
- "cost_delta_1k": threshold +25%, evaluation_window 60m, action "cost-cap"
- "synthetic_journey_pass_rate": threshold 95%, evaluation_window 15m, action "hold"

Cutover procedure (step-by-step):
Pre-cutover (T-minus 2 hours):
1. Notify stakeholders via Cutover channel and update status page.
2. Run a full synthetic run (3 iterations) from multiple regions; validate gate assertions locally.
3. Ensure autoscaling groups are warm and KV caches are primed with representative prefixes.

At cutover (execution window):
1. Gradual DNS / routing change: shift 10% of production traffic to target region for 10 minutes. Observe telemetry gates in real time.
2. Increase to 30% for 15 minutes if gates remain green; run synthetic journeys.
3. Proceed to 60% for 20 minutes; monitor cost delta closely.
4. Final 100% switchover only allowed if all gate assertions remained green across the incremental windows.

Automated failback and remediation rules:
- Immediate automatic failback to previous region (or queued fallback model pool) if any gate with action "failback" trips (e.g., error_budget_burn).
- Throttled rollback (graceful): if "hold" gates trip, cut traffic back by 25% and run diagnostics.
- Cost-cap trigger: when cost_delta_1k > threshold, automatically enable token-rate limiters for non-critical routes and notify CS/FinOps.

Failback runbook (SRE execution):
1) On alert, acknowledge and open incident in PagerDuty with tag "cross-regional-cutover".
2) Execute automated rollback script: /opt/redwood/bin/region-failback --tenant $TENANT --target-region $PRIMARY --reason $ALERT_TAG
3) If rollback script fails, perform manual routing rollback via the routing console (steps included in appendix).
4) Post-rollback: collect the following artifacts within first 30 minutes: trace slices (x-trace ids), pod logs, gatekeeper assertion logs, synthetic journey results. Attach to incident.

Cost-gating and emergency billing protection:
- Emergency cost cap: default = 200% of daily expected spend (tenant-configurable). When reached: (a) pause low-priority embedding jobs and batch ingestion, (b) enable per-API token caps, (c) notify FinOps and customer billing contact.
- Tiered actions by severity:
  - Severity 1 (blocker): immediate pause of all non-essential routes and rollback to previous topology.
  - Severity 2 (high): apply 50% token rate reduction to best-effort routes, keep primary customer transactions.
  - Severity 3 (medium): raise alert to CS and schedule QBR follow-up to review FinOps anomalies.

Communication templates (use in Cutover channel and customer emails):
- Short update (while cutover in progress): "Cutover to region eu-west-1 at 10:10 UTC: currently 30% traffic, gates nominal. Will update in 15m or on exception."
- Failback notice: "Automated failback executed for tenant <TENANT>. Reason: <GATE_NAME> tripped. CS lead <NAME> coordinating next steps. Incident: <PD_LINK>"

Post-cutover QBR & SLA evaluation (first 72 hours):
- Metrics to collect and include in QBR: 99/95/50 latency percentiles by route, token success rate, per-model cost per 1k tokens, synthetic journey pass rate, error budget burn curves, and customer-facing error traces.
- Evaluation framework: compare each metric to pre-cutover baseline and flag regressions >10% for prioritized RCA.
- Suggested agenda items (30–45 minute review): cutover timeline, telemetry gate events, cost delta analysis, customer impact summary, action items and owners.

Appendix A — Example Gatekeeper assertion JSON (deploy via gatekeeper-api):
{
  "assertions": [
    {"name": "latency_p99_short_chat", "type": "p99_latency", "threshold_ms": 350, "window_minutes": 30, "action": "hold"},
    {"name": "cost_delta_1k", "type": "unit_cost_delta", "threshold_pct": 25, "window_minutes": 60, "action": "cost-cap"}
  ]
}

Appendix B — Routing console quick commands:
- Check active weights: curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' https://routing-console.prod/api/tenants/$TENANT/weights
- Set weight: POST to /api/tenants/$TENANT/weights {"region":"eu-west-1","weight":30}

Appendix C — Escalation contacts (order):
1. CS on-call: cs-oncall@redwood.ai (Primary: Aisha Gupta)
2. Eng-SRE on-call: sre-oncall@redwood.ai (Primary: Ethan Park)
3. Solutions Engineering: se-lead@redwood.ai (Primary: Marta Lopez)
4. Finance alerts: finops@redwood.ai

Related artifacts and references:
- Telemetry Gatekeeper onboarding guide: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/integration-guides/telemetry-gatekeeper-onboarding-and-qbr-integration-guide-2026
- Dedicated tenant cutover safety net (reference): /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/dedicated-tenant-cutover-safety-net-playbook-2026

Change log:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Aisha Gupta.
- 2026-03-03: Added cost-cap automation and synthetic journey guidance (review: Samir Khan).
- 2026-06-02: Updated failback automation command and adjusted latency SLO to 350ms (review: Ethan Park).

End of document

source.title: Hosted API: Intermittent streaming disconnects (March 2026) - known issue + tracking
source.author: Naomi Feldman
source.body:
## Overview
Some customers have reported intermittent disconnections during streaming responses on the Hosted API (OpenAI-compatible streaming/SSE). The symptoms often present as truncated completions, missing final chunk/[DONE], or client-side errors such as "unexpected EOF" or "socket hang up".

This page documents the known issue pattern observed in March 2026, affected environments, and where to track active customer cases.

## Customer-visible symptoms
- Streaming ends mid-generation (partial text returned)
- Client receives "connection closed" / "unexpected EOF"
- Missing final "done" signal
- Retries usually succeed

## Affected surfaces
- Hosted API OpenAI-compatible endpoints (chat.completions streaming=true)
- Regions observed: us-east, eu-west

## Workarounds (customer guidance)
1. Implement retries when a stream terminates unexpectedly (treat as retryable error).
2. Increase client read timeout for long generations.
3. Ensure SSE parser tolerates missing final chunk and does not deadlock UI state.

## Root cause (current understanding)
Edge/proxy configuration on streaming routes can prematurely close connections under certain backpressure/idle patterns, especially during/after edge rollouts. Model layer errors are typically not present.

## Active case tracking (MUST keep complete)
The following customer escalation cases are currently associated with this known issue pattern in March 2026:
- SUP-4821 (Acme Analytics, us-east)
- SUP-4877 (BetaAI, eu-west)
- SUP-4899 (CloudCart, eu-west)

If you see a new customer report that matches the symptoms, add the SUP key here and link to any Slack incident threads.

## Related incident threads
- Slack #incidents thread_ts=1773815400 (eu-west streaming disconnect ratio alert)

## Ownership
- Support triage: Customer Support
- Investigation: Platform + SRE
- Customer comms: CSM/Support
