source.title: Error Budget & SLO Escalation Playbook (Hosted vs Dedicated)
source.author: Rachel Kim
source.body:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how Customer Success (CS), Support, and Solutions Engineering (SE) engage SRE during **SLO / error budget** events. It covers:
- How to route reliability escalations (Hosted vs Dedicated)
- What data is required (to avoid slow back-and-forth)
- What we can and cannot commit to in customer-facing communications
- Expected response/updates by tier and severity

This is a **joint CS/SRE** process. It is designed to align with the tiered SLO program and error budget enforcement so that customer escalations map cleanly to what Engineering is measuring and acting on.

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Production impact on Redwood API request paths: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming (SSE/WebSocket where applicable)
- Reliability symptoms: elevated 5xx, timeouts, increased latency (p95/p99), streaming disconnects
- Hosted (shared) and Dedicated (reserved capacity)

**Out of scope (route to other playbooks/processes)**
- Feature requests / model onboarding requests (Product intake)
- Security incidents (Security & Compliance incident process)
- Billing questions / credit requests (Finance/RevOps + Contract/SLA process)

---

## Definitions (customer-facing safe language)
**SLO (Service Level Objective)**
Internal target for measured reliability (availability, latency, streaming reliability) over a rolling time window.

**Error budget**
The allowed amount of unreliability within the SLO window. When the budget is being rapidly consumed (“burning”), Engineering may apply mitigations and/or restrict risky changes.

**SLA (Service Level Agreement)**
Contractual commitment. CS should not quote SLA terms from memory; always reference the approved SLA mapping doc and the customer’s contract.

**Tier**
- **Hosted:** Shared infrastructure; best-effort commitments and broad status communication.
- **Dedicated:** Reserved capacity; tier-specific SLO dashboards and stronger comms/operational expectations.

---

## Quick decision tree (what to do first)
1) **Identify tier + region + endpoint**
   - Dedicated vs Hosted
   - Region(s) impacted
   - Endpoint class: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming

2) **Determine if this is: incident-level vs investigation**
   - Incident-level if any of the following are true:
     - Dedicated customer reports sustained elevated 5xx/timeouts OR severe latency regression that breaks their app
     - Multiple customers report similar symptoms in same region/model
     - Internal burn-rate alert firing (or SRE confirms budget burn)
     - Streaming disconnects are widespread (not limited to one client integration)

3) **Open the right channel(s) and collect required data (below)**

4) **Provide the customer an initial acknowledgement with an update cadence**
   - Do not promise a fix time.
   - Do not confirm “SLA breach” during an active event.

---

## Intake requirements (must-have data)
Use the shared intake template: **Google Drive → customer-success → “SLO escalation intake template”**.

### Minimum required fields (all tiers)
Provide the following in the escalation message/ticket:
- Customer name + account identifier (HubSpot ID if available)
- Tier: Hosted or Dedicated (include deployment name if Dedicated)
- Time window: start/end timestamps in UTC (and whether ongoing)
- Region(s) (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west)
- Endpoint class (chat/completions, embeddings, streaming)
- Models involved (as specified by the customer)
- Symptom type:
  - Elevated 5xx/4xx? (include a sample of status codes)
  - Latency increase? (include observed p95/p99 and baseline)
  - Streaming disconnects? (include frequency, client library, and error text)
- Volume impact estimate (approx RPS or requests affected)
- Any customer-provided request IDs, trace IDs, or X-Request-Id headers (minimum 5 examples spanning the window)
- Client details: SDK version, retries enabled, timeouts, streaming client type

### Dedicated-only additional fields (strongly requested)
- Dedicated deployment/cluster identifier (from Console)
- If customer has access: a screenshot or link to their tiered SLO dashboard view (do not ask them to share internal-only URLs; screenshots are ok)
- Whether the issue is isolated to a single region or all their configured regions
- Recent changes on customer side (deploys, prompt changes, traffic spikes)

### Common pitfalls (avoid escalation churn)
- “High latency” without timestamps/region is not actionable.
- Streaming disconnect reports must include whether the client saw a normal end-of-stream vs abrupt connection close.
- A single request ID from hours ago is rarely enough; collect a small set across the incident window.

---

## Routing and escalation channels
### Where to route (primary)
- **Dedicated customer production impact:**
  - Create/Update the customer-facing support ticket (Jira Support) and mark as **Priority: P1** or **P2** per severity rubric below.
  - Post in **#eng-sre** with the intake summary and ticket link.
  - If severe and ongoing, page SRE via on-call (see “When to page” below).

- **Hosted production impact affecting multiple customers or a region:**
  - Post in **#eng-sre** with intake summary.
  - Create a Support umbrella ticket if there isn’t one.

- **Single-customer Hosted issue likely integration-related:**
  - Triage within Support/SE first; only escalate to SRE if you can show service-side symptoms (5xx increase, latency regression vs baseline, multiple request IDs that fail similarly).

### When to page SRE (on-call)
Page SRE if any of the following are true:
- Dedicated customer cannot use the service (sustained 5xx/timeouts) and issue is ongoing
- Strong evidence of a platform incident (multiple customers, multiple regions, broad model impact)
- You see an internal **burn-rate alert** posted in the incidents channel OR SRE confirms budget burn

If unsure: post in **#eng-sre** and ask for guidance. Do not page for single-request anomalies.

### Who owns what (RACI)
- **CS/Support/SE:** customer communication, intake quality, expectation-setting, internal coordination
- **SRE:** incident triage, validation of SLO/budget state, mitigation, engineering comms to CS
- **Infra/Fleet:** capacity actions when requested by SRE
- **Product (Hosted/Dedicated):** SLA/SLO policy questions, customer-facing commitments, post-incident account follow-ups when needed

---

## Severity rubric (CS/Support view)
Use these categories for consistent internal handling:

### P1 (Critical)
- Dedicated customer: sustained inability to complete requests (timeouts/5xx), or extreme latency rendering service unusable
- Multi-customer impact in a region with evidence of a platform regression

**Action:** page SRE + open incident workflow if not already active.

### P2 (High)
- Dedicated: material latency regression (p95/p99) with partial success; or elevated error rates impacting key workflows
- Hosted: widespread streaming disconnects or elevated 5xx over sustained period

**Action:** escalate in #eng-sre; page only if ongoing and severe.

### P3 (Medium)
- Intermittent issues, limited scope, or suspected client-side retry/timeouts

**Action:** Support-led triage; escalate to SRE only with strong evidence.

### P4 (Low)
- Questions about SLO definitions, dashboards, error budget meaning, or historical reports

**Action:** respond using approved references; involve SRE only for clarifications or if documentation gaps exist.

---

## What to tell customers (safe messaging)
### General guidance
- Use measured language: “We are investigating elevated errors/latency in <region> affecting <endpoint>.”
- Set an update cadence (e.g., every 30–60 minutes during active incident).
- Do not promise:
  - A specific recovery time
  - Specific capacity allocations
  - SLA credits or breach confirmation

### Customer update templates
**Initial acknowledgement (Dedicated / P1-P2)**
> We’re actively investigating increased <errors/latency/streaming disconnects> impacting <region> for <endpoint>. We’ll share another update by <time in UTC>. If you can share 5–10 example request IDs from the last <N> minutes, it will help us pinpoint the failing path.

**Initial acknowledgement (Hosted / P2-P3)**
> We’re looking into reports of elevated <symptom> in <region>. We’ll update you once we’ve confirmed the scope and mitigation steps. If you have timestamps (UTC) and example request IDs, please share them.

**After mitigation applied**
> We’ve applied a mitigation to reduce <errors/latency>. We’re continuing to monitor. If you still see impact after <time>, please send additional request IDs and confirm which region(s) you’re hitting.

**If issue appears client/integration-specific**
> We’re not seeing a platform-wide issue at this time. To help isolate, could you confirm <SDK version>, <timeout settings>, and share <request IDs and timestamps>? We’ll keep investigating in parallel.

---

## How error budget state affects expectations
SRE may reference the tier’s current state as:
- **Green:** within budget; no active burn
- **Yellow:** elevated burn or near-threshold; mitigation may be underway; changes may be limited
- **Red:** budget exhausted or rapid burn; aggressive mitigations and release restrictions likely

### What CS should do when SRE says a tier is Yellow/Red
- Increase customer update cadence for Dedicated (and key Hosted escalations).
- Expect potential mitigation actions that can change behavior (e.g., routing/fallback changes, overload shedding).
- If the customer asks “does this count against the SLO?” do not speculate; respond:
  - “We measure reliability using specific definitions and exclusions. We can share the definitions and confirm the measured impact once the event is stabilized.”

---

## Dedicated vs Hosted: operational expectations
### Dedicated
- Higher urgency and tighter coordination expectations.
- Tier-specific dashboards exist and are used in incident validation.
- Comms should explicitly call out whether impact is limited to the customer’s deployment/region or broader.

### Hosted
- Often broader blast radius; status comms may be more general.
- Customer impact can vary based on model/route and traffic patterns.
- Focus on: region + endpoint + timeframe + request IDs to help SRE isolate the affected slice without exploding metric cardinality.

---

## Data handling and confidentiality
- Do not paste customer secrets, prompts, or full payloads into Slack.
- Request IDs/trace IDs are preferred for debugging.
- If payload examples are required, use approved secure channels and redact sensitive content.
- For enterprise Dedicated customers, assume logs/trace links are **customer-sensitive**; limit distribution to need-to-know.

---

## During an active incident: CS/SRE coordination loop
1) CS/Support opens or updates the primary ticket (customer-visible).
2) CS posts intake summary in #eng-sre with:
   - Tier, region, endpoint, timestamps, severity, ticket link, request IDs
3) SRE confirms whether an incident is active and whether burn-rate alerts are firing.
4) CS publishes customer updates on the agreed cadence.
5) If a status page update is needed, follow the Tiered Status Communications Guidelines page (linked).

**Do:** keep one source of truth per customer (support ticket) + one internal thread (incident channel or #eng-sre).

**Don’t:** create parallel Slack threads with different data; it slows triage.

---

## After the event: follow-up and closure
### Closure criteria (Support ticket)
Close the escalation loop when:
- SRE confirms stability (metrics back to baseline and no active burn)
- Customer confirms impact is resolved or materially improved
- Any interim mitigations that change behavior have been communicated (e.g., routing changes)

### Post-incident artifacts
Depending on severity:
- Incident link and summary (if an incident was declared)
- Customer-facing summary (Dedicated P1/P2) coordinated with SRE + PM Dedicated
- If customer asks for SLO/budget reporting, route to the approved reporting process and reference the SLO/SLA mapping page

### Documentation feedback loop
If escalation revealed confusion about definitions (e.g., streaming disconnect accounting), file a doc bug against the relevant Confluence page and tag:
- Elliot Price (customer-facing documentation lead)
- Aisha Bello (implementation lead)

---

## Appendix A: Internal escalation message format (copy/paste)
**Subject:** [SLO Escalation] <Customer> <Tier> <Region> <Symptom> <Severity>

**Summary:**
- Customer: <name> (<id>)
- Tier: Hosted | Dedicated (<deployment>)
- Severity: P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
- Region(s): <...>
- Endpoint: chat/completions | embeddings | streaming
- Window (UTC): <start> → <end/ongoing>
- Symptom: <5xx/timeout/latency/stream disconnect>
- Observed metrics (customer): <p95/p99, error rate, sample codes>
- Request IDs / trace IDs: <list>
- Ticket link: <Jira>

**Asks:**
- Validate if this aligns with current SLO/burn alerts
- Advise mitigation and comms guidance

---

## Appendix B: FAQ
**Q: Can we tell the customer they are “in SLA breach”?**
A: No. We can acknowledge impact and that we are investigating. SLA determination is contract + policy based and is handled post-event with Product/Legal guidance.

**Q: The customer asks whether streaming disconnects count against SLO. What do we say?**
A: Share that we track streaming reliability and classify disconnects based on server-side error vs client abort, with defined exclusions. Offer to follow up with measured impact after stabilization and point to the SLO definitions page.

**Q: The customer wants an error budget report.**
A: Dedicated customers may receive tier dashboard views and periodic reporting per their plan; coordinate with PM Dedicated + SRE. Hosted reporting is typically aggregate; do not promise account-specific error budget numbers unless explicitly supported.

**Q: Do we escalate every latency complaint?**
A: No. Escalate when there is clear deviation from baseline with timestamps/regions and sufficient request IDs, or when multiple customers report similar symptoms, or when SRE burn-rate alerts are firing.

- Aisha Bello (implementation lead)

---

## Appendix A: Internal escalation message format (copy/paste)
**Subject:** [SLO Escalation] <Customer> <Tier> <Region> <Symptom> <Severity>

**Summary:**
- Customer: <name> (<id>)
- Tier: Hosted | Dedicated (<deployment>)
- Severity: P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
- Region(s): <...>
- Endpoint: chat/completions | embeddings | streaming
- Window (UTC): <start> → <end/ongoing>
- Symptom: <5xx/timeout/latency/stream disconnect>
- Observed metrics (customer): <p95/p99, error rate, sample codes>
- Request IDs / trace IDs: <list>
- Ticket link: <Jira>

**Asks:**
- Validate if this aligns with current SLO/burn alerts
- Advise mitigation and comms guidance

---

## Appendix B: FAQ
**Q: Can we tell the customer they are “in SLA breach”?**
A: No. We can acknowledge impact and that we are investigating. SLA determination is contract + policy based and is handled post-event with Product/Legal guidance.

**Q: The customer asks whether streaming disconnects count against SLO. What do we say?**
A: Share that we track streaming reliability and classify disconnects based on server-side error vs client abort, with defined exclusions. Offer to follow up with measured impact after stabilization and point to the SLO definitions page.

**Q: The customer wants an error budget report.**
A: Dedicated customers may receive tier dashboard views and periodic reporting per their plan; coordinate with PM Dedicated + SRE. Hosted reporting is typically aggregate; do not promise account-specific error budget numbers unless explicitly supported.

**Q: Do we escalate every latency complaint?**
A: No. Escalate when there is clear deviation from baseline with timestamps/regions and sufficient request IDs, or when multiple customers report similar symptoms, or when SRE burn-rate alerts are firing.
text:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how Customer Success (CS), Support, and Solutions Engineering (SE) engage SRE during **SLO / error budget** events. It covers:
- How to route reliability escalations (Hosted vs Dedicated)
- What data is required (to avoid slow back-and-forth)
- What we can and cannot commit to in customer-facing communications
- Expected response/updates by tier and severity

This is a **joint CS/SRE** process. It is designed to align with the tiered SLO program and error budget enforcement so that customer escalations map cleanly to what Engineering is measuring and acting on.

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Production impact on Redwood API request paths: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming (SSE/WebSocket where applicable)
- Reliability symptoms: elevated 5xx, timeouts, increased latency (p95/p99), streaming disconnects
- Hosted (shared) and Dedicated (reserved capacity)

**Out of scope (route to other playbooks/processes)**
- Feature requests / model onboarding requests (Product intake)
- Security incidents (Security & Compliance incident process)
- Billing questions / credit requests (Finance/RevOps + Contract/SLA process)

---

## Definitions (customer-facing safe language)
**SLO (Service Level Objective)**
Internal target for measured reliability (availability, latency, streaming reliability) over a rolling time window.

**Error budget**
The allowed amount of unreliability within the SLO window. When the budget is being rapidly consumed (“burning”), Engineering may apply mitigations and/or restrict risky changes.

**SLA (Service Level Agreement)**
Contractual commitment. CS should not quote SLA terms from memory; always reference the approved SLA mapping doc and the customer’s contract.

**Tier**
- **Hosted:** Shared infrastructure; best-effort commitments and broad status communication.
- **Dedicated:** Reserved capacity; tier-specific SLO dashboards and stronger comms/operational expectations.

---

## Quick decision tree (what to do first)
1) **Identify tier + region + endpoint**
   - Dedicated vs Hosted
   - Region(s) impacted
   - Endpoint class: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming

2) **Determine if this is: incident-level vs investigation**
   - Incident-level if any of the following are true:
     - Dedicated customer reports sustained elevated 5xx/timeouts OR severe latency regression that breaks their app
     - Multiple customers report similar symptoms in same region/model
     - Internal burn-rate alert firing (or SRE confirms budget burn)
     - Streaming disconnects are widespread (not limited to one client integration)

3) **Open the right channel(s) and collect required data (below)**

4) **Provide the customer an initial acknowledgement with an update cadence**
   - Do not promise a fix time.
   - Do not confirm “SLA breach” during an active event.

---

## Intake requirements (must-have data)
Use the shared intake template: **Google Drive → customer-success → “SLO escalation intake template”**.

### Minimum required fields (all tiers)
Provide the following in the escalation message/ticket:
- Customer name + account identifier (HubSpot ID if available)
- Tier: Hosted or Dedicated (include deployment name if Dedicated)
- Time window: start/end timestamps in UTC (and whether ongoing)
- Region(s) (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west)
- Endpoint class (chat/completions, embeddings, streaming)
- Models involved (as specified by the customer)
- Symptom type:
  - Elevated 5xx/4xx? (include a sample of status codes)
  - Latency increase? (include observed p95/p99 and baseline)
  - Streaming disconnects? (include frequency, client library, and error text)
- Volume impact estimate (approx RPS or requests affected)
- Any customer-provided request IDs, trace IDs, or X-Request-Id headers (minimum 5 examples spanning the window)
- Client details: SDK version, retries enabled, timeouts, streaming client type

### Dedicated-only additional fields (strongly requested)
- Dedicated deployment/cluster identifier (from Console)
- If customer has access: a screenshot or link to their tiered SLO dashboard view (do not ask them to share internal-only URLs; screenshots are ok)
- Whether the issue is isolated to a single region or all their configured regions
- Recent changes on customer side (deploys, prompt changes, traffic spikes)

### Common pitfalls (avoid escalation churn)
- “High latency” without timestamps/region is not actionable.
- Streaming disconnect reports must include whether the client saw a normal end-of-stream vs abrupt connection close.
- A single request ID from hours ago is rarely enough; collect a small set across the incident window.

---

## Routing and escalation channels
### Where to route (primary)
- **Dedicated customer production impact:**
  - Create/Update the customer-facing support ticket (Jira Support) and mark as **Priority: P1** or **P2** per severity rubric below.
  - Post in **#eng-sre** with the intake summary and ticket link.
  - If severe and ongoing, page SRE via on-call (see “When to page” below).

- **Hosted production impact affecting multiple customers or a region:**
  - Post in **#eng-sre** with intake summary.
  - Create a Support umbrella ticket if there isn’t one.

- **Single-customer Hosted issue likely integration-related:**
  - Triage within Support/SE first; only escalate to SRE if you can show service-side symptoms (5xx increase, latency regression vs baseline, multiple request IDs that fail similarly).

### When to page SRE (on-call)
Page SRE if any of the following are true:
- Dedicated customer cannot use the service (sustained 5xx/timeouts) and issue is ongoing
- Strong evidence of a platform incident (multiple customers, multiple regions, broad model impact)
- You see an internal **burn-rate alert** posted in the incidents channel OR SRE confirms budget burn

If unsure: post in **#eng-sre** and ask for guidance. Do not page for single-request anomalies.

### Who owns what (RACI)
- **CS/Support/SE:** customer communication, intake quality, expectation-setting, internal coordination
- **SRE:** incident triage, validation of SLO/budget state, mitigation, engineering comms to CS
- **Infra/Fleet:** capacity actions when requested by SRE
- **Product (Hosted/Dedicated):** SLA/SLO policy questions, customer-facing commitments, post-incident account follow-ups when needed

---

## Severity rubric (CS/Support view)
Use these categories for consistent internal handling:

### P1 (Critical)
- Dedicated customer: sustained inability to complete requests (timeouts/5xx), or extreme latency rendering service unusable
- Multi-customer impact in a region with evidence of a platform regression

**Action:** page SRE + open incident workflow if not already active.

### P2 (High)
- Dedicated: material latency regression (p95/p99) with partial success; or elevated error rates impacting key workflows
- Hosted: widespread streaming disconnects or elevated 5xx over sustained period

**Action:** escalate in #eng-sre; page only if ongoing and severe.

### P3 (Medium)
- Intermittent issues, limited scope, or suspected client-side retry/timeouts

**Action:** Support-led triage; escalate to SRE only with strong evidence.

### P4 (Low)
- Questions about SLO definitions, dashboards, error budget meaning, or historical reports

**Action:** respond using approved references; involve SRE only for clarifications or if documentation gaps exist.

---

## What to tell customers (safe messaging)
### General guidance
- Use measured language: “We are investigating elevated errors/latency in <region> affecting <endpoint>.”
- Set an update cadence (e.g., every 30–60 minutes during active incident).
- Do not promise:
  - A specific recovery time
  - Specific capacity allocations
  - SLA credits or breach confirmation

### Customer update templates
**Initial acknowledgement (Dedicated / P1-P2)**
> We’re actively investigating increased <errors/latency/streaming disconnects> impacting <region> for <endpoint>. We’ll share another update by <time in UTC>. If you can share 5–10 example request IDs from the last <N> minutes, it will help us pinpoint the failing path.

**Initial acknowledgement (Hosted / P2-P3)**
> We’re looking into reports of elevated <symptom> in <region>. We’ll update you once we’ve confirmed the scope and mitigation steps. If you have timestamps (UTC) and example request IDs, please share them.

**After mitigation applied**
> We’ve applied a mitigation to reduce <errors/latency>. We’re continuing to monitor. If you still see impact after <time>, please send additional request IDs and confirm which region(s) you’re hitting.

**If issue appears client/integration-specific**
> We’re not seeing a platform-wide issue at this time. To help isolate, could you confirm <SDK version>, <timeout settings>, and share <request IDs and timestamps>? We’ll keep investigating in parallel.

---

## How error budget state affects expectations
SRE may reference the tier’s current state as:
- **Green:** within budget; no active burn
- **Yellow:** elevated burn or near-threshold; mitigation may be underway; changes may be limited
- **Red:** budget exhausted or rapid burn; aggressive mitigations and release restrictions likely

### What CS should do when SRE says a tier is Yellow/Red
- Increase customer update cadence for Dedicated (and key Hosted escalations).
- Expect potential mitigation actions that can change behavior (e.g., routing/fallback changes, overload shedding).
- If the customer asks “does this count against the SLO?” do not speculate; respond:
  - “We measure reliability using specific definitions and exclusions. We can share the definitions and confirm the measured impact once the event is stabilized.”

---

## Dedicated vs Hosted: operational expectations
### Dedicated
- Higher urgency and tighter coordination expectations.
- Tier-specific dashboards exist and are used in incident validation.
- Comms should explicitly call out whether impact is limited to the customer’s deployment/region or broader.

### Hosted
- Often broader blast radius; status comms may be more general.
- Customer impact can vary based on model/route and traffic patterns.
- Focus on: region + endpoint + timeframe + request IDs to help SRE isolate the affected slice without exploding metric cardinality.

---

## Data handling and confidentiality
- Do not paste customer secrets, prompts, or full payloads into Slack.
- Request IDs/trace IDs are preferred for debugging.
- If payload examples are required, use approved secure channels and redact sensitive content.
- For enterprise Dedicated customers, assume logs/trace links are **customer-sensitive**; limit distribution to need-to-know.

---

## During an active incident: CS/SRE coordination loop
1) CS/Support opens or updates the primary ticket (customer-visible).
2) CS posts intake summary in #eng-sre with:
   - Tier, region, endpoint, timestamps, severity, ticket link, request IDs
3) SRE confirms whether an incident is active and whether burn-rate alerts are firing.
4) CS publishes customer updates on the agreed cadence.
5) If a status page update is needed, follow the Tiered Status Communications Guidelines page (linked).

**Do:** keep one source of truth per customer (support ticket) + one internal thread (incident channel or #eng-sre).

**Don’t:** create parallel Slack threads with different data; it slows triage.

---

## After the event: follow-up and closure
### Closure criteria (Support ticket)
Close the escalation loop when:
- SRE confirms stability (metrics back to baseline and no active burn)
- Customer confirms impact is resolved or materially improved
- Any interim mitigations that change behavior have been communicated (e.g., routing changes)

### Post-incident artifacts
Depending on severity:
- Incident link and summary (if an incident was declared)
- Customer-facing summary (Dedicated P1/P2) coordinated with SRE + PM Dedicated
- If customer asks for SLO/budget reporting, route to the approved reporting process and reference the SLO/SLA mapping page

### Documentation feedback loop
If escalation revealed confusion about definitions (e.g., streaming disconnect accounting), file a doc bug against the relevant Confluence page and tag:
- Elliot Price (customer-facing documentation lead)
- Aisha Bello (implementation lead)

---

## Appendix A: Internal escalation message format (copy/paste)
**Subject:** [SLO Escalation] <Customer> <Tier> <Region> <Symptom> <Severity>

**Summary:**
- Customer: <name> (<id>)
- Tier: Hosted | Dedicated (<deployment>)
- Severity: P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
- Region(s): <...>
- Endpoint: chat/completions | embeddings | streaming
- Window (UTC): <start> → <end/ongoing>
- Symptom: <5xx/timeout/latency/stream disconnect>
- Observed metrics (customer): <p95/p99, error rate, sample codes>
- Request IDs / trace IDs: <list>
- Ticket link: <Jira>

**Asks:**
- Validate if this aligns with current SLO/burn alerts
- Advise mitigation and comms guidance

---

## Appendix B: FAQ
**Q: Can we tell the customer they are “in SLA breach”?**
A: No. We can acknowledge impact and that we are investigating. SLA determination is contract + policy based and is handled post-event with Product/Legal guidance.

**Q: The customer asks whether streaming disconnects count against SLO. What do we say?**
A: Share that we track streaming reliability and classify disconnects based on server-side error vs client abort, with defined exclusions. Offer to follow up with measured impact after stabilization and point to the SLO definitions page.

**Q: The customer wants an error budget report.**
A: Dedicated customers may receive tier dashboard views and periodic reporting per their plan; coordinate with PM Dedicated + SRE. Hosted reporting is typically aggregate; do not promise account-specific error budget numbers unless explicitly supported.

**Q: Do we escalate every latency complaint?**
A: No. Escalate when there is clear deviation from baseline with timestamps/regions and sufficient request IDs, or when multiple customers report similar symptoms, or when SRE burn-rate alerts are firing.

source.title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)
source.author: Jasmine Liu
source.content:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how Customer Success (CS) and Support partner with Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), and Security/GRC during late-stage security reviews. It standardizes:
- When and how to loop in Security/GRC
- How to handle urgent deadlines and incomplete questionnaires
- Escalation tiers, response SLAs, and handoff points
- How we avoid unapproved claims and keep a clear audit trail

This is an internal operating document. It references restricted evidence processes (e.g., SOC 2 report) but does not include restricted artifacts.

---

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following occur:
- A prospect/customer requests SOC 2 report, pen test summary, subprocessor list, or other formal evidence.
- A prospect/customer sends a security questionnaire, RFP security section, or needs a security addendum.
- Procurement/security introduces a hard deadline (\<= 5 business days) or threatens deal delay without answers.
- The customer requires deployment-specific assurances (Dedicated vs Private vs Hosted) around data flows, retention, encryption, KMS, logging, SSO/RBAC, or incident notification.

Not in scope:
- Product roadmap commitments or “custom features” to satisfy a questionnaire. Use the Exceptions Policy for non-supported controls.

---

## Roles and responsibilities (who does what)
### Primary owners
- Sales / AE: owns customer timeline, coordinates meetings, maintains CRM notes, ensures customer has correct links.
- Solutions Engineering (SE): owns technical narrative (deployment mode, architecture diagrams, controls explanations).
- Security Engineering: validates technical claims, clarifies implementation details, reviews non-standard security language.
- GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance): owns audit/compliance language and evidence distribution process (SOC 2 under NDA, artifact lists, timelines).
- Legal: owns contractual language (DPA, security addendum, breach notification terms).
- CS / Support: owns the escalation workflow, ensures correct process is followed, prevents churn/relationship damage, and creates a clean handoff to post-close operations.

### CS/Support “single-threaded owner” (STO)
For any security review that is time-bound or involves evidence requests, CS/Support assigns a single-threaded owner who:
- Creates/updates the tracking ticket(s)
- Confirms next steps and internal owners
- Drives SLA compliance and escalations
- Ensures final approved language is used

---

## Golden rules (do / don’t)
### Do
- Use the published Security FAQ and standard answer blocks as the starting point.
- Confirm the customer’s deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) before answering.
- Route formal evidence requests through the Evidence Request Playbook (NDA checks, controlled distribution).
- Track all customer asks, versions, and approvals in a ticket and in CRM.

### Don’t
- Don’t claim certifications we do not have (e.g., “ISO 27001 certified”) or imply “compliant with X” unless approved.
- Don’t promise data residency beyond what is available in the customer’s selected region/deployment mode.
- Don’t share restricted evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test summary) over email or Slack without following the controlled process.
- Don’t edit standard RFP/security language “just to make it fit.” Use the approval workflow.

---

## Intake checklist (required information before escalating)
CS/Support STO should collect the following before pulling in Security/GRC:
1) Account + deal context
- Customer/prospect name, segment, region
- Stage (evaluation, procurement, renewal, expansion)
- Business impact and date the deal will stall without response

2) Deployment mode and architecture
- Hosted API / Dedicated / Private (VPC or on-prem)
- Target cloud/region(s) and any data residency constraints
- Any special requirements (HIPAA/BAA request, financial services controls, air-gapped constraints)

3) Customer asks (exact wording)
- Questionnaire/RFP sections pasted verbatim or attached
- Specific artifacts requested (SOC 2, policies, subprocessor list, pen test summary)
- Required response format and due date/time zone

4) Constraints
- NDA status (executed? pending? required?)
- Customer’s requested response channel (portal, email, GRC tool)
- Any customer “red lines” (e.g., retention must be configurable to X days)

5) Internal context
- Existing approved snippets used (link to FAQ section or RFP library block)
- Any planned deviations from standard language (must be explicitly flagged)

If any of the above is missing, proceed with escalation but mark as “incomplete intake” and request missing items in parallel.

---

## Escalation tiers and SLAs
CS/Support uses the following tiers to set expectations and drive responsiveness.

### P0 — Imminent deadline / deal-stopping within 24 hours
Criteria:
- Customer deadline in \<= 1 business day OR live procurement call requiring answers
- Customer requires evidence distribution (SOC 2) to proceed immediately

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge internally within 30 minutes (business hours)
- First response plan within 2 hours

Approvals:
- Any non-standard claim requires Security + GRC approval (and Legal if contractual)

### P1 — Time-bound security review (2–5 business days)
Criteria:
- RFP/questionnaire due in 2–5 business days
- Customer is blocked pending “security sign-off” meeting

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge internally within 4 business hours
- Draft response/plan within 1 business day

### P2 — Standard review (\>= 6 business days)
Criteria:
- No imminent risk; customer requests information for internal review

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge within 1 business day
- Provide materials/plan within 3 business days

Business hours assumption: Mon–Fri, local team coverage. If customer is in EMEA/APAC and timing is critical, treat as one tier higher to account for time zone compression.

---

## Step-by-step workflow (CS/Support STO)
### Step 1 — Create a tracking ticket and identify the STO
Create a ticket in the internal system of record (Support/Jira or the designated GTM intake queue) with:
- Tier (P0/P1/P2)
- Due date/time zone
- Links/attachments to the questionnaire/RFP portal
- Requested artifacts list
- Deployment mode and region

Add watchers:
- AE + SE lead on the account
- GRC on-call analyst (or team alias)
- Security Engineering reviewer (if technical claims are involved)
- Legal reviewer (if contract language requested)

### Step 2 — Send the internal “first plan” message
Within the SLA window, post a clear plan (in the ticket and in the designated internal channel):
- What we will answer with standard language
- What requires review/approval
- What evidence requires NDA + controlled process
- Who owns each workstream and deadlines

### Step 3 — Separate “language” work from “evidence” work
Run two tracks in parallel:
A) Response language
- Use the Security FAQ and Standard Answers as the baseline.
- If the questionnaire asks for non-supported controls, use the Exceptions Policy to propose alternative language.
- If the customer requires a custom architecture/security narrative, SE drafts and Security validates.

B) Evidence requests
- Follow the SOC 2 Customer Evidence Request Playbook.
- Confirm NDA status before any restricted artifact is shared.
- Log exactly which artifacts were shared, when, and via what method.

### Step 4 — Run a time-boxed review cycle
For P0/P1, use a single redline pass with explicit approvers:
- Security Engineering: technical controls, logging, encryption, runtime behavior
- GRC: compliance posture wording, audit periods, “report under NDA” phrasing
- Legal: DPA/addendum terms, breach notification, liability language

If edits exceed one pass or the customer changes scope, re-baseline and reset expectations with Sales.

### Step 5 — Customer-facing delivery
CS/Support STO ensures:
- Final answers use approved language and correct deployment mode caveats
- Restricted artifacts are delivered only via the approved method
- The customer receives a concise “what we sent + how to follow up” summary

Recommended customer email structure:
- One-paragraph overview
- Bullets of attached/linked documents
- Evidence request steps (if applicable)
- Offer a 30-minute security review call with Security/GRC if needed

### Step 6 — Close the loop and create post-close handoff notes
Once the security review is complete (or “good enough” for procurement), capture:
- Final list of commitments made (e.g., log retention option selected, region selection)
- Any exceptions granted and their approvers
- Any open items that must be implemented/configured during onboarding

Add the notes to CRM and to the account’s implementation plan.

---

## Common scenarios and how to handle them
### Scenario A: “Send us your SOC 2 report today”
1) Confirm whether NDA is executed.
2) If NDA is not executed, coordinate with Legal/Sales immediately; do not share the report.
3) Use the approved wording: “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA upon request.”
4) Open an evidence request per the playbook and track distribution.

### Scenario B: Customer asks “Do you train on our data?”
- Use the exact FAQ language and definitions (training vs transient processing; prompts/logs vs model weights).
- Do not introduce new qualifiers. If customer asks for contract language, route to Legal.

### Scenario C: EU data residency objection (must stay in EU)
- Confirm which regions are available for the chosen deployment mode.
- Use the approved residency language (no blanket promises). If constraints can only be met with Private, have SE present options.
- Escalate to GRC/Security if the customer requests written guarantees beyond standard documentation.

### Scenario D: Questionnaire asks for a control we do not support (e.g., specific IdP feature, custom audit event)
- Do not answer “Yes” with future intent.
- Use the Exceptions Policy to provide alternative language and document compensating controls.
- If customer insists on a “must-have,” flag to Product Security PM for capability alignment (without committing roadmap).

---

## Communication channels and meeting guidance
### Internal channels (recommended)
- Use the designated security review intake channel and link the tracking ticket.
- Keep decisions in the ticket. Slack is for coordination, not for the authoritative record.

### Customer call structure (30–45 minutes)
1) Confirm deployment mode + region + data flow expectations
2) Walk through top 5 controls customer cares about (usually: data use, encryption, access controls, logging, retention)
3) Evidence requests: explain NDA gating and timelines
4) Capture remaining blockers and assign owners

Avoid:
- Live “off the cuff” answers to compliance/certification questions. Offer to follow up with approved language.

---

## Handback to CS/Support after close (operationalization)
If any of the following were discussed, CS must validate configuration during onboarding:
- Audit log export destination (SIEM/S3) and retention window
- SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning requirements
- RBAC roles and least-privilege model for admins
- Data retention/deletion configuration for the chosen deployment mode
- Private deployment network boundaries (VPC peering, egress controls) and key management (KMS/HSM)

Create an onboarding checklist item for each requirement and confirm acceptance criteria with the customer security contact.

---

## Templates
### Internal escalation summary (paste into ticket)
Account:
Deployment mode:
Region(s):
Tier (P0/P1/P2):
Customer deadline (date/time zone):
Top asks (verbatim):
Artifacts requested:
NDA status:
Draft response plan (what we can answer with standard language vs needs review):
Owners:

### Customer follow-up email (minimal)
Subject: Redwood security review materials + next steps

Hi <Name>,

Sharing the requested security materials for your review:
- <Doc link 1>
- <Doc link 2>
- <Audit logging / retention overview>

For the SOC 2 Type II report (restricted), we can provide it under NDA via our controlled evidence request process. If you confirm NDA status, we will initiate distribution.

Happy to schedule a 30-minute security review call to walk through data handling, encryption, access controls, and audit logging.

Best,
<Name>

---

## Metrics and continuous improvement
CS/Support STO should log the following for quarterly review:
- Time-to-acknowledge and time-to-first-draft by tier
- # of evidence requests and average turnaround time
- Top repeated questionnaire questions and where our standard language was insufficient
- Any instances of “unapproved language” caught in review (and required remediation)

Feedback loop:
- If a question repeats \>= 3 times in a month, open a request to update the Security FAQ or RFP library rather than rewriting per deal.

---

## Ownership and review cadence
Owner: Customer Success (CS sponsor), in partnership with Security/GRC.
Review cadence: Quarterly, or immediately after any material change to security posture, evidence availability, or product behavior affecting common questionnaire answers.

Last review highlights (2026-02-21):
- Tightened P0/P1 SLA expectations and clarified NDA gating for evidence.
- Standardized separation of “language” vs “evidence” workstreams.
- Added explicit post-close operationalization checklist items (SSO/SCIM, audit logs, retention).

- Track all customer asks, versions, and approvals in a ticket and in CRM.

### Don’t
- Don’t claim certifications we do not have (e.g., “ISO 27001 certified”) or imply “compliant with X” unless approved.
- Don’t promise data residency beyond what is available in the customer’s selected region/deployment mode.
- Don’t share restricted evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test summary) over email or Slack without following the controlled process.
- Don’t edit standard RFP/security language “just to make it fit.” Use the approval workflow.

---

## Intake checklist (required information before escalating)
CS/Support STO should collect the following before pulling in Security/GRC:
1) Account + deal context
- Customer/prospect name, segment, region
- Stage (evaluation, procurement, renewal, expansion)
- Business impact and date the deal will stall without response

2) Deployment mode and architecture
- Hosted API / Dedicated / Private (VPC or on-prem)
- Target cloud/region(s) and any data residency constraints
- Any special requirements (HIPAA/BAA request, financial services controls, air-gapped constraints)

3) Customer asks (exact wording)
- Questionnaire/RFP sections pasted verbatim or attached
- Specific artifacts requested (SOC 2, policies, subprocessor list, pen test summary)
- Required response format and due date/time zone

4) Constraints
- NDA status (executed? pending? required?)
- Customer’s requested response channel (portal, email, GRC tool)
- Any customer “red lines” (e.g., retention must be configurable to X days)

5) Internal context
- Existing approved snippets used (link to FAQ section or RFP library block)
- Any planned deviations from standard language (must be explicitly flagged)

If any of the above is missing, proceed with escalation but mark as “incomplete intake” and request missing items in parallel.

---

## Escalation tiers and SLAs
CS/Support uses the following tiers to set expectations and drive responsiveness.

### P0 — Imminent deadline / deal-stopping within 24 hours
Criteria:
- Customer deadline in \<= 1 business day OR live procurement call requiring answers
- Customer requires evidence distribution (SOC 2) to proceed immediately

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge internally within 30 minutes (business hours)
- First response plan within 2 hours

Approvals:
- Any non-standard claim requires Security + GRC approval (and Legal if contractual)

### P1 — Time-bound security review (2–5 business days)
Criteria:
- RFP/questionnaire due in 2–5 business days
- Customer is blocked pending “security sign-off” meeting

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge internally within 4 business hours
- Draft response/plan within 1 business day

### P2 — Standard review (\>= 6 business days)
Criteria:
- No imminent risk; customer requests information for internal review

SLA targets:
- Acknowledge within 1 business day
- Provide materials/plan within 3 business days

Business hours assumption: Mon–Fri, local team coverage. If customer is in EMEA/APAC and timing is critical, treat as one tier higher to account for time zone compression.

---

## Step-by-step workflow (CS/Support STO)
### Step 1 — Create a tracking ticket and identify the STO
Create a ticket in the internal system of record (Support/Jira or the designated GTM intake queue) with:
- Tier (P0/P1/P2)
- Due date/time zone
- Links/attachments to the questionnaire/RFP portal
- Requested artifacts list
- Deployment mode and region

Add watchers:
- AE + SE lead on the account
- GRC on-call analyst (or team alias)
- Security Engineering reviewer (if technical claims are involved)
- Legal reviewer (if contract language requested)

### Step 2 — Send the internal “first plan” message
Within the SLA window, post a clear plan (in the ticket and in the designated internal channel):
- What we will answer with standard language
- What requires review/approval
- What evidence requires NDA + controlled process
- Who owns each workstream and deadlines

### Step 3 — Separate “language” work from “evidence” work
Run two tracks in parallel:
A) Response language
- Use the Security FAQ and Standard Answers as the baseline.
- If the questionnaire asks for non-supported controls, use the Exceptions Policy to propose alternative language.
- If the customer requires a custom architecture/security narrative, SE drafts and Security validates.

B) Evidence requests
- Follow the SOC 2 Customer Evidence Request Playbook.
- Confirm NDA status before any restricted artifact is shared.
- Log exactly which artifacts were shared, when, and via what method.

### Step 4 — Run a time-boxed review cycle
For P0/P1, use a single redline pass with explicit approvers:
- Security Engineering: technical controls, logging, encryption, runtime behavior
- GRC: compliance posture wording, audit periods, “report under NDA” phrasing
- Legal: DPA/addendum terms, breach notification, liability language

If edits exceed one pass or the customer changes scope, re-baseline and reset expectations with Sales.

### Step 5 — Customer-facing delivery
CS/Support STO ensures:
- Final answers use approved language and correct deployment mode caveats
- Restricted artifacts are delivered only via the approved method
- The customer receives a concise “what we sent + how to follow up” summary

Recommended customer email structure:
- One-paragraph overview
- Bullets of attached/linked documents
- Evidence request steps (if applicable)
- Offer a 30-minute security review call with Security/GRC if needed

### Step 6 — Close the loop and create post-close handoff notes
Once the security review is complete (or “good enough” for procurement), capture:
- Final list of commitments made (e.g., log retention option selected, region selection)
- Any exceptions granted and their approvers
- Any open items that must be implemented/configured during onboarding

Add the notes to CRM and to the account’s implementation plan.

---

## Common scenarios and how to handle them
### Scenario A: “Send us your SOC 2 report today”
1) Confirm whether NDA is executed.
2) If NDA is not executed, coordinate with Legal/Sales immediately; do not share the report.
3) Use the approved wording: “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA upon request.”
4) Open an evidence request per the playbook and track distribution.

### Scenario B: Customer asks “Do you train on our data?”
- Use the exact FAQ language and definitions (training vs transient processing; prompts/logs vs model weights).
- Do not introduce new qualifiers. If customer asks for contract language, route to Legal.

### Scenario C: EU data residency objection (must stay in EU)
- Confirm which regions are available for the chosen deployment mode.
- Use the approved residency language (no blanket promises). If constraints can only be met with Private, have SE present options.
- Escalate to GRC/Security if the customer requests written guarantees beyond standard documentation.

### Scenario D: Questionnaire asks for a control we do not support (e.g., specific IdP feature, custom audit event)
- Do not answer “Yes” with future intent.
- Use the Exceptions Policy to provide alternative language and document compensating controls.
- If customer insists on a “must-have,” flag to Product Security PM for capability alignment (without committing roadmap).

---

## Communication channels and meeting guidance
### Internal channels (recommended)
- Use the designated security review intake channel and link the tracking ticket.
- Keep decisions in the ticket. Slack is for coordination, not for the authoritative record.

### Customer call structure (30–45 minutes)
1) Confirm deployment mode + region + data flow expectations
2) Walk through top 5 controls customer cares about (usually: data use, encryption, access controls, logging, retention)
3) Evidence requests: explain NDA gating and timelines
4) Capture remaining blockers and assign owners

Avoid:
- Live “off the cuff” answers to compliance/certification questions. Offer to follow up with approved language.

---

## Handback to CS/Support after close (operationalization)
If any of the following were discussed, CS must validate configuration during onboarding:
- Audit log export destination (SIEM/S3) and retention window
- SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning requirements
- RBAC roles and least-privilege model for admins
- Data retention/deletion configuration for the chosen deployment mode
- Private deployment network boundaries (VPC peering, egress controls) and key management (KMS/HSM)

Create an onboarding checklist item for each requirement and confirm acceptance criteria with the customer security contact.

---

## Templates
### Internal escalation summary (paste into ticket)
Account:
Deployment mode:
Region(s):
Tier (P0/P1/P2):
Customer deadline (date/time zone):
Top asks (verbatim):
Artifacts requested:
NDA status:
Draft response plan (what we can answer with standard language vs needs review):
Owners:

### Customer follow-up email (minimal)
Subject: Redwood security review materials + next steps

Hi <Name>,

Sharing the requested security materials for your review:
- <Doc link 1>
- <Doc link 2>
- <Audit logging / retention overview>

For the SOC 2 Type II report (restricted), we can provide it under NDA via our controlled evidence request process. If you confirm NDA status, we will initiate distribution.

Happy to schedule a 30-minute security review call to walk through data handling, encryption, access controls, and audit logging.

Best,
<Name>

---

## Metrics and continuous improvement
CS/Support STO should log the following for quarterly review:
- Time-to-acknowledge and time-to-first-draft by tier
- # of evidence requests and average turnaround time
- Top repeated questionnaire questions and where our standard language was insufficient
- Any instances of “unapproved language” caught in review (and required remediation)

Feedback loop:
- If a question repeats \>= 3 times in a month, open a request to update the Security FAQ or RFP library rather than rewriting per deal.

---

## Ownership and review cadence
Owner: Customer Success (CS sponsor), in partnership with Security/GRC.
Review cadence: Quarterly, or immediately after any material change to security posture, evidence availability, or product behavior affecting common questionnaire answers.

Last review highlights (2026-02-21):
- Tightened P0/P1 SLA expectations and clarified NDA gating for evidence.
- Standardized separation of “language” vs “evidence” workstreams.
- Added explicit post-close operationalization checklist items (SSO/SCIM, audit logs, retention).

## Purpose
This playbook defines how Customer Success (CS) and Support partner with Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), and Security/GRC during late-stage security reviews. It standardizes:
- When and how to loop in Security/GRC
- How to handle urgent deadlines and incomplete questionnaires
- Escalation tiers, response SLAs, and handoff points
- How we avoid unapproved claims and keep a clear audit trail

This is an internal operating document. It references restricted evidence processes (e.g., SOC 2 report) but does not include restricted artifacts.

---

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following occur:
- A prospect/customer requests SOC 2 report, pen test summary, subprocessor list, or other formal evidence.
- A prospect/customer sends a security questionnaire, RFP security section, or needs a security addendum.
- Procurement/security introduces a hard deadline (\<= 5 business days) or threatens deal delay without answers.
- The customer requires deployment-specific assurances (Dedicated vs Private vs Hosted) around data flows, retention, encryption, KMS, logging, SSO/RBAC, or incident notification.

Not in scope:
- Product roadmap commitments or “custom features” to satisfy a questionnaire. Use the Exceptions Policy for non-supported controls.

---

## Roles and responsibilities (who does what)
### Primary owners
- Sales / AE: owns customer timeline, coordinates meetings, maintains CRM notes, ensures customer has correct links.
- Solutions Engineering (SE): owns technical narrative (deployment mode, architecture diagrams, controls explanations).
- Security Engineering: validates technical claims, clarifies implementation details, reviews non-standard security language.
- GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance): owns audit/compliance language and evidence distribution process (SOC 2 under NDA, artifact lists, timelines).
- Legal: owns contractual language (DPA, security addendum, breach notification terms).
- CS / Support: owns the escalation workflow, ensures correct process is followed, prevents churn/relationship damage, and creates a clean handoff to post-close operations.

### CS/Support “single-threaded owner” (STO)
For any security review that is time-bound or involves evidence requests, CS/Support assigns a single-threaded owner who:
- Creates/updates the tracking ticket(s)

- Drives SLA compliance and escalations

3) Customer asks (exact wording)

4) Constraints
- NDA status (executed? pending? required?)

5) Internal context

If any of the above is missing, proceed with escalation but mark as “incomplete intake” and request missing items in parallel.

### P2 — Standard review (\>= 6 business days)

Business hours assumption: Mon–Fri, local team coverage. If customer is in EMEA/APAC and timing is critical, treat as one tier higher to account for time zone compression.

---

## Step-by-step workflow (CS/Support STO)
### Step 1 — Create a tracking ticket and identify the STO

source.title: Proactive Telemetry & SLA Onboarding Checklist
source.author: Asha Patel
source.content:
Overview
---------
This document is a pragmatic, step-by-step checklist for onboarding a new enterprise customer with proactive telemetry, SLO mapping, and QBR-ready artifacts. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, and the customer engineering contact. The goal is to ensure that from day zero we have the signals, dashboards, and alerts required to operate the tenant within agreed SLAs and to produce reliable QBR metrics.

Scope
-----
- Applies to Hosted and Dedicated customers (Private deployments need network-specific gating described in the ‘Network & Security’ section).
- Covers: metric collection, trace and request tagging, SLO definition, alert thresholds, dashboarding, and QBR artifacts.
- Does not replace incident runbooks — use the incident-runbook specific to the service for live response.

Prerequisites (Customer / Redwood)
--------------------------------
1. Signed onboarding form with agreed SLOs and data-sharing consent.
2. Customer engineering contact has Redwoods' integration keys and sandbox tenant (if requested).
3. Access to customer's telemetry sink or permission to forward metrics/traces to Redwood telemetry gatekeeper.
4. Redwood onboarding engineer assigned and a shared Slack/Email channel established.

Key Telemetry Signals (required)
-------------------------------
Collect these signals with the tags described below. Each signal must be emitted with request_id and tenant_id (or dedicated_tenant) for correlation.

- Request counters: requests.total, requests.success, requests.error (label by model, route, tenant_id)
- Latency histograms: response.latency_ms (p50/p90/p99 buckets)
- Token counters: tokens.generated (per-model, per-route)
- Queue/backlog: serve.queue_length, serve.pending_batches
- Resource utilization: gpu.utilization, gpu.memory_free_mb, cpu.utilization
- Cache effectiveness: kvcache.hit, kvcache.miss, kvcache.evictions
- Retries and fallbacks: request.fallback_count, model.switch_count
- Error classes: error.type (rate-limited, infra, model_invalid_input, model_timeout)

Tagging and Naming Conventions
------------------------------
- tenant_id: required for multi-tenant customers. For Dedicated, use dedicated_tenant.
- model: canonical model id used by platform (e.g., gpt-j-8k-v2)
- route: api route or product feature (e.g., chat.generate, embeddings.index)
- env: prod | staging | canary
- shard/region: for geo routing visibility

SLO Mapping Template (example)
------------------------------
- Availability SLO: 99.9% successful_requests / total_requests measured monthly (error types: infra AND model_timeout count against SLO).
- Latency SLO: 95% of responses <= 300ms for short-sequence generation paths (<= 512 tokens).
- Token-cost guardrail (informational): average tokens.generated per request <= 120 for interactive route.

Instrumentation Checklist (step-by-step)
---------------------------------------
1. Verify SDK/sidecar instrumentation is deployed and reporting to telemetry-gatekeeper: run the sample curl below and confirm metrics appear in the metrics view.
   - Example curl: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/demo -H 'Authorization: Bearer <key>' -d '{"prompt":"test"}'
2. Confirm request_id and tenant_id labels are present on all request metrics. Missing labels block tenant-level SLO calculation.
3. Ensure histogram buckets include 10ms..10s range for response.latency_ms.
4. Validate GPU telemetry: check gpu.utilization and gpu.memory_free_mb are reported by node exporter or GPU agent.
5. Enable KV cache metrics on the model-serving config if using prefix caching.
6. If streaming is used, instrument stream.chunk.count and stream.first_byte_latency_ms.

Dashboards to Create (minimum set)
----------------------------------
- Tenant SLO Overview (single panel with availability, latency p95 and p99 for primary routes)
- Model Economy (tokens.generated, cost_estimate_per_minute by model)
- Resource Pressure (gpu.utilization heatmap across nodes serving the tenant)
- Cache Health (kvcache hit ratio and evictions over time)
- Error Breakdown (top error types and top endpoints producing errors)
- Queue and Backlog (pending_batches and average batch size)

Alerting & Thresholds (recommended)
----------------------------------
Please treat thresholds as starting points that must be tuned with customer-specific traffic shape. Alerts are split into P1 (actionable immediate response) and P2 (investigate next business day).

P1 (page on-call):
| Signal | Condition | Notes |
| gpu.utilization | > 95% sustained for 3m | Risk of degraded tail latency |
| requests.error | error_rate > 0.5% over 5m AND error.type in (infra, model_timeout) | Blamable against SLO |
| serve.pending_batches | > 100 for 5m | Backlog causing latency spikes |

P2 (notify Slack Ops or owner):
| Signal | Condition | Notes |
| response.latency.p99 | > 1.5x SLO for 15m | Degradation trend |
| kvcache.evictions | > baseline*3 in 10m | Potential cache misconfiguration |
| tokens.generated.avg | > expected_per_request*2 over 30m | Cost anomaly watch |

Rollout & Validation Procedure
------------------------------
1. Dry-run: Enable telemetry collection on a canary subset (1–5% of traffic). Validate event schema and sample queries.
2. Full-rollout: Switch the production routing and enable SLO calculation.
3. Observe for 48–72 hours: confirm alerts noise is acceptable and dashboards show expected baselines.
4. Post-rollout review: produce a short onboarding summary for the QBR pack (see QBR Artifacts).

QBR Artifacts (what we deliver at first QBR)
-------------------------------------------
- Tenant SLO report (month-to-date and rolling-30d) with annotations for incidents.
- Cost-per-1k-tokens trend by route and model.
- Top 5 recommendations to improve latency/cost (batching, quantization, route split).
- List of action items and owners with ETA.

Validations & Sample Queries
---------------------------
Prometheus / M3 style examples:
- Availability: sum(rate(requests_success_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[30d])) / sum(rate(requests_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[30d]))
- P95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(response_latency_ms_bucket{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) by (le))
- KV cache hit rate: sum(rate(kvcache_hit_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) / (sum(rate(kvcache_hit_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) + sum(rate(kvcache_miss_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])))

Example SQL for event store (clickhouse/postgres style):
SELECT tenant_id, count(*) as requests, sum(case when status = 'success' then 1 else 0 end) as success_requests, 100.0 * success_requests / count(*) as availability
FROM requests
WHERE ts >= now() - interval '30 day'
GROUP BY tenant_id;

Ownership Matrix (example)
-------------------------
- Customer Success (owner): onboarding coordination, QBR artifacts, SLO sign-off.
- Solutions Engineering: instrumentation support, sample queries, dashboard templates.
- Eng SRE: alert tuning and escalation owner for P1s.
- Customer Engineering Contact: provide access to telemetry sinks and validate sample runs.

Escalation Path (short)
----------------------
1. On-call SRE (P1) — page via PagerDuty.
2. Eng SRE runbook execution and mitigation.
3. Customer Success: notify customer success manager and schedule cross-team bridge.
4. CTO/VP Eng (if unresolved after 4 hours or business-impacting).

Network & Security Notes for Private Deploys
-------------------------------------------
- For Private deployments where telemetry cannot leave customer VPC, establish a signed collector (sidecar) that forwards aggregated metrics to Redwood’s metrics API over an approved egress path.
- Verify TLS mutual auth and KMS configuration prior to enabling telemetry export.

Onboarding Checklist (compact)
-----------------------------
- [ ] SLOs documented and signed by customer
- [ ] Instrumentation enabled and labels verified
- [ ] Dashboards created and shared with customer
- [ ] P1/P2 alerts configured and tested in canary
- [ ] QBR artifact skeleton ready (templates populated)
- [ ] Post-rollout review scheduled

Appendix: Common Gotchas & Troubleshooting Tips
----------------------------------------------
- Missing tenant_id label: SLOs will show as aggregated across customers — fix at SDK/sidecar config.
- Burst traffic causing batch queue growth: tune autoscaler target and batch_time_ms.
- Sudden token-cost spike: check client prompt changes, new feature releases, or retry loops.
- High GPU memory pressure: check model shard placement and local cache sizing.

Contacts & Links
---------------
- Onboarding template: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/onboarding-handback-and-first-90-days-ops-guide-2026
- SRE runbooks: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks
- Dashboard templates repo: git@github.com:redwood-inference/dashboard-templates.git

- 2026-02-10: Created by Asha Patel.
- 2026-02-20: Added P1/P2 thresholds and private deployment notes (reviewed by Liam O'Connor, Priya Singh).

title: Proactive Telemetry & SLA Onboarding Checklist
preview:
Overview
---------
This document is a pragmatic, step-by-step checklist for onboarding a new enterprise customer with proactive telemetry, SLO mapping, and QBR-ready artifacts. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, and the customer engineering contact. The goal is to ensure that from day zero we have the signals, dashboards, and alerts required to operate the tenant within agreed SLAs and to produce reliable QBR metrics.

Scope
-----
- Applies to Hosted and Dedicated customers (Private deployments need network-specific gating described in the ‘Network & Security’ section).
- Covers: metric collection, trace and request tagging, SLO definition, alert thresholds, dashboarding, and QBR artifacts.
- Does not replace incident runbooks — use the incident-runbook specific to the service for live response.

Prerequisites (Customer / Redwood)
--------------------------------
1. Signed onboarding form with agreed SLOs and data-sharing consent.
2. Customer engineering contact has Redwoods' integration keys and sandbox tenant (if requested).
3. Access to customer's telemetry sink or permission to forward metrics/traces to Redwood telemetry gatekeeper.
4. Redwood onboarding engineer assigned and a shared Slack/Email channel established.

Key Telemetry Signals (required)
-------------------------------
Collect these signals with the tags described below. Each signal must be emitted with request_id and tenant_id (or dedicated_tenant) for correlation.

- Request counters: requests.total, requests.success, requests.error (label by model, route, tenant_id)
- Latency histograms: response.latency_ms (p50/p90/p99 buckets)
- Token counters: tokens.generated (per-model, per-route)
- Queue/backlog: serve.queue_length, serve.pending_batches
- Resource utilization: gpu.utilization, gpu.memory_free_mb, cpu.utilization
- Cache effectiveness: kvcache.hit, kvcache.miss, kvcache.evictions
- Retries and fallbacks: request.fallback_count, model.switch_count
- Error classes: error.type (rate-limited, infra, model_invalid_input, model_timeout)

Tagging and Naming Conventions
------------------------------
- tenant_id: required for multi-tenant customers. For Dedicated, use dedicated_tenant.
- model: canonical model id used by platform (e.g., gpt-j-8k-v2)
- route: api route or product feature (e.g., chat.generate, embeddings.index)
- env: prod | staging | canary
- shard/region: for geo routing visibility

SLO Mapping Template (example)
------------------------------
- Availability SLO: 99.9% successful_requests / total_requests measured monthly (error types: infra AND model_timeout count against SLO).
- Latency SLO: 95% of responses <= 300ms for short-sequence generation paths (<= 512 tokens).
- Token-cost guardrail (informational): average tokens.generated per request <= 120 for interactive route.

Instrumentation Checklist (step-by-step)
---------------------------------------
1. Verify SDK/sidecar instrumentation is deployed and reporting to telemetry-gatekeeper: run the sample curl below and confirm metrics appear in the metrics view.
   - Example curl: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/demo -H 'Authorization: Bearer <key>' -d '{"prompt":"test"}'
2. Confirm request_id and tenant_id labels are present on all request metrics. Missing labels block tenant-level SLO calculation.
3. Ensure histogram buckets include 10ms..10s range for response.latency_ms.
4. Validate GPU telemetry: check gpu.utilization and gpu.memory_free_mb are reported by node exporter or GPU agent.
5. Enable KV cache metrics on the model-serving config if using prefix caching.
6. If streaming is used, instrument stream.chunk.count and stream.first_byte_latency_ms.

Dashboards to Create (minimum set)
----------------------------------
- Tenant SLO Overview (single panel with availability, latency p95 and p99 for primary routes)
- Model Economy (tokens.generated, cost_estimate_per_minute by model)
- Resource Pressure (gpu.utilization heatmap across nodes serving the tenant)
- Cache Health (kvcache hit ratio and evictions over time)
- Error Breakdown (top error types and top endpoints producing errors)
- Queue and Backlog (pending_batches and average batch size)

Alerting & Thresholds (recommended)
----------------------------------
Please treat thresholds as starting points that must be tuned with customer-specific traffic shape. Alerts are split into P1 (actionable immediate response) and P2 (investigate next business day).

P1 (page on-call):
| Signal | Condition | Notes |
| gpu.utilization | > 95% sustained for 3m | Risk of degraded tail latency |
| requests.error | error_rate > 0.5% over 5m AND error.type in (infra, model_timeout) | Blamable against SLO |
| serve.pending_batches | > 100 for 5m | Backlog causing latency spikes |

P2 (notify Slack Ops or owner):
| Signal | Condition | Notes |
| response.latency.p99 | > 1.5x SLO for 15m | Degradation trend |
| kvcache.evictions | > baseline*3 in 10m | Potential cache misconfiguration |
| tokens.generated.avg | > expected_per_request*2 over 30m | Cost anomaly watch |

Rollout & Validation Procedure
------------------------------
1. Dry-run: Enable telemetry collection on a canary subset (1–5% of traffic). Validate event schema and sample queries.
2. Full-rollout: Switch the production routing and enable SLO calculation.
3. Observe for 48–72 hours: confirm alerts noise is acceptable and dashboards show expected baselines.
4. Post-rollout review: produce a short onboarding summary for the QBR pack (see QBR Artifacts).

QBR Artifacts (what we deliver at first QBR)
-------------------------------------------
- Tenant SLO report (month-to-date and rolling-30d) with annotations for incidents.
- Cost-per-1k-tokens trend by route and model.
- Top 5 recommendations to improve latency/cost (batching, quantization, route split).
- List of action items and owners with ETA.

Validations & Sample Queries
---------------------------
Prometheus / M3 style examples:
- Availability: sum(rate(requests_success_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[30d])) / sum(rate(requests_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[30d]))
- P95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(response_latency_ms_bucket{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) by (le))
- KV cache hit rate: sum(rate(kvcache_hit_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) / (sum(rate(kvcache_hit_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])) + sum(rate(kvcache_miss_total{tenant_id="TENANT_X"}[5m])))

Example SQL for event store (clickhouse/postgres style):
SELECT tenant_id, count(*) as requests, sum(case when status = 'success' then 1 else 0 end) as success_requests, 100.0 * success_requests / count(*) as availability
FROM requests
WHERE ts >= now() - interval '30 day'
GROUP BY tenant_id;

Ownership Matrix (example)
-------------------------
- Customer Success (owner): onboarding coordination, QBR artifacts, SLO sign-off.
- Solutions Engineering: instrumentation support, sample queries, dashboard templates.
- Eng SRE: alert tuning and escalation owner for P1s.
- Customer Engineering Contact: provide access to telemetry sinks and validate sample runs.

Escalation Path (short)
----------------------
1. On-call SRE (P1) — page via PagerDuty.
2. Eng SRE runbook execution and mitigation.
3. Customer Success: notify customer success manager and schedule cross-team bridge.
4. CTO/VP Eng (if unresolved after 4 hours or business-impacting).

Network & Security Notes for Private Deploys
-------------------------------------------
- For Private deployments where telemetry cannot leave customer VPC, establish a signed collector (sidecar) that forwards aggregated metrics to Redwood’s metrics API over an approved egress path.
- Verify TLS mutual auth and KMS configuration prior to enabling telemetry export.

Onboarding Checklist (compact)
-----------------------------
- [ ] SLOs documented and signed by customer
- [ ] Instrumentation enabled and labels verified
- [ ] Dashboards created and shared with customer
- [ ] P1/P2 alerts configured and tested in canary
- [ ] QBR artifact skeleton ready (templates populated)
- [ ] Post-rollout review scheduled

Appendix: Common Gotchas & Troubleshooting Tips
----------------------------------------------
- Missing tenant_id label: SLOs will show as aggregated across customers — fix at SDK/sidecar config.
- Burst traffic causing batch queue growth: tune autoscaler target and batch_time_ms.
- Sudden token-cost spike: check client prompt changes, new feature releases, or retry loops.
- High GPU memory pressure: check model shard placement and local cache sizing.

Contacts & Links
---------------
- Onboarding template: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/onboarding-handback-and-first-90-days-ops-guide-2026
- SRE runbooks: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks
- Dashboard templates repo: git@github.com:redwood-inference/dashboard-templates.git

Notes and change history:
- 2026-02-10: Created by Asha Patel.
- 2026-02-20: Added P1/P2 thresholds and private deployment notes (reviewed by Liam O'Connor, Priya Singh).
