| Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations | Upgrade hangs or fails during migration step; rollback uncertainty; repeated retries worsen | Tier: Private · Region: Customer VPC/on-prem · Model: N/A | /customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/workaround-private-installer-upgrade-stuck-migrations | Active | 2026-02-12 | Priya Natarajan (Platform) | Escalate immediately if production Private deployment is blocked, rollback is required, or customer is in a regulated window; collect diagnostics bundle/logs per article before paging. |
| Audit log delivery latency (lag > expected) | Audit logs delayed (e.g., 15–60+ min), customer compliance concerns, SIEM pipeline appears stale | Tier: Hosted Enterprise, Private · Region: Any · Model: N/A | /customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/workaround-audit-log-delivery-latency | Active | 2026-02-14 | Allison Grant (Observability) | Escalate if lag exceeds enterprise expectation threshold (per SLA policy), if multiple accounts show lag, or if customer states compliance breach risk; include timestamps + tenant/org ID via secure internal channel only. |

---

## Additional high-frequency issues (candidate / watchlist)
These are **not** fully published as Known Issues unless linked to a KB article with validated workaround. Keep an eye on trend changes and file tickets with consistent repro info.

| Issue (watchlist) | Symptoms | Tags | Current guidance | Owner | Last reviewed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---:|
| Model version pin perceived “ignored” during fallback | Customer expects pinned version but sees different behavior during degradation | Tier: Hosted/Dedicated · Region: Any · Model: Any | Use routing metrics + policy review; confirm fallback configuration and whether pinned version allows compatible variants. Escalate if metrics show fallback contradicts policy. | Mina Haddad | 2026-02-15 |
| Console latency dashboard missing region breakdown | eu-west (or other region) missing from Console filters; customers ask for interim reporting | Tier: Hosted/Dedicated · Region: Console · Model: N/A | Provide interim metrics export guidance; open Platform telemetry ticket if confirmed. | Ethan Park | 2026-02-15 |
| Embeddings endpoint timeouts (eu-west) | Increased timeout rate; customer sees retries and degraded throughput | Tier: Hosted · Region: eu-west · Model: Embeddings | Follow triage checklist; if multiple customers affected, move to #incidents and consider status-page criteria. | Sean Gallagher | 2026-02-15 |
| SAML SSO login loop in Console | Redirect loop after IdP login, cannot access Console | Tier: Enterprise Hosted/Private · Region: Any · Model: N/A | Collect IdP metadata + Console auth logs request; escalate to Control Plane if reproducible. | Ethan Park | 2026-02-15 |

---

## Maintenance policy (ownership + freshness)
- Each Known Issue must have a **Primary owner** and at least one **reviewer**.
- Owners review entries **weekly during the sprint window** and **biweekly** afterwards.
- If an issue is marked **Fixed pending rollout**, update this index once rollout is complete and record the date.
- If an issue becomes **Resolved**, keep it listed for **30–60 days** with a note pointing to the fix/release, then remove from the index.

**Change control:**
- Edits that change escalation thresholds or SLA language require review from **Support Ops (Dev Patel)** and **SRE (Sean Gallagher or Rafael Mendes)**.

---

## Where to link this from
- Jira SUP ticket templates/macros (auto-suggest this index for new tickets)
- Support triage intake checklist
- Slack bridge workflow pinned message
- CS QBR prep checklist (link relevant known issues for top accounts)

---

## Changelog (recent)
- 2026-02-15: Added watchlist section; clarified escalation minimum context; refreshed owners/reviewers. (Elliot Price)
- 2026-02-14: Published initial set of sprint-known issues (streaming, 429/quota, Dedicated autoscaling, Private upgrade, audit log lag). (Docs/Support/Eng reviewers)

source.title: Workaround: Private installer upgrade stuck at "Running migrations"
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.content:
## Summary
Private deployments occasionally encounter upgrade failures where the **redwood-private-installer** reports progress but appears stuck at **"Running migrations"** (or repeatedly retries). This page describes:
- Common failure modes and how to identify them
- Customer-safe mitigations and **safe rollback guidance**
- The **artifacts Support must collect** before escalating
- When/how to escalate to **Platform → Private Deployments**

This article is intended for Support/CS and on-call partners. Keep customer communications factual and avoid requesting sensitive materials (secrets, private keys, full DB dumps).

---

## Customer-visible symptoms
Customers may report one or more of the following during a Private upgrade:
- Installer UI/CLI stuck at **"Running migrations"** for > 15–30 minutes (duration varies by DB size; see “Expected timing” below)
- Repeated lines indicating migration retries, timeouts, or "acquiring lock" failures
- Control plane components (Console, API gateway, auth) intermittently unavailable during the upgrade window
- Kubernetes shows one or more migration jobs in **CrashLoopBackOff**, **Error**, or never completing

---

## Expected timing (rule of thumb)
Migration time depends on DB size, IO, and cluster health.
- **Small/typical** environments: 2–10 minutes
- **Large** environments or constrained IO: 10–30 minutes
- If **no progress** or repeated failures for **> 30 minutes**, treat as abnormal and proceed with diagnostics.

Support guidance: ask the customer what the installer is showing *now*, when the upgrade started, and whether there were recent cluster changes (Kubernetes upgrade, storage changes, node re-provisioning).

---

## Most common failure modes (what to look for)
### 1) Migration job failing (app-level error)
Indicators:
- Kubernetes Job/Pod for migrations is failing fast
- Logs show schema/constraint errors, missing permissions, or incompatible version transitions

Examples of log phrases:
- "permission denied for relation …"
- "duplicate key value violates unique constraint"
- "cannot alter type …"
- "migration failed: …"

### 2) Database connectivity / DNS / network path issues
Indicators:
- Migration Pod logs show timeouts connecting to the DB
- Intermittent DNS failures in cluster events
- Private/VPC customers may have security group / firewall changes coincident with upgrade

Example log phrases:
- "dial tcp … i/o timeout"
- "no such host"
- "connection refused"

### 3) Storage pressure / IO throttling / node issues
Indicators:
- Cluster events show disk pressure, evictions, or storage attach failures
- Migration job runs extremely slowly and then times out

Example event phrases:
- "Evicted: The node had condition: [DiskPressure]"
- "failed to provision volume"

### 4) Migration lock contention / previous migration left running
Indicators:
- Logs show lock acquisition failures or "another migration is running"
- A previous failed upgrade left a migration job/pod running or stuck

### 5) Installer/controller state drift
Indicators:
- Installer reports a step that doesn’t match cluster reality (e.g., job completed but installer still waiting)
- Upgrade controller logs show reconciliation errors

---

## Immediate triage (Support checklist)
Before recommending changes, collect the minimum set of facts.

### A) Required environment details (customer-safe)
Ask for:
- Private deployment version **from** → **to** (installer version and platform version if available)
- Kubernetes version and cluster distribution (EKS/GKE/AKS/on-prem)
- Approximate upgrade start time and whether any steps completed successfully
- Whether the customer customized DB (external Postgres vs bundled) and storage class

### B) Required technical artifacts to collect
These are the artifacts we need to diagnose quickly. Prefer a redacted paste of relevant excerpts over huge dumps.

1) Installer logs
- Ask for the installer logs around the time it enters “Running migrations” and the subsequent retries/failure.
- If the customer uses the installer CLI, ask for the console output plus any log bundle they can export.

2) Kubernetes: migration job/pod status + logs
Request outputs (customer can run; Support can guide):
- `kubectl -n redwood get jobs`
- `kubectl -n redwood get pods | egrep -i 'migrat|upgrade|install'`
- `kubectl -n redwood describe pod <migration-pod>`
- `kubectl -n redwood logs <migration-pod> --all-containers --tail=200`

3) Kubernetes events (high signal)
- `kubectl -n redwood get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -n 50`

4) Cluster health signals
- `kubectl -n redwood get deploy`
- `kubectl -n redwood get statefulset`
- `kubectl get nodes`

5) If using external Postgres (only if customer can provide safely)
- Confirmation that DB is reachable from the cluster (no credentials required).
- Any DB-side alerts the customer sees (connection limit, storage full, failover).

### C) What NOT to request
Do not request or accept via Slack/email:
- Full database dumps
- Kubernetes secrets (`kubectl get secret … -o yaml`)
- Private keys, SSO certificates, KMS material
- Full cluster-wide logs unrelated to the redwood namespace

If credentials are absolutely required for engineering reproduction, escalate first and use an approved secure channel.

---

## Customer-safe mitigations / workarounds
Use the least invasive steps first. If the environment is production-critical or currently down, move quickly to “When to escalate”.

### 1) Confirm whether migrations are actively progressing
If logs show ongoing statements and timestamps advancing, advise the customer to allow additional time, but:
- Set a checkpoint (e.g., re-check in 10–15 minutes)
- Ask for updates on whether new errors appear

### 2) If the migration job is crash-looping, capture logs before restarting
Guidance:
- Capture the last ~200–500 lines of logs.
- Capture `describe pod` output.

Only after capture, the customer can try a single retry:
- Delete the failed migration pod (Kubernetes will recreate if part of a Job/controller):
  - `kubectl -n redwood delete pod <migration-pod>`

If it fails again with the same error, stop and escalate.

### 3) If events indicate resource pressure (DiskPressure / OOM / evictions)
Customer-safe suggestions:
- Ensure adequate free disk on nodes and in the storage backend.
- Temporarily scale down non-essential workloads (customer discretion).
- If the migration pod is OOMKilled, confirm resource limits for the migration job (engineering may need to advise; do not guess). 

### 4) If connectivity/DNS is suspected
Customer-safe suggestions:
- Ask whether any network/security policy changes occurred recently.
- Validate cluster DNS is healthy (CoreDNS running) and that the DB endpoint resolves from within cluster.
- If customer has a standard “connectivity test pod” practice, suggest testing DB reachability from the redwood namespace.

### 5) If lock contention is suspected
Do NOT advise direct DB manipulation (e.g., deleting lock rows) from Support.
- Capture logs showing lock errors.
- Escalate to Platform → Private Deployments with artifacts.

---

## Safe rollback guidance (Private)
Rollback should be treated as a controlled operation because downgrades may not be compatible with partially-applied migrations.

### Principles
- **Do not attempt a version downgrade** (platform/app) without explicit approval from Platform → Private Deployments.
- If the installer supports “rollback” or “abort upgrade” semantics, prefer that over manual changes.
- If the upgrade is stuck but core services are still up, prioritize stability and data safety over speed.

### What Support can say (customer-safe)
- "We want to avoid any action that risks data integrity. Before attempting rollback or downgrade, we’ll review the migration state and advise the safest next step."

### If the customer must restore service urgently
Escalate immediately (P0/P1 depending on impact) and include:
- Whether the cluster is currently serving traffic
- Whether any components are crash-looping
- Whether the customer has a recent snapshot/backup (do not request the backup itself; only confirmation)

---

## Decision: when to escalate
Escalate to Platform → Private Deployments if any of the following are true:
- Upgrade stuck at migrations for **> 30 minutes** with no evidence of progress
- Migration job/pod is in **Error/CrashLoopBackOff**
- Customer production environment is down or materially degraded
- Logs indicate schema errors, lock contention, or compatibility issues
- Customer asks to rollback/downgrade

Severity guidance (Support):
- **P0**: production down (API/control plane unavailable), multiple users blocked, no workaround
- **P1**: major degradation during upgrade (timeouts/partial outage), workaround uncertain
- **P2**: upgrade blocked but production remains stable (planned maintenance window impacted)

Follow the escalation playbook for paging and Slack bridging.

---

## Escalation path (internal)
### Who owns this
Primary engineering owner: **Platform → Private Deployments** (control plane + installer)
- If cluster health/resource pressure is dominant, loop in **SRE** per escalation triggers.

### How to escalate
1) Open/Update the SUP ticket with:
- Customer, tier = Private, region/cluster type
- Exact “from → to” version
- Timeline (start time, when stuck)
- Artifacts listed below (or note what is pending)

2) Start a Slack bridge (if P0/P1 or time-sensitive)
- Use the Support bridge convention and include:
  - SUP ticket link
  - Cluster type and versions
  - Current customer impact
  - What steps were already attempted

3) Page the correct on-call
- Use the escalation directory routing for **Private installer/upgrade** issues.

### Minimum artifacts to include in escalation
- Installer logs excerpt around “Running migrations”
- `kubectl -n redwood get jobs` and `get pods` output
- Migration pod `describe` and `logs` (tail)
- Recent `kubectl -n redwood get events` tail
- Any clear error strings (copy/paste)

---

## Resolution notes (what to document after)
When the issue is mitigated/resolved, update the SUP ticket and (if applicable) the known-issues index with:
- Root cause category (job failure vs connectivity vs resource pressure vs state drift)
- The concrete fix applied (e.g., rerun with corrected config, resource adjustment, installer patch)
- Whether a product follow-up is needed (diagnostics bundle, clearer error output)
- Whether the workaround is safe to publish broadly vs customer-specific

---

## Support macro (customer-safe text)
Use/adapt the following when acknowledging an upgrade stuck at migrations:

"Thanks for the details—an upgrade can pause at the migrations step if the migration job is retrying or blocked by cluster/DB conditions. To help us triage quickly (without sharing sensitive data), please send:
1) The installer output/log excerpt around when it entered ‘Running migrations’
2) `kubectl -n redwood get jobs` and `kubectl -n redwood get pods | egrep -i 'migrat|upgrade|install'`
3) `kubectl -n redwood logs <migration-pod> --all-containers --tail=200`
4) `kubectl -n redwood get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -n 50`

If production is currently down or this is blocking a maintenance window, tell us and we’ll engage our on-call team immediately."

---

## Internal follow-ups / improvements
If this occurs repeatedly or lacks sufficient diagnostics, link to the engineering follow-up:
- ENG-4828 (improved diagnostics bundle and clearer error output for upgrade/migrations)

Owner reminder: ensure this KB stays aligned to the installer’s current UX (labels/step names) and does not drift from on-call guidance.

Indicators:
- Installer reports a step that doesn’t match cluster reality (e.g., job completed but installer still waiting)
- Upgrade controller logs show reconciliation errors

---

## Immediate triage (Support checklist)
Before recommending changes, collect the minimum set of facts.

### A) Required environment details (customer-safe)
Ask for:
- Private deployment version **from** → **to** (installer version and platform version if available)
- Kubernetes version and cluster distribution (EKS/GKE/AKS/on-prem)
- Approximate upgrade start time and whether any steps completed successfully
- Whether the customer customized DB (external Postgres vs bundled) and storage class

### B) Required technical artifacts to collect
These are the artifacts we need to diagnose quickly. Prefer a redacted paste of relevant excerpts over huge dumps.

1) Installer logs
- Ask for the installer logs around the time it enters “Running migrations” and the subsequent retries/failure.
- If the customer uses the installer CLI, ask for the console output plus any log bundle they can export.

2) Kubernetes: migration job/pod status + logs
Request outputs (customer can run; Support can guide):
- `kubectl -n redwood get jobs`
- `kubectl -n redwood get pods | egrep -i 'migrat|upgrade|install'`
- `kubectl -n redwood describe pod <migration-pod>`
- `kubectl -n redwood logs <migration-pod> --all-containers --tail=200`

3) Kubernetes events (high signal)
- `kubectl -n redwood get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -n 50`

4) Cluster health signals
- `kubectl -n redwood get deploy`
- `kubectl -n redwood get statefulset`
- `kubectl get nodes`

5) If using external Postgres (only if customer can provide safely)
- Confirmation that DB is reachable from the cluster (no credentials required).
- Any DB-side alerts the customer sees (connection limit, storage full, failover).

### C) What NOT to request
Do not request or accept via Slack/email:
- Full database dumps
- Kubernetes secrets (`kubectl get secret … -o yaml`)
- Private keys, SSO certificates, KMS material
- Full cluster-wide logs unrelated to the redwood namespace

If credentials are absolutely required for engineering reproduction, escalate first and use an approved secure channel.

---

## Customer-safe mitigations / workarounds
Use the least invasive steps first. If the environment is production-critical or currently down, move quickly to “When to escalate”.

### 1) Confirm whether migrations are actively progressing
If logs show ongoing statements and timestamps advancing, advise the customer to allow additional time, but:
- Set a checkpoint (e.g., re-check in 10–15 minutes)
- Ask for updates on whether new errors appear

### 2) If the migration job is crash-looping, capture logs before restarting
Guidance:
- Capture the last ~200–500 lines of logs.
- Capture `describe pod` output.

Only after capture, the customer can try a single retry:
- Delete the failed migration pod (Kubernetes will recreate if part of a Job/controller):
  - `kubectl -n redwood delete pod <migration-pod>`

If it fails again with the same error, stop and escalate.

### 3) If events indicate resource pressure (DiskPressure / OOM / evictions)
Customer-safe suggestions:
- Ensure adequate free disk on nodes and in the storage backend.
- Temporarily scale down non-essential workloads (customer discretion).
- If the migration pod is OOMKilled, confirm resource limits for the migration job (engineering may need to advise; do not guess). 

### 4) If connectivity/DNS is suspected
Customer-safe suggestions:
- Ask whether any network/security policy changes occurred recently.
- Validate cluster DNS is healthy (CoreDNS running) and that the DB endpoint resolves from within cluster.
- If customer has a standard “connectivity test pod” practice, suggest testing DB reachability from the redwood namespace.

### 5) If lock contention is suspected
Do NOT advise direct DB manipulation (e.g., deleting lock rows) from Support.
- Capture logs showing lock errors.
- Escalate to Platform → Private Deployments with artifacts.

---

## Safe rollback guidance (Private)
Rollback should be treated as a controlled operation because downgrades may not be compatible with partially-applied migrations.

### Principles
- **Do not attempt a version downgrade** (platform/app) without explicit approval from Platform → Private Deployments.
- If the installer supports “rollback” or “abort upgrade” semantics, prefer that over manual changes.
- If the upgrade is stuck but core services are still up, prioritize stability and data safety over speed.

### What Support can say (customer-safe)
- "We want to avoid any action that risks data integrity. Before attempting rollback or downgrade, we’ll review the migration state and advise the safest next step."

### If the customer must restore service urgently
Escalate immediately (P0/P1 depending on impact) and include:
- Whether the cluster is currently serving traffic
- Whether any components are crash-looping
- Whether the customer has a recent snapshot/backup (do not request the backup itself; only confirmation)

---

## Decision: when to escalate
Escalate to Platform → Private Deployments if any of the following are true:
- Upgrade stuck at migrations for **> 30 minutes** with no evidence of progress
- Migration job/pod is in **Error/CrashLoopBackOff**
- Customer production environment is down or materially degraded
- Logs indicate schema errors, lock contention, or compatibility issues
- Customer asks to rollback/downgrade

Severity guidance (Support):
- **P0**: production down (API/control plane unavailable), multiple users blocked, no workaround
- **P1**: major degradation during upgrade (timeouts/partial outage), workaround uncertain
- **P2**: upgrade blocked but production remains stable (planned maintenance window impacted)

Follow the escalation playbook for paging and Slack bridging.

---

## Escalation path (internal)
### Who owns this
Primary engineering owner: **Platform → Private Deployments** (control plane + installer)
- If cluster health/resource pressure is dominant, loop in **SRE** per escalation triggers.

### How to escalate
1) Open/Update the SUP ticket with:
- Customer, tier = Private, region/cluster type
- Exact “from → to” version
- Timeline (start time, when stuck)
- Artifacts listed below (or note what is pending)

2) Start a Slack bridge (if P0/P1 or time-sensitive)
- Use the Support bridge convention and include:
  - SUP ticket link
  - Cluster type and versions
  - Current customer impact
  - What steps were already attempted

3) Page the correct on-call
- Use the escalation directory routing for **Private installer/upgrade** issues.

### Minimum artifacts to include in escalation
- Installer logs excerpt around “Running migrations”
- `kubectl -n redwood get jobs` and `get pods` output
- Migration pod `describe` and `logs` (tail)
- Recent `kubectl -n redwood get events` tail
- Any clear error strings (copy/paste)

---

## Resolution notes (what to document after)
When the issue is mitigated/resolved, update the SUP ticket and (if applicable) the known-issues index with:
- Root cause category (job failure vs connectivity vs resource pressure vs state drift)
- The concrete fix applied (e.g., rerun with corrected config, resource adjustment, installer patch)
- Whether a product follow-up is needed (diagnostics bundle, clearer error output)
- Whether the workaround is safe to publish broadly vs customer-specific

---

## Support macro (customer-safe text)
Use/adapt the following when acknowledging an upgrade stuck at migrations:

"Thanks for the details—an upgrade can pause at the migrations step if the migration job is retrying or blocked by cluster/DB conditions. To help us triage quickly (without sharing sensitive data), please send:
1) The installer output/log excerpt around when it entered ‘Running migrations’
2) `kubectl -n redwood get jobs` and `kubectl -n redwood get pods | egrep -i 'migrat|upgrade|install'`
3) `kubectl -n redwood logs <migration-pod> --all-containers --tail=200`
4) `kubectl -n redwood get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -n 50`

If production is currently down or this is blocking a maintenance window, tell us and we’ll engage our on-call team immediately."

---

## Internal follow-ups / improvements
If this occurs repeatedly or lacks sufficient diagnostics, link to the engineering follow-up:
- ENG-4828 (improved diagnostics bundle and clearer error output for upgrade/migrations)

Owner reminder: ensure this KB stays aligned to the installer’s current UX (labels/step names) and does not drift from on-call guidance.

source.title: Support SLA Policy by Customer Tier (Hosted, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise)
source.author: Rachel Kim
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference Support SLAs by customer tier (Self-serve Hosted API, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise). It standardizes:
- **First response targets** (time to first human acknowledgement)
- **Update cadence** (how often we provide meaningful progress updates)
- **Escalation thresholds** (when Support must page Engineering/SRE and/or start a bridge)
- **After-hours policy** (what is covered 24/7 vs business-hours only)
- **Credits requests & contract exceptions** (how to handle safely and consistently)

**Important:** These are *internal operating targets* and default policies. If a customer contract includes custom SLAs/terms, the contract governs. When in doubt, follow the “Contract exceptions” section and engage Legal/CS leadership.

---

## Definitions (quick)
- **Tier**: support coverage and targets based on a customer’s commercial plan.
- **Priority (P0–P4)**: Support ticket priority (see severity rubric for examples and mapping to incident severity).
- **First response**: a human response confirming receipt + requesting any missing diagnostics + stating next update time.
- **Update**: a non-empty change (new findings, mitigation, ETA change, or explicit “no change; still investigating; next update at X”).
- **Business hours (BH)**: Mon–Fri, 09:00–17:00 in the customer’s primary region where possible (default: PT for North America customers, GMT for EMEA customers). If a team member is not staffed for that timezone, use PT and note it in the first response.

---

## Customer tiers covered by this policy
### Self-serve (Hosted API)
- Typical: PLG / pay-as-you-go Hosted API customers.
- Coverage: **Business hours** (BH) default.

### Dedicated
- Reserved capacity customers (dedicated GPU pools, SLA-backed performance/uptime terms).
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4.

### Private
- VPC and/or on-prem deployments (regulated environments, customer-managed networking).
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4.

### Enterprise
- Enterprise plan customers (may be Hosted, Dedicated, and/or Private) with contract terms and executive visibility.
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4 (unless contract states otherwise).

---

## SLA targets by tier and priority
Use this table after setting ticket priority per the severity rubric.

### Table A — First response and update cadence
> If a ticket spans multiple systems (e.g., Hosted API + Console + billing), set priority to the *highest-impact* issue and coordinate internally; do not create separate parallel customer threads.

| Priority | Typical impact (summary) | Self-serve (Hosted API) | Dedicated | Private | Enterprise |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **P0** | Service down, major security/compliance risk, or severe degradation with broad impact | **First response:** 1 hour (BH) \| **Updates:** every 2 hours (BH) \| **Bridge:** if multi-customer/widespread | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min |
| **P1** | Material feature outage or high error/latency impacting core workloads; workaround may exist but isn’t acceptable for most users | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) or when material change | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) |
| **P2** | Degradation with partial workaround; isolated region/model; limited subset of users | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** every 2 business days | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 2 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) |
| **P3** | Minor issue, question, documentation/UX confusion; low business impact | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change |
| **P4** | How-to requests, enhancement asks, non-urgent follow-ups | **First response:** 5 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available |

Notes:
- For **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise P0/P1**, updates are expected **even if Engineering is still investigating**. Support owns keeping the customer looped in.
- For **Self-serve**, Support does not run 24/7 coverage. If a Self-serve incident is **widespread** (multiple customers, multi-region, status-page-worthy), Support should follow the incident process and escalation triggers below.

---

## Escalation thresholds (paging + bridges)
Escalations must follow the escalation playbook and severity rubric. The goal is to page consistently with Engineering/SRE on-call expectations and avoid ad-hoc bypassing.

### Table B — When Support must escalate beyond standard triage
| Priority | Self-serve (Hosted API) | Dedicated | Private | Enterprise |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | Escalate if any of: (1) multi-customer impact, (2) region-wide degradation, (3) auth/outage patterns, (4) safety/compliance concern. Start Support bridge and move to **#incidents** if it qualifies as an incident. | **Page immediately** per routing matrix. Start Support bridge within 15 min. If SRE declares incident, move to **#incidents**. | **Page immediately** to Private Deployments/Platform on-call + SRE as needed. Start bridge within 15 min. | **Page immediately** + notify CS lead. Start bridge within 15 min. Consider executive escalation protocol if revenue/brand risk is high. |
| **P1** | Escalate if: sustained errors/latency beyond 30 minutes affecting multiple customers OR no meaningful next step after 1 BH. | Escalate if no mitigation path within 60 min or if dashboard signals indicate continued customer impact. | Escalate if mitigation requires config change/rollback or installer/deploy actions beyond Support playbooks. | Escalate if customer indicates business-critical impact, legal/compliance risk, or explicit SLA language invoked. |
| **P2–P4** | Escalate only when: reproducible product defect with clear evidence, or repeated tickets indicate a known issue needing publication, or safety/compliance concerns. | Escalate when repeated impact or no progress for >2 BH and customer’s workload is at risk. | Escalate when there is risk of customer environment instability or unsafe change guidance. | Escalate when there is contract/SLA interpretation needed or customer pressure requires leadership alignment. |

### Signal-based escalation guidance (use with the severity rubric)
Support should not guess incident state solely from a single ticket. Use available signals:
- **Error rate / gateway errors:** sudden spikes in 5xx, connection resets, or 429 patterns correlated across customers.
- **Latency:** p95/p99 deviation from baseline with confirmed customer impact.
- **Routing / fallback:** any evidence of fallback activation that contradicts customer expectations (e.g., model pinning concerns) should be validated using the routing/fallback metrics.
- **Region scope:** if multiple regions show correlated symptoms, treat as potential incident.

When escalating, include the required context template (request IDs, region, model/version, SDK version, timeframe, and any recent deploys).

---

## After-hours policy
### What Support covers after-hours
- **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise:**
  - **P0/P1:** covered **24/7** for acknowledgement, coordination, and customer updates.
  - **P2–P4:** handled during **business hours** unless explicitly upgraded to P0/P1.
- **Self-serve:**
  - Default is **business hours only**.
  - If a widespread incident is detected after-hours via monitoring/on-call processes, incident response proceeds through Engineering/SRE. Support will provide customer updates when staffed; do not promise 24/7 comms for Self-serve.

### Handoffs and continuity
- Use the escalation playbook handoff rules: summarize current state, what changed since last update, customer impact statement, and next actions.
- Pin the customer-impact summary in the bridge channel and keep Jira updated so the next shift can continue without re-triage.

---

## “Waiting on Customer” and SLA clocks
To avoid SLA confusion and missed comms:
- If **additional customer info is required** (logs, request IDs, reproduction), set status to **Waiting on Customer** in Jira.
- Post a clear ask with:
  - exact fields needed (e.g., request IDs, timestamps, region, model/version)
  - why it’s needed
  - a suggested safe way to share (no secrets; redact tokens)
- **SLA clock policy (internal):** While Waiting on Customer, we do not count the period toward “next update due,” but we still send a reminder per tier:
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: remind after **1 business day** (or sooner for P1)
  - Self-serve: remind after **2 business days**
- If the customer responds with partial info, move back to **In Progress** and re-commit to the next update time.

---

## Credits requests (guidance + approvals)
### Principles
- Do **not** promise credits in a Support ticket.
- Credits decisions depend on: contract terms, measured impact, prior credits, and Finance/CS approvals.
- Support’s responsibility is to provide an accurate timeline and impact summary and route the request correctly.

### When to route for credits consideration
Route to CS lead / account team when any of the following are true:
- Customer explicitly requests credits or references an SLA breach.
- Incident is P0 and there is clear unavailability/degradation beyond SLO targets.
- Enterprise account signals renewal risk due to operational impact.

### What Support should provide (copy/paste)
Include in the internal escalation summary:
- Ticket ID(s) and incident/bridge link
- Affected product surface (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), region(s), model(s)
- Impact window (start/end time in UTC) and customer-confirmed symptoms
- Mitigation steps attempted and final resolution state
- Comms timeline (first response time, update cadence adherence)
- Any contractual notes (if known) + link to CRM record

### Approval path (default)
- **Enterprise/Dedicated/Private:** CS lead (Rachel Kim or delegate) + **VP CS (Jasmine Liu)** approval; Finance/legal review if contract language is involved.
- **Self-serve:** typically handled via standard billing/support policy; escalate only for exceptional cases.

---

## Contract exceptions and custom SLAs
If a customer has a custom SLA or special coverage terms:
1. Confirm the terms via the account team (CSM/AE) and/or the contract repository.
2. Add a note in CRM and link it in the Jira ticket.
3. Follow the stricter of (a) contract requirement or (b) this policy.
4. If interpretation is unclear, escalate to CS leadership and Legal (Sofia Mendes) before committing to specific numbers in customer-facing communication.

**Operational note:** Customers may describe expectations informally (“we need 15-minute updates”). Only commit to an update cadence if it is (a) aligned with tier policy/contract and (b) realistically supportable with the current incident state.

---

## Customer communication expectations (alignment)
For P0/P1 incidents:
- Use comms templates and keep language consistent between:
  - Support email responses
  - Slack bridge summaries (internal)
  - Status page updates (if applicable)
- Always include:
  - current impact statement
  - what we’re doing now
  - what customers can do (workaround if validated)
  - next update time

See the incident comms alignment page for when to post a status event vs a docs banner vs a console alert.

---

## Operational checklist for Support (per ticket)
1. Set **Priority (P0–P4)** using the severity rubric.
2. Confirm **tier** (Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise) and document it in Jira.
3. Commit to **first response** and **next update time** per Table A.
4. If escalation threshold is met, follow Table B:
   - create bridge channel (per conventions)
   - page correct on-call group
   - link/pin Jira ticket(s)
5. Keep Jira current with: timestamps, mitigation attempts, and customer-visible summary.
6. If credits are requested, route internally with the required timeline summary.

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-15:** Clarified P0/P1 update cadence language (30 min vs 60 min) and standardized after-hours coverage notes; added explicit “Waiting on Customer” reminder policy.
- **2026-02-10:** Added contract exception workflow and credits approvals guidance.
- **2026-02-06:** Initial publication as part of escalation playbook refresh.

| **P2** | Degradation with partial workaround; isolated region/model; limited subset of users | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** every 2 business days | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 2 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) |

| **P4** | How-to requests, enhancement asks, non-urgent follow-ups | **First response:** 5 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available |

Start Support bridge and move to **#incidents** if it qualifies as an incident. | **Page immediately** per routing matrix. Start Support bridge within 15 min. If SRE declares incident, move to **#incidents**. | **Page immediately** to Private Deployments/Platform on-call + SRE as needed. Start bridge within 15 min. | **Page immediately** + notify CS lead. Start bridge within 15 min. Consider executive escalation protocol if revenue/brand risk is high. |
| **P1** | Escalate if: sustained errors/latency beyond 30 minutes affecting multiple customers OR no meaningful next step after 1 BH. | Escalate if no mitigation path within 60 min or if dashboard signals indicate continued customer impact. | Escalate if mitigation requires config change/rollback or installer/deploy actions beyond Support playbooks. | Escalate if customer indicates business-critical impact, legal/compliance risk, or explicit SLA language invoked. |

---

## After-hours policy
### What Support covers after-hours
- **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise:**
  - **P0/P1:** covered **24/7** for acknowledgement, coordination, and customer updates.

- **Self-serve:**
  - Default is **business hours only**.
  - If a widespread incident is detected after-hours via monitoring/on-call processes, incident response proceeds through Engineering/SRE. Support will provide customer updates when staffed; do not promise 24/7 comms for Self-serve.

### Handoffs and continuity

- **SLA clock policy (internal):** While Waiting on Customer, we do not count the period toward “next update due,” but we still send a reminder per tier:
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: remind after **1 business day** (or sooner for P1)
  - Self-serve: remind after **2 business days**
- If the customer responds with partial info, move back to **In Progress** and re-commit to the next update time.

---

## Credits requests (guidance + approvals)
### Principles
- Do **not** promise credits in a Support ticket.
- Credits decisions depend on: contract terms, measured impact, prior credits, and Finance/CS approvals.
- Support’s responsibility is to provide an accurate timeline and impact summary and route the request correctly.

### When to route for credits consideration
Route to CS lead / account team when any of the following are true:
- Customer explicitly requests credits or references an SLA breach.
- Incident is P0 and there is clear unavailability/degradation beyond SLO targets.
- Enterprise account signals renewal risk due to operational impact.

### What Support should provide (copy/paste)
Include in the internal escalation summary:
- Ticket ID(s) and incident/bridge link
- Affected product surface (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), region(s), model(s)
- Impact window (start/end time in UTC) and customer-confirmed symptoms
- Mitigation steps attempted and final resolution state
- Comms timeline (first response time, update cadence adherence)
- Any contractual notes (if known) + link to CRM record

### Approval path (default)
- **Enterprise/Dedicated/Private:** CS lead (Rachel Kim or delegate) + **VP CS (Jasmine Liu)** approval; Finance/legal review if contract language is involved.
- **Self-serve:** typically handled via standard billing/support policy; escalate only for exceptional cases.

---

## Contract exceptions and custom SLAs
If a customer has a custom SLA or special coverage terms:
1. Confirm the terms via the account team (CSM/AE) and/or the contract repository.
2. Add a note in CRM and link it in the Jira ticket.
3. Follow the stricter of (a) contract requirement or (b) this policy.
4. If interpretation is unclear, escalate to CS leadership and Legal (Sofia Mendes) before committing to specific numbers in customer-facing communication.

**Operational note:** Customers may describe expectations informally (“we need 15-minute updates”). Only commit to an update cadence if it is (a) aligned with tier policy/contract and (b) realistically supportable with the current incident state.

---

## Customer communication expectations (alignment)
For P0/P1 incidents:
- Use comms templates and keep language consistent between:
  - Support email responses
  - Slack bridge summaries (internal)
  - Status page updates (if applicable)
- Always include:
  - current impact statement
  - what we’re doing now
  - what customers can do (workaround if validated)
  - next update time

See the incident comms alignment page for when to post a status event vs a docs banner vs a console alert.

---

## Operational checklist for Support (per ticket)
1. Set **Priority (P0–P4)** using the severity rubric.
2. Confirm **tier** (Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise) and document it in Jira.
3. Commit to **first response** and **next update time** per Table A.
4. If escalation threshold is met, follow Table B:
   - create bridge channel (per conventions)
   - page correct on-call group
   - link/pin Jira ticket(s)
5. Keep Jira current with: timestamps, mitigation attempts, and customer-visible summary.
6. If credits are requested, route internally with the required timeline summary.

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-15:** Clarified P0/P1 update cadence language (30 min vs 60 min) and standardized after-hours coverage notes; added explicit “Waiting on Customer” reminder policy.
- **2026-02-10:** Added contract exception workflow and credits approvals guidance.
- **2026-02-06:** Initial publication as part of escalation playbook refresh.
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference Support SLAs by customer tier (Self-serve Hosted API, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise). It standardizes:
- **First response targets** (time to first human acknowledgement)
- **Update cadence** (how often we provide meaningful progress updates)
- **Escalation thresholds** (when Support must page Engineering/SRE and/or start a bridge)
- **After-hours policy** (what is covered 24/7 vs business-hours only)
- **Credits requests & contract exceptions** (how to handle safely and consistently)

**Important:** These are *internal operating targets* and default policies. If a customer contract includes custom SLAs/terms, the contract governs. When in doubt, follow the “Contract exceptions” section and engage Legal/CS leadership.

---

## Definitions (quick)
- **Tier**: support coverage and targets based on a customer’s commercial plan.
- **Priority (P0–P4)**: Support ticket priority (see severity rubric for examples and mapping to incident severity).
- **First response**: a human response confirming receipt + requesting any missing diagnostics + stating next update time.
- **Update**: a non-empty change (new findings, mitigation, ETA change, or explicit “no change; still investigating; next update at X”).
- **Business hours (BH)**: Mon–Fri, 09:00–17:00 in the customer’s primary region where possible (default: PT for North America customers, GMT for EMEA customers). If a team member is not staffed for that timezone, use PT and note it in the first response.

---

## Customer tiers covered by this policy
### Self-serve (Hosted API)
- Typical: PLG / pay-as-you-go Hosted API customers.
- Coverage: **Business hours** (BH) default.

### Dedicated
- Reserved capacity customers (dedicated GPU pools, SLA-backed performance/uptime terms).
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4.

### Private
- VPC and/or on-prem deployments (regulated environments, customer-managed networking).
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4.

### Enterprise
- Enterprise plan customers (may be Hosted, Dedicated, and/or Private) with contract terms and executive visibility.
- Coverage: **24/7 for P0/P1**, BH for P2–P4 (unless contract states otherwise).

---

## SLA targets by tier and priority
Use this table after setting ticket priority per the severity rubric.

### Table A — First response and update cadence
> If a ticket spans multiple systems (e.g., Hosted API + Console + billing), set priority to the *highest-impact* issue and coordinate internally; do not create separate parallel customer threads.

| Priority | Typical impact (summary) | Self-serve (Hosted API) | Dedicated | Private | Enterprise |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **P0** | Service down, major security/compliance risk, or severe degradation with broad impact | **First response:** 1 hour (BH) \| **Updates:** every 2 hours (BH) \| **Bridge:** if multi-customer/widespread | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min | **First response:** 15 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 30 min (24/7) \| **Bridge:** start within 15 min |
| **P1** | Material feature outage or high error/latency impacting core workloads; workaround may exist but isn’t acceptable for most users | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) or when material change | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) | **First response:** 30 min (24/7) \| **Updates:** every 60 min while actively mitigating (24/7) |
| **P2** | Degradation with partial workaround; isolated region/model; limited subset of users | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** every 2 business days | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 4 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) | **First response:** 2 hours (BH) \| **Updates:** daily (BH) |
| **P3** | Minor issue, question, documentation/UX confusion; low business impact | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change | **First response:** 1 business day \| **Updates:** weekly or upon change |
| **P4** | How-to requests, enhancement asks, non-urgent follow-ups | **First response:** 5 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available | **First response:** 2 business days \| **Updates:** as available |

Notes:
- For **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise P0/P1**, updates are expected **even if Engineering is still investigating**. Support owns keeping the customer looped in.
- For **Self-serve**, Support does not run 24/7 coverage. If a Self-serve incident is **widespread** (multiple customers, multi-region, status-page-worthy), Support should follow the incident process and escalation triggers below.

---

## Escalation thresholds (paging + bridges)
Escalations must follow the escalation playbook and severity rubric. The goal is to page consistently with Engineering/SRE on-call expectations and avoid ad-hoc bypassing.

### Table B — When Support must escalate beyond standard triage
| Priority | Self-serve (Hosted API) | Dedicated | Private | Enterprise |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | Escalate if any of: (1) multi-customer impact, (2) region-wide degradation, (3) auth/outage patterns, (4) safety/compliance concern. Start Support bridge and move to **#incidents** if it qualifies as an incident. | **Page immediately** per routing matrix. Start Support bridge within 15 min. If SRE declares incident, move to **#incidents**. | **Page immediately** to Private Deployments/Platform on-call + SRE as needed. Start bridge within 15 min. | **Page immediately** + notify CS lead. Start bridge within 15 min. Consider executive escalation protocol if revenue/brand risk is high. |
| **P1** | Escalate if: sustained errors/latency beyond 30 minutes affecting multiple customers OR no meaningful next step after 1 BH. | Escalate if no mitigation path within 60 min or if dashboard signals indicate continued customer impact. | Escalate if mitigation requires config change/rollback or installer/deploy actions beyond Support playbooks. | Escalate if customer indicates business-critical impact, legal/compliance risk, or explicit SLA language invoked. |
| **P2–P4** | Escalate only when: reproducible product defect with clear evidence, or repeated tickets indicate a known issue needing publication, or safety/compliance concerns. | Escalate when repeated impact or no progress for >2 BH and customer’s workload is at risk. | Escalate when there is risk of customer environment instability or unsafe change guidance. | Escalate when there is contract/SLA interpretation needed or customer pressure requires leadership alignment. |

### Signal-based escalation guidance (use with the severity rubric)
Support should not guess incident state solely from a single ticket. Use available signals:
- **Error rate / gateway errors:** sudden spikes in 5xx, connection resets, or 429 patterns correlated across customers.
- **Latency:** p95/p99 deviation from baseline with confirmed customer impact.
- **Routing / fallback:** any evidence of fallback activation that contradicts customer expectations (e.g., model pinning concerns) should be validated using the routing/fallback metrics.
- **Region scope:** if multiple regions show correlated symptoms, treat as potential incident.

When escalating, include the required context template (request IDs, region, model/version, SDK version, timeframe, and any recent deploys).

---

## After-hours policy
### What Support covers after-hours
- **Dedicated/Private/Enterprise:**
  - **P0/P1:** covered **24/7** for acknowledgement, coordination, and customer updates.
  - **P2–P4:** handled during **business hours** unless explicitly upgraded to P0/P1.
- **Self-serve:**
  - Default is **business hours only**.
  - If a widespread incident is detected after-hours via monitoring/on-call processes, incident response proceeds through Engineering/SRE. Support will provide customer updates when staffed; do not promise 24/7 comms for Self-serve.

### Handoffs and continuity
- Use the escalation playbook handoff rules: summarize current state, what changed since last update, customer impact statement, and next actions.
- Pin the customer-impact summary in the bridge channel and keep Jira updated so the next shift can continue without re-triage.

---

## “Waiting on Customer” and SLA clocks
To avoid SLA confusion and missed comms:
- If **additional customer info is required** (logs, request IDs, reproduction), set status to **Waiting on Customer** in Jira.
- Post a clear ask with:
  - exact fields needed (e.g., request IDs, timestamps, region, model/version)
  - why it’s needed
  - a suggested safe way to share (no secrets; redact tokens)
- **SLA clock policy (internal):** While Waiting on Customer, we do not count the period toward “next update due,” but we still send a reminder per tier:
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: remind after **1 business day** (or sooner for P1)
  - Self-serve: remind after **2 business days**
- If the customer responds with partial info, move back to **In Progress** and re-commit to the next update time.

---

## Credits requests (guidance + approvals)
### Principles
- Do **not** promise credits in a Support ticket.
- Credits decisions depend on: contract terms, measured impact, prior credits, and Finance/CS approvals.
- Support’s responsibility is to provide an accurate timeline and impact summary and route the request correctly.

### When to route for credits consideration
Route to CS lead / account team when any of the following are true:
- Customer explicitly requests credits or references an SLA breach.
- Incident is P0 and there is clear unavailability/degradation beyond SLO targets.
- Enterprise account signals renewal risk due to operational impact.

### What Support should provide (copy/paste)
Include in the internal escalation summary:
- Ticket ID(s) and incident/bridge link
- Affected product surface (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), region(s), model(s)
- Impact window (start/end time in UTC) and customer-confirmed symptoms
- Mitigation steps attempted and final resolution state
- Comms timeline (first response time, update cadence adherence)
- Any contractual notes (if known) + link to CRM record

### Approval path (default)
- **Enterprise/Dedicated/Private:** CS lead (Rachel Kim or delegate) + **VP CS (Jasmine Liu)** approval; Finance/legal review if contract language is involved.
- **Self-serve:** typically handled via standard billing/support policy; escalate only for exceptional cases.

---

## Contract exceptions and custom SLAs
If a customer has a custom SLA or special coverage terms:
1. Confirm the terms via the account team (CSM/AE) and/or the contract repository.
2. Add a note in CRM and link it in the Jira ticket.
3. Follow the stricter of (a) contract requirement or (b) this policy.
4. If interpretation is unclear, escalate to CS leadership and Legal (Sofia Mendes) before committing to specific numbers in customer-facing communication.

**Operational note:** Customers may describe expectations informally (“we need 15-minute updates”). Only commit to an update cadence if it is (a) aligned with tier policy/contract and (b) realistically supportable with the current incident state.

---

## Customer communication expectations (alignment)
For P0/P1 incidents:
- Use comms templates and keep language consistent between:
  - Support email responses
  - Slack bridge summaries (internal)
  - Status page updates (if applicable)
- Always include:
  - current impact statement
  - what we’re doing now
  - what customers can do (workaround if validated)
  - next update time

See the incident comms alignment page for when to post a status event vs a docs banner vs a console alert.

---

## Operational checklist for Support (per ticket)
1. Set **Priority (P0–P4)** using the severity rubric.
2. Confirm **tier** (Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise) and document it in Jira.
3. Commit to **first response** and **next update time** per Table A.
4. If escalation threshold is met, follow Table B:
   - create bridge channel (per conventions)
   - page correct on-call group
   - link/pin Jira ticket(s)
5. Keep Jira current with: timestamps, mitigation attempts, and customer-visible summary.
6. If credits are requested, route internally with the required timeline summary.

---

## Changelog
- **2026-02-15:** Clarified P0/P1 update cadence language (30 min vs 60 min) and standardized after-hours coverage notes; added explicit “Waiting on Customer” reminder policy.
- **2026-02-10:** Added contract exception workflow and credits approvals guidance.
- **2026-02-06:** Initial publication as part of escalation playbook refresh.

source.title: Customer Success Observability Onboarding Kit
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production health within 10 minutes of cutover for the key inference and cost SLOs, and to provide clear stop/rollback criteria if signals indicate instability.

Scope:
- Applies to enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments where Redwood operates a telemetry gatekeeper or where the customer forwards metrics/traces/logs into our observability pipeline.
- Excludes ad-hoc POCs and single-request evaluation accounts.

Audience:
- Customer Success Onboarding PMs
- Platform/Infra Engineers
- Solutions Engineers and SREs

Key outcomes after following this kit:
1) Instrumentation baseline is validated (metrics, traces, logs).
2) Synthetic and real-traffic smoke tests pass for latency, error rate, and cost-attribution.
3) Dashboards and QBR metric bundles are populated and accessible to the customer and CS.
4) Clear criteria for rollback or quick mitigation are agreed and automated alerts are in place.

Owners & Roles:
- Customer Success (owner): overall coordination, customer comms, QBR handoff.
- Platform SRE (owner): observability integration, alerting, runbook edits.
- Solutions Engineering: agent side configuration, test harness.
- Customer Infra Lead: network/firewall and metric export configuration.

Pre-Cutover Checklist (to be completed in sequence):
1. Confirm network connectivity and firewall rules: dedicated tenant endpoints reachable from customer egress IPs (tests included below).
2. Telemetry pipeline: ensure metrics, traces, and logs have been configured in the customer’s telemetry project and namespaced with tenant_id.
3. Sampling and volume controls: verify request sample rate and trace sampling are set per the table below to avoid ingestion spikes.
4. Dashboard access: create and share the tenant-specific dashboard with read-only links to CS and customer stakeholders.
5. Alerting: enable the standard go‑live alert set and confirm proper escalation targets.
6. Runbook reviewed and assigned to oncall rotation for the first 72 hours post-cutover.

Instrumentation Requirements (reference table):
| Signal | Metric name (examples) | Required labels/tags | Target sampling/retention |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Request counters | inference.requests.total | tenant_id, model, route | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Latency histograms | inference.latency.ms | tenant_id, model, stage | Full histogram, 30d retention |
| Errors | inference.errors.total | tenant_id, model, code | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Token cost | inference.tokens.consumed | tenant_id, model, billing_unit | Aggregate per-minute, 365d retention |
| Traces | rpc.trace | tenant_id, request_id, route | 5-10% sample for heavy traffic, 100% for synthetic tests |

Note: If customer is forwarding telemetry to their own project, ensure label mapping includes our tenant_id for cross-correlation.

Configuration examples:
- Prometheus scrape relabel config (snippet):
  - job_name: 'redwood-tenant-<TENANT_ID>'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['redwood-telemetry.gw.internal:9100']
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: ['__address__']
        target_label: 'instance'

- Trace sampling policy (example):
  service: inference-gateway
  sampling:
    default: 0.05
    routes:
      /api/v1/stream: 0.10
      /api/v1/generate: 0.05

Validation Test Plan (must pass before signalling go‑live):
1. Connectivity smoke (5 min):
   - From customer IP: curl /health -> 200, TLS cert valid, latency < 250ms to control plane.
2. Synthetic throughput ramp (30 min):
   - Inject synthetic requests that match expected 95th percentile token length. Ramp 0 -> 200 reqs/min.
   - Verify request success >= 99.5% and p95 latency remains under the agreed SLO.
3. Trace correlation test (10 min):
   - Trigger a request with X-Request-ID and verify full trace spans appear in tenant traces within 60s.
4. Cost attribution & token accounting (10 min):
   - Run 100 sample requests and confirm tokens consumed match the accounting logs and billing metrics for that tenant.
5. Dashboard & Alert exercise (15 min):
   - Force a non-prod threshold breach (e.g., a synthetic high-latency spike) and ensure alert fires, route to correct paging targets, and incident playbook opens.

SLOs and Operational Thresholds (recommended defaults to sign off go‑live):
- Availability (5m window): 99.95% (errors < 0.05% of requests)
- Latency (tail): p95 < 800ms for standard routes; p95 < 1200ms for streaming routes
- Cost drift: minute-by-minute token-cost delta < 15% from expected baseline during first 48 hours
- Tracing latency: trace ingestion delay < 60s for synthetic tests

Alerting & Paging matrix (examples):
- Sev 1: tenant-wide error rate > 2% for 2 consecutive 1-minute windows -> Platform SRE paging (pager duty) + notify CS escalations channel.
- Sev 2: p95 latency above SLO for 10 minutes -> SRE async + CS on-call notification.
- Billing anomaly (cost drift > 50% sustained over 10 minutes): CS & Finance alert (email + Slack).

Rollback Criteria & Quick Mitigations:
Execute rollback immediately if any of the following occur within the first 6 hours: 
- Availability drops below 99.5% for > 5 minutes and does not recover after automated scaling attempt.
- Customer experiences sustained token-cost blowouts > 200% of baseline for 15 minutes.
- Trace correlation fails for > 30% of requests and blocks triage.

Rollback steps (ordered):
1. Engage SRE and CS leads and mark customer as degraded in status page.
2. Switch traffic to the pre-cutover stable model variant (routing flag) or to fallback region (automated policy).
3. Disable any new batching/caching optimizations introduced during cutover.
4. If unstable after steps 2–3, perform tenant-level quiesce and failback to prior reserved capacity (see tenant quiesce playbook).
5. Initiate postmortem and customer comms within 2 hours of rollback decision.

Post-Cutover 72-hour Monitoring Runbook:
- First 10 minutes: monitor synthetic smoke dashboards and verify no Sev1.
- 10–60 minutes: validate trace ingestion, cost accounting, and token distribution.
- Hourly checks for first 24 hours: availability, p95 latency, billing delta.
- Daily QBR bundle: aggregate 24h metrics for the first 7 days and present in the initial QBR.

Communication templates (example Slack + Email snippets):
- Slack (to customer): "Cutover start: initiating traffic switch at 2026-02-15T09:00Z. We will monitor latency, errors, and billing closely. Expected window is 60 minutes. Will update every 15 minutes or on deviation."
- Customer-facing postmortem template (summary + impact + mitigation + next steps) is attached to this page as a downloadable doc.

FAQ & Troubleshooting tips:
Q: What if dashboards show no metrics for tenant after cutover?
A: Confirm relabel mapping includes tenant_id. If forwarding, check that exporters are writing labels and that our telemetry gatekeeper is receiving samples (curl /telemetry/health).

Q: Who authorizes rollback?
A: The oncall SRE in coordination with CS lead and customer Infra lead. If disagreement, default to SRE for platform stability decisions.

Appendix A — Quick CLI checks:
- curl -sS https://<control-plane>/health | jq .status
- grpcurl -plaintext <inference-host>:<port> inference.Service/HealthCheck

Appendix B — Dashboard links and saved queries:
- Tenant perf dashboard: /dashboards/tenant/<TENANT_ID>/perf
- Cost attribution query: saved_query:cost_attribution_tenant_<TENANT_ID>

Related pages:
- Telemetry Gatekeeper Onboarding and QBR Integration Guide (see related_pages)
- Tenant quiesce and safe failback playbook

Change log:
- 2025-11-02: Created by Ava Chen as onboarding kit draft.
- 2026-01-10: Added rollback criteria and sampling recommendations (review by Diego Martinez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized and published after pilot with AcmeCorp (notes appended).

1. Engage SRE and CS leads and mark customer as degraded in status page.
2. Switch traffic to the pre-cutover stable model variant (routing flag) or to fallback region (automated policy).
3. Disable any new batching/caching optimizations introduced during cutover.
4. If unstable after steps 2–3, perform tenant-level quiesce and failback to prior reserved capacity (see tenant quiesce playbook).
5. Initiate postmortem and customer comms within 2 hours of rollback decision.

Post-Cutover 72-hour Monitoring Runbook:
- First 10 minutes: monitor synthetic smoke dashboards and verify no Sev1.
- 10–60 minutes: validate trace ingestion, cost accounting, and token distribution.
- Hourly checks for first 24 hours: availability, p95 latency, billing delta.
- Daily QBR bundle: aggregate 24h metrics for the first 7 days and present in the initial QBR.

Communication templates (example Slack + Email snippets):
- Slack (to customer): "Cutover start: initiating traffic switch at 2026-02-15T09:00Z. We will monitor latency, errors, and billing closely. Expected window is 60 minutes. Will update every 15 minutes or on deviation."
- Customer-facing postmortem template (summary + impact + mitigation + next steps) is attached to this page as a downloadable doc.

FAQ & Troubleshooting tips:
Q: What if dashboards show no metrics for tenant after cutover?
A: Confirm relabel mapping includes tenant_id. If forwarding, check that exporters are writing labels and that our telemetry gatekeeper is receiving samples (curl /telemetry/health).

Q: Who authorizes rollback?
A: The oncall SRE in coordination with CS lead and customer Infra lead. If disagreement, default to SRE for platform stability decisions.

Appendix A — Quick CLI checks:
- curl -sS https://<control-plane>/health | jq .status
- grpcurl -plaintext <inference-host>:<port> inference.Service/HealthCheck

Appendix B — Dashboard links and saved queries:
- Tenant perf dashboard: /dashboards/tenant/<TENANT_ID>/perf
- Cost attribution query: saved_query:cost_attribution_tenant_<TENANT_ID>

Related pages:
- Telemetry Gatekeeper Onboarding and QBR Integration Guide (see related_pages)
- Tenant quiesce and safe failback playbook

Change log:
- 2025-11-02: Created by Ava Chen as onboarding kit draft.
- 2026-01-10: Added rollback criteria and sampling recommendations (review by Diego Martinez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized and published after pilot with AcmeCorp (notes appended).
text:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production health within 10 minutes of cutover for the key inference and cost SLOs, and to provide clear stop/rollback criteria if signals indicate instability.

Scope:
- Applies to enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments where Redwood operates a telemetry gatekeeper or where the customer forwards metrics/traces/logs into our observability pipeline.
- Excludes ad-hoc POCs and single-request evaluation accounts.

Audience:
- Customer Success Onboarding PMs
- Platform/Infra Engineers
- Solutions Engineers and SREs

Key outcomes after following this kit:
1) Instrumentation baseline is validated (metrics, traces, logs).
2) Synthetic and real-traffic smoke tests pass for latency, error rate, and cost-attribution.
3) Dashboards and QBR metric bundles are populated and accessible to the customer and CS.
4) Clear criteria for rollback or quick mitigation are agreed and automated alerts are in place.

Owners & Roles:
- Customer Success (owner): overall coordination, customer comms, QBR handoff.
- Platform SRE (owner): observability integration, alerting, runbook edits.
- Solutions Engineering: agent side configuration, test harness.
- Customer Infra Lead: network/firewall and metric export configuration.

Pre-Cutover Checklist (to be completed in sequence):
1. Confirm network connectivity and firewall rules: dedicated tenant endpoints reachable from customer egress IPs (tests included below).
2. Telemetry pipeline: ensure metrics, traces, and logs have been configured in the customer’s telemetry project and namespaced with tenant_id.
3. Sampling and volume controls: verify request sample rate and trace sampling are set per the table below to avoid ingestion spikes.
4. Dashboard access: create and share the tenant-specific dashboard with read-only links to CS and customer stakeholders.
5. Alerting: enable the standard go‑live alert set and confirm proper escalation targets.
6. Runbook reviewed and assigned to oncall rotation for the first 72 hours post-cutover.

Instrumentation Requirements (reference table):
| Signal | Metric name (examples) | Required labels/tags | Target sampling/retention |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Request counters | inference.requests.total | tenant_id, model, route | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Latency histograms | inference.latency.ms | tenant_id, model, stage | Full histogram, 30d retention |
| Errors | inference.errors.total | tenant_id, model, code | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Token cost | inference.tokens.consumed | tenant_id, model, billing_unit | Aggregate per-minute, 365d retention |
| Traces | rpc.trace | tenant_id, request_id, route | 5-10% sample for heavy traffic, 100% for synthetic tests |

Note: If customer is forwarding telemetry to their own project, ensure label mapping includes our tenant_id for cross-correlation.

Configuration examples:
- Prometheus scrape relabel config (snippet):
  - job_name: 'redwood-tenant-<TENANT_ID>'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['redwood-telemetry.gw.internal:9100']
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: ['__address__']
        target_label: 'instance'

- Trace sampling policy (example):
  service: inference-gateway
  sampling:
    default: 0.05
    routes:
      /api/v1/stream: 0.10
      /api/v1/generate: 0.05

Validation Test Plan (must pass before signalling go‑live):
1. Connectivity smoke (5 min):
   - From customer IP: curl /health -> 200, TLS cert valid, latency < 250ms to control plane.
2. Synthetic throughput ramp (30 min):
   - Inject synthetic requests that match expected 95th percentile token length. Ramp 0 -> 200 reqs/min.
   - Verify request success >= 99.5% and p95 latency remains under the agreed SLO.
3. Trace correlation test (10 min):
   - Trigger a request with X-Request-ID and verify full trace spans appear in tenant traces within 60s.
4. Cost attribution & token accounting (10 min):
   - Run 100 sample requests and confirm tokens consumed match the accounting logs and billing metrics for that tenant.
5. Dashboard & Alert exercise (15 min):
   - Force a non-prod threshold breach (e.g., a synthetic high-latency spike) and ensure alert fires, route to correct paging targets, and incident playbook opens.

SLOs and Operational Thresholds (recommended defaults to sign off go‑live):
- Availability (5m window): 99.95% (errors < 0.05% of requests)
- Latency (tail): p95 < 800ms for standard routes; p95 < 1200ms for streaming routes
- Cost drift: minute-by-minute token-cost delta < 15% from expected baseline during first 48 hours
- Tracing latency: trace ingestion delay < 60s for synthetic tests

Alerting & Paging matrix (examples):
- Sev 1: tenant-wide error rate > 2% for 2 consecutive 1-minute windows -> Platform SRE paging (pager duty) + notify CS escalations channel.
- Sev 2: p95 latency above SLO for 10 minutes -> SRE async + CS on-call notification.
- Billing anomaly (cost drift > 50% sustained over 10 minutes): CS & Finance alert (email + Slack).

Rollback Criteria & Quick Mitigations:
Execute rollback immediately if any of the following occur within the first 6 hours: 
- Availability drops below 99.5% for > 5 minutes and does not recover after automated scaling attempt.
- Customer experiences sustained token-cost blowouts > 200% of baseline for 15 minutes.
- Trace correlation fails for > 30% of requests and blocks triage.

Rollback steps (ordered):
1. Engage SRE and CS leads and mark customer as degraded in status page.
2. Switch traffic to the pre-cutover stable model variant (routing flag) or to fallback region (automated policy).
3. Disable any new batching/caching optimizations introduced during cutover.
4. If unstable after steps 2–3, perform tenant-level quiesce and failback to prior reserved capacity (see tenant quiesce playbook).
5. Initiate postmortem and customer comms within 2 hours of rollback decision.

Post-Cutover 72-hour Monitoring Runbook:
- First 10 minutes: monitor synthetic smoke dashboards and verify no Sev1.
- 10–60 minutes: validate trace ingestion, cost accounting, and token distribution.
- Hourly checks for first 24 hours: availability, p95 latency, billing delta.
- Daily QBR bundle: aggregate 24h metrics for the first 7 days and present in the initial QBR.

Communication templates (example Slack + Email snippets):
- Slack (to customer): "Cutover start: initiating traffic switch at 2026-02-15T09:00Z. We will monitor latency, errors, and billing closely. Expected window is 60 minutes. Will update every 15 minutes or on deviation."
- Customer-facing postmortem template (summary + impact + mitigation + next steps) is attached to this page as a downloadable doc.

FAQ & Troubleshooting tips:
Q: What if dashboards show no metrics for tenant after cutover?
A: Confirm relabel mapping includes tenant_id. If forwarding, check that exporters are writing labels and that our telemetry gatekeeper is receiving samples (curl /telemetry/health).

Q: Who authorizes rollback?
A: The oncall SRE in coordination with CS lead and customer Infra lead. If disagreement, default to SRE for platform stability decisions.

Appendix A — Quick CLI checks:
- curl -sS https://<control-plane>/health | jq .status
- grpcurl -plaintext <inference-host>:<port> inference.Service/HealthCheck

Appendix B — Dashboard links and saved queries:
- Tenant perf dashboard: /dashboards/tenant/<TENANT_ID>/perf
- Cost attribution query: saved_query:cost_attribution_tenant_<TENANT_ID>

Related pages:
- Telemetry Gatekeeper Onboarding and QBR Integration Guide (see related_pages)
- Tenant quiesce and safe failback playbook
