- 2025-11-02: Created by Ava Chen as onboarding kit draft.
- 2026-01-10: Added rollback criteria and sampling recommendations (review by Diego Martinez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized and published after pilot with AcmeCorp (notes appended).

source.title: sensitive-data-exposure-escalation-and-remediation-playbook-2026
source.author: Maria Gomez
source.content:
Overview:
This playbook defines the end-to-end escalation, containment, remediation, and post-incident review process for confirmed or strongly suspected exposures of customer-sensitive data (PII, proprietary customer content, model prompts with sensitive inputs, or retained telemetry). It applies to hosted Redwood API, Redwood Dedicated, and Redwood Private deployments. The document emphasizes fast containment, clear communication, legal and compliance coordination, and preserving evidence for forensic review.

Scope and definitions:
- Sensitive data: anything classified as PII, PCI, PHI, proprietary trade secrets, or customer-designated restricted data.
- Exposure: data confirmed to have been transmitted to an unauthorized destination (external cloud, public internet, third-party vendor without contract), or accessible to unauthorized internal actors.
- Suspected exposure: credible indicators (logs, alerts, customer report) that merit immediate containment investigation but are not yet confirmed.
- Affected customer: the tenant or organization whose data may be exposed.

Detection triggers (examples):
1) Customer report of seeing data returned in unrelated request.
2) IDS/IPS or Data Loss Prevention (DLP) alert referencing PII patterns in outbound traffic from an inference node.
3) Unexpected S3/object-store public ACL changes, presigned URL issuance to unfamiliar IPs.
4) Audit log showing API key usage from an IP/geography inconsistent with customer config or Dedicated/private infra.
5) Telemetry or evals showing KV cache collisions or prefix leakage during rollouts.

Immediate (first 30 minutes) checklist — Triage and containment (owner: oncall-sre + security-oncall):
1) Acknowledge the alert and start an incident in the incident tracker. Record timeline, reporter, and initial evidence.
2) Classify severity:
   - Sev-1: Confirmed exposure to external party or broad public disclosure.
   - Sev-2: Confirmed exposure limited to small set of actors (internal user or partner) or likely exposure requiring further action.
   - Sev-3: Suspected exposure with low confidence.
3) If Sev-1 or Sev-2, notify Security On-Call (pager) and Customer Success lead for the account within 15 minutes.
4) Preserve evidence: snapshot relevant instances, collect kernel logs, container stdout/stderr, inference node audit logs, networking flow logs, and any object-store access logs. Follow forensic collection checklist below.
5) Apply immediate containment:
   - For hosted API: rotate the impacted API key(s) scoped to the customer; issue short-lived replacement keys via the Console or API.
   - For Dedicated: isolate affected GPU nodes in maintenance mode, disable public egress if configured, and detach storage mounts suspected of leakage.
   - For Private (customer-managed VPC/on-prem): coordinate with customer and SRE on network ACLs and cross-connects; request temporary kube/node cordon if allowed.
6) If customer reports live exfiltration or public posting, consider a takedown request (legal) and activating external comms.

Escalation tree and responsibilities:
- Level 0 (Support Tier 1): initial intake, validate reporter identity, collect basic artifacts (request ID, timestamps, sample payloads). Owner: Support Engineer.
- Level 1 (Support Tier 2 / Oncall SRE): reproduce request trace, check API gateway logs, rate-limit or throttle offending keys, apply tactical containment (rotate keys, suspend routes). Owner: SRE oncall.
- Level 2 (Security Incident Response): if confirmed or likely sensitive exposure, engage Security IR lead, open a Security Incident channel, involve legal and compliance. Owner: Security Oncall.
- Level 3 (Executive / Customer Success and Legal): when material customer impact, regulatory exposure, or external disclosure is likely. Owner: Security Lead + CSM + Legal.

Containment and tactical remediation (detailed steps):
- Revoke and rotate credentials (IMMEDIATE):
  Command examples (hosted):
    # Revoke API key (CLI)
    redwoodctl api-keys revoke --id <api_key_id> --reason "sensitive-data-exposure"

    # Issue short-lived replacement (Console automation recommended)
    redwoodctl api-keys create --tenant <tenant_id> --ttl 3600 --scopes "restricted"

- Network isolation (Dedicated/Private):
  - Update egress rules to block external destinations for affected nodes.
  - If presigned URLs are implicated, invalidate by rotating signing keys and listing/expiring presigned objects.

- Storage/object exposure:
  - Set object ACLs to private; for S3-compatible stores, enforce bucket policy to disallow public read.
  - Consider archiving exposed objects into WORM evidence store for forensics and then secure-delete according to legal guidance.

- Model / cache-related leakage:
  - Flush KV caches and persistent prefix caches for impacted model versions.
  - If model variant or rollout may have caused cross-customer responses, rollback to a known-good model version and block the suspect variant from serving.

Evidence collection checklist (forensics team):
- Collect: gateway logs (request_id, timestamp, source IP, user-agent), inference node logs, OS-level journald logs, kernel ring buffer if crash suspected, container runtimes, S3/object access logs, ACL history, ACL changelogs, IAM audit trail, and any customer-submitted samples.
- Preserve reproducibility: record exact model version, config (batching, truncation, sampling), and rollout ID.
- Hash and store evidence in central secure bucket with strict RBAC; record who accessed the evidence.

Customer communication templates (initial contact):
- Short acknowledgement (within 1 hour):
  "Thanks for reporting. We have opened an investigation (INC-<id>) and are actively containing and collecting evidence. We will update you within 4 hours or sooner if new critical information emerges. For immediate concerns, your CSM <name> is available."
- Status cadence: provide an ETA for next update, summary of actions taken, and next deliverable (containment confirmation / remediation plan). Legal should review wording for regulated customers.

Regulatory and legal coordination:
- If data falls under regulated categories (PII/PHI/PCI/GDPR personal data), notify Legal and Compliance within 1 hour of confirmation.
- Legal owner determines breach notification obligations, timelines, and templates for customer and regulator notices.

Post-incident remediation and recovery steps (owner: Security + SRE + CSM):
1) Complete root cause analysis (RCA) with a timeline, causal factors, and corrective actions.
2) Implement permanent fixes: code changes, configuration hardening, improved presigned URL lifecycle, stricter bucket policies, kernel param fixes for memory isolation, or model-serving sandboxing.
3) Track remediation tasks as JIRA stories with owners and completion dates.
4) Communicate a final report to the affected customer: timeline, impact assessment, mitigation steps taken, and recommended customer actions (e.g., rotate their own credentials if used externally).

SLOs / response targets (internal):
- Initial acknowledgement to customer: <= 60 minutes for Sev-1 or customer-reported exposure.
- Containment actions initiated: <= 2 hours for Sev-1.
- Forensic evidence collection snapshot completed: <= 24 hours.
- Final remediation plan proposed: <= 72 hours.

Preventive controls and recommended runbook updates:
- Ensure DLP rules are tuned for common customer data patterns (SSNs, emails, tokens) and outbound flows are monitored.
- Harden default bucket policies for hosted assets and fail closed for presigned URL issuance.
- Add dedicated automated checks during model rollouts to detect prompt/response cross-tenant similarity spikes.
- Regularly review KV cache eviction policies and implement tenant isolation guards for near-term rollouts.

Metrics and postmortem guidance (RCA template):
- What happened? timeline of events.
- Impact: customers affected, data types, volume of records exposed.
- Root cause: primary and contributing factors.
- Detection: how it was detected and by whom.
- Containment and remediation: actions taken and timeline.
- Action items: owners, JIRA IDs, and deadlines.
- Lessons learned and prevention plan.

Runbook links and tools:
- Forensic collection scripts: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/forensic-collection-scripts (link)
- API key rotation automation: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/api-key-rotation-automation
- KV cache flush utility: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/kvcache-flush-utility

Appendix A — Quick checklist (for Support Engineers):
1) Verify reporter identity and obtain sample evidence.
2) Create incident and set initial severity.
3) Notify Security Oncall for Sev-1/Sev-2.
4) Rotate credentials implicated and suppress suspect routes.
5) Preserve logs and escalate to Forensics.
6) Keep customer updated with at least 4-hour cadence until containment.

Appendix B — Example CLI commands and notes:
- Rotate API key (hosted): redwoodctl api-keys revoke --id <api_key>
- Suspend model rollout: redwoodctl rollouts suspend --id <rollout_id> --reason "potential-data-leak"
- Flush KV cache on runtime: redwoodctl runtime flush-cache --node <node_id> --cache-type kv

Ownership and contacts (examples — update pager/team rota links in team wiki):
- Security Oncall: pager duty link (security-oncall)
- SRE Oncall: pager duty link (sre-oncall)
- CSM escalation: customer-success-oncall@redwood.example (CSM team)
- Legal and Compliance: legal-esc@redwood.example

Review cadence and maintenance:
- This playbook should be reviewed quarterly and after any Sev-1 event. Security and Customer Success must coordinate updates to the communication templates and containment checklists.

Document history and approvals:
- Drafted by: Maria Gomez (Security) 2025-11-20
- Reviewed by: Ethan Park, Aisha Rahman, Ravi Patel 2025-12-08
- Approved and published: 2026-02-15

text:
Overview:
This playbook defines the end-to-end escalation, containment, remediation, and post-incident review process for confirmed or strongly suspected exposures of customer-sensitive data (PII, proprietary customer content, model prompts with sensitive inputs, or retained telemetry). It applies to hosted Redwood API, Redwood Dedicated, and Redwood Private deployments. The document emphasizes fast containment, clear communication, legal and compliance coordination, and preserving evidence for forensic review.

- Sensitive data: anything classified as PII, PCI, PHI, proprietary trade secrets, or customer-designated restricted data.
- Exposure: data confirmed to have been transmitted to an unauthorized destination (external cloud, public internet, third-party vendor without contract), or accessible to unauthorized internal actors.
- Suspected exposure: credible indicators (logs, alerts, customer report) that merit immediate containment investigation but are not yet confirmed.
- Affected customer: the tenant or organization whose data may be exposed.

Detection triggers (examples):
1) Customer report of seeing data returned in unrelated request.
2) IDS/IPS or Data Loss Prevention (DLP) alert referencing PII patterns in outbound traffic from an inference node.
3) Unexpected S3/object-store public ACL changes, presigned URL issuance to unfamiliar IPs.
4) Audit log showing API key usage from an IP/geography inconsistent with customer config or Dedicated/private infra.
5) Telemetry or evals showing KV cache collisions or prefix leakage during rollouts.

1) Acknowledge the alert and start an incident in the incident tracker. Record timeline, reporter, and initial evidence.
2) Classify severity:
   - Sev-1: Confirmed exposure to external party or broad public disclosure.
   - Sev-2: Confirmed exposure limited to small set of actors (internal user or partner) or likely exposure requiring further action.
   - Sev-3: Suspected exposure with low confidence.
3) If Sev-1 or Sev-2, notify Security On-Call (pager) and Customer Success lead for the account within 15 minutes.
4) Preserve evidence: snapshot relevant instances, collect kernel logs, container stdout/stderr, inference node audit logs, networking flow logs, and any object-store access logs. Follow forensic collection checklist below.
5) Apply immediate containment:
   - For hosted API: rotate the impacted API key(s) scoped to the customer; issue short-lived replacement keys via the Console or API.
   - For Dedicated: isolate affected GPU nodes in maintenance mode, disable public egress if configured, and detach storage mounts suspected of leakage.
   - For Private (customer-managed VPC/on-prem): coordinate with customer and SRE on network ACLs and cross-connects; request temporary kube/node cordon if allowed.
6) If customer reports live exfiltration or public posting, consider a takedown request (legal) and activating external comms.

Escalation tree and responsibilities:
- Level 0 (Support Tier 1): initial intake, validate reporter identity, collect basic artifacts (request ID, timestamps, sample payloads). Owner: Support Engineer.
- Level 1 (Support Tier 2 / Oncall SRE): reproduce request trace, check API gateway logs, rate-limit or throttle offending keys, apply tactical containment (rotate keys, suspend routes). Owner: SRE oncall.
- Level 2 (Security Incident Response): if confirmed or likely sensitive exposure, engage Security IR lead, open a Security Incident channel, involve legal and compliance. Owner: Security Oncall.
- Level 3 (Executive / Customer Success and Legal): when material customer impact, regulatory exposure, or external disclosure is likely. Owner: Security Lead + CSM + Legal.

Containment and tactical remediation (detailed steps):
- Revoke and rotate credentials (IMMEDIATE):
  Command examples (hosted):
    # Revoke API key (CLI)
    redwoodctl api-keys revoke --id <api_key_id> --reason \"sensitive-data-exposure\"

    # Issue short-lived replacement (Console automation recommended)
    redwoodctl api-keys create --tenant <tenant_id> --ttl 3600 --scopes \"restricted\"

- Network isolation (Dedicated/Private):
  - Update egress rules to block external destinations for affected nodes.
  - If presigned URLs are implicated, invalidate by rotating signing keys and listing/expiring presigned objects.

- Storage/object exposure:
  - Set object ACLs to private; for S3-compatible stores, enforce bucket policy to disallow public read.
  - Consider archiving exposed objects into WORM evidence store for forensics and then secure-delete according to legal guidance.

- Model / cache-related leakage:
  - Flush KV caches and persistent prefix caches for impacted model versions.
  - If model variant or rollout may have caused cross-customer responses, rollback to a known-good model version and block the suspect variant from serving.

Evidence collection checklist (forensics team):
- Collect: gateway logs (request_id, timestamp, source IP, user-agent), inference node logs, OS-level journald logs, kernel ring buffer if crash suspected, container runtimes, S3/object access logs, ACL history, ACL changelogs, IAM audit trail, and any customer-submitted samples.
- Preserve reproducibility: record exact model version, config (batching, truncation, sampling), and rollout ID.
- Hash and store evidence in central secure bucket with strict RBAC; record who accessed the evidence.

Customer communication templates (initial contact):
- Short acknowledgement (within 1 hour):
  \"Thanks for reporting. We have opened an investigation (INC-<id>) and are actively containing and collecting evidence. We will update you within 4 hours or sooner if new critical information emerges. For immediate concerns, your CSM <name> is available.\"
- Status cadence: provide an ETA for next update, summary of actions taken, and next deliverable (containment confirmation / remediation plan). Legal should review wording for regulated customers.

Regulatory and legal coordination:
- If data falls under regulated categories (PII/PHI/PCI/GDPR personal data), notify Legal and Compliance within 1 hour of confirmation.
- Legal owner determines breach notification obligations, timelines, and templates for customer and regulator notices.

Post-incident remediation and recovery steps (owner: Security + SRE + CSM):
1) Complete root cause analysis (RCA) with a timeline, causal factors, and corrective actions.
2) Implement permanent fixes: code changes, configuration hardening, improved presigned URL lifecycle, stricter bucket policies, kernel param fixes for memory isolation, or model-serving sandboxing.
3) Track remediation tasks as JIRA stories with owners and completion dates.
4) Communicate a final report to the affected customer: timeline, impact assessment, mitigation steps taken, and recommended customer actions (e.g., rotate their own credentials if used externally).

SLOs / response targets (internal):
- Initial acknowledgement to customer: <= 60 minutes for Sev-1 or customer-reported exposure.
- Containment actions initiated: <= 2 hours for Sev-1.
- Forensic evidence collection snapshot completed: <= 24 hours.
- Final remediation plan proposed: <= 72 hours.

Preventive controls and recommended runbook updates:
- Ensure DLP rules are tuned for common customer data patterns (SSNs, emails, tokens) and outbound flows are monitored.
- Harden default bucket policies for hosted assets and fail closed for presigned URL issuance.
- Add dedicated automated checks during model rollouts to detect prompt/response cross-tenant similarity spikes.
- Regularly review KV cache eviction policies and implement tenant isolation guards for near-term rollouts.

Metrics and postmortem guidance (RCA template):
- What happened? timeline of events.
- Impact: customers affected, data types, volume of records exposed.
- Root cause: primary and contributing factors.
- Detection: how it was detected and by whom.
- Containment and remediation: actions taken and timeline.
- Action items: owners, JIRA IDs, and deadlines.
- Lessons learned and prevention plan.

Runbook links and tools:
- Forensic collection scripts: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/forensic-collection-scripts (link)
- API key rotation automation: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/api-key-rotation-automation
- KV cache flush utility: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/kvcache-flush-utility

Appendix A — Quick checklist (for Support Engineers):
1) Verify reporter identity and obtain sample evidence.
2) Create incident and set initial severity.
3) Notify Security Oncall for Sev-1/Sev-2.
4) Rotate credentials implicated and suppress suspect routes.
5) Preserve logs and escalate to Forensics.
6) Keep customer updated with at least 4-hour cadence until containment.

Appendix B — Example CLI commands and notes:
- Rotate API key (hosted): redwoodctl api-keys revoke --id <api_key>
- Suspend model rollout: redwoodctl rollouts suspend --id <rollout_id> --reason \"potential-data-leak\"
- Flush KV cache on runtime: redwoodctl runtime flush-cache --node <node_id> --cache-type kv

Ownership and contacts (examples — update pager/team rota links in team wiki):
- Security Oncall: pager duty link (security-oncall)
- SRE Oncall: pager duty link (sre-oncall)
- CSM escalation: customer-success-oncall@redwood.example (CSM team)
- Legal and Compliance: legal-esc@redwood.example

Review cadence and maintenance:
- This playbook should be reviewed quarterly and after any Sev-1 event. Security and Customer Success must coordinate updates to the communication templates and containment checklists.

Document history and approvals:
- Drafted by: Maria Gomez (Security) 2025-11-20
- Reviewed by: Ethan Park, Aisha Rahman, Ravi Patel 2025-12-08
- Approved and published: 2026-02-15

source.title: Customer Comms Templates (Support + CS)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.body:
## Purpose
Use these templates for consistent, customer-safe communication during escalations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers. These templates are designed for:
- Initial acknowledgement
- Mitigation / investigation updates
- Workaround guidance (known issues)
- Resolution + follow-up (including next steps and optional RCA workflow)

These templates are written to reduce back-and-forth, align to Support SLAs, and avoid overpromising.

## Tone + content guidance (read before using)
**Be clear, calm, and specific.** Customers mostly want: confirmation we see the issue, what they should do now, what we’re doing, and when they’ll hear from us next.

### Do
- Acknowledge impact in the customer’s terms (errors, timeouts, latency, blocked workflow).
- Ask for the minimum viable diagnostics: request IDs, timestamps, region, model, SDK version, and whether streaming/non-streaming.
- Provide a concrete next update time (“Next update by 14:30 PT”).
- If a workaround exists, provide it as copy/paste steps and clearly label it as **temporary**.
- Use “We believe / We’re investigating / Our current hypothesis” when not confirmed.
- If relevant, state what is safe to share (e.g., “No action needed” vs “Please retry with…”).

### Don’t (what not to promise)
- **Do not promise ETAs** unless SRE/Eng has provided an externally shareable estimate and you have confirmed it is appropriate for the customer tier.
- Do not speculate on root cause (“kernel bug”, “bad deploy”, “GPU failures”) unless confirmed and approved for external sharing.
- Do not imply contractual outcomes (credits, SLA breach confirmation) in-line. Route to the credits process and exec escalation protocol when needed.
- Do not ask for sensitive data (full prompts, customer datasets, secrets). Prefer request IDs and sanitized logs. If logs are needed: provide explicit redaction instructions and use approved secure channels.
- Do not imply that pinned model versions or routing policies are “guaranteed” in all failure modes—use the documented policy language.

### Consistency rules
- **Use the same severity language across channels.** If you use P0/P1 internally, translate externally to “high impact / degraded / intermittent” unless the customer already uses P-levels.
- **Status page vs email:** Follow the Status Page + Comms Alignment doc for when something qualifies as a status event.
- **Update cadence:** Follow the customer’s tier SLA table. If you’re unsure, default to the stricter cadence for the tier and confirm with Support lead/on-call.

---

## Variables (replace placeholders)
Use these placeholders to keep comms consistent:
- {customer_name}
- {account_tier} (self_serve | dedicated | private | enterprise)
- {case_id} (SUP-####)
- {region} (us-east, eu-west, etc.)
- {model} (e.g., redwood/llama-3-70b-instruct)
- {endpoint} (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, streaming)
- {time_window} (e.g., “since 09:10–09:35 PT”)
- {request_ids} (a few representative IDs)
- {current_status} (investigating | identified | monitoring | mitigated | resolved)
- {next_update_time}
- {workaround_steps}
- {kb_link} (internal-to-customer-safe link)
- {mitigation_applied} (what we changed, in customer-safe language)
- {customer_actions}
- {support_signature} (name, role, timezone)

---

## Template 1 — Initial acknowledgement (incident / high impact)
**Channel:** Email (primary), optional ticket comment in customer portal

**Subject options (pick one):**
- Update: We’re investigating {symptom} impacting {endpoint} in {region}
- Acknowledged: {symptom} — {region} — Next update by {next_update_time}

**Body:**
Hi {customer_name},

Thanks for flagging this. We’re seeing and actively investigating **{symptom}** affecting **{endpoint}** in **{region}** {time_window}.

**What we know so far**
- Impact: {impact_summary} (e.g., increased timeouts / stream disconnects / elevated 5xx)
- Scope: {scope_summary} (e.g., intermittent; specific model; subset of requests)

**What we need from you (if available)**
If you can share any of the below, it will help us pinpoint the issue quickly:
- 3–5 request IDs: {request_ids}
- Approx. timestamps + timezone: {time_window}
- SDK + version (or raw HTTP): {sdk_version}
- Whether retries are enabled (and retry policy): {retry_details}

**What you can do now**
{customer_actions}

We’ll send the next update **by {next_update_time}** (or sooner if we identify a mitigation).

Regards,
{support_signature}

**Notes for Support (internal):**
- Link SUP ticket to the Slack bridge if created.
- If threshold meets escalation triggers, page the appropriate on-call per escalation paths doc.

---

## Template 2 — Initial acknowledgement (non-incident / investigation)
**Channel:** Email + ticket comment

**Subject:** Re: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region}) — investigating

Hi {customer_name},

Acknowledged—thank you for the details. We’re investigating **{symptom}** for **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

To help us narrow this down, could you confirm:
1) {model} and any model version pinning (if applicable)
2) Your approximate request rate (RPS) and whether requests are batched
3) 2–3 request IDs from the last hour: {request_ids}

We’ll follow up with an update by **{next_update_time}**.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 3 — Mitigation / investigation update (no workaround yet)
**Channel:** Email; use for recurring cadence

**Subject options:**
- Update {#}: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region}) — {current_status}
- Update: Continuing investigation — next update by {next_update_time}

Hi {customer_name},

Update on **{symptom}** impacting **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

**Current status:** {current_status}

**What we’ve done since the last update**
- {action_1} (customer-safe wording)
- {action_2}

**What we’re seeing now**
- Impact level: {impact_level_now} (improving / stable degradation / worsening)
- Scope: {scope_now}

**Next steps**
- {next_step_1}
- {next_step_2}

Next update **by {next_update_time}**.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 4 — Mitigation update (mitigation applied / monitoring)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Update: Mitigation applied for {symptom} in {region} — monitoring

Hi {customer_name},

We’ve applied a mitigation for **{symptom}** affecting **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

**What changed (customer-safe):**
- {mitigation_applied}

**What you should see:**
- {expected_outcome} (e.g., reduced timeout rate; fewer stream resets)

**What we’re monitoring:**
- {monitoring_summary}

**If you’re still seeing issues:**
Please reply with 2–3 recent request IDs and timestamps after {mitigation_time}.

Next update **by {next_update_time}** (or sooner if we confirm full resolution).

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 5 — Workaround guidance (Known Issue / temporary mitigation)
**Channel:** Email; link to KB article when customer-safe

**Subject options:**
- Suggested workaround: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region})
- Temporary mitigation available: {symptom} — steps inside

Hi {customer_name},

Based on your symptoms and the request IDs shared, this aligns with a known issue we’re tracking: **{known_issue_name}**.

**Impact**
- {impact_summary}

**Recommended workaround (temporary)**
Please try the following:
{workaround_steps}

**What to expect**
- {expected_behavior}

**Reference**
- {kb_link}

**When to escalate back to us immediately**
Reply and reference {case_id} if:
- {escalation_condition_1} (e.g., error rate remains >X% after workaround)
- {escalation_condition_2} (e.g., streaming still disconnects within <N seconds)
- {escalation_condition_3} (e.g., issue expands to additional regions/models)

We’re continuing to work on a permanent fix; we’ll keep you updated on progress.

Regards,
{support_signature}

**Internal checklist (don’t include externally):**
- Confirm workaround validated by SRE/Eng for the customer’s tier.
- Mark as “temporary” and avoid implying the workaround is risk-free (call out tradeoffs if any).

---

## Template 6 — Rate limits / 429 clarification (quota vs headers)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Clarification on 429s and rate-limit headers ({region})

Hi {customer_name},

Thanks for the examples. A 429 can be returned for a few distinct reasons (rate limiting, quota enforcement, or request bursts that exceed configured limits). We want to make sure we’re addressing the correct one.

**Please share**
- 2–3 request IDs that returned 429
- The full set of response headers for those requests (redact tokens/credentials)
- Your approximate RPS and whether you use concurrent streaming connections

**In the meantime, try**
- Reduce concurrency for {endpoint} temporarily (especially streaming)
- Use exponential backoff with jitter on retries
- Ensure you’re not re-sending identical requests due to timeouts (idempotency)

If helpful, here is the troubleshooting reference: {kb_link}

Next update by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 7 — Private deployment comms (upgrade stuck / migrations)
**Channel:** Email; be explicit about safe diagnostics and rollback safety

**Subject:** Private deployment upgrade issue — diagnostics requested ({cluster_name})

Hi {customer_name},

Acknowledged. For Private upgrades that appear stuck at migrations, we typically need a small diagnostics bundle to confirm the failure mode and the safest recovery path.

**Please collect (safe + minimal) and share via the approved support channel**
- Installer logs from {path_or_command}
- Kubernetes events from the installer namespace
- Current installer version + target version
- Timestamp when the upgrade started

**Do not include** secrets, private keys, or application data. If any output contains sensitive values, please redact them before sharing.

**Immediate safety note**
Please avoid repeated re-runs of the upgrade until we confirm the state—this can complicate rollback in some scenarios.

We’ll review the artifacts and respond with next steps by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 8 — Resolution note (issue resolved)
**Channel:** Email + ticket resolution comment

**Subject options:**
- Resolved: {symptom} on {endpoint} in {region}
- Update: Issue resolved — {case_id}

Hi {customer_name},

Confirming this issue is now **resolved**.

**Summary**
- Symptom: {symptom}
- Impact window: {impact_start} to {impact_end} ({timezone})
- Affected: {endpoint} in {region} (and {model} if applicable)

**What we changed (customer-safe):**
- {mitigation_applied_or_fix}

**What you should do**
- {customer_actions_post_resolution} (often “no action needed”)

If you continue to see symptoms, please reply with new request IDs and we’ll re-open the investigation.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 9 — Resolution follow-up (offer RCA / post-incident review)
**Channel:** Email (enterprise/dedicated/private)

**Subject:** Follow-up: {case_id} resolution + next steps

Hi {customer_name},

Following up now that {case_id} is resolved. If helpful, we can provide either:
- A short written summary of contributing factors and mitigations (customer-safe), and/or
- A brief call to review what happened and prevention measures

**Proposed agenda (30 minutes)**
1) What you observed and business impact
2) Our findings at a high level (customer-safe)
3) Mitigations applied + prevention work in progress
4) Any configuration recommendations for your workload

If you’d like to schedule, please share a few times that work for your team.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 10 — Waiting on customer (keep SLA expectations clear)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Action needed: {case_id} — requesting details to proceed

Hi {customer_name},

To continue investigating {symptom}, we’ll need one of the following:
- 3–5 request IDs + timestamps from the last {N} hours, or
- A minimal reproduction (sanitized) and the response/error message

Once we have that, we’ll resume updates on the standard cadence for your tier. If you’re blocked and can’t provide request IDs, tell us what constraints you’re under and we’ll propose alternative options.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Quick “blessed phrasing” (copy/paste)
- “We’re actively investigating and will provide the next update by {time}.”
- “We’ve applied a mitigation and are monitoring service health.”
- “This is a temporary workaround; we’re working on a permanent fix.”
- “We don’t have a confirmed ETA yet; next update by {time}.”
- “Please share request IDs (preferred) rather than payload content.”

## Approval / escalation reminders (internal)
- If the customer asks for credits or alleges an SLA breach: follow the exec escalation protocol and tier SLA guidance. Do not confirm breach in writing without approvals.
- If comms includes root cause details, scope beyond the customer, or planned release dates: confirm with on-call/SRE and use customer-safe language.
- If you think we should post a status event: follow the status page approval flow and keep email/status copy consistent.

**Subject:** Clarification on 429s and rate-limit headers ({region})

Hi {customer_name},

Thanks for the examples. A 429 can be returned for a few distinct reasons (rate limiting, quota enforcement, or request bursts that exceed configured limits). We want to make sure we’re addressing the correct one.

**Please share**
- 2–3 request IDs that returned 429
- The full set of response headers for those requests (redact tokens/credentials)
- Your approximate RPS and whether you use concurrent streaming connections

**In the meantime, try**
- Reduce concurrency for {endpoint} temporarily (especially streaming)
- Use exponential backoff with jitter on retries
- Ensure you’re not re-sending identical requests due to timeouts (idempotency)

If helpful, here is the troubleshooting reference: {kb_link}

Next update by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 7 — Private deployment comms (upgrade stuck / migrations)
**Channel:** Email; be explicit about safe diagnostics and rollback safety

**Subject:** Private deployment upgrade issue — diagnostics requested ({cluster_name})

Hi {customer_name},

Acknowledged. For Private upgrades that appear stuck at migrations, we typically need a small diagnostics bundle to confirm the failure mode and the safest recovery path.

**Please collect (safe + minimal) and share via the approved support channel**
- Installer logs from {path_or_command}
- Kubernetes events from the installer namespace
- Current installer version + target version
- Timestamp when the upgrade started

**Do not include** secrets, private keys, or application data. If any output contains sensitive values, please redact them before sharing.

**Immediate safety note**
Please avoid repeated re-runs of the upgrade until we confirm the state—this can complicate rollback in some scenarios.

We’ll review the artifacts and respond with next steps by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 8 — Resolution note (issue resolved)
**Channel:** Email + ticket resolution comment

**Subject options:**
- Resolved: {symptom} on {endpoint} in {region}
- Update: Issue resolved — {case_id}

Hi {customer_name},

Confirming this issue is now **resolved**.

**Summary**
- Symptom: {symptom}
- Impact window: {impact_start} to {impact_end} ({timezone})
- Affected: {endpoint} in {region} (and {model} if applicable)

**What we changed (customer-safe):**
- {mitigation_applied_or_fix}

**What you should do**
- {customer_actions_post_resolution} (often “no action needed”)

If you continue to see symptoms, please reply with new request IDs and we’ll re-open the investigation.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 9 — Resolution follow-up (offer RCA / post-incident review)
**Channel:** Email (enterprise/dedicated/private)

**Subject:** Follow-up: {case_id} resolution + next steps

Hi {customer_name},

Following up now that {case_id} is resolved. If helpful, we can provide either:
- A short written summary of contributing factors and mitigations (customer-safe), and/or
- A brief call to review what happened and prevention measures

**Proposed agenda (30 minutes)**
1) What you observed and business impact
2) Our findings at a high level (customer-safe)
3) Mitigations applied + prevention work in progress
4) Any configuration recommendations for your workload

If you’d like to schedule, please share a few times that work for your team.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 10 — Waiting on customer (keep SLA expectations clear)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Action needed: {case_id} — requesting details to proceed

Hi {customer_name},

To continue investigating {symptom}, we’ll need one of the following:
- 3–5 request IDs + timestamps from the last {N} hours, or
- A minimal reproduction (sanitized) and the response/error message

Once we have that, we’ll resume updates on the standard cadence for your tier. If you’re blocked and can’t provide request IDs, tell us what constraints you’re under and we’ll propose alternative options.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Quick “blessed phrasing” (copy/paste)
- “We’re actively investigating and will provide the next update by {time}.”
- “We’ve applied a mitigation and are monitoring service health.”
- “This is a temporary workaround; we’re working on a permanent fix.”
- “We don’t have a confirmed ETA yet; next update by {time}.”
- “Please share request IDs (preferred) rather than payload content.”

## Approval / escalation reminders (internal)
- If the customer asks for credits or alleges an SLA breach: follow the exec escalation protocol and tier SLA guidance. Do not confirm breach in writing without approvals.
- If comms includes root cause details, scope beyond the customer, or planned release dates: confirm with on-call/SRE and use customer-safe language.
- If you think we should post a status event: follow the status page approval flow and keep email/status copy consistent.
text:
## Purpose
Use these templates for consistent, customer-safe communication during escalations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers. These templates are designed for:
- Initial acknowledgement
- Mitigation / investigation updates
- Workaround guidance (known issues)
- Resolution + follow-up (including next steps and optional RCA workflow)

These templates are written to reduce back-and-forth, align to Support SLAs, and avoid overpromising.

## Tone + content guidance (read before using)
**Be clear, calm, and specific.** Customers mostly want: confirmation we see the issue, what they should do now, what we’re doing, and when they’ll hear from us next.

### Do
- Acknowledge impact in the customer’s terms (errors, timeouts, latency, blocked workflow).
- Ask for the minimum viable diagnostics: request IDs, timestamps, region, model, SDK version, and whether streaming/non-streaming.
- Provide a concrete next update time (“Next update by 14:30 PT”).
- If a workaround exists, provide it as copy/paste steps and clearly label it as **temporary**.
- Use “We believe / We’re investigating / Our current hypothesis” when not confirmed.
- If relevant, state what is safe to share (e.g., “No action needed” vs “Please retry with…”).

### Don’t (what not to promise)
- **Do not promise ETAs** unless SRE/Eng has provided an externally shareable estimate and you have confirmed it is appropriate for the customer tier.
- Do not speculate on root cause (“kernel bug”, “bad deploy”, “GPU failures”) unless confirmed and approved for external sharing.
- Do not imply contractual outcomes (credits, SLA breach confirmation) in-line. Route to the credits process and exec escalation protocol when needed.
- Do not ask for sensitive data (full prompts, customer datasets, secrets). Prefer request IDs and sanitized logs. If logs are needed: provide explicit redaction instructions and use approved secure channels.
- Do not imply that pinned model versions or routing policies are “guaranteed” in all failure modes—use the documented policy language.

### Consistency rules
- **Use the same severity language across channels.** If you use P0/P1 internally, translate externally to “high impact / degraded / intermittent” unless the customer already uses P-levels.
- **Status page vs email:** Follow the Status Page + Comms Alignment doc for when something qualifies as a status event.
- **Update cadence:** Follow the customer’s tier SLA table. If you’re unsure, default to the stricter cadence for the tier and confirm with Support lead/on-call.

---

## Variables (replace placeholders)
Use these placeholders to keep comms consistent:
- {customer_name}
- {account_tier} (self_serve | dedicated | private | enterprise)
- {case_id} (SUP-####)
- {region} (us-east, eu-west, etc.)
- {model} (e.g., redwood/llama-3-70b-instruct)
- {endpoint} (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, streaming)
- {time_window} (e.g., “since 09:10–09:35 PT”)
- {request_ids} (a few representative IDs)
- {current_status} (investigating | identified | monitoring | mitigated | resolved)
- {next_update_time}
- {workaround_steps}
- {kb_link} (internal-to-customer-safe link)
- {mitigation_applied} (what we changed, in customer-safe language)
- {customer_actions}
- {support_signature} (name, role, timezone)

---

## Template 1 — Initial acknowledgement (incident / high impact)
**Channel:** Email (primary), optional ticket comment in customer portal

**Subject options (pick one):**
- Update: We’re investigating {symptom} impacting {endpoint} in {region}
- Acknowledged: {symptom} — {region} — Next update by {next_update_time}

**Body:**
Hi {customer_name},

Thanks for flagging this. We’re seeing and actively investigating **{symptom}** affecting **{endpoint}** in **{region}** {time_window}.

**What we know so far**
- Impact: {impact_summary} (e.g., increased timeouts / stream disconnects / elevated 5xx)
- Scope: {scope_summary} (e.g., intermittent; specific model; subset of requests)

**What we need from you (if available)**
If you can share any of the below, it will help us pinpoint the issue quickly:
- 3–5 request IDs: {request_ids}
- Approx. timestamps + timezone: {time_window}
- SDK + version (or raw HTTP): {sdk_version}
- Whether retries are enabled (and retry policy): {retry_details}

**What you can do now**
{customer_actions}

We’ll send the next update **by {next_update_time}** (or sooner if we identify a mitigation).

Regards,
{support_signature}

**Notes for Support (internal):**
- Link SUP ticket to the Slack bridge if created.
- If threshold meets escalation triggers, page the appropriate on-call per escalation paths doc.

---

## Template 2 — Initial acknowledgement (non-incident / investigation)
**Channel:** Email + ticket comment

**Subject:** Re: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region}) — investigating

Hi {customer_name},

Acknowledged—thank you for the details. We’re investigating **{symptom}** for **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

To help us narrow this down, could you confirm:
1) {model} and any model version pinning (if applicable)
2) Your approximate request rate (RPS) and whether requests are batched
3) 2–3 request IDs from the last hour: {request_ids}

We’ll follow up with an update by **{next_update_time}**.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 3 — Mitigation / investigation update (no workaround yet)
**Channel:** Email; use for recurring cadence

**Subject options:**
- Update {#}: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region}) — {current_status}
- Update: Continuing investigation — next update by {next_update_time}

Hi {customer_name},

Update on **{symptom}** impacting **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

**Current status:** {current_status}

**What we’ve done since the last update**
- {action_1} (customer-safe wording)
- {action_2}

**What we’re seeing now**
- Impact level: {impact_level_now} (improving / stable degradation / worsening)
- Scope: {scope_now}

**Next steps**
- {next_step_1}
- {next_step_2}

Next update **by {next_update_time}**.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 4 — Mitigation update (mitigation applied / monitoring)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Update: Mitigation applied for {symptom} in {region} — monitoring

Hi {customer_name},

We’ve applied a mitigation for **{symptom}** affecting **{endpoint}** in **{region}**.

**What changed (customer-safe):**
- {mitigation_applied}

**What you should see:**
- {expected_outcome} (e.g., reduced timeout rate; fewer stream resets)

**What we’re monitoring:**
- {monitoring_summary}

**If you’re still seeing issues:**
Please reply with 2–3 recent request IDs and timestamps after {mitigation_time}.

Next update **by {next_update_time}** (or sooner if we confirm full resolution).

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 5 — Workaround guidance (Known Issue / temporary mitigation)
**Channel:** Email; link to KB article when customer-safe

**Subject options:**
- Suggested workaround: {symptom} on {endpoint} ({region})
- Temporary mitigation available: {symptom} — steps inside

Hi {customer_name},

Based on your symptoms and the request IDs shared, this aligns with a known issue we’re tracking: **{known_issue_name}**.

**Impact**
- {impact_summary}

**Recommended workaround (temporary)**
Please try the following:
{workaround_steps}

**What to expect**
- {expected_behavior}

**Reference**
- {kb_link}

**When to escalate back to us immediately**
Reply and reference {case_id} if:
- {escalation_condition_1} (e.g., error rate remains >X% after workaround)
- {escalation_condition_2} (e.g., streaming still disconnects within <N seconds)
- {escalation_condition_3} (e.g., issue expands to additional regions/models)

We’re continuing to work on a permanent fix; we’ll keep you updated on progress.

Regards,
{support_signature}

**Internal checklist (don’t include externally):**
- Confirm workaround validated by SRE/Eng for the customer’s tier.
- Mark as “temporary” and avoid implying the workaround is risk-free (call out tradeoffs if any).

---

## Template 6 — Rate limits / 429 clarification (quota vs headers)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Clarification on 429s and rate-limit headers ({region})

Hi {customer_name},

Thanks for the examples. A 429 can be returned for a few distinct reasons (rate limiting, quota enforcement, or request bursts that exceed configured limits). We want to make sure we’re addressing the correct one.

**Please share**
- 2–3 request IDs that returned 429
- The full set of response headers for those requests (redact tokens/credentials)
- Your approximate RPS and whether you use concurrent streaming connections

**In the meantime, try**
- Reduce concurrency for {endpoint} temporarily (especially streaming)
- Use exponential backoff with jitter on retries
- Ensure you’re not re-sending identical requests due to timeouts (idempotency)

If helpful, here is the troubleshooting reference: {kb_link}

Next update by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 7 — Private deployment comms (upgrade stuck / migrations)
**Channel:** Email; be explicit about safe diagnostics and rollback safety

**Subject:** Private deployment upgrade issue — diagnostics requested ({cluster_name})

Hi {customer_name},

Acknowledged. For Private upgrades that appear stuck at migrations, we typically need a small diagnostics bundle to confirm the failure mode and the safest recovery path.

**Please collect (safe + minimal) and share via the approved support channel**
- Installer logs from {path_or_command}
- Kubernetes events from the installer namespace
- Current installer version + target version
- Timestamp when the upgrade started

**Do not include** secrets, private keys, or application data. If any output contains sensitive values, please redact them before sharing.

**Immediate safety note**
Please avoid repeated re-runs of the upgrade until we confirm the state—this can complicate rollback in some scenarios.

We’ll review the artifacts and respond with next steps by {next_update_time}.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 8 — Resolution note (issue resolved)
**Channel:** Email + ticket resolution comment

**Subject options:**
- Resolved: {symptom} on {endpoint} in {region}
- Update: Issue resolved — {case_id}

Hi {customer_name},

Confirming this issue is now **resolved**.

**Summary**
- Symptom: {symptom}
- Impact window: {impact_start} to {impact_end} ({timezone})
- Affected: {endpoint} in {region} (and {model} if applicable)

**What we changed (customer-safe):**
- {mitigation_applied_or_fix}

**What you should do**
- {customer_actions_post_resolution} (often “no action needed”)

If you continue to see symptoms, please reply with new request IDs and we’ll re-open the investigation.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 9 — Resolution follow-up (offer RCA / post-incident review)
**Channel:** Email (enterprise/dedicated/private)

**Subject:** Follow-up: {case_id} resolution + next steps

Hi {customer_name},

Following up now that {case_id} is resolved. If helpful, we can provide either:
- A short written summary of contributing factors and mitigations (customer-safe), and/or
- A brief call to review what happened and prevention measures

**Proposed agenda (30 minutes)**
1) What you observed and business impact
2) Our findings at a high level (customer-safe)
3) Mitigations applied + prevention work in progress
4) Any configuration recommendations for your workload

If you’d like to schedule, please share a few times that work for your team.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Template 10 — Waiting on customer (keep SLA expectations clear)
**Channel:** Email

**Subject:** Action needed: {case_id} — requesting details to proceed

Hi {customer_name},

To continue investigating {symptom}, we’ll need one of the following:
- 3–5 request IDs + timestamps from the last {N} hours, or
- A minimal reproduction (sanitized) and the response/error message

Once we have that, we’ll resume updates on the standard cadence for your tier. If you’re blocked and can’t provide request IDs, tell us what constraints you’re under and we’ll propose alternative options.

Regards,
{support_signature}

---

## Quick “blessed phrasing” (copy/paste)
- “We’re actively investigating and will provide the next update by {time}.”
- “We’ve applied a mitigation and are monitoring service health.”
- “This is a temporary workaround; we’re working on a permanent fix.”
- “We don’t have a confirmed ETA yet; next update by {time}.”
- “Please share request IDs (preferred) rather than payload content.”

## Approval / escalation reminders (internal)
- If the customer asks for credits or alleges an SLA breach: follow the exec escalation protocol and tier SLA guidance. Do not confirm breach in writing without approvals.
- If comms includes root cause details, scope beyond the customer, or planned release dates: confirm with on-call/SRE and use customer-safe language.

source.title: Technical Health Sentinel: Enterprise Post-Provision Monitoring & QBR Feed Playbook
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Summary
=======
This playbook defines the Technical Health Sentinel (THS): a prescriptive set of telemetry monitors, smoke checks, and QBR signal extractions that Customer Success (CS) uses for enterprise post-provision monitoring (first 90 days + quarterly health reviews). THS is intended to catch operational, performance, and quality regressions before they impact the customer and to provide a repeatable set of artifacts and dashboards for QBRs.

Goals
-----
- Detect regressions in availability, latency, correctness, and cost within 1 business hour of occurrence.
- Provide a structured triage path to resolve common classes of customer-impacting issues within SLOs.
- Standardize QBR inputs so CS and Product can make data-driven decisions.

When to use this playbook
------------------------
Use THS for:
- All enterprise customers during the 90-day post-provision window.
- Any Dedicated or Private deployment cutoff (when handing over to customer ops).
- Ongoing QBR preparation for customers with >$5k/month spend or latencies close to SLO.

Definitions and ownership
-------------------------
- THS Owner: Customer Success (Primary) — Maya Chen (authored).
- Technical Escalation: Eng-SRE (secondary) — Pager rotations in #oncall-redwood.
- Model/Quality Escalation: Applied ML & Evals team.
- Observability & Dashboards: Eng-Platform owns instrumentation.
- Data retention for QBR artifacts: Finance/Legal policy defines how long telemetries can be stored, default 18 months.

High-level workflow
-------------------
1. Post-provision (days 0–14): enable THS baseline monitors and run onboarding smoke tests.
2. Stabilization (days 15–90): monitor trends, perform weekly health reviews, fix configuration/backfills.
3. Handover / QBR (day 90 and quarterly): produce QBR packet with THS extracts and recommendations.

Automated sentinel monitors (recommended list)
----------------------------------------------
We recommend creating these monitors in the customer’s telemetry workspace (or forward via telemetry-gatekeeper):
- Availability check (heartbeat) — frequency: 30s; threshold: missing 3 samples -> P1 alarm.
- Request error rate — window: 5m; P99 target: < 0.5% (critical if > 1.5% for 5m).
- Tail latency (95th/99th) — window: 5m; thresholds: 95p > 350ms for hosted small models or > 700ms for long-context models -> P2.
- Token-level throughput / saturation — window: 1m; alert when GPU utilization > 88% and queue length grows > 12 requests.
- Model quality delta (eval set drift) — daily job comparing baseline eval set (seeded prompts) -> trigger when BLEU/ROUGE/metric drops > 4 points or human-quality score drops >10%.
- Cost per 1k tokens anomaly — daily; notify on >30% variance vs baseline 7-day median.
- KV cache hit rate — window: 15m; target: depends on workload, recommend > 60% for chat-heavy prefixes; alert if < 40% and latency increases.
- Telemetry forwarding lag — window: 5m; alert if event lag > 2m (indicates tracing / ingestion problem).

Alert severity mapping
----------------------
- P1 (customer-visible outage): availability fail, persistent error rate >5% for 15m, or model-serving API returning 5xx for >10% of requests. Assign to Eng-SRE tower.
- P2 (degradation): tail latency exceeded, cost spike, or quality regression in golden set; owner: Customer Success + Eng Platform.
- P3 (informational): telemetry lag, non-impacting config drift. Owner: Customer Success.

Pre-go-live health gates (checklist)
-----------------------------------
Run these before the customer is marked "go-live" in the billing system:  
1) Smoke traffic test — ramp to 2x expected peak for 10m with synthetic prompts (document payloads).
2) Model correctness gate — run golden prompt set (example: 100 prompts) and compare aggregated pass rate vs baseline (>92%).
3) Latency SLO verification — 95p latency under target for 30m sustained.
4) Telemetry sanity — traces, logs, and metrics flowing; sample trace includes request id, model version, node id.
5) Failover drill — force a single-node outage and validate automatic fallback to warm pool within 60s.
6) Billing & quotas check — verify token accounting matches expected.

Playbook: Common scenarios and triage steps
-----------------------------------------
Scenario A: Latency spike on customer route
1. Confirm alert and scope: check route_id, model_version, region.
2. Validate ingestion: Confirm no ingestion lag (telemetry lag monitor).
3. Check GPU & host metrics: utilization, memory errors, host load. Command examples:
   - kubectl -n runtime get pods -l route=<route_id>
   - query: sum(rate(gpu_utilization[1m])) by (instance)
4. Verify KV cache hit rate and batching behavior; if hit rate drops, identify cache-miss prefix regression.
5. If hosts saturated and autoscale not triggering, run: rwdctl scale --route <route_id> --delta 2 (Eng-Platform only).
6. If root cause is model variant change, rollback to previous pinned model_version via console or API.
7. Communicate to customer: status update within 15 minutes for P1/P2.

Scenario B: Quality regression detected by eval harness
1. Confirm regression on seeded prompts and a random sample of live requests (sanitized).
2. If reproducible, pin traffic to previous model variant immediately and open a ticket to Applied ML.
3. Run localized A/B (5% traffic) comparing outputs and log label for failed prompts.
4. Produce QBR-quality artifact: sampling of 30 representative failures + 5 human annotations.

Scenario C: Unexpected cost anomaly
1. Validate anomaly: cross-check billing pipeline and usage catalyst (7-day median).
2. Check client-side changes: prompt length, new embeddings/long-context usage, and new batch jobs.
3. If runaway jobs present, temporarily apply quota hold at route level and notify customer.
4. Reconfigure batching or suggest quantization profile (Eng-Platform assisted).

Escalation & communication templates
----------------------------------
Use the following cadence and channels:
- T+0–15m: Acknowledge to customer via designated channel (Slack/email) with immediate mitigation steps.
- T+15–60m: Cause analysis summary and expected ETA.
- T+60m+: Regular updates every 30–60m until resolved.
- Post-mortem: deliver within 3 business days for P1; 7 business days for P2.

Template: initial customer message (P1)
"We’ve detected an availability issue impacting your inference route <route_id>. Our mitigation team is engaged; we’ve rerouted traffic and are working on a fix. We will follow up within 15 minutes with an ETA. Contact: Maya Chen (CS), Eng-SRE oncall."

Observability artifacts & queries (examples)
-------------------------------------------
Prometheus/Datadog example queries you should include in the shared dashboard: 
- 95th latency: p95(api_request_duration_ms{route_id=\"<route_id>\"})
- Error rate over 5m: increase(error_count{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[5m]) / increase(request_count{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[5m])
- Token cost: sum(rate(token_emitted_total{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[1m])) * cost_per_token
- KV cache hit rate: sum(kv_cache_hits{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}) / sum(kv_cache_lookups{route_id=\"<route_id>\"})

Example alert JSON (ingestable by gatekeeper)
---------------------------------------------
{
  "name": "THS - High 95p latency - <route_id>",
  "expr": "p95(api_request_duration_ms{route_id=\\\"<route_id>\\\"}) > 350",
  "for": "5m",
  "severity": "P2",
  "owners": ["customer-success:maya.chen", "eng-platform:oncall"]
}

QBR feed and required metrics
----------------------------
Prepare the following artifacts for every QBR packet:
- Executive summary (1 page): availability, cost delta, net customer impact.
- Key metrics (30/90/365 day views):
  - Availability (percent uptime), target: 99.95% for hosted; 99.9% for Dedicated.
  - 95p / 99p latency by route and model.
  - Error rate (5m and 30d rolling).
  - Cost per 1k tokens and trend delta vs prior quarter.
  - Quality regressions count and triaged outcomes.
- Recent incidents and status of action items.
- Recommendations: batching, cache tuning, suggested model quantization, or Dedicated capacity increase.

Dashboard export checklist (what to attach)
- Time-range: include 30-, 90-, and 365-day graphs.
- Raw samples: 200 sample request/response pairs with PII removed (sanitization script included below).
- Alert history: print last 90 days of alerts with state changes.

Sanitization script (python snippet)
-----------------------------------
# This script removes obvious PII fields from JSON payloads. Run before attaching samples.
import json, re
PII_KEYS = ["email", "ssn", "phone", "credit_card"]
pat = re.compile(r'\\b\\w+@\\w+\\.\\w+\\b')

def sanitize(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        return {k: ("<REDACTED>" if k in PII_KEYS else sanitize(v)) for k,v in obj.items()}
    if isinstance(obj, list):
        return [sanitize(x) for x in obj]
    if isinstance(obj, str):
        return pat.sub('<REDACTED_EMAIL>', obj)
    return obj

Operational handoff & runbooks
------------------------------
- Day-90 handoff package must include: route configuration export, pinned model_version, telemetry dashboard links, escalation contacts, and artifact bundle (sanitized samples + eval results).
- Handoff checklist: owner signs off that remaining action items have owners and due dates.

Template: Day-90 Handover checklist (table)
- Route config export: yes/no
- Golden set baseline: attached
- Alerts enabled: list
- SLOs documented: yes/no and values
- Cost controls: quotas set (amount)

Known limitations & caveats
--------------------------
- Quality regression detection relies on seeded prompt sets — novel prompts from end-users may still cause silent regressions.
- Telemetry sampling rates may omit cold-start anomalies; we recommend 100% sampling for first 24 hours post-go-live.
- Private deployments with restricted networking may delay telemetry; adjust lag thresholds accordingly.

Appendix: Quick links & contacts
--------------------------------
- Oncall channel: #oncall-redwood (Slack)
- Incident creation: /inc create (internal CLI)
- Telemetry gatekeeper doc: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts
- CS leads: maya.chen@redwood.com, diego.alvarez@redwood.com

Change log
----------
- 2025-11-10: Initial draft (Maya Chen).
- 2026-01-22: Added KV cache guidance and cost anomaly playbook (review: Diego Alvarez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized QBR feed list and added templated alert JSON (published).

Acknowledgements
----------------
Thanks to Eng-SRE (Jordan Blake) and Eng-Platform (Priya Nair) for monitoring requirements and thresholds.

- All enterprise customers during the 90-day post-provision window.

- Ongoing QBR preparation for customers with >$5k/month spend or latencies close to SLO.

Definitions and ownership
-------------------------
- THS Owner: Customer Success (Primary) — Maya Chen (authored).
- Technical Escalation: Eng-SRE (secondary) — Pager rotations in #oncall-redwood.
- Model/Quality Escalation: Applied ML & Evals team.
- Observability & Dashboards: Eng-Platform owns instrumentation.
- Data retention for QBR artifacts: Finance/Legal policy defines how long telemetries can be stored, default 18 months.

High-level workflow
-------------------
1. Post-provision (days 0–14): enable THS baseline monitors and run onboarding smoke tests.
2. Stabilization (days 15–90): monitor trends, perform weekly health reviews, fix configuration/backfills.
3. Handover / QBR (day 90 and quarterly): produce QBR packet with THS extracts and recommendations.

Automated sentinel monitors (recommended list)
----------------------------------------------
We recommend creating these monitors in the customer’s telemetry workspace (or forward via telemetry-gatekeeper):
- Availability check (heartbeat) — frequency: 30s; threshold: missing 3 samples -> P1 alarm.
- Request error rate — window: 5m; P99 target: < 0.5% (critical if > 1.5% for 5m).
- Tail latency (95th/99th) — window: 5m; thresholds: 95p > 350ms for hosted small models or > 700ms for long-context models -> P2.
- Token-level throughput / saturation — window: 1m; alert when GPU utilization > 88% and queue length grows > 12 requests.
- Model quality delta (eval set drift) — daily job comparing baseline eval set (seeded prompts) -> trigger when BLEU/ROUGE/metric drops > 4 points or human-quality score drops >10%.
- Cost per 1k tokens anomaly — daily; notify on >30% variance vs baseline 7-day median.
- KV cache hit rate — window: 15m; target: depends on workload, recommend > 60% for chat-heavy prefixes; alert if < 40% and latency increases.
- Telemetry forwarding lag — window: 5m; alert if event lag > 2m (indicates tracing / ingestion problem).

Alert severity mapping
----------------------
- P1 (customer-visible outage): availability fail, persistent error rate >5% for 15m, or model-serving API returning 5xx for >10% of requests. Assign to Eng-SRE tower.
- P2 (degradation): tail latency exceeded, cost spike, or quality regression in golden set; owner: Customer Success + Eng Platform.
- P3 (informational): telemetry lag, non-impacting config drift. Owner: Customer Success.

Pre-go-live health gates (checklist)
-----------------------------------
Run these before the customer is marked "go-live" in the billing system:  
1) Smoke traffic test — ramp to 2x expected peak for 10m with synthetic prompts (document payloads).
2) Model correctness gate — run golden prompt set (example: 100 prompts) and compare aggregated pass rate vs baseline (>92%).
3) Latency SLO verification — 95p latency under target for 30m sustained.
4) Telemetry sanity — traces, logs, and metrics flowing; sample trace includes request id, model version, node id.
5) Failover drill — force a single-node outage and validate automatic fallback to warm pool within 60s.
6) Billing & quotas check — verify token accounting matches expected.

Playbook: Common scenarios and triage steps
-----------------------------------------
Scenario A: Latency spike on customer route
1. Confirm alert and scope: check route_id, model_version, region.
2. Validate ingestion: Confirm no ingestion lag (telemetry lag monitor).
3. Check GPU & host metrics: utilization, memory errors, host load. Command examples:
   - kubectl -n runtime get pods -l route=<route_id>
   - query: sum(rate(gpu_utilization[1m])) by (instance)
4. Verify KV cache hit rate and batching behavior; if hit rate drops, identify cache-miss prefix regression.
5. If hosts saturated and autoscale not triggering, run: rwdctl scale --route <route_id> --delta 2 (Eng-Platform only).
6. If root cause is model variant change, rollback to previous pinned model_version via console or API.
7. Communicate to customer: status update within 15 minutes for P1/P2.

Scenario B: Quality regression detected by eval harness
1. Confirm regression on seeded prompts and a random sample of live requests (sanitized).
2. If reproducible, pin traffic to previous model variant immediately and open a ticket to Applied ML.
3. Run localized A/B (5% traffic) comparing outputs and log label for failed prompts.
4. Produce QBR-quality artifact: sampling of 30 representative failures + 5 human annotations.

Scenario C: Unexpected cost anomaly
1. Validate anomaly: cross-check billing pipeline and usage catalyst (7-day median).
2. Check client-side changes: prompt length, new embeddings/long-context usage, and new batch jobs.
3. If runaway jobs present, temporarily apply quota hold at route level and notify customer.
4. Reconfigure batching or suggest quantization profile (Eng-Platform assisted).

Escalation & communication templates
----------------------------------
Use the following cadence and channels:
- T+0–15m: Acknowledge to customer via designated channel (Slack/email) with immediate mitigation steps.
- T+15–60m: Cause analysis summary and expected ETA.
- T+60m+: Regular updates every 30–60m until resolved.
- Post-mortem: deliver within 3 business days for P1; 7 business days for P2.

Template: initial customer message (P1)
"We’ve detected an availability issue impacting your inference route <route_id>. Our mitigation team is engaged; we’ve rerouted traffic and are working on a fix. We will follow up within 15 minutes with an ETA. Contact: Maya Chen (CS), Eng-SRE oncall."

Observability artifacts & queries (examples)
-------------------------------------------
Prometheus/Datadog example queries you should include in the shared dashboard: 
- 95th latency: p95(api_request_duration_ms{route_id=\"<route_id>\"})
- Error rate over 5m: increase(error_count{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[5m]) / increase(request_count{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[5m])
- Token cost: sum(rate(token_emitted_total{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}[1m])) * cost_per_token
- KV cache hit rate: sum(kv_cache_hits{route_id=\"<route_id>\"}) / sum(kv_cache_lookups{route_id=\"<route_id>\"})

Example alert JSON (ingestable by gatekeeper)
---------------------------------------------
{
  "name": "THS - High 95p latency - <route_id>",
  "expr": "p95(api_request_duration_ms{route_id=\\\"<route_id>\\\"}) > 350",
  "for": "5m",
  "severity": "P2",
  "owners": ["customer-success:maya.chen", "eng-platform:oncall"]
}

QBR feed and required metrics
----------------------------
Prepare the following artifacts for every QBR packet:
- Executive summary (1 page): availability, cost delta, net customer impact.
- Key metrics (30/90/365 day views):
  - Availability (percent uptime), target: 99.95% for hosted; 99.9% for Dedicated.
  - 95p / 99p latency by route and model.
  - Error rate (5m and 30d rolling).
  - Cost per 1k tokens and trend delta vs prior quarter.
  - Quality regressions count and triaged outcomes.
- Recent incidents and status of action items.
- Recommendations: batching, cache tuning, suggested model quantization, or Dedicated capacity increase.

Dashboard export checklist (what to attach)
- Time-range: include 30-, 90-, and 365-day graphs.
- Raw samples: 200 sample request/response pairs with PII removed (sanitization script included below).
- Alert history: print last 90 days of alerts with state changes.

Sanitization script (python snippet)
-----------------------------------
# This script removes obvious PII fields from JSON payloads. Run before attaching samples.
import json, re
PII_KEYS = ["email", "ssn", "phone", "credit_card"]
pat = re.compile(r'\\b\\w+@\\w+\\.\\w+\\b')

def sanitize(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        return {k: ("<REDACTED>" if k in PII_KEYS else sanitize(v)) for k,v in obj.items()}
    if isinstance(obj, list):
        return [sanitize(x) for x in obj]
    if isinstance(obj, str):
        return pat.sub('<REDACTED_EMAIL>', obj)
    return obj

Operational handoff & runbooks
------------------------------
- Day-90 handoff package must include: route configuration export, pinned model_version, telemetry dashboard links, escalation contacts, and artifact bundle (sanitized samples + eval results).
- Handoff checklist: owner signs off that remaining action items have owners and due dates.

Template: Day-90 Handover checklist (table)
- Route config export: yes/no
- Golden set baseline: attached
- Alerts enabled: list
- SLOs documented: yes/no and values
- Cost controls: quotas set (amount)

Known limitations & caveats
--------------------------
- Quality regression detection relies on seeded prompt sets — novel prompts from end-users may still cause silent regressions.
- Telemetry sampling rates may omit cold-start anomalies; we recommend 100% sampling for first 24 hours post-go-live.
- Private deployments with restricted networking may delay telemetry; adjust lag thresholds accordingly.

Appendix: Quick links & contacts
--------------------------------
- Oncall channel: #oncall-redwood (Slack)
- Incident creation: /inc create (internal CLI)
- Telemetry gatekeeper doc: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts
- CS leads: maya.chen@redwood.com, diego.alvarez@redwood.com

Change log
----------
- 2025-11-10: Initial draft (Maya Chen).
- 2026-01-22: Added KV cache guidance and cost anomaly playbook (review: Diego Alvarez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized QBR feed list and added templated alert JSON (published).

Acknowledgements
----------------
Thanks to Eng-SRE (Jordan Blake) and Eng-Platform (Priya Nair) for monitoring requirements and thresholds.
