**Quick checks** (what to ask for)
**Workaround / mitigation** (numbered)
**Escalate when** (explicit triggers)
**Notes / long-term fix**
**Last updated / owner**

### B. Required escalation context (minimum viable page)
- Customer + tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- Region + model + endpoint
- Request IDs (3–5 examples)
- Start time + current status
- Error rates / symptoms summary
- Recent changes (SDK upgrade, deploy, config)
- What has already been attempted (and result)
text:
## Overview
This sprint is a focused Customer Success & Support initiative to reduce repeat tickets and speed customer mitigation by publishing validated, copy/paste-ready workaround articles for our most common, highest-impact issues across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

This plan defines:
- Selection criteria for sprint issues (what qualifies and what does not).
- Owners/reviewers and the publication workflow.
- A publication checklist to ensure articles are safe, accurate, and consistent with Support escalation workflows.
- Target metrics and how we will measure impact.

**Sprint window:** 2 weeks (Planning week + Execution week) with a follow-up week for adoption/cleanup.

---

## Goals
1. **Reduce time-to-triage** for high-frequency issues by making troubleshooting steps and required diagnostics consistent.
2. **Reduce time-to-mitigation** for customer-impacting incidents via validated temporary workarounds (and clear “when to escalate” guidance).
3. **Decrease repeat tickets** by improving self-serve and Support-agent deflection (KB and macros linking).
4. **Standardize language** across Support (Jira priorities) and Engineering (incident severity and on-call thresholds).
5. **Ensure discoverability**: known-issues index as the landing page, consistently linked from Support workflows and relevant SDK docs.

## Non-goals (explicitly out of scope)
- Broad product roadmap or feature work not tied to top escalations.
- Long-form training programs (we will ship quick reference + templates only).
- Publishing customer-sensitive details (exact account names, unique payloads, internal-only dashboards) into broadly visible docs.

---

## Scope: What we will publish
Each known-issue article must include:
- **Symptoms** (what the customer sees) and quick confirmation checks.
- **Impact** (expected degradation, functional breakage, affected endpoints).
- **Affected scope**: tiers (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), models, regions, SDK versions.
- **Workarounds / mitigations** (validated by SRE/Engineering; clearly marked temporary when applicable).
- **Support steps** (what to ask for: request IDs, region, model, SDK version; safe log collection).
- **When to escalate**: explicit triggers mapped to the severity matrix.
- **Last updated** and ownership (who maintains it).

---

## Selection criteria (how issues are chosen)
Issues are selected using a weighted score across **impact**, **frequency**, and **workaround readiness**.

### Must-have criteria
- Appears in the top recurring Support themes (ticket volume, escalations, or time sink).
- Has a **repeatable symptom pattern** (not a one-off customer misconfiguration only).
- Has a **validated workaround or mitigation** that Support can safely recommend.
- Can be documented without exposing restricted customer details.

### Strongly preferred
- Impacts multiple customers or a material enterprise account.
- Frequently causes bridge/escalation churn due to missing context or inconsistent diagnostics.
- Has an Engineering follow-up ticket to permanently fix or improve observability/errors.

### Exclusions
- Issues requiring bespoke customer configuration that does not generalize.
- Items where “workaround” is effectively “we need Engineering to investigate from scratch.”
- Purely contractual/commercial topics (handled by CS/Sales enablement, not KB).

### Severity threshold guidance
Prioritize issues that commonly present as **Support P0/P1** and/or routinely trigger **Sev0/Sev1** escalation.

---

## Candidate shortlist for 2026-02 (initial set)
This sprint’s initial known-issues set (subject to final validation during planning):
1. **Streaming timeouts / connection resets / truncated streams** (Hosted, multi-region)
2. **429 quota mismatch + rate-limit header confusion** (Hosted; SDK upgrade correlation)
3. **Dedicated autoscaling oscillation causing latency spikes** (Dedicated)
4. **Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations** (Private)
5. **Audit log delivery latency (lag > expected)** (Enterprise/Private control plane)

Note: Additional issues may be added only if we can complete validation + publication without jeopardizing the above.

---

## Owners and review model
### RACI (high-level)
| Workstream | Responsible (R) | Accountable (A) | Consulted (C) | Informed (I) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sprint execution + coordination | Dev Patel | Jasmine Liu | Rachel Kim, Talia Benson | Support, CS |
| Article drafting (Hosted) | Wei Chen | Elliot Price | Owen Phillips, SRE on-call | Support |
| Article drafting (Dedicated/Private) | Kira Thompson | Elliot Price | Platform owners, SRE | Support, CS |
| Workaround validation | SRE partner (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes) | Priya Natarajan (Eng sponsor) | Platform/Runtime owners | Support |
| Publication + IA (index/links) | Elliot Price | Dev Patel | DevEx/Docs, Design | Company |
| Macro/snippet alignment | Priya Shah | Dev Patel | Owen Phillips | Support |

### Required reviewers by category
- **Streaming / runtime / gateway behaviors:** SRE + Runtime/Serving representative
- **Dedicated autoscaling:** SRE + Capacity/Dedicated owner
- **Private installer:** Private Deployments/Platform + SRE
- **Audit logs / compliance-adjacent:** Platform Control Plane + Security/Compliance guidance (Naomi Feldman as needed)

---

## Timeline and milestones
### Week 0 (Prep: 1–2 days)
- Confirm shortlist from Support trends and current escalations.
- Assign doc owners + reviewers.
- Create tracking rows in the sprint spreadsheet (internal working doc).

### Week 1 (Planning week)
**Deliverables by end of week:**
- Final shortlist and score justification.
- Draft outlines for each article (headings, symptoms, workaround steps, escalation triggers).
- Confirm where each article will live and the tags to apply in the index.

**Key meetings:**
- 30 min kickoff (Support + Docs + SRE + Platform)
- 30 min midweek validation sync (confirm workaround safety)

### Week 2 (Execution week)
**Deliverables by end of week:**
- All articles published with consistent formatting and “when to escalate” sections.
- Known-issues index updated with tags and recency.
- Support macros updated to link to relevant known-issue articles.
- Cross-links from SDK troubleshooting pages where applicable.

### Week 3 (Adoption + cleanup)
**Deliverables:**
- Jira automation tweaks and field validation updates (if applicable).
- Backlog triage: convert repeat tickets into known-issue references.
- Metrics check-in: early deflection + time-to-mitigation signals.
- Ownership handoff: who maintains each article and update cadence.

---

## Publication checklist (definition of done)
Every article must pass the checklist below before being marked **Published**.

### Content quality
- [ ] Clear title that matches customer language (“timeouts”, “429”, “upgrade stuck”).
- [ ] “Symptoms” section includes 2–5 concrete examples (errors, behaviors) without customer identifiers.
- [ ] “Quick checks” include what Support should ask for (region, model, SDK version, request IDs).
- [ ] “Workaround” steps are numbered, copy/paste-ready, and include safe defaults.
- [ ] “When to escalate” includes thresholds/conditions and references the severity matrix.
- [ ] “Resolution / long-term fix” notes whether a permanent fix is planned and where to track it (internal ticket reference is okay; avoid sensitive details).

### Accuracy and validation
- [ ] Workaround validated by SRE/Engineering reviewer (explicit sign-off in comments or Jira).
- [ ] Any config change includes rollback guidance.
- [ ] Any client retry guidance includes idempotency and rate-limit considerations.

### Safety, confidentiality, and compliance
- [ ] No customer names, contract terms, or account-specific details.
- [ ] No internal-only dashboards/URLs unless the page is restricted (default: avoid).
- [ ] Log collection instructions are customer-safe (no secrets; explain redaction expectations).

### Discoverability and workflow integration
- [ ] Article linked from the **Known Issues Index** with appropriate tags (tier/region/model).
- [ ] Support macro/snippet updated to reference the article.
- [ ] If applicable, SDK troubleshooting docs cross-link to the article.
- [ ] Jira triage guidance updated (auto-suggest known issues where feasible).

---

## Metrics (targets + measurement plan)
We will track the following for the 2 weeks prior (baseline) and 2–4 weeks after publication.

### Primary success metrics
1. **Ticket deflection (repeat-ticket reduction)**
   - Definition: reduction in new tickets that match the known-issue categories (by tags/keywords + Support classification).
   - Target: **15–25% reduction** in weekly repeat tickets for the published issue set.

2. **Time-to-mitigation (TTM) for escalations in scope**
   - Definition: time from Support intake to first validated mitigation/workaround communicated to customer.
   - Target: **30% reduction** for P0/P1 tickets matching the published issue set.

3. **Time-to-triage (TTT)**
   - Definition: time from ticket creation to “root cause category identified” (or confirmed known-issue match) and next-step plan.
   - Target: **25% reduction** for tickets in scope.

### Secondary metrics
- **Fewer back-and-forth loops**: median number of Support replies before “actionable next step” is provided.
- **Macro adoption**: % of in-scope tickets where the updated macro is used.
- **Escalation quality**: % of pages/bridges that include required context (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys).

### Data sources
- Jira: ticket timestamps, priorities, labels, resolution notes.
- Slack: bridge creation timestamps and context completeness (spot-check).
- Docs analytics (if available): pageviews for known-issues index + articles.
- SRE incident tooling: references to mitigations and related SUP tickets.

---

## Workflow: How Support should use known-issues content
1. During intake, check the **Known Issues Index** for matching symptoms/tags.
2. If a match is found, apply the linked macro and request the exact diagnostics listed.
3. Provide the workaround steps exactly as written (avoid ad-hoc edits that change meaning).
4. If the issue meets “when to escalate” criteria, open/attach the escalation bridge and link the SUP ticket.
5. Add a resolution note referencing the known-issue article and whether the workaround resolved the customer impact.

---

## Risks and mitigations
- **Risk: Publishing incorrect or unsafe mitigations.**
  - Mitigation: required SRE/Eng sign-off + rollback guidance for any config change.

- **Risk: Customer-sensitive details leak into broadly visible docs.**
  - Mitigation: explicit confidentiality checklist; Docs review focused on redaction.

- **Risk: Articles become stale quickly.**
  - Mitigation: ownership field per article + quarterly review reminder (or on fix release).

- **Risk: Low discoverability / inconsistent linking.**
  - Mitigation: index as canonical landing page; macros and triage checklist updated; cross-links from SDK docs.

---

## Communication plan
- Internal announcement in #announcements once the index + first batch of articles are published.
- 10-minute Support standup walkthrough: where to find the index, what changed in macros, and escalation trigger reminders.
- Post-sprint update to CS leadership: metrics baseline + early impact and remaining gaps.

---

## Appendices
### A. Standard article template (copy/paste)
**Summary**
**Symptoms**
**Impact**
**Affected scope** (tier/region/model/SDK)
**Quick checks** (what to ask for)
**Workaround / mitigation** (numbered)
**Escalate when** (explicit triggers)
**Notes / long-term fix**
**Last updated / owner**

### B. Required escalation context (minimum viable page)
- Customer + tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- Region + model + endpoint
- Request IDs (3–5 examples)
- Start time + current status
- Error rates / symptoms summary
- Recent changes (SDK upgrade, deploy, config)
- What has already been attempted (and result)

source.title: Enterprise priority: 429 / quota / overload behavior (Support guide)
source.author: Dev Patel
source.content:
## Purpose
This page is a Support/CS reference for explaining **429** responses after the **Hot-route capacity protection** rollout. It covers:
- What customers may see (payload + headers)
- How to distinguish **quota** vs **rate-limit** vs **overload shedding/admission control**
- Recommended customer-side retry/backoff behavior
- When and how to escalate (and what data to collect)

This doc is written for Support and TAMs. For implementation details, see the Platform design/ADR pages linked in Related pages.

---

## What changed (customer-visible)
Beginning with the enterprise priority rollout, Redwood enforces **tier-aware capacity protection** on a set of “protected routes” (commonly: `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`).

Customers may observe:
- **More predictable 429s** for best-effort/self-serve traffic during regional pressure.
- **Enterprise traffic preserved** longer (priority routing + per-tier budgets), but enterprise requests can still receive 429s when:
  - the account’s own limits are reached, or
  - the route is in sustained overload and the enterprise budget is exhausted.
- **New reason codes** and additional headers to explain why the request was rejected.

Important framing for customers:
- 429 is **not** a “generic outage” signal. It is a deliberate backpressure mechanism to protect overall fleet health and enterprise SLOs.
- 429 responses are **safe to retry** when the reason indicates transient pressure, using the guidance below.

---

## 429 response format (what Support should look for)

### Common JSON fields (all 429s)
Most clients will receive an error payload shaped like:
- `error.type`: `rate_limit_exceeded` or `quota_exceeded` or `overloaded`
- `error.code`: a stable machine-readable reason code (see table below)
- `error.message`: human-readable summary
- `error.request_id`: request identifier (also returned in response headers)

If a customer claims the payload is missing or inconsistent, ask for:
- raw response body
- full response headers
- SDK version / client library

### Standard headers (may vary by route)
Support should request and record these headers when present:
- `x-redwood-request-id`: request ID used for internal tracing
- `retry-after`: seconds to wait before retry (only set for some transient reasons)
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`: millisecond retry hint (preferred when present)
- `x-redwood-limit-scope`: `account` | `api_key` | `route` (where enforcement applies)
- `x-redwood-limit-policy`: policy identifier (internal-friendly string; customer can share as opaque)
- `x-redwood-429-reason`: short reason token (maps to reason codes below)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-limit`: numeric limit (requests per window) when applicable
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-remaining`: remaining requests in current window (when applicable)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-reset`: epoch time or seconds-until-reset (when applicable)

Notes:
- Some enterprise customers will only see a subset of limit headers depending on contract configuration and route group settings.
- Absence of headers is not automatically a bug; it can indicate a rejection occurred upstream of quota evaluation (e.g., admission control).

---

## Reason codes (how to interpret 429s)
Use the following table to classify the issue and advise the customer.

### A) Quota- and rate-limit-driven 429s (customer-specific)
These are expected when a customer exceeds their configured limits.

| Category | error.type | x-redwood-429-reason (typical) | Meaning | Customer guidance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hard quota exceeded | quota_exceeded | quota_window_exhausted | Customer has consumed their quota for the current window. | Do not retry immediately. Wait until reset. Consider limit increase request. |
| Rate limit exceeded | rate_limit_exceeded | rate_limit_account | Customer exceeded account-level request rate. | Retry with exponential backoff + jitter. Reduce concurrency / smooth bursts. |
| Route-group rate limit | rate_limit_exceeded | rate_limit_route_group | Customer exceeded a per-route or route-group cap. | Same as above; ask them to spread load or request a higher burst allowance. |
| Token / compute guardrail | rate_limit_exceeded | token_budget_exceeded | Customer exceeded a token/compute budget (if enabled for contract). | Reduce max_tokens / request size; smooth traffic; discuss contract sizing. |

### B) Overload protection / admission control 429s (fleet pressure)
These indicate transient capacity protection behavior.

| Category | error.type | x-redwood-429-reason (typical) | Meaning | Customer guidance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Admission control (best-effort shed) | overloaded | shed_best_effort | Fleet/route is under pressure; best-effort traffic is being throttled first. | Retry with exponential backoff + jitter. Prefer queueing client-side. |
| Admission control (enterprise budget exhausted) | overloaded | shed_enterprise_budget | Protected route is overloaded and enterprise budget is fully utilized. | Retry using Retry-After if present; reduce concurrency; contact Support if sustained. |
| Regional capacity constraint | overloaded | region_capacity | Selected region is capacity constrained; request rejected to prevent tail latency cascade. | Retry with backoff; if multi-region is enabled, allow client failover. |
| Policy evaluation deny | overloaded | policy_deny_overload | Policy engine denied admission due to active overload rule (e.g., queue depth trigger). | Treat as transient. Retry with backoff. Provide request IDs for investigation if frequent. |

Support positioning:
- For enterprise accounts: “This is protective backpressure to preserve latency/availability for protected routes. It can occur during unusual bursts or regional pressure.”
- For self-serve/best-effort: “During peak pressure, the platform prioritizes higher-SLA traffic; your requests may be throttled earlier.”

---

## Recommended customer retry & backoff guidance
Provide the following general guidance (tailor to the customer’s stack):

### When to retry
- Retry 429s when:
  - `error.type = overloaded` (most cases)
  - `error.type = rate_limit_exceeded`
- Do NOT aggressively retry when:
  - `error.type = quota_exceeded` and the response includes a reset hint (wait until reset)

### How to retry (recommended algorithm)
- Use **exponential backoff with full jitter**.
- Start with 250–500ms base delay.
- Cap max delay at 10–30s.
- Add client-side request queueing to avoid stampedes.
- Respect `retry-after` and/or `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` when present (prefer the ms header).

Suggested pseudocode guidance to share (language-agnostic):
1) if `retry-after` present → sleep for that duration (+ small jitter) then retry
2) else backoff = random(0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt))
3) stop after a reasonable max attempts (typically 5–8) and surface a retriable error to the application

### Concurrency guidance
Customers with high parallelism should:
- Implement a per-route concurrency limiter (token bucket / semaphore)
- Smooth bursty workloads (batching where possible)
- Avoid synchronized retries across many workers (jitter)

### Idempotency guidance
- For non-streaming requests, retries are generally safe.
- For streaming requests, see “Streaming semantics” below.

---

## Streaming semantics during shedding (common ticket driver)
Some customers report “inconsistent termination” on streaming responses during load shedding.

Support guidance:
- If a request is rejected **before** streaming begins, they should receive a normal 429 with reason + headers.
- If a request is accepted and then terminated due to downstream pressure, behavior depends on the transport:
  - Customers should treat early stream termination as retriable if they did not receive a completed response.
  - Advise customers to log the `x-redwood-request-id` and last event received.

Escalate if:
- customers see a successful HTTP status but the stream ends without an explicit termination signal and without consistent client-visible error handling, or
- the customer provides multiple request IDs showing premature termination correlated with new overload reasons.

---

## What Support should ask for (intake checklist)
Request the following in the initial reply to reduce ping-pong:
1) Affected route(s): e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`
2) Region/endpoint base URL (if multi-region)
3) Time window (with timezone) and approximate request rate
4) Sample `x-redwood-request-id` values (3–10 IDs)
5) Full 429 response headers + body (raw)
6) Whether they are using streaming
7) Their current retry policy (backoff, max attempts, concurrency)
8) Whether it’s isolated to a single API key or all keys in the account

Internal-only (if available to TAM/CS): contract tier (Hosted API vs Dedicated), priority class, and any approved exceptions.

---

## Escalation criteria (when to page/loop in Engineering)
Escalate to SRE/Platform when ANY of the following are true:

### Enterprise impact thresholds
- Enterprise customer reports sustained 429s on a protected route for **>10 minutes** AND:
  - reason indicates overload protection (`overloaded/*`), OR
  - they claim traffic is within contracted limits but still seeing 429s
- Enterprise customer reports p95/p99 latency degradation plus 429 spikes (possible mis-tuned thresholds)

### Suspected misconfiguration
- `x-redwood-limit-policy` changes unexpectedly after rollout / config migration
- A single tenant is denied while peers are not, and headers indicate route-group limits that don’t match the contract

### Possible platform bug
- Missing/incorrect reason codes across multiple requests
- `retry-after` values that are clearly wrong (e.g., negative, extremely large, inconsistent with window)
- Streaming termination inconsistencies correlated with overload events

### Security / access concerns
- Customer requests details about other tenants, fleet state, or priority policy internals beyond what we disclose. Route via TAM/CS + Security as needed.

---

## Escalation process (internal)
1) Create/attach a ticket in the Support queue and tag: `overload-protection` + `enterprise`.
2) Post in `#incidents` (if active event suspected) or `#eng-sre` with:
   - customer/account
   - affected routes/regions
   - timeframe
   - reason codes + sample request IDs
   - whether Retry-After was present and followed
3) Link the relevant dashboards (SRE on-call will pull). If you have access, include:
   - enterprise route SLO dashboard
   - overload protection shed-rate panel
   - admission control trigger annotations

If immediate paging is warranted, follow the runbook: “Overload protection and admission control” (Related pages).

---

## Approved external explanation (copy/paste)
Use this when responding to customers (edit for their tier/route):

“HTTP 429 indicates the request was throttled or temporarily rejected to protect latency and availability. For protected enterprise routes, Redwood prioritizes higher-SLA traffic under peak load to prevent fleet-wide degradation. Please implement exponential backoff with jitter and respect any Retry-After headers. If you share a few request IDs and the full response headers/body, we can confirm whether the 429s are due to your configured limits (quota/rate) or transient overload protection.”

---

## FAQ (Support)

**Q: Is a 429 an outage?**
A: Not necessarily. For many overload cases, it’s intentional backpressure to avoid tail latency/5xx cascading failures.

**Q: Can enterprise customers still get 429s?**
A: Yes—if they exceed their contracted limits, or in rare cases when a protected route’s enterprise budget is fully consumed during sustained overload.

**Q: Should customers increase retry aggressiveness to “push through”?**
A: No. Aggressive retries worsen overload. Encourage backoff + jitter + client-side queueing.

**Q: Customer says they stayed under their “RPM” but still got 429.**
A: Ask for headers and request IDs. They may be hitting a route-group limit, a token/compute guardrail, or experiencing transient admission control without Retry-After being honored.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-06: Added streaming termination guidance + escalation triggers for inconsistent SSE termination.
- 2026-01-23: Updated reason-code table to include `shed_enterprise_budget` and `region_capacity`.
- 2026-01-09: Initial publication for Support enablement ahead of GA rollout.

- Cap max delay at 10–30s.
- Add client-side request queueing to avoid stampedes.
- Respect `retry-after` and/or `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` when present (prefer the ms header).

Suggested pseudocode guidance to share (language-agnostic):
1) if `retry-after` present → sleep for that duration (+ small jitter) then retry
2) else backoff = random(0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt))
3) stop after a reasonable max attempts (typically 5–8) and surface a retriable error to the application

### Concurrency guidance
Customers with high parallelism should:
- Implement a per-route concurrency limiter (token bucket / semaphore)
- Smooth bursty workloads (batching where possible)
- Avoid synchronized retries across many workers (jitter)

### Idempotency guidance
- For non-streaming requests, retries are generally safe.
- For streaming requests, see “Streaming semantics” below.

---

## Streaming semantics during shedding (common ticket driver)
Some customers report “inconsistent termination” on streaming responses during load shedding.

Support guidance:
- If a request is rejected **before** streaming begins, they should receive a normal 429 with reason + headers.
- If a request is accepted and then terminated due to downstream pressure, behavior depends on the transport:
  - Customers should treat early stream termination as retriable if they did not receive a completed response.
  - Advise customers to log the `x-redwood-request-id` and last event received.

Escalate if:
- customers see a successful HTTP status but the stream ends without an explicit termination signal and without consistent client-visible error handling, or
- the customer provides multiple request IDs showing premature termination correlated with new overload reasons.

---

## What Support should ask for (intake checklist)
Request the following in the initial reply to reduce ping-pong:
1) Affected route(s): e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`
2) Region/endpoint base URL (if multi-region)
3) Time window (with timezone) and approximate request rate
4) Sample `x-redwood-request-id` values (3–10 IDs)
5) Full 429 response headers + body (raw)
6) Whether they are using streaming
7) Their current retry policy (backoff, max attempts, concurrency)
8) Whether it’s isolated to a single API key or all keys in the account

Internal-only (if available to TAM/CS): contract tier (Hosted API vs Dedicated), priority class, and any approved exceptions.

---

## Escalation criteria (when to page/loop in Engineering)
Escalate to SRE/Platform when ANY of the following are true:

### Enterprise impact thresholds
- Enterprise customer reports sustained 429s on a protected route for **>10 minutes** AND:
  - reason indicates overload protection (`overloaded/*`), OR
  - they claim traffic is within contracted limits but still seeing 429s
- Enterprise customer reports p95/p99 latency degradation plus 429 spikes (possible mis-tuned thresholds)

### Suspected misconfiguration
- `x-redwood-limit-policy` changes unexpectedly after rollout / config migration
- A single tenant is denied while peers are not, and headers indicate route-group limits that don’t match the contract

### Possible platform bug
- Missing/incorrect reason codes across multiple requests
- `retry-after` values that are clearly wrong (e.g., negative, extremely large, inconsistent with window)
- Streaming termination inconsistencies correlated with overload events

### Security / access concerns
- Customer requests details about other tenants, fleet state, or priority policy internals beyond what we disclose. Route via TAM/CS + Security as needed.

---

## Escalation process (internal)
1) Create/attach a ticket in the Support queue and tag: `overload-protection` + `enterprise`.
2) Post in `#incidents` (if active event suspected) or `#eng-sre` with:
   - customer/account
   - affected routes/regions
   - timeframe
   - reason codes + sample request IDs
   - whether Retry-After was present and followed
3) Link the relevant dashboards (SRE on-call will pull). If you have access, include:
   - enterprise route SLO dashboard
   - overload protection shed-rate panel
   - admission control trigger annotations

If immediate paging is warranted, follow the runbook: “Overload protection and admission control” (Related pages).

---

## Approved external explanation (copy/paste)
Use this when responding to customers (edit for their tier/route):

“HTTP 429 indicates the request was throttled or temporarily rejected to protect latency and availability. For protected enterprise routes, Redwood prioritizes higher-SLA traffic under peak load to prevent fleet-wide degradation. Please implement exponential backoff with jitter and respect any Retry-After headers. If you share a few request IDs and the full response headers/body, we can confirm whether the 429s are due to your configured limits (quota/rate) or transient overload protection.”

---

## FAQ (Support)

**Q: Is a 429 an outage?**
A: Not necessarily. For many overload cases, it’s intentional backpressure to avoid tail latency/5xx cascading failures.

**Q: Can enterprise customers still get 429s?**
A: Yes—if they exceed their contracted limits, or in rare cases when a protected route’s enterprise budget is fully consumed during sustained overload.

**Q: Should customers increase retry aggressiveness to “push through”?**
A: No. Aggressive retries worsen overload. Encourage backoff + jitter + client-side queueing.

**Q: Customer says they stayed under their “RPM” but still got 429.**
A: Ask for headers and request IDs. They may be hitting a route-group limit, a token/compute guardrail, or experiencing transient admission control without Retry-After being honored.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-06: Added streaming termination guidance + escalation triggers for inconsistent SSE termination.
- 2026-01-23: Updated reason-code table to include `shed_enterprise_budget` and `region_capacity`.
- 2026-01-09: Initial publication for Support enablement ahead of GA rollout.
text:
## Purpose
This page is a Support/CS reference for explaining **429** responses after the **Hot-route capacity protection** rollout. It covers:
- What customers may see (payload + headers)
- How to distinguish **quota** vs **rate-limit** vs **overload shedding/admission control**
- Recommended customer-side retry/backoff behavior
- When and how to escalate (and what data to collect)

This doc is written for Support and TAMs. For implementation details, see the Platform design/ADR pages linked in Related pages.

---

## What changed (customer-visible)
Beginning with the enterprise priority rollout, Redwood enforces **tier-aware capacity protection** on a set of “protected routes” (commonly: `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`).

Customers may observe:
- **More predictable 429s** for best-effort/self-serve traffic during regional pressure.
- **Enterprise traffic preserved** longer (priority routing + per-tier budgets), but enterprise requests can still receive 429s when:
  - the account’s own limits are reached, or
  - the route is in sustained overload and the enterprise budget is exhausted.
- **New reason codes** and additional headers to explain why the request was rejected.

Important framing for customers:
- 429 is **not** a “generic outage” signal. It is a deliberate backpressure mechanism to protect overall fleet health and enterprise SLOs.
- 429 responses are **safe to retry** when the reason indicates transient pressure, using the guidance below.

---

## 429 response format (what Support should look for)

### Common JSON fields (all 429s)
Most clients will receive an error payload shaped like:
- `error.type`: `rate_limit_exceeded` or `quota_exceeded` or `overloaded`
- `error.code`: a stable machine-readable reason code (see table below)
- `error.message`: human-readable summary
- `error.request_id`: request identifier (also returned in response headers)

If a customer claims the payload is missing or inconsistent, ask for:
- raw response body
- full response headers
- SDK version / client library

### Standard headers (may vary by route)
Support should request and record these headers when present:
- `x-redwood-request-id`: request ID used for internal tracing
- `retry-after`: seconds to wait before retry (only set for some transient reasons)
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`: millisecond retry hint (preferred when present)
- `x-redwood-limit-scope`: `account` | `api_key` | `route` (where enforcement applies)
- `x-redwood-limit-policy`: policy identifier (internal-friendly string; customer can share as opaque)
- `x-redwood-429-reason`: short reason token (maps to reason codes below)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-limit`: numeric limit (requests per window) when applicable
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-remaining`: remaining requests in current window (when applicable)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-reset`: epoch time or seconds-until-reset (when applicable)

Notes:
- Some enterprise customers will only see a subset of limit headers depending on contract configuration and route group settings.
- Absence of headers is not automatically a bug; it can indicate a rejection occurred upstream of quota evaluation (e.g., admission control).

---

## Reason codes (how to interpret 429s)
Use the following table to classify the issue and advise the customer.

### A) Quota- and rate-limit-driven 429s (customer-specific)
These are expected when a customer exceeds their configured limits.

| Category | error.type | x-redwood-429-reason (typical) | Meaning | Customer guidance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hard quota exceeded | quota_exceeded | quota_window_exhausted | Customer has consumed their quota for the current window. | Do not retry immediately. Wait until reset. Consider limit increase request. |
| Rate limit exceeded | rate_limit_exceeded | rate_limit_account | Customer exceeded account-level request rate. | Retry with exponential backoff + jitter. Reduce concurrency / smooth bursts. |
| Route-group rate limit | rate_limit_exceeded | rate_limit_route_group | Customer exceeded a per-route or route-group cap. | Same as above; ask them to spread load or request a higher burst allowance. |
| Token / compute guardrail | rate_limit_exceeded | token_budget_exceeded | Customer exceeded a token/compute budget (if enabled for contract). | Reduce max_tokens / request size; smooth traffic; discuss contract sizing. |

### B) Overload protection / admission control 429s (fleet pressure)
These indicate transient capacity protection behavior.

| Category | error.type | x-redwood-429-reason (typical) | Meaning | Customer guidance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Admission control (best-effort shed) | overloaded | shed_best_effort | Fleet/route is under pressure; best-effort traffic is being throttled first. | Retry with exponential backoff + jitter. Prefer queueing client-side. |
| Admission control (enterprise budget exhausted) | overloaded | shed_enterprise_budget | Protected route is overloaded and enterprise budget is fully utilized. | Retry using Retry-After if present; reduce concurrency; contact Support if sustained. |
| Regional capacity constraint | overloaded | region_capacity | Selected region is capacity constrained; request rejected to prevent tail latency cascade. | Retry with backoff; if multi-region is enabled, allow client failover. |
| Policy evaluation deny | overloaded | policy_deny_overload | Policy engine denied admission due to active overload rule (e.g., queue depth trigger). | Treat as transient. Retry with backoff. Provide request IDs for investigation if frequent. |

Support positioning:
- For enterprise accounts: “This is protective backpressure to preserve latency/availability for protected routes. It can occur during unusual bursts or regional pressure.”
- For self-serve/best-effort: “During peak pressure, the platform prioritizes higher-SLA traffic; your requests may be throttled earlier.”

---

## Recommended customer retry & backoff guidance
Provide the following general guidance (tailor to the customer’s stack):

### When to retry
- Retry 429s when:
  - `error.type = overloaded` (most cases)
  - `error.type = rate_limit_exceeded`
- Do NOT aggressively retry when:
  - `error.type = quota_exceeded` and the response includes a reset hint (wait until reset)

### How to retry (recommended algorithm)
- Use **exponential backoff with full jitter**.
- Start with 250–500ms base delay.
- Cap max delay at 10–30s.
- Add client-side request queueing to avoid stampedes.
- Respect `retry-after` and/or `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` when present (prefer the ms header).

Suggested pseudocode guidance to share (language-agnostic):
1) if `retry-after` present → sleep for that duration (+ small jitter) then retry
2) else backoff = random(0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt))
3) stop after a reasonable max attempts (typically 5–8) and surface a retriable error to the application

### Concurrency guidance
Customers with high parallelism should:
- Implement a per-route concurrency limiter (token bucket / semaphore)
- Smooth bursty workloads (batching where possible)
- Avoid synchronized retries across many workers (jitter)

### Idempotency guidance
- For non-streaming requests, retries are generally safe.
- For streaming requests, see “Streaming semantics” below.

---

## Streaming semantics during shedding (common ticket driver)
Some customers report “inconsistent termination” on streaming responses during load shedding.

Support guidance:
- If a request is rejected **before** streaming begins, they should receive a normal 429 with reason + headers.
- If a request is accepted and then terminated due to downstream pressure, behavior depends on the transport:
  - Customers should treat early stream termination as retriable if they did not receive a completed response.
  - Advise customers to log the `x-redwood-request-id` and last event received.

Escalate if:
- customers see a successful HTTP status but the stream ends without an explicit termination signal and without consistent client-visible error handling, or
- the customer provides multiple request IDs showing premature termination correlated with new overload reasons.

---

## What Support should ask for (intake checklist)
Request the following in the initial reply to reduce ping-pong:
1) Affected route(s): e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`
2) Region/endpoint base URL (if multi-region)
3) Time window (with timezone) and approximate request rate
4) Sample `x-redwood-request-id` values (3–10 IDs)
5) Full 429 response headers + body (raw)
6) Whether they are using streaming
7) Their current retry policy (backoff, max attempts, concurrency)
8) Whether it’s isolated to a single API key or all keys in the account

Internal-only (if available to TAM/CS): contract tier (Hosted API vs Dedicated), priority class, and any approved exceptions.

---

## Escalation criteria (when to page/loop in Engineering)
Escalate to SRE/Platform when ANY of the following are true:

### Enterprise impact thresholds
- Enterprise customer reports sustained 429s on a protected route for **>10 minutes** AND:
  - reason indicates overload protection (`overloaded/*`), OR
  - they claim traffic is within contracted limits but still seeing 429s
- Enterprise customer reports p95/p99 latency degradation plus 429 spikes (possible mis-tuned thresholds)

### Suspected misconfiguration
- `x-redwood-limit-policy` changes unexpectedly after rollout / config migration
- A single tenant is denied while peers are not, and headers indicate route-group limits that don’t match the contract

### Possible platform bug
- Missing/incorrect reason codes across multiple requests
- `retry-after` values that are clearly wrong (e.g., negative, extremely large, inconsistent with window)
- Streaming termination inconsistencies correlated with overload events

### Security / access concerns
- Customer requests details about other tenants, fleet state, or priority policy internals beyond what we disclose. Route via TAM/CS + Security as needed.

---

## Escalation process (internal)
1) Create/attach a ticket in the Support queue and tag: `overload-protection` + `enterprise`.
2) Post in `#incidents` (if active event suspected) or `#eng-sre` with:
   - customer/account
   - affected routes/regions
   - timeframe
   - reason codes + sample request IDs
   - whether Retry-After was present and followed
3) Link the relevant dashboards (SRE on-call will pull). If you have access, include:
   - enterprise route SLO dashboard
   - overload protection shed-rate panel
   - admission control trigger annotations

If immediate paging is warranted, follow the runbook: “Overload protection and admission control” (Related pages).

---

## Approved external explanation (copy/paste)
Use this when responding to customers (edit for their tier/route):

“HTTP 429 indicates the request was throttled or temporarily rejected to protect latency and availability. For protected enterprise routes, Redwood prioritizes higher-SLA traffic under peak load to prevent fleet-wide degradation. Please implement exponential backoff with jitter and respect any Retry-After headers. If you share a few request IDs and the full response headers/body, we can confirm whether the 429s are due to your configured limits (quota/rate) or transient overload protection.”

---

## FAQ (Support)

**Q: Is a 429 an outage?**
A: Not necessarily. For many overload cases, it’s intentional backpressure to avoid tail latency/5xx cascading failures.

**Q: Can enterprise customers still get 429s?**
A: Yes—if they exceed their contracted limits, or in rare cases when a protected route’s enterprise budget is fully consumed during sustained overload.

**Q: Should customers increase retry aggressiveness to “push through”?**
A: No. Aggressive retries worsen overload. Encourage backoff + jitter + client-side queueing.

**Q: Customer says they stayed under their “RPM” but still got 429.**
A: Ask for headers and request IDs. They may be hitting a route-group limit, a token/compute guardrail, or experiencing transient admission control without Retry-After being honored.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-06: Added streaming termination guidance + escalation triggers for inconsistent SSE termination.
- 2026-01-23: Updated reason-code table to include `shed_enterprise_budget` and `region_capacity`.
- 2026-01-09: Initial publication for Support enablement ahead of GA rollout.

source.title: SOP: intake and coordination for customer-managed audit log export requests
source.author: Lauren Bishop
source.content:
# Purpose
This SOP describes how Customer Success (CS) and Support intake, scope, and coordinate **customer-managed audit log export** requests across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

# When to use
- A customer asks to export Redwood audit logs to their cloud storage or SIEM pipeline.
- A security questionnaire requires audit log delivery details and customer wants automated export.

# Request intake checklist (CS/Support)
Collect the following in the internal-support Jira ticket:
- Customer company
- Deployment mode: Hosted | Dedicated | Private
- Regions in use
- Desired sink: S3 | GCS | Azure Blob
- Destination details:
  - bucket/container name
  - prefix
  - destination region
- Identity method:
  - AWS assume-role / IRSA
  - GCP workload identity
  - Azure workload identity
- Retention intent (customer-owned)
- Operational contact + escalation contact
- Target date (if tied to an audit)

# Handoff and approvals
## 1) Create the internal-support Jira ticket
- Project: internal-support
- Issue type: Support Request
- Priority guidance:
  - P2 for standard requests
  - P1 if tied to a customer audit deadline within 14 days
  - P0 only if this blocks a live incident response obligation

## 2) Security approval
- Assign Security reviewer and request approval per the Security controls page.
- Attach destination policy snippets and encryption confirmation.

## 3) Legal/Privacy approval (conditional)
Escalate to Legal/Privacy if:
- cross-region export is requested
- customer requests additional PII fields beyond standard audit schema
- contract includes bespoke data residency clauses

# Execution routing
After approvals:
- Hosted: route to Eng Platform oncall / platform ops
- Dedicated: route to Eng Infra
- Private: route to Private Deployments engineering (and the customer’s operator)

# Customer communication template (email)
Subject: Audit log export enablement – next steps

Body:
- Thanks for the request. We can enable automated export of Redwood platform audit logs to your [S3/GCS/Azure] destination.
- Please confirm you have created the destination bucket/container and enabled encryption at rest.
- Please provide the workload identity / role configuration using the attached template.
- Once received and approved, we will enable export and ask you to confirm objects are landing under the agreed prefix.
- Typical initial enablement time: 3–5 business days (may vary by deployment mode and approvals).

# Validation responsibilities
- Redwood: verifies connector health metrics and initial delivery to the destination.
- Customer: verifies ingestion into their SIEM and configures their own retention/alerts.

# Post-enablement notes
- CS should confirm the customer understands delivery semantics (at-least-once, possible duplicates).
- CS should remind the customer that the destination is customer-owned (storage cost, lifecycle policies).

# Related pages
- Security controls and approval gates
- Hosted runbook
- Dedicated runbook
- Private configuration guide

source.title: Activation SLA Exception & Timebox Playbook
source.author: Riya Kapoor
source.content:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook defines a deterministic process for handling activation-window SLA exceptions during enterprise customer onboarding. It provides timeboxed steps for containment, investigation, mitigation, escalation, and customer communications to minimize time-to-stability while protecting cost and product quality.

When to use this playbook:
- Customer experiences degraded latency, elevated error rates, or cost-velocity exceeding onboarding projections within the first 30 days of activation.
- Automated health checks or customer-reported incidents indicate sustained SLA breach during activation that may impact go-live commitments.
- Normal runbooks (standard incident or SRE playbooks) have been attempted and the issue persists beyond the defined timebox thresholds.

Goals:
- Contain impact within defined timeboxes (Investigation: 60m, Mitigation candidate deploy: 120m, Customer stabilization: 8h).
- Preserve telemetry needed for RCA while limiting downstream cost exposure.
- Provide standardized customer-facing messaging and governance for SLA exceptions and credits.

Ownership and roles
- Customer Success (CS) — Primary communications owner. Owning the customer relationship and timely updates.
- Oncall SRE — Technical triage lead for production incidents; responsible for topology, routing, and infra mitigations.
- Serving Runtime Team — Deep model-serving investigations, KV-cache, batching, and model variant rollout.
- Solutions/Integration Engineer — Tenant configuration, API contract verification, quota/rate-limit checks.
- Product Ops / Finance — Engage if cost-velocity or billing exceptions are likely.

Timeboxes and Expected Actions
1) T0 — Alert/Report (0 minutes):
- Trigger: automated health check or CS report.
- CS sends initial acknowledgement to customer (template below) within 15 minutes.
- Triage owner assigned (SRE oncall or Solutions eng).

2) Investigation Window — T+60 minutes:
- Goal: determine impact domain (serving, networking, model variant, tenant misconfig).
- Primary checks (execute in parallel):
  - Ingress gateway metrics: 5xx rate, connection errors, TLS handshake failures.
  - Per-tenant rate and quota counters (requests/min, tokens/min).
  - Model-level latency P95/P99 and token-emit rates.
  - KV cache hit-rate and cache TTL evictions.
  - Autoscaler events and pod/gpu scheduling failures.
- Outcome: classify as one of: Infrastructure, Serving Runtime, Model Quality/Variant, Client Misuse (unexpected traffic), or Billing/Quota.

3) Mitigation Window — T+120 minutes from alert start (if unresolved):
- Apply the fastest safe mitigation depending on classification:
  - Infrastructure: shift traffic to warm fallback region or enable burst pool.
  - Serving Runtime: switch to lower-memory model variant, disable aggressive batching, or clear stale KV cache for tenant.
  - Model issue: pin previous model version and enable canary rollback.
  - Client misuse: apply temporary throttling or temporary API key suspension and coordinate with customer.
- All mitigations require a short preflight checklist and a rollback plan; prefer configuration changes over code deploys.

4) Stabilize & Monitor — T+8 hours (target):
- Stabilization defined as 95% of requests within SLO for 1 hour and no sign of cost-velocity growth.
- If stabilizing, produce a stabilization note (CS + SRE) and schedule RCA.
- If not stabilized after 8 hours, escalate to incident review and cross-functional war room (see escalation matrix).

Escalation Matrix
- Level 1: Oncall SRE, CS owner, Solutions Eng (T0–T+2h).
- Level 2: Serving Runtime Lead, Platform Engineering Manager (T+2–8h) if mitigations incomplete.
- Level 3: Head of Customer Success, Head of Engineering, Product Lead (T>8h or major SLA breach with contractual impact).

Telemetry checklist (ordered)
1) Correlate alert ID to tenant ID and request examples (x-request-id) for replay.
2) Capture production traces (retain for 72h beyond default retention for this tenant).
3) Snapshot relevant dashboards (ingress, model latency, gpu telemetry, cost per-tenant).
4) Export KV cache stats, batching histograms, and model-variant routing logs.

Quick containment commands (examples)
- Throttle tenant to safe rate (k8s/ingress example):
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
  apiVersion: networking.example.com/v1
  kind: RateLimit
  metadata:
    name: throttle-tenant-1234
  spec:
    tenant: tenant-1234
    requestsPerMinute: 600
  EOF

- Pin model rollout (runtime CLI):
  runtimectl set-tenant-model --tenant tenant-1234 --model redwood-model-v2 --force-pin

- Clear tenant KV cache (runtime RPC):
  curl -X POST -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN' https://runtime-api.internal/tenants/tenant-1234/cache/clear

Decision table: mitigation selection
| Classification       | Fast mitigation (<=120m)                      | Rollback safety notes |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| Infrastructure       | Route to warm fallback region                 | Ensure data residency allowed for fallback |
| Serving Runtime      | Switch to smaller quantized variant; disable batching | May increase token-cost; monitor latency |
| Model Quality        | Roll model to prior pinned version            | Validate sample prompts prior to switch |
| Client Misuse        | Apply temporary throttle or API key freeze    | Confirm with CS before permanent block |

Customer communication templates
- Initial acknowledgement (within 15m):
  Subject: "[Redwood] We received your report — investigating"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,\n\nThank you for reporting this. Our team has acknowledged the issue and started triage. We will provide an initial update within 60 minutes. If you have any immediate logs or examples (request IDs) please share them in reply. — Riya (Customer Success)"

- Update at Investigation completion (T+60m):
  Subject: "[Redwood] Update: preliminary cause identified for activation disruption"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,\n\nWe’ve completed an initial investigation and identified the likely cause: <short-classification>. We are applying a temporary mitigation and will follow up with stabilization status within 2 hours. Current impact: <summary>. — CS & SRE"

- Stabilization summary (T+8h):
  Subject: "[Redwood] Stabilized: activation window incident summary"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,\n\nThe issue has been stabilized as of <timestamp>. Next steps: full RCA and timeline for permanent fix within 3 business days. If you'd like a joint review session, we can schedule one. — CS"

Governance and SLA crediting rules (summary)
- If the customer experiences >2 hours of degraded throughput or >4 hours of elevated error rates during the agreed activation window and the root cause is provider-side, triggering of credit review is automatic.
- Finance/Product Ops approves credit estimates. CS files a credit request ticket referencing the incident ID and attaches stabilization snapshot and mitigation timeline.

RCA & Follow-up (post-stabilization)
- Required artifacts: timeline.csv, traces.zip, snapshot-dashboards.png, mitigation-decisions.md.
- RCA author: Serving Runtime or SRE (depending on root cause) with CS contribution for impact timeline.
- RCA timeline: draft within 48 hours; final RCA and action-items in 7 business days.

Preventive measures and playbook improvements
- For each activation exception, the owning team must propose at least one preventive control: autoscaler tuning, pre-warmed capacity pool, tiered rate limits, model quantization guidance, or preflight telemetry assertions.
- Add any identified new telemetry assertions to the onboarding synthetic suite and QBR telemetry storyboard.

Appendices
A) Sample incident tagging conventions
- tag:activation-window, tag:tenant-<id>, tag:sla-exception, tag:playbook-v1
\B) Example alert payload (JSON) — use for debugging and replaying the event:
{
  "alert_id": "alert-20260112-9243",
  "tenant_id": "tenant-1234",
  "metric": "p99_latency_ms",
  "value": "3120",
  "threshold": "2000",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-12T10:22:00Z"
}

C) Postmortem checklist (for RCA owner)
- Confirm timeline and attach trace samples (x-request-id list)
- Validate mitigation and document rollback path taken
- Identify owner and ETA for permanent fix(s)
- Confirm crediting decision or rejection with Finance
- Schedule QBR slot to review trends if similar incidents repeat across customers

Related resources
- Onboarding telemetry storyboard (link above)
- Runtime rollback playbook (internal link to runtime runbooks)

Revision history
- 2026-01-12: Created by Riya Kapoor (initial draft)
- 2026-02-03: Reviewed by Ethan Park and Sofia Martinez; clarified timeboxes and added customer communication templates

Contact points
- CS Pager: cs-pager@redwood.internal
- SRE Oncall Channel: #sre-oncall
- Runtime Lead: runtime-lead@redwood.internal

title: Activation SLA Exception & Timebox Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook defines a deterministic process for handling activation-window SLA exceptions during enterprise customer onboarding. It provides timeboxed steps for containment, investigation, mitigation, escalation, and customer communications to minimize time-to-stability while protecting cost and product quality.

When to use this playbook:
- Customer experiences degraded latency, elevated error rates, or cost-velocity exceeding onboarding projections within the first 30 days of activation.
- Automated health checks or customer-reported incidents indicate sustained SLA breach during activation that may impact go-live commitments.
- Normal runbooks (standard incident or SRE playbooks) have been attempted and the issue persists beyond the defined timebox thresholds.

Goals:
- Contain impact within defined timeboxes (Investigation: 60m, Mitigation candidate deploy: 120m, Customer stabilization: 8h).
- Preserve telemetry needed for RCA while limiting downstream cost exposure.
- Provide standardized customer-facing messaging and governance for SLA exceptions and credits.

Ownership and roles
- Customer Success (CS) — Primary communications owner. Owning the customer relationship and timely updates.
- Oncall SRE — Technical triage lead for production incidents; responsible for topology, routing, and infra mitigations.
- Serving Runtime Team — Deep model-serving investigations, KV-cache, batching, and model variant rollout.
- Solutions/Integration Engineer — Tenant configuration, API contract verification, quota/rate-limit checks.
- Product Ops / Finance — Engage if cost-velocity or billing exceptions are likely.

Timeboxes and Expected Actions
1) T0 — Alert/Report (0 minutes):
- Trigger: automated health check or CS report.
- CS sends initial acknowledgement to customer (template below) within 15 minutes.
- Triage owner assigned (SRE oncall or Solutions eng).

2) Investigation Window — T+60 minutes:
- Goal: determine impact domain (serving, networking, model variant, tenant misconfig).
- Primary checks (execute in parallel):
  - Ingress gateway metrics: 5xx rate, connection errors, TLS handshake failures.
  - Per-tenant rate and quota counters (requests/min, tokens/min).
  - Model-level latency P95/P99 and token-emit rates.
  - KV cache hit-rate and cache TTL evictions.
  - Autoscaler events and pod/gpu scheduling failures.
- Outcome: classify as one of: Infrastructure, Serving Runtime, Model Quality/Variant, Client Misuse (unexpected traffic), or Billing/Quota.

3) Mitigation Window — T+120 minutes from alert start (if unresolved):
- Apply the fastest safe mitigation depending on classification:
  - Infrastructure: shift traffic to warm fallback region or enable burst pool.
  - Serving Runtime: switch to lower-memory model variant, disable aggressive batching, or clear stale KV cache for tenant.
  - Model issue: pin previous model version and enable canary rollback.
  - Client misuse: apply temporary throttling or temporary API key suspension and coordinate with customer.
- All mitigations require a short preflight checklist and a rollback plan; prefer configuration changes over code deploys.

4) Stabilize & Monitor — T+8 hours (target):
- Stabilization defined as 95% of requests within SLO for 1 hour and no sign of cost-velocity growth.
- If stabilizing, produce a stabilization note (CS + SRE) and schedule RCA.
- If not stabilized after 8 hours, escalate to incident review and cross-functional war room (see escalation matrix).

Escalation Matrix
- Level 1: Oncall SRE, CS owner, Solutions Eng (T0–T+2h).
- Level 2: Serving Runtime Lead, Platform Engineering Manager (T+2–8h) if mitigations incomplete.
- Level 3: Head of Customer Success, Head of Engineering, Product Lead (T>8h or major SLA breach with contractual impact).

Telemetry checklist (ordered)
1) Correlate alert ID to tenant ID and request examples (x-request-id) for replay.
2) Capture production traces (retain for 72h beyond default retention for this tenant).
3) Snapshot relevant dashboards (ingress, model latency, gpu telemetry, cost per-tenant).
4) Export KV cache stats, batching histograms, and model-variant routing logs.

Quick containment commands (examples)
- Throttle tenant to safe rate (k8s/ingress example):
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
  apiVersion: networking.example.com/v1
  kind: RateLimit
  metadata:
    name: throttle-tenant-1234
  spec:
    tenant: tenant-1234
    requestsPerMinute: 600
  EOF

- Pin model rollout (runtime CLI):
  runtimectl set-tenant-model --tenant tenant-1234 --model redwood-model-v2 --force-pin

- Clear tenant KV cache (runtime RPC):
  curl -X POST -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN' https://runtime-api.internal/tenants/tenant-1234/cache/clear

Decision table: mitigation selection
| Classification       | Fast mitigation (<=120m)                      | Rollback safety notes |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| Infrastructure       | Route to warm fallback region                 | Ensure data residency allowed for fallback |
| Serving Runtime      | Switch to smaller quantized variant; disable batching | May increase token-cost; monitor latency |
| Model Quality        | Roll model to prior pinned version            | Validate sample prompts prior to switch |
| Client Misuse        | Apply temporary throttle or API key freeze    | Confirm with CS before permanent block |

Customer communication templates
- Initial acknowledgement (within 15m):
  Subject: "[Redwood] We received your report — investigating"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,

Thank you for reporting this. Our team has acknowledged the issue and started triage. We will provide an initial update within 60 minutes. If you have any immediate logs or examples (request IDs) please share them in reply. — Riya (Customer Success)"

- Update at Investigation completion (T+60m):
  Subject: "[Redwood] Update: preliminary cause identified for activation disruption"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,

We’ve completed an initial investigation and identified the likely cause: <short-classification>. We are applying a temporary mitigation and will follow up with stabilization status within 2 hours. Current impact: <summary>. — CS & SRE"

- Stabilization summary (T+8h):
  Subject: "[Redwood] Stabilized: activation window incident summary"
  Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,

The issue has been stabilized as of <timestamp>. Next steps: full RCA and timeline for permanent fix within 3 business days. If you'd like a joint review session, we can schedule one. — CS"

Governance and SLA crediting rules (summary)
- If the customer experiences >2 hours of degraded throughput or >4 hours of elevated error rates during the agreed activation window and the root cause is provider-side, triggering of credit review is automatic.
- Finance/Product Ops approves credit estimates. CS files a credit request ticket referencing the incident ID and attaches stabilization snapshot and mitigation timeline.

RCA & Follow-up (post-stabilization)
- Required artifacts: timeline.csv, traces.zip, snapshot-dashboards.png, mitigation-decisions.md.
- RCA author: Serving Runtime or SRE (depending on root cause) with CS contribution for impact timeline.
- RCA timeline: draft within 48 hours; final RCA and action-items in 7 business days.

Preventive measures and playbook improvements
- For each activation exception, the owning team must propose at least one preventive control: autoscaler tuning, pre-warmed capacity pool, tiered rate limits, model quantization guidance, or preflight telemetry assertions.
- Add any identified new telemetry assertions to the onboarding synthetic suite and QBR telemetry storyboard.

Appendices
A) Sample incident tagging conventions
- tag:activation-window, tag:tenant-<id>, tag:sla-exception, tag:playbook-v1
\B) Example alert payload (JSON) — use for debugging and replaying the event:
{
  "alert_id": "alert-20260112-9243",
  "tenant_id": "tenant-1234",
  "metric": "p99_latency_ms",
  "value": "3120",
  "threshold": "2000",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-12T10:22:00Z"
}

C) Postmortem checklist (for RCA owner)
- Confirm timeline and attach trace samples (x-request-id list)
- Validate mitigation and document rollback path taken
- Identify owner and ETA for permanent fix(s)
- Confirm crediting decision or rejection with Finance
- Schedule QBR slot to review trends if similar incidents repeat across customers

Related resources
- Onboarding telemetry storyboard (link above)
- Runtime rollback playbook (internal link to runtime runbooks)

Revision history
- 2026-01-12: Created by Riya Kapoor (initial draft)
- 2026-02-03: Reviewed by Ethan Park and Sofia Martinez; clarified timeboxes and added customer communication templates

Contact points
- CS Pager: cs-pager@redwood.internal
- SRE Oncall Channel: #sre-oncall
- Runtime Lead: runtime-lead@redwood.internal
