source.title: DNS and Certificate Onboarding Runbook — Private & Dedicated Tenants
source.author: Maya Singh
source.body:
Summary:
This runbook documents the DNS and TLS/certificate acceptance, validation, and remediation steps required for enterprise customers during cutover to a Dedicated or Private deployment. It focuses on tenant-provided DNS records and customer-managed TLS certificates, and includes automated checks, manual verification commands, acceptance criteria, rollback options, and escalation contacts.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Tenant DNS records (A/CNAME/ALIAS), DNS delegation patterns, split-horizon considerations, TLS certificate ingestion and rotation, ACME vs uploaded certs, OCSP stapling, and monitoring configuration for Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Audience: Customer Success engineers (CSE), Solutions Engineers (SE), SRE owners, and customer network/security teams.

Preconditions (what customer must provide before cutover):
1) FQDN(s) to be used for production (one or more).
2) Proof of control for those domains (DNS TXT for ACME or signed CSR for uploaded certs).
3) DNS zone admin access or a documented change control window with the customer’s network team.
4) Expected TTL targets and global region routing (if applicable).
5) If using Private deployment, internal DNS resolution plan (split-horizon or private DNS forwarders).

Stakeholders and ownership:
- Customer Success (owner): coordinate cutover, validate runbook steps, own post-cutover handback.
- Customer Network Team (action): implement DNS changes and respond to validation requests.
- SRE/Infra (owner): enable Redwood-side routing, apply certificates, configure frontends, and verify observability.

Acceptance criteria (must be satisfied before traffic cutover):
- DNS: All declared resolvers return the expected A/CNAME/ALIAS for the FQDN within the agreed propagation window. Recommended TTL <= 300s for cutover window.
- TLS: Certificate chain validates against a public trust root (or customer-specified private CA previously approved), SANs match requested FQDNs, expiry > 30 days at time of cutover.
- OCSP/Stapling: Frontend returns stapled OCSP response without error for public certs.
- TLS protocol: Server negotiates TLS 1.2 or 1.3; weak ciphers disabled per Redwood policy.
- Healthcheck: Application health endpoint returns 200 for at least 3 consecutive probes from two distinct regions.
- Monitoring: Alert rule for cert expiry and DNS drift is created and tested in customer-visible console.

Quick validation checklist (commands and expected outputs):
1) DNS resolution (public):
   - dig +short example.customer.com A
     Expected: one or more public IPs or CNAME pointing to Redwood ingress domain.
   - dig +short CNAME example.customer.com
     Expected: canonical host if using CNAME/ALIAS; verify there is no unintended additional CNAME chaining.
   - dig @8.8.8.8 +trace example.customer.com
     Use to detect delegation or resolver-specific issues.
2) Split-horizon / internal DNS test (Private deployments):
   - nslookup example.customer.com internal-dns-server
     Expected: internal IP(s) that map to internal load balancer. Document the internal resolver used by tenants.
3) TLS certificate and chain check (server-facing):
   - openssl s_client -connect example.customer.com:443 -servername example.customer.com -showcerts
     Expected: certificate chain present, verify subject/SANs and expiry dates in the cert block.
   - openssl x509 -noout -dates -in <leaf-cert.pem>
     Expected: notBefore <= now <= notAfter and notAfter more than 30 days in future.
4) OCSP stapling test:
   - openssl s_client -connect example.customer.com:443 -status -servername example.customer.com
     Expected: OCSP response present and successful. If OCSP is absent for a public CA cert, record and escalate per exceptions.
5) HTTP/HTTPS header checks (HSTS, security headers):
   - curl -I https://example.customer.com/health --resolve example.customer.com:443:<ip>
     Expected: 200 OK; include HSTS header when applicable (Strict-Transport-Security).

Automated verification script (example snippet):
- A minimal shell snippet used by CSE to pre-validate the tenant before scheduled cutover.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e
DOMAIN=$1
# DNS check
dig +short $DOMAIN | grep -q \"\" || true # intentionally permissive in template
# TLS check
openssl s_client -connect ${DOMAIN}:443 -servername ${DOMAIN} -brief >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo \"TLS check failed\"

(Replace with Redwood-managed script during actual runbook execution.)

Configuration examples (what to request from customer):
- Uploaded cert bundle (PEM): leaf + intermediate(s) in order, single file. Filename: tenant.example.com.bundle.pem
- CSR requirements: RSA 2048+ or ECDSA P-256/P-384 recommended, CN optional when SANs are present.
- ACME: When using ACME, customer should create a TXT record at _acme-challenge.example.customer.com with the token provided by Redwood.

Sample JSON for tenant cert metadata (for internal intake systems):
{
  \"domain\": \"example.customer.com\",
  \"certificate_type\": \"uploaded|acme\",
  \"contact\": \"ops-team@example-customer.com\",
  \"expiry_check_days\": \"30\"
}

Acceptance table (short):
| Check | Pass condition | Owner | Tool |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Public DNS A/CNAME | Expected record resolves globally within propagation window | Customer Network | dig/nslookup |
| Private DNS mapping | Internal resolvers return correct private IPs | Customer Network | nslookup/dig |
| Certificate chain | Chain validates to trust anchor, SANs present, expiry > 30d | Customer Security | openssl |
| OCSP stapling | Stapled OCSP present and valid | SRE | openssl s_client |
| TLS negotiation | TLS 1.2/1.3, no weak ciphers | SRE | tls-observability scanner |
| Health endpoint | 200 OK across test regions | Application Owner | curl/monitoring probes |

Cutover procedure (high level):
1) Pre-cutover (T-48h to T-1h):
   - Confirm all DNS changes staged in customer DNS provider with low TTLs.
   - Verify certs uploaded or ACME token validated.
   - Create monitoring alerts for DNS drift (record mismatch) and cert expiry.
   - Schedule cutover window and notify stakeholders.
2) Cutover (execution window):
   - Apply DNS change (customer action).
   - Redwood SRE validates propagated records across 3 public resolvers and internal resolvers (for private deployments).
   - Apply certificate to frontend (if uploaded) or mark ACME-issued cert as active.
   - Smoke test: curl health endpoints from two regions and run TLS/OCSP checks.
   - If smoke tests pass, flip traffic gradually (canary to 5% → 25% → 100%), observe metrics for 30 minutes at each stage.
3) Post-cutover (T+0.5h to T+24h):
   - Run full suite of observability checks (latency, error rate, token usage).
   - Confirm monitoring alerts are green and cert expiry monitors are configured.
   - Handback to customer with a short report and runbook summary.

Rollback and remediation options:
- If DNS did not propagate or resolves to wrong target: ask customer to revert DNS change to previous value (document previous TTL and records in advance). Use Redwood-hosted failover DNS record if previously configured.
- If cert invalid or chain errors: re-upload corrected bundle or enable temporary Redwood-managed wildcard cert (requires security approval for the tenant).
- If OCSP stapling fails: escalate to SRE; as temporary mitigation, enable short-lived fallback cert or disable strict stapling enforcement for the tenant for a minimal period (documented exception).

Escalation matrix (ordered):
1) CSE on-call (customer-facing) – immediate coordination and initial validation (15m SLA).
2) SRE routing owner – deep TLS/front-end config debugging (30m SLA).
3) Infra/PKI engineer – certificate issuance and ACME troubleshooting (1h SLA).
4) Engineering manager – cross-team decisions and customer executive communication (2h SLA).

Contacts and templates:
- CSE on-call: cse-oncall@redwood.ai
- SRE escalation alias: sre-escalations@redwood.ai
- PKI lead: pki@redwood.ai
- Customer comms template: (use oncall canned templates repository, subject: \"DNS/TLS cutover status for <customer>\")

Monitoring and long-term safeguards:
- Configure cert expiry alerts at 30/14/7/3 days and a pager at 3 days.
- Record the last-known-good DNS records in the tenant onboarding doc to allow fast rollback.
- For Private deployments, ensure that the customer’s DNS-forwarding or split-horizon docs are committed to the tenant runbook and synced with the customer’s change control system.

Postmortem triggers (examples):
- Unexpected TLS handshake failures observed for > 10% of traffic for more than 30 minutes.
- DNS divergence across resolvers leading to > 5% failed healthchecks after cutover.
- Certificate expiry discovered without configured alerts.

Related references and templates:
- Certificate ingestion template (internal): /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/cert-ingestion-template
- ACME validation checklist: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/integration-guides/acme-validation-and-automation-playbook (internal)

Revision history:
- 2025-03-12 — Initial draft by Maya Singh.
- 2025-07-08 — Added OCSP and private DNS sections, reviewed by Ethan Park.
- 2026-01-15 — Updated cutover cadence and monitoring thresholds, reviewed by Lina Alvarez.

Appendix: Troubleshooting quick tips
- If openssl s_client shows an incomplete chain, ask customer for intermediates in PEM order.
- If dig shows different results from customer DNS and public resolvers, check for split-horizon or propagation TTLs.
- If ACME token fails, ensure TXT record contains exact token string (no extra quotes) and that TTL is low during validation.

End of runbook.

title: DNS and Certificate Onboarding Runbook — Private & Dedicated Tenants
preview:
Summary:
This runbook documents the DNS and TLS/certificate acceptance, validation, and remediation steps required for enterprise customers during cutover to a Dedicated or Private deployment. It focuses on tenant-provided DNS records and customer-managed TLS certificates, and includes automated checks, manual verification commands, acceptance criteria, rollback options, and escalation contacts.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Tenant DNS records (A/CNAME/ALIAS), DNS delegation patterns, split-horizon considerations, TLS certificate ingestion and rotation, ACME vs uploaded certs, OCSP stapling, and monitoring configuration for Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Audience: Customer Success engineers (CSE), Solutions Engineers (SE), SRE owners, and customer network/security teams.

Preconditions (what customer must provide before cutover):
1) FQDN(s) to be used for production (one or more).
2) Proof of control for those domains (DNS TXT for ACME or signed CSR for uploaded certs).
3) DNS zone admin access or a documented change control window with the customer’s network team.
4) Expected TTL targets and global region routing (if applicable).
5) If using Private deployment, internal DNS resolution plan (split-horizon or private DNS forwarders).

Stakeholders and ownership:
- Customer Success (owner): coordinate cutover, validate runbook steps, own post-cutover handback.
- Customer Network Team (action): implement DNS changes and respond to validation requests.
- SRE/Infra (owner): enable Redwood-side routing, apply certificates, configure frontends, and verify observability.

Acceptance criteria (must be satisfied before traffic cutover):
- DNS: All declared resolvers return the expected A/CNAME/ALIAS for the FQDN within the agreed propagation window. Recommended TTL <= 300s for cutover window.
- TLS: Certificate chain validates against a public trust root (or customer-specified private CA previously approved), SANs match requested FQDNs, expiry > 30 days at time of cutover.
- OCSP/Stapling: Frontend returns stapled OCSP response without error for public certs.
- TLS protocol: Server negotiates TLS 1.2 or 1.3; weak ciphers disabled per Redwood policy.
- Healthcheck: Application health endpoint returns 200 for at least 3 consecutive probes from two distinct regions.
- Monitoring: Alert rule for cert expiry and DNS drift is created and tested in customer-visible console.

Quick validation checklist (commands and expected outputs):
1) DNS resolution (public):
   - dig +short example.customer.com A
     Expected: one or more public IPs or CNAME pointing to Redwood ingress domain.
   - dig +short CNAME example.customer.com
     Expected: canonical host if using CNAME/ALIAS; verify there is no unintended additional CNAME chaining.
   - dig @8.8.8.8 +trace example.customer.com
     Use to detect delegation or resolver-specific issues.
2) Split-horizon / internal DNS test (Private deployments):
   - nslookup example.customer.com internal-dns-server
     Expected: internal IP(s) that map to internal load balancer. Document the internal resolver used by tenants.
3) TLS certificate and chain check (server-facing):
   - openssl s_client -connect example.customer.com:443 -servername example.customer.com -showcerts
     Expected: certificate chain present, verify subject/SANs and expiry dates in the cert block.
   - openssl x509 -noout -dates -in <leaf-cert.pem>
     Expected: notBefore <= now <= notAfter and notAfter more than 30 days in future.
4) OCSP stapling test:
   - openssl s_client -connect example.customer.com:443 -status -servername example.customer.com
     Expected: OCSP response present and successful. If OCSP is absent for a public CA cert, record and escalate per exceptions.
5) HTTP/HTTPS header checks (HSTS, security headers):
   - curl -I https://example.customer.com/health --resolve example.customer.com:443:<ip>
     Expected: 200 OK; include HSTS header when applicable (Strict-Transport-Security).

Automated verification script (example snippet):
- A minimal shell snippet used by CSE to pre-validate the tenant before scheduled cutover.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e
DOMAIN=$1
# DNS check
dig +short $DOMAIN | grep -q \"\" || true # intentionally permissive in template
# TLS check
openssl s_client -connect ${DOMAIN}:443 -servername ${DOMAIN} -brief >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo \"TLS check failed\"

(Replace with Redwood-managed script during actual runbook execution.)

Configuration examples (what to request from customer):
- Uploaded cert bundle (PEM): leaf + intermediate(s) in order, single file. Filename: tenant.example.com.bundle.pem
- CSR requirements: RSA 2048+ or ECDSA P-256/P-384 recommended, CN optional when SANs are present.
- ACME: When using ACME, customer should create a TXT record at _acme-challenge.example.customer.com with the token provided by Redwood.

Sample JSON for tenant cert metadata (for internal intake systems):
{
  \"domain\": \"example.customer.com\",
  \"certificate_type\": \"uploaded|acme\",
  \"contact\": \"ops-team@example-customer.com\",
  \"expiry_check_days\": \"30\"
}

Acceptance table (short):
| Check | Pass condition | Owner | Tool |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Public DNS A/CNAME | Expected record resolves globally within propagation window | Customer Network | dig/nslookup |
| Private DNS mapping | Internal resolvers return correct private IPs | Customer Network | nslookup/dig |
| Certificate chain | Chain validates to trust anchor, SANs present, expiry > 30d | Customer Security | openssl |
| OCSP stapling | Stapled OCSP present and valid | SRE | openssl s_client |
| TLS negotiation | TLS 1.2/1.3, no weak ciphers | SRE | tls-observability scanner |
| Health endpoint | 200 OK across test regions | Application Owner | curl/monitoring probes |

Cutover procedure (high level):
1) Pre-cutover (T-48h to T-1h):
   - Confirm all DNS changes staged in customer DNS provider with low TTLs.
   - Verify certs uploaded or ACME token validated.
   - Create monitoring alerts for DNS drift (record mismatch) and cert expiry.
   - Schedule cutover window and notify stakeholders.
2) Cutover (execution window):
   - Apply DNS change (customer action).
   - Redwood SRE validates propagated records across 3 public resolvers and internal resolvers (for private deployments).
   - Apply certificate to frontend (if uploaded) or mark ACME-issued cert as active.
   - Smoke test: curl health endpoints from two regions and run TLS/OCSP checks.
   - If smoke tests pass, flip traffic gradually (canary to 5% → 25% → 100%), observe metrics for 30 minutes at each stage.
3) Post-cutover (T+0.5h to T+24h):
   - Run full suite of observability checks (latency, error rate, token usage).
   - Confirm monitoring alerts are green and cert expiry monitors are configured.
   - Handback to customer with a short report and runbook summary.

Rollback and remediation options:
- If DNS did not propagate or resolves to wrong target: ask customer to revert DNS change to previous value (document previous TTL and records in advance). Use Redwood-hosted failover DNS record if previously configured.
- If cert invalid or chain errors: re-upload corrected bundle or enable temporary Redwood-managed wildcard cert (requires security approval for the tenant).
- If OCSP stapling fails: escalate to SRE; as temporary mitigation, enable short-lived fallback cert or disable strict stapling enforcement for the tenant for a minimal period (documented exception).

Escalation matrix (ordered):
1) CSE on-call (customer-facing) – immediate coordination and initial validation (15m SLA).
2) SRE routing owner – deep TLS/front-end config debugging (30m SLA).
3) Infra/PKI engineer – certificate issuance and ACME troubleshooting (1h SLA).
4) Engineering manager – cross-team decisions and customer executive communication (2h SLA).

Contacts and templates:
- CSE on-call: cse-oncall@redwood.ai
- SRE escalation alias: sre-escalations@redwood.ai
- PKI lead: pki@redwood.ai
- Customer comms template: (use oncall canned templates repository, subject: \"DNS/TLS cutover status for <customer>\")

Monitoring and long-term safeguards:
- Configure cert expiry alerts at 30/14/7/3 days and a pager at 3 days.
- Record the last-known-good DNS records in the tenant onboarding doc to allow fast rollback.
- For Private deployments, ensure that the customer’s DNS-forwarding or split-horizon docs are committed to the tenant runbook and synced with the customer’s change control system.

Postmortem triggers (examples):
- Unexpected TLS handshake failures observed for > 10% of traffic for more than 30 minutes.
- DNS divergence across resolvers leading to > 5% failed healthchecks after cutover.
- Certificate expiry discovered without configured alerts.

Related references and templates:
- Certificate ingestion template (internal): /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/cert-ingestion-template
- ACME validation checklist: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/integration-guides/acme-validation-and-automation-playbook (internal)

Revision history:
- 2025-03-12 — Initial draft by Maya Singh.
- 2025-07-08 — Added OCSP and private DNS sections, reviewed by Ethan Park.
- 2026-01-15 — Updated cutover cadence and monitoring thresholds, reviewed by Lina Alvarez.

Appendix: Troubleshooting quick tips
- If openssl s_client shows an incomplete chain, ask customer for intermediates in PEM order.
- If dig shows different results from customer DNS and public resolvers, check for split-horizon or propagation TTLs.
- If ACME token fails, ensure TXT record contains exact token string (no extra quotes) and that TTL is low during validation.

End of runbook.

source.title: Status Page Customer Escalation Playbook (Disputed Status Accuracy)
source.author: Dev Patel
source.content:
## Purpose
This playbook is used by Support and Customer Success when a customer reports that the public status page does not reflect the impact they are experiencing ("status page says everything is OK, but we are failing"). It provides:
- A standard intake checklist to quickly validate what the customer is seeing.
- A clear escalation path to SRE/on-call and the incident Comms Lead.
- Approved customer-facing responses and guardrails.
- A consistent way to correct status page inaccuracies without increasing on-call burden.

This is a customer trust workflow. Our goals are (1) fast acknowledgement, (2) accurate classification to the right public component(s), and (3) avoiding speculative or customer-sensitive disclosures.

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following are true:
- Customer claims status page indicates "Operational" but they have errors/latency.
- Customer claims wrong component/region is marked degraded.
- Customer claims incident is missing (no active incident) while multiple customers are impacted.
- Customer requests proof/evidence of impact or asks for a post-incident summary while the status page remains green.

Do NOT use this playbook for:
- Single-tenant Dedicated or Private issues isolated to a single customer environment without broader platform impact (see "Dedicated/Private guidance").
- Pure how-to questions about interpreting the status page components (route to Docs/DevEx guidance).

## Definitions and guardrails
### What the public status page represents
- The status page reflects *platform component health* by product surface and region (e.g., Hosted API – US-East, Streaming – EU-West).
- It is not a guarantee that every request is successful for every customer, and it does not expose customer-specific capacity constraints.

### Required guardrails (must follow)
- Do not disclose: customer-specific capacity, noisy-neighbor details, account identifiers, or security-sensitive root cause.
- Do not over-promise SLA credits or contractual remedies in Support channels.
- Do not speculate on root cause. Stick to observed symptoms, mitigations underway, and next update timing.
- For Sev0/Sev1: do not publish new public status updates yourself unless explicitly authorized (see escalation section). Support/CS can draft wording, but SRE/IC owns publish unless delegated.

## Roles
- **Support L1 (Frontline):** intake, collect evidence, acknowledge customer, initiate escalation when criteria are met.
- **Support L2 / Escalations (Support Ops):** ensure complete evidence, coordinate with CS owner, maintain customer thread.
- **CS Owner / CSM:** manage stakeholder expectations, coordinate timelines and post-incident follow-up requests.
- **SRE On-call:** validates signals, confirms impacted public components, initiates or updates incident.
- **Incident Commander (IC):** owns incident handling and severity.
- **Comms Lead:** owns customer-facing updates (status page + internal alignment), ensures consistent wording.

## Targets (internal operating targets; not customer-facing commitments)
- **Acknowledgement to customer:** within 15 minutes for Sev0/Sev1 claims during business hours; within 30 minutes otherwise.
- **Time to escalate to SRE (if criteria met):** within 10 minutes of receiving required evidence.
- **Status page correction (if inaccurate):** as soon as SRE validates; prioritize before deep RCA when customer impact is ongoing.

## Step 1 — Intake checklist (copy/paste)
Collect the following information in the customer ticket/thread before escalating.

**Customer / environment**
- Customer name + contract type: Hosted API / Dedicated / Private
- Primary region(s) impacted (customer’s view): US-East / US-West / EU-West / APAC-SG, etc.
- Workspace/org ID (internal only) and support plan tier

**Time window**
- Impact start time (with timezone) and whether ongoing
- Whether impact is intermittent or continuous

**Symptoms (choose all that apply)**
- Elevated 5xx / 429 / timeouts
- Latency regression (p50/p95/p99)
- Streaming disconnects / stalled streams
- Embeddings failures
- Reranking failures
- Console access issues

**Request evidence**
- 3–5 example request IDs (or correlation IDs) with timestamps
- Example error messages and HTTP status codes
- Endpoint/route (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings) and SDK/version
- If streaming: transport details (SSE/websocket), and client timeout settings

**Customer impact**
- Severity from customer perspective (prod down vs degraded)
- Approximate failure rate if known (e.g., 10–20% of requests)
- Business impact (optional, customer-provided)

## Step 2 — Quick self-validation (Support/CS)
Before escalating, do a 3–5 minute check to avoid false alarms:
1) Check https://status.redwoodinference.com for active incidents and impacted components/regions.
2) Check internal dashboards for the relevant component/region (links in "Internal validation links").
3) Verify whether the symptom aligns with a component-specific failure mode (e.g., streaming disconnects may not show in generic API error rate).
4) If there is a mismatch, proceed to escalation.

If dashboards are fully green and evidence is weak (no request IDs, no timestamps), ask the customer for the missing items using the template in "Customer follow-up for missing data".

## Escalation criteria (when to page SRE)
Escalate to SRE On-call immediately if ANY are true:
- Multiple customers report similar symptoms in the same region within 30 minutes.
- Customer provides request IDs showing elevated 5xx/timeouts and internal dashboards show any correlated increase.
- Streaming disconnect spikes are reported (these are historically under-represented in generic error views).
- Enterprise customer indicates material production impact and the status page remains green.
- The customer disputes an active incident’s scope (wrong region/component) with evidence.

Escalate to SRE during business hours (non-page) if:
- Customer reports mild latency regression with no errors, and internal latency is elevated but below incident thresholds.
- Customer requests a retro/post-incident summary formatting change (route to Comms Lead + PM, not on-call).

## Step 3 — How to escalate (Slack + ticket)
### Slack channel routing
- Primary: **#support-escalations** (start here; keep customer context centralized)
- Add as needed:
  - **#incidents** (if you believe it should be treated as an incident)
  - **#eng-oncall** (for quick on-call attention if criteria met)
  - **#statuspage** (if your org uses a dedicated channel for status updates)

### Who to tag
- Tag **SRE On-call** (PagerDuty user: "SRE Oncall") when criteria are met.
- If an incident exists, tag the current **IC** and **Comms Lead** (from the incident channel topic / incident-bot output).

### Required escalation message format (copy/paste)
Subject: Potential status page mismatch — [Component?] [Region] — customer reports [symptom]

- Customer: [name] ([Hosted/Dedicated/Private], plan tier)
- Reported region(s): [US-East/EU-West/etc]
- Symptoms: [errors/latency/streaming disconnects/embeddings]
- Impact window: [start time] to [now]
- Evidence: request IDs + timestamps: [list]
- Current status page: [Operational / Incident ongoing / Wrong component]
- Our quick validation: [dashboard summary in 1–2 lines]
- Ask: please validate component mapping and advise whether to update status page / open incident

### Ticket hygiene
- Ensure the customer ticket includes the intake checklist.
- Add an internal note: "Status page accuracy dispute — escalated to SRE".
- If enterprise: notify the assigned CSM in the ticket and in Slack.

## Step 4 — What Support/CS should do while SRE validates
- Acknowledge the customer quickly using an approved template (below).
- Ask for any missing evidence (request IDs, timestamps) if needed.
- Do not state that an incident exists until SRE/IC confirms.
- If the customer is blocked, suggest short-term mitigations that are generally safe (e.g., retry with backoff, enable fallback model if they already use it, reduce concurrency) without implying a platform defect.

## Step 5 — If status page is inaccurate (correction workflow)
If SRE confirms a mismatch (wrong component/region or missing incident), the expected flow is:
1) SRE/IC identifies the correct public components (per taxonomy) and confirms status signal evidence.
2) Comms Lead posts or adjusts the status page update using the incident templates.
3) Support/CS follows up with the customer linking to the incident and summarizing the correction.

If the incident is Sev0/Sev1 and an approval step is required, Comms Lead will obtain the required approval (IC or Support lead, depending on incident workflow). Support should be ready to rapidly review wording for clarity (not for technical accuracy).

## Dedicated / Private guidance (important)
Dedicated and Private deployments may have customer-specific incidents that are not appropriate for the public status page.

Approved guidance:
- If a Dedicated/Private issue is isolated: acknowledge and treat it as a customer incident via the dedicated/private support path. Do not push for a public incident unless SRE confirms shared control-plane or multi-tenant platform impact.
- If the issue appears tied to shared control plane (auth, console, model registry, routing) affecting multiple dedicated customers: escalate as a potential public component impact ("Dedicated Control Plane") per taxonomy.

Never say:
- "Your cluster ran out of capacity" or "another customer caused this".

Preferred language:
- "We’re investigating your environment’s health signals and will share updates in this ticket. If we confirm broader platform impact, we’ll reflect that on our status page." 

## Internal validation links (reference)
Use the following internal assets for quick checks (do not share screenshots externally unless approved by Comms Lead + Legal for an enterprise exception):
- Status page component taxonomy (definitions + ownership): oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-component-taxonomy
- Status page update runbook (how to verify and post): oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/status-page-update-runbook
- Customer-facing status signals standard (thresholds, anti-flap, what qualifies as degraded): architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/customer-facing-status-signals-standard
- Component health dashboards (per region/capability): eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/status-page-component-health-dashboards
- SLO alignment (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private): eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/status-page-transparency-slo-alignment

## Customer-facing templates (approved)
### 1) Acknowledgement (status page currently green)
"Thanks for flagging this. We’re investigating the errors/latency you’re seeing and comparing them against our regional health signals now. If we confirm broader impact, we’ll reflect it on our public status page. In the meantime, could you share 3–5 example request IDs (with timestamps/timezone) so we can trace the failures end-to-end?"

### 2) Acknowledgement (active incident exists but scope disputed)
"Thanks — we see the active incident on our status page and we’re validating whether the impacted components/regions match what you’re experiencing. We’ll update the incident if we confirm additional scope. If you can share a few request IDs (with timestamps), we can validate routing/region and align the public component status accurately."

### 3) We confirmed a mismatch and updated status page
"We confirmed broader impact consistent with what you reported and have updated our public status page to reflect the affected components/regions. You can follow updates here: https://status.redwoodinference.com. We’ll continue to post progress there until fully resolved."

### 4) We did NOT confirm broader impact (yet)
"We’ve reviewed our platform health signals for the relevant region/components and we’re not currently seeing widespread impact. That said, we’re continuing to investigate your specific requests. If you can share additional request IDs (and the region/endpoint), we can narrow down whether this is route-specific, configuration-related, or isolated to a subset of traffic."

### 5) Customer follow-up for missing data
"To help us investigate quickly, could you provide: (1) 3–5 request IDs (or correlation IDs), (2) timestamps with timezone, (3) the endpoint you’re calling (chat/embeddings/reranking/streaming), and (4) whether the issue is continuous or intermittent? With those, we can trace the requests and confirm whether a status page update is warranted."

### 6) Post-incident summary request
"Yes — once the incident is resolved, we publish a customer-facing summary with impact and remediation details when appropriate. If you have specific information requirements (e.g., impact window, regions, mitigations), reply here and we’ll ensure they’re addressed in the published summary or in a direct follow-up if customer-specific." 

## Decision guide: common scenarios
### Scenario A: "Embeddings failing but API says Operational"
Likely causes:
- Component taxonomy split (Embeddings vs Chat) not reflected or misclassified.
Actions:
- Collect evidence: endpoint must be /v1/embeddings (or SDK embedding call), request IDs.
- Escalate with emphasis: "possible component misclassification".

### Scenario B: "Streaming disconnects" with low 5xx
Likely causes:
- Streaming-specific failure mode; generic error rate dashboards may remain green.
Actions:
- Collect: client type, timeout, disconnect rate estimate, request IDs.
- Escalate: include "streaming disconnect" keyword.

### Scenario C: Latency-only degradation
Likely causes:
- Capacity pressure, kernel regression, routing shift; may not cross incident thresholds.
Actions:
- Gather p95/p99 and endpoints; check status signal standard for latency thresholds.
- Escalate non-page unless enterprise-prod-down or corroborated by dashboards.

### Scenario D: Dedicated customer disputes public status
Likely causes:
- Dedicated environment isolated; public status page remains green.
Actions:
- Follow Dedicated/Private guidance; escalate to dedicated on-call; do not push public update unless shared control-plane.

## Escalation ladder (summary)
1) Support L1: intake + acknowledge + quick validation.
2) Support L2 / Escalations: ensure evidence complete; coordinate with CSM.
3) SRE On-call: validate internal signals + mapping; advise on public components.
4) IC/Comms Lead: publish/adjust status page updates; maintain incident timeline for post-incident summary.
5) Post-incident: link customer to published summary; capture feedback as an input to status accuracy audits.

## Logging and follow-ups
- Tag the ticket: "Status Page Accuracy".
- Add an internal note with:
  - affected component(s) suspected
  - region(s)
  - request IDs
  - whether a status page change occurred
- For enterprise disputes: create a short internal recap in #support-escalations and link the ticket.
- If status page was incorrect, file a follow-up task against the component mapping owner (SRE/Observability) with the evidence and timestamps.

## FAQ (Support/CS)
**Q: Can Support update the status page directly?**
A: Only if explicitly delegated for a specific incident by the IC/Comms Lead. Default is SRE/Comms Lead publishes.

**Q: Customer asks "Why wasn’t this on the status page?"**
A: Use neutral language: "The status page tracks platform component health; we’re validating whether this reflects broader impact. We will update it if we confirm it does." Avoid details about capacity partitioning or other customers.

**Q: Customer asks for dashboards/screenshots as proof.**
A: Do not share internal dashboards by default. If needed for an enterprise security review, request approval from Comms Lead + Legal (Michael Grant) and provide a sanitized written summary instead of screenshots whenever possible.

## Change log
- 2026-01-12: Aligned templates and escalation ladder with Comms Lead role and status page component taxonomy; clarified Dedicated/Private guidance; added streaming-specific scenario guidance.
- 2025-11-19: Initial version created as part of Status Page Transparency Upgrades.

title: Status Page Customer Escalation Playbook (Disputed Status Accuracy)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook is used by Support and Customer Success when a customer reports that the public status page does not reflect the impact they are experiencing ("status page says everything is OK, but we are failing"). It provides:
- A standard intake checklist to quickly validate what the customer is seeing.
- A clear escalation path to SRE/on-call and the incident Comms Lead.
- Approved customer-facing responses and guardrails.
- A consistent way to correct status page inaccuracies without increasing on-call burden.

This is a customer trust workflow. Our goals are (1) fast acknowledgement, (2) accurate classification to the right public component(s), and (3) avoiding speculative or customer-sensitive disclosures.

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following are true:
- Customer claims status page indicates "Operational" but they have errors/latency.
- Customer claims wrong component/region is marked degraded.
- Customer claims incident is missing (no active incident) while multiple customers are impacted.
- Customer requests proof/evidence of impact or asks for a post-incident summary while the status page remains green.

Do NOT use this playbook for:
- Single-tenant Dedicated or Private issues isolated to a single customer environment without broader platform impact (see "Dedicated/Private guidance").
- Pure how-to questions about interpreting the status page components (route to Docs/DevEx guidance).

## Definitions and guardrails
### What the public status page represents
- The status page reflects *platform component health* by product surface and region (e.g., Hosted API – US-East, Streaming – EU-West).
- It is not a guarantee that every request is successful for every customer, and it does not expose customer-specific capacity constraints.

### Required guardrails (must follow)
- Do not disclose: customer-specific capacity, noisy-neighbor details, account identifiers, or security-sensitive root cause.
- Do not over-promise SLA credits or contractual remedies in Support channels.
- Do not speculate on root cause. Stick to observed symptoms, mitigations underway, and next update timing.
- For Sev0/Sev1: do not publish new public status updates yourself unless explicitly authorized (see escalation section). Support/CS can draft wording, but SRE/IC owns publish unless delegated.

## Roles
- **Support L1 (Frontline):** intake, collect evidence, acknowledge customer, initiate escalation when criteria are met.
- **Support L2 / Escalations (Support Ops):** ensure complete evidence, coordinate with CS owner, maintain customer thread.
- **CS Owner / CSM:** manage stakeholder expectations, coordinate timelines and post-incident follow-up requests.
- **SRE On-call:** validates signals, confirms impacted public components, initiates or updates incident.
- **Incident Commander (IC):** owns incident handling and severity.
- **Comms Lead:** owns customer-facing updates (status page + internal alignment), ensures consistent wording.

## Targets (internal operating targets; not customer-facing commitments)
- **Acknowledgement to customer:** within 15 minutes for Sev0/Sev1 claims during business hours; within 30 minutes otherwise.
- **Time to escalate to SRE (if criteria met):** within 10 minutes of receiving required evidence.
- **Status page correction (if inaccurate):** as soon as SRE validates; prioritize before deep RCA when customer impact is ongoing.

## Step 1 — Intake checklist (copy/paste)
Collect the following information in the customer ticket/thread before escalating.

**Customer / environment**
- Customer name + contract type: Hosted API / Dedicated / Private
- Primary region(s) impacted (customer’s view): US-East / US-West / EU-West / APAC-SG, etc.
- Workspace/org ID (internal only) and support plan tier

**Time window**
- Impact start time (with timezone) and whether ongoing
- Whether impact is intermittent or continuous

**Symptoms (choose all that apply)**
- Elevated 5xx / 429 / timeouts
- Latency regression (p50/p95/p99)
- Streaming disconnects / stalled streams
- Embeddings failures
- Reranking failures
- Console access issues

**Request evidence**
- 3–5 example request IDs (or correlation IDs) with timestamps
- Example error messages and HTTP status codes
- Endpoint/route (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings) and SDK/version
- If streaming: transport details (SSE/websocket), and client timeout settings

**Customer impact**
- Severity from customer perspective (prod down vs degraded)
- Approximate failure rate if known (e.g., 10–20% of requests)
- Business impact (optional, customer-provided)

## Step 2 — Quick self-validation (Support/CS)
Before escalating, do a 3–5 minute check to avoid false alarms:
1) Check https://status.redwoodinference.com for active incidents and impacted components/regions.
2) Check internal dashboards for the relevant component/region (links in "Internal validation links").
3) Verify whether the symptom aligns with a component-specific failure mode (e.g., streaming disconnects may not show in generic API error rate).
4) If there is a mismatch, proceed to escalation.

If dashboards are fully green and evidence is weak (no request IDs, no timestamps), ask the customer for the missing items using the template in "Customer follow-up for missing data".

## Escalation criteria (when to page SRE)
Escalate to SRE On-call immediately if ANY are true:
- Multiple customers report similar symptoms in the same region within 30 minutes.
- Customer provides request IDs showing elevated 5xx/timeouts and internal dashboards show any correlated increase.
- Streaming disconnect spikes are reported (these are historically under-represented in generic error views).
- Enterprise customer indicates material production impact and the status page remains green.
- The customer disputes an active incident’s scope (wrong region/component) with evidence.

Escalate to SRE during business hours (non-page) if:
- Customer reports mild latency regression with no errors, and internal latency is elevated but below incident thresholds.
- Customer requests a retro/post-incident summary formatting change (route to Comms Lead + PM, not on-call).

## Step 3 — How to escalate (Slack + ticket)
### Slack channel routing
- Primary: **#support-escalations** (start here; keep customer context centralized)
- Add as needed:
  - **#incidents** (if you believe it should be treated as an incident)
  - **#eng-oncall** (for quick on-call attention if criteria met)
  - **#statuspage** (if your org uses a dedicated channel for status updates)

### Who to tag
- Tag **SRE On-call** (PagerDuty user: "SRE Oncall") when criteria are met.
- If an incident exists, tag the current **IC** and **Comms Lead** (from the incident channel topic / incident-bot output).

### Required escalation message format (copy/paste)
Subject: Potential status page mismatch — [Component?] [Region] — customer reports [symptom]

- Customer: [name] ([Hosted/Dedicated/Private], plan tier)
- Reported region(s): [US-East/EU-West/etc]
- Symptoms: [errors/latency/streaming disconnects/embeddings]
- Impact window: [start time] to [now]
- Evidence: request IDs + timestamps: [list]
- Current status page: [Operational / Incident ongoing / Wrong component]
- Our quick validation: [dashboard summary in 1–2 lines]
- Ask: please validate component mapping and advise whether to update status page / open incident

### Ticket hygiene
- Ensure the customer ticket includes the intake checklist.
- Add an internal note: "Status page accuracy dispute — escalated to SRE".
- If enterprise: notify the assigned CSM in the ticket and in Slack.

## Step 4 — What Support/CS should do while SRE validates
- Acknowledge the customer quickly using an approved template (below).
- Ask for any missing evidence (request IDs, timestamps) if needed.
- Do not state that an incident exists until SRE/IC confirms.
- If the customer is blocked, suggest short-term mitigations that are generally safe (e.g., retry with backoff, enable fallback model if they already use it, reduce concurrency) without implying a platform defect.

## Step 5 — If status page is inaccurate (correction workflow)
If SRE confirms a mismatch (wrong component/region or missing incident), the expected flow is:
1) SRE/IC identifies the correct public components (per taxonomy) and confirms status signal evidence.
2) Comms Lead posts or adjusts the status page update using the incident templates.
3) Support/CS follows up with the customer linking to the incident and summarizing the correction.

If the incident is Sev0/Sev1 and an approval step is required, Comms Lead will obtain the required approval (IC or Support lead, depending on incident workflow). Support should be ready to rapidly review wording for clarity (not for technical accuracy).

## Dedicated / Private guidance (important)
Dedicated and Private deployments may have customer-specific incidents that are not appropriate for the public status page.

Approved guidance:
- If a Dedicated/Private issue is isolated: acknowledge and treat it as a customer incident via the dedicated/private support path. Do not push for a public incident unless SRE confirms shared control-plane or multi-tenant platform impact.
- If the issue appears tied to shared control plane (auth, console, model registry, routing) affecting multiple dedicated customers: escalate as a potential public component impact ("Dedicated Control Plane") per taxonomy.

Never say:
- "Your cluster ran out of capacity" or "another customer caused this".

Preferred language:
- "We’re investigating your environment’s health signals and will share updates in this ticket. If we confirm broader platform impact, we’ll reflect that on our status page." 

## Internal validation links (reference)
Use the following internal assets for quick checks (do not share screenshots externally unless approved by Comms Lead + Legal for an enterprise exception):
- Status page component taxonomy (definitions + ownership): oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-component-taxonomy
- Status page update runbook (how to verify and post): oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/status-page-update-runbook
- Customer-facing status signals standard (thresholds, anti-flap, what qualifies as degraded): architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/customer-facing-status-signals-standard
- Component health dashboards (per region/capability): eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/status-page-component-health-dashboards
- SLO alignment (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private): eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/status-page-transparency-slo-alignment

## Customer-facing templates (approved)

"Thanks for flagging this. We’re investigating the errors/latency you’re seeing and comparing them against our regional health signals now. If we confirm broader impact, we’ll reflect it on our public status page. In the meantime, could you share 3–5 example request IDs (with timestamps/timezone) so we can trace the failures end-to-end?"

### 2) Acknowledgement (active incident exists but scope disputed)
"Thanks — we see the active incident on our status page and we’re validating whether the impacted components/regions match what you’re experiencing. We’ll update the incident if we confirm additional scope. If you can share a few request IDs (with timestamps), we can validate routing/region and align the public component status accurately."

### 3) We confirmed a mismatch and updated status page
"We confirmed broader impact consistent with what you reported and have updated our public status page to reflect the affected components/regions. You can follow updates here: https://status.redwoodinference.com. We’ll continue to post progress there until fully resolved."

### 4) We did NOT confirm broader impact (yet)
"We’ve reviewed our platform health signals for the relevant region/components and we’re not currently seeing widespread impact. That said, we’re continuing to investigate your specific requests. If you can share additional request IDs (and the region/endpoint), we can narrow down whether this is route-specific, configuration-related, or isolated to a subset of traffic."

### 5) Customer follow-up for missing data
"To help us investigate quickly, could you provide: (1) 3–5 request IDs (or correlation IDs), (2) timestamps with timezone, (3) the endpoint you’re calling (chat/embeddings/reranking/streaming), and (4) whether the issue is continuous or intermittent? With those, we can trace the requests and confirm whether a status page update is warranted."

### 6) Post-incident summary request
"Yes — once the incident is resolved, we publish a customer-facing summary with impact and remediation details when appropriate. If you have specific information requirements (e.g., impact window, regions, mitigations), reply here and we’ll ensure they’re addressed in the published summary or in a direct follow-up if customer-specific."

"Thanks for flagging this. We’re investigating the errors/latency you’re seeing and comparing them against our regional health signals now. If we confirm broader impact, we’ll reflect it on our public status page. In the meantime, could you share 3–5 example request IDs (with timestamps/timezone) so we can trace the failures end-to-end?"

### 2) Acknowledgement (active incident exists but scope disputed)
"Thanks — we see the active incident on our status page and we’re validating whether the impacted components/regions match what you’re experiencing. We’ll update the incident if we confirm additional scope. If you can share a few request IDs (with timestamps), we can validate routing/region and align the public component status accurately."

### 3) We confirmed a mismatch and updated status page
"We confirmed broader impact consistent with what you reported and have updated our public status page to reflect the affected components/regions. You can follow updates here: https://status.redwoodinference.com. We’ll continue to post progress there until fully resolved."

### 4) We did NOT confirm broader impact (yet)
"We’ve reviewed our platform health signals for the relevant region/components and we’re not currently seeing widespread impact. That said, we’re continuing to investigate your specific requests. If you can share additional request IDs (and the region/endpoint), we can narrow down whether this is route-specific, configuration-related, or isolated to a subset of traffic."

### 5) Customer follow-up for missing data
"To help us investigate quickly, could you provide: (1) 3–5 request IDs (or correlation IDs), (2) timestamps with timezone, (3) the endpoint you’re calling (chat/embeddings/reranking/streaming), and (4) whether the issue is continuous or intermittent? With those, we can trace the requests and confirm whether a status page update is warranted."

### 6) Post-incident summary request
"Yes — once the incident is resolved, we publish a customer-facing summary with impact and remediation details when appropriate. If you have specific information requirements (e.g., impact window, regions, mitigations), reply here and we’ll ensure they’re addressed in the published summary or in a direct follow-up if customer-specific." 

## Decision guide: common scenarios
### Scenario A: "Embeddings failing but API says Operational"
Likely causes:
- Component taxonomy split (Embeddings vs Chat) not reflected or misclassified.
Actions:
- Collect evidence: endpoint must be /v1/embeddings (or SDK embedding call), request IDs.
- Escalate with emphasis: "possible component misclassification".

### Scenario B: "Streaming disconnects" with low 5xx
Likely causes:
- Streaming-specific failure mode; generic error rate dashboards may remain green.
Actions:
- Collect: client type, timeout, disconnect rate estimate, request IDs.
- Escalate: include "streaming disconnect" keyword.

### Scenario C: Latency-only degradation
Likely causes:
- Capacity pressure, kernel regression, routing shift; may not cross incident thresholds.
Actions:
- Gather p95/p99 and endpoints; check status signal standard for latency thresholds.
- Escalate non-page unless enterprise-prod-down or corroborated by dashboards.

### Scenario D: Dedicated customer disputes public status
Likely causes:
- Dedicated environment isolated; public status page remains green.
Actions:
- Follow Dedicated/Private guidance; escalate to dedicated on-call; do not push public update unless shared control-plane.

## Escalation ladder (summary)
1) Support L1: intake + acknowledge + quick validation.
2) Support L2 / Escalations: ensure evidence complete; coordinate with CSM.
3) SRE On-call: validate internal signals + mapping; advise on public components.
4) IC/Comms Lead: publish/adjust status page updates; maintain incident timeline for post-incident summary.
5) Post-incident: link customer to published summary; capture feedback as an input to status accuracy audits.

## Logging and follow-ups
- Tag the ticket: "Status Page Accuracy".
- Add an internal note with:
  - affected component(s) suspected
  - region(s)
  - request IDs
  - whether a status page change occurred
- For enterprise disputes: create a short internal recap in #support-escalations and link the ticket.
- If status page was incorrect, file a follow-up task against the component mapping owner (SRE/Observability) with the evidence and timestamps.

## FAQ (Support/CS)
**Q: Can Support update the status page directly?**
A: Only if explicitly delegated for a specific incident by the IC/Comms Lead. Default is SRE/Comms Lead publishes.

**Q: Customer asks "Why wasn’t this on the status page?"**
A: Use neutral language: "The status page tracks platform component health; we’re validating whether this reflects broader impact. We will update it if we confirm it does." Avoid details about capacity partitioning or other customers.

**Q: Customer asks for dashboards/screenshots as proof.**
A: Do not share internal dashboards by default. If needed for an enterprise security review, request approval from Comms Lead + Legal (Michael Grant) and provide a sanitized written summary instead of screenshots whenever possible.

## Change log
- 2026-01-12: Aligned templates and escalation ladder with Comms Lead role and status page component taxonomy; clarified Dedicated/Private guidance; added streaming-specific scenario guidance.
- 2025-11-19: Initial version created as part of Status Page Transparency Upgrades.

source.title: Streaming chat responses can hang or stall (SSE): symptoms, causes, and workarounds
source.author: Vanessa Ortiz
source.body:
h1. Overview
Some customers report that *streaming chat completions* (SSE, stream=true) appear to *hang* or *stall* mid-response. The connection may stay open but no new chunks arrive, and the client may never receive the terminal *[DONE]* marker.

This page consolidates known symptoms, likely causes, and recommended mitigations for Support/CS.

h1. Customer-visible symptoms
* Stream starts and then stops emitting chunks (customer wording: "hang", "freeze", "stuck").
* No explicit error returned by the API; request appears in-flight indefinitely.
* Some clients retry and create duplicate assistant messages.

Common alternate descriptions seen in tickets:
* "SSE freezes"
* "stream stops after first chunk"
* "never sends [DONE]"
* "EventSource waits forever"

h1. Likely causes (multiple can apply)
h2. 1) Network/proxy idle timeouts when the stream goes quiet
If the backend pauses token emission (e.g., long decode steps, tool-calling, or internal buffering), some proxies/load balancers may consider the connection idle and interfere.

Mitigation:
* Ensure the gateway emits *SSE heartbeat frames* (comment line) when no tokens are emitted.
* For customer-managed proxies (NGINX), disable response buffering and increase read timeouts.

h2. 2) Client-side timeouts misinterpreted as a hang
Some SDKs/clients set an overall request timeout that does not reset on streamed bytes. Long generations can appear as "hung" when the client aborts.

Mitigation:
* For Typescript SDK, upgrade to a version that supports `streamTimeoutMs` and does not apply a short overall timeout by default for SSE.

h2. 3) True backend stalls (rare)
In some cases, the serving runtime may stop making forward progress under specific resource-pressure conditions. This manifests as an open stream with no additional tokens even though keepalive/heartbeat is active.

Mitigation:
* Collect request IDs, trace IDs, and peak-load context. Escalate to Serving Runtime team.

h1. What Support should ask for
1. Does this happen on *hosted*, *dedicated*, or *private*? Region?
2. Client type (browser EventSource, curl, Python requests, Node fetch, etc.).
3. Presence of proxies/ingress (NGINX, corporate proxy, CDN).
4. Example request IDs and approximate timestamps.

h1. Known related support tickets
* SUP-1842 (AcmeCo, hosted): stall mid-response; mitigated by gateway heartbeat.
* SUP-1869 (Northwind Health, private): intermittent freeze; resolved by private upgrade + NGINX settings.
* SUP-1901 (Globex, dedicated): stops after first chunk; mitigated by heartbeat rollout + proxy exception.
* SUP-1917 (Initech, self-serve): TS SDK timeout made it appear hung; resolved by SDK upgrade.
* SUP-1933 (Umbrella, dedicated): suspected runtime stall under peak load; investigation ongoing.

h1. Slack context
See #support thread_ts=1764717303 for cross-ticket summary and current owners.

source.title: Hosted API overload errors: support guidance, retry/backoff, and escalation
source.author: Dev Patel
source.content:
## Purpose
This page helps Support/CS respond to customer reports of new “overload protection” errors (circuit breakers + load shedding) on Redwood Hosted API.

Goals:
- Explain what the customer is seeing (stable semantics vs ambiguous timeouts/5xx).
- Provide canonical retry/backoff guidance to prevent retry storms.
- Show how to interpret correlation IDs so Engineering can quickly trace requests.
- Define escalation criteria and the minimum information to collect.

## What changed (customer-facing)
When the platform is saturated (GPU capacity, scheduler backlog, downstream dependency brownout), Redwood may reject requests *early* and predictably rather than allowing them to time out or generate random 5xx.

Customers may now see:
- **429** responses for admission / quota / per-tenant caps.
- **503** responses when a regional/model circuit breaker is open or the system is actively shedding load.

These responses:
- Include stable **error codes** and a machine-readable JSON payload.
- Typically include a **Retry-After** header to indicate safe retry timing.
- Include Redwood request/correlation IDs for tracing.

## Quick triage checklist (Support)
1) Confirm this is an overload-protection response (see “How to recognize” below).
2) Verify the customer is following recommended retry/backoff (or advise them to change).
3) Collect required debug info (IDs, time window, region/model, response headers).
4) Determine if this is expected transient overload vs a bug/misconfig (missing Retry-After, incorrect status mapping, fallback not triggering).
5) Escalate to Eng Oncall when criteria are met (see “When to escalate”).

## How to recognize overload-protection responses
### Common HTTP statuses
- **429 Too Many Requests**
  - Used for admission controls such as per-tenant concurrency caps, token buckets, or tier limits.
  - Usually safe to retry with backoff if Retry-After is provided.

- **503 Service Unavailable**
  - Used when the platform is overloaded at the regional/model level (breaker open) or actively shedding load to protect SLOs.
  - Usually safe to retry with backoff and/or reduce load. May persist until capacity recovers.

### Expected headers (may vary by route)
- **Retry-After**: integer seconds is preferred (e.g., `Retry-After: 2`).
- **x-redwood-request-id**: unique ID for a single request attempt.
- **x-redwood-correlation-id**: groups related attempts across services (gateway → routing → runtime). Some clients/logs only show one of these; either is helpful.

If the response is 429/503 *and* includes a Redwood overload error code in the JSON body, treat it as overload protection.

## Error payload: what customers see
Overload errors are additive and JSON-compatible with existing response parsing.

Typical shape (example):
- `error.type`: `overload_error` (or `rate_limit_error` in some cases)
- `error.code`: stable machine code
- `error.message`: human-readable
- `error.request_id`: mirrors `x-redwood-request-id` where available
- `error.correlation_id`: mirrors `x-redwood-correlation-id` where available

Example snippet (illustrative only):
{
  "error": {
    "type": "overload_error",
    "code": "BREAKER_OPEN",
    "message": "The service is temporarily overloaded. Please retry after the specified delay.",
    "request_id": "req_01J9...",
    "correlation_id": "corr_01J9..."
  }
}

## Canonical mapping: status → meaning → what we tell customers
Use this table to drive consistent customer guidance.

| HTTP | Typical meaning | What Support should say | Customer action |
|------|------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 429 | Admission control / rate limiting / tier cap hit | “Your request was rejected to protect system stability. This is not a model failure; it indicates you are sending requests faster than the current allowed rate/concurrency.” | Respect Retry-After; implement exponential backoff + jitter; reduce concurrency; consider batching; if persistent, discuss tier/capacity options. |
| 503 | Regional/model breaker open or load shedding | “Redwood is temporarily overloaded in the requested region/model. We are rejecting early to avoid timeouts and protect overall reliability.” | Respect Retry-After; backoff + jitter; optionally route to another region/model if configured; reduce max_tokens/streaming concurrency; retry with bounded attempts. |

Notes:
- If the customer reports **timeouts** with no JSON error, treat as a separate issue (possible client/network or upstream timeouts) and escalate based on impact.
- If the customer gets **5xx** without the overload payload, collect details; it may be an unrelated incident.

## Retry & backoff guidance (what we should recommend)
### Golden rules
- **Always respect Retry-After** when provided.
- **Use exponential backoff with jitter**; do not retry immediately.
- **Cap retries** to avoid amplifying overload.
- **Reduce concurrency** rather than increasing it during overload.
- Prefer **idempotency keys** for safe retries on non-streaming requests.

### Recommended algorithm (support-approved)
If `Retry-After` is present:
- Sleep `Retry-After` seconds, then retry.

If `Retry-After` is missing:
- Backoff schedule suggestion: 0.5s, 1s, 2s, 4s, then stop (max 4 retries), each with ±20–40% jitter.

Bounds:
- Maximum total retry time budget: **~10–20 seconds** for interactive apps, **~60 seconds** for batch jobs.
- Maximum concurrent retries: keep to a small fraction of steady-state concurrency (e.g., 10–20%).

### Streaming-specific guidance
Streaming workloads can worsen overload if clients open many concurrent streams.
- Recommend **lower streaming concurrency** during peak periods.
- If the customer can tolerate it, recommend **shorter outputs** (reduce `max_tokens`) and/or **shorter prompts**.
- If a stream fails mid-response with an overload error on reconnect attempts, advise them to back off and resume with a bounded number of attempts.

### What NOT to recommend
- “Just retry in a tight loop.”
- “Retry immediately with the same concurrency.”
- “Spin up many parallel retries to get through.”

## How to interpret and use correlation IDs
### Which IDs to ask for
Ask the customer for (in order of preference):
1) `x-redwood-correlation-id` (best for cross-service tracing)
2) `x-redwood-request-id` (single request attempt)
3) Timestamp + region + model + route if IDs are not available

### Where customers can find them
- Response headers in their HTTP client.
- SDK exception fields (Python/TypeScript SDKs surface request IDs; newer versions surface correlation IDs when present).
- Their application logs if they log response headers.

### How Support should use them internally
- Paste the ID(s) into the internal log search / tracing tool.
- Capture:
  - the **error.code**
  - the **route** (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/responses`, embeddings endpoint)
  - the **region** (requested + served)
  - whether **Retry-After** was present and its value

If Support cannot access dashboards/tracing, escalate with the IDs and all details in the escalation template below.

## Common customer questions + approved responses
### “Is Redwood down?”
Approved response:
“Not necessarily. These errors indicate Redwood is protecting service reliability during temporary saturation in a specific region/model. We reject early with clear retry guidance to avoid timeouts and degraded performance. Please follow the Retry-After header and exponential backoff. If the issue persists beyond a short window, share correlation IDs and we’ll investigate.”

### “Why am I seeing 503 if this is overload?”
Approved response:
“503 is used when the platform is temporarily unable to serve the request due to load shedding or an open circuit breaker. It’s a deliberate, controlled response (with stable error codes) to prevent cascading failures and long tail latency.”

### “Should I fail over to another model/region?”
Guidance:
- If they have **fallback routing configured** (Dedicated/enterprise Hosted tiers or configured routing policies), yes—recommend they enable/verify it.
- Otherwise, advise them to implement client-side fallback only if they can validate output compatibility and safety.

### “Can you increase my limits?”
Guidance:
- Confirm whether they are hitting **429** (likely limit-related) vs **503** (regional/model saturation).
- If 429 is persistent at normal traffic levels, route to CS for capacity/tier discussion.
- If 503 is persistent, treat as reliability issue and escalate to Eng.

## Known issues (Support-facing)
1) **Retry-After header missing on some overload responses**
- Symptom: Customer receives 429/503 overload payload but no Retry-After.
- Workaround: Recommend exponential backoff with jitter as above.
- Action: Escalate if reproducible; include route + correlation IDs.

2) **Fallback model not triggering as expected**
- Symptom: Customer has configured fallback but still sees repeated 503 without failover.
- Workaround: Customer can temporarily specify an alternate model/region explicitly (if acceptable) while we investigate.
- Action: Escalate; include tenant ID, configured fallback policy (what they intended), and correlation IDs.

3) **Customer interprets 503 as “platform outage”**
- Guidance: Use the approved response language; direct them to Retry-After and backoff guidance.

## When to escalate to Engineering (Eng Oncall / SRE)
Escalate immediately if any of the following are true:
- **Enterprise customer** reporting sustained overload errors impacting production.
- Overload errors persist for **>15 minutes** with correct backoff behavior.
- **No Retry-After** observed on multiple responses for the same route/region/model.
- Suspected **misclassification** (e.g., 500s that should be 429/503 overload, or overload codes returned with 200/stream interruptions).
- **Fallback routing configured** but not activating.
- Customer reports sharp regression (new errors) coincident with a canary/rollout and provides IDs.

## Escalation template (copy/paste)
Subject: Hosted API overload errors – customer impact

- Customer / tenant: 
- Tier: (Self-serve Hosted / Enterprise Hosted / Dedicated)
- Primary route(s): (e.g., /v1/chat/completions)
- Model(s): 
- Region(s): requested / observed
- Time window (with timezone): 
- Error HTTP status: 429 / 503
- Error code(s) from body: 
- Retry-After present? value(s): 
- x-redwood-correlation-id(s): 
- x-redwood-request-id(s): 
- Customer retry behavior (describe): 
- Business impact: (prod down / degraded / batch delay)
- Attachments: sample responses (headers + body), client logs

## Internal-only: where to look (for Support with access)
- Overload dashboards: shed rate, breaker-open rate, queue depth, saturation signals by region/model.
- Incident channels: #eng-oncall, #incidents (look for breaker announcements).
- Runbook: “Hosted overload / circuit breaker runbook” (linked above).

## Revision history
- 2025-11-10: Initial publication for overload error rollout (Support readiness).
- 2025-12-05: Updated retry guidance; clarified correlation IDs and escalation thresholds.
