Automated health queries (examples)
- Runtime p95 latency (Prometheus):
  curl -G 'https://prom.redwood.internal/api/v1/query' --data-urlencode 'query=histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_bucket[14d])) by (le, service))'

- Ticket backlog (Zendesk API):
  curl -s -u user:token 'https://redwood.zendesk.com/api/v2/search.json?query=type:ticket tags:customer-<shortname> status<solved'

Health-based playbook actions
1. Watch (score 60–79) — Trigger: weekly score in Watch for 2 consecutive weeks
   - CSM action: schedule technical health sync within 3 business days; prioritize top 3 impediments.
   - SE action: run targeted perf profile and provide tuning recommendations within 5 business days.
   - If no improvement in 14 days, create cross-functional incident review and escalate to Customer Success Manager Lead.

2. At-Risk (score < 60) — Trigger: score below 60 or a P1 incident
   - Immediate: CSM to notify customer and schedule a priority remediation call within 24 hours.
   - Support: open a P1 ticket and assign on-call engineer; aim MTTR <= 8 hours for infra-related issues.
   - Engineering: owner from eng-platform assigned within 4 hours.
   - Execute rollback/fallback policy if degradation impacts business-critical flows.

Communication templates
- Initial health alert email (CSM -> customer & internal):
  Subject: "Redwood: Health alert for <customer> — action requested"
  Body: "Summary of triggered condition, current score, immediate mitigation steps, scheduled remediation call (time), owner list."

- Internal escalation Slack template (channel: #cs-escalations):
  /create-escalation customer=<shortname> severity=watch score=72 owner=@samira

QBR and cadence
- 30/60/90 checkpoints (structured):
  - 30 day: Technical validation complete, baseline report accepted, initial health score recorded.
  - 60 day: Integration complete, canary pass, go/no-go decision.
  - 90 day: Go-live, baseline of production SLOs, initial Optimize recommendations applied.

- Quarterly Business Review (QBR) cadence:
  - Pre-QBR (2 weeks before): collect usage trends, cost delta, top incidents, CSAT. SE provides performance retrospective and Optimize outcomes.
  - QBR agenda: Exec summary, Adoption metrics, Reliability review, Cost & optimization plan, Roadmap for next quarter, Ask & support items.
  - Artifacts: QBR deck, one-pager action plan with owners and due dates (stored on customer Confluence page).

Runbooks & playbooks (links and attachments)
- Canary deployment checklist (attachment): steps to execute 5% canary, metrics to watch, rollback criteria.
- P1 incident runbook (support-process): escalation path, paging list, incident comms templates.
- Cost spike investigation playbook: telemetry queries, billing drill-down steps, temporary throttling guidance.

Ownership & SLAs
- CSM: overall customer relationship, adoption milestones, QBR owner. SLA: respond to health alerts within 1 business day.
- SE (Onboarding): technical integration, validation, baseline testing. SLA: action on technical escalations within 2 business days (urgent incidents within 4 hours).
- Support: ticket triage & resolution; P1 MTTR target 8 hours, P2 target 48 hours.
- Engineering (eng-platform): take ownership of platform incidents routed by Support; respond to escalations within 4 hours.

Templates and artifacts (copyable content)
1) Baseline report template (CSV/Markdown)
- Customer, deployment type, model(s), test date, p50/p95 latency, throughput, error rate, recommended batching/cache/quantization profile, notes/action items.

2) Onboarding calendar (sample week-by-week)
- Week 0: Kickoff, environment setup
- Week 1: Baseline tests, SDK integration
- Week 2–3: Integration & retries, build test harness
- Week 4: Canary & tuning
- Week 6–8: Scale to production traffic
- Week 12: Optimization & QBR prep

Operational notes & known workarounds
- Private VPCs: If customer reports intermittent egress connection resets when KV cache is enabled, recommend toggling kv-cache.ttl from 30s -> 10s and enable connection keepalives on client side as an interim workaround. Escalate to eng-platform for root cause analysis.
- Quantization-induced hallucinations: For customers using 4-bit quantized variants, if quality regressions appear on specific prompt families, pin to a higher-fidelity model variant for those routes and file a model-quality ticket with Applied ML team (label: model-quality/<customer>).

Measurement and reporting
- Dashboard: CSM Health dashboard (Grafana) updated daily by automated ETL. Manual health review monthly during CSM 1:1.
- QBR deliverables: deck, action items sheet, consolidated health score history (CSV).

Appendices
- Appendix A: Access checklist (required credentials, network ranges, KMS config, support contacts)
- Appendix B: Sample Jira labels and workflows for onboarding (use label onboarding/<customer-shortname> and component=onboarding)
- Appendix C: SLA summary table (P1/P2 MTTR, alert response windows)

How to use this doc
- CSMs: follow activation milestones sequentially and update the customer Confluence page at each milestone.
- SE: attach baseline reports and performance notes to the customer page.
- Support: triage tickets in the context of current health state; notify CSM on any P1/P2 creation.

Revision history
- 2025-01-15: Created by Samira Patel (initial draft).
- 2025-03-12: Updated health weights and Watch/At-Risk thresholds after pilot with three enterprise customers (reviewers: Ethan Park, Maya Lin).
- 2025-06-02: Published with added automation queries and runbook links.

source.title: Dedicated Throughput SLO Escalation Playbook
source.author: Dev Patel
source.content:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the end-to-end process for handling Dedicated **Throughput SLO** misses or suspected misses. It is designed for Customer Success (CS) and Support to:
- Triage quickly and consistently.
- Engage the correct on-call teams (SRE / Infra / Observability) at the right time.
- Communicate clearly with customers, including status updates and written summaries.
- Route service-credit / compensation requests through the correct approval path.

This page is **restricted** because it includes internal paging triggers and credit approval guidance.

---

## What is the Dedicated Throughput SLO (customer-facing summary)
Dedicated Throughput SLO guarantees a **minimum sustained throughput floor** for an eligible Dedicated deployment under defined conditions.

**High-level definition (internal summary):**
- Metric: throughput measured as **billable output tokens per second**, computed per deployment, aggregated over a rolling window.
- Scope: Dedicated deployments only (not Hosted API, not eval/sandbox deployments).
- Eligibility: model/version and configuration must be within the customer’s contracted Dedicated configuration (e.g., pinned model family/version, supported max context, supported shape).

**Common non-eligibility / exclusions (not exhaustive):**
- Customer-side throttling or client concurrency limits causing underutilization.
- Customer workload violating sizing assumptions (e.g., contexts above configured limit, unsupported parameter combinations).
- Warm-up periods after scale-from-zero or cold start (tier-dependent; see “Warm pool / startup” below).
- Incidents due to customer networking/VPC misconfig in Private connectivity paths (if applicable).

When in doubt, treat as potentially eligible, start the triage, and engage SRE.

---

## Roles and ownership during an SLO event
**Support/CS (Incident Driver for customer comms)**
- Owns the customer thread, updates, and expectation management.
- Owns the case record: timeline, impact, and customer confirmations.
- Initiates escalation and ensures correct teams are engaged.

**SRE On-Call (Technical Incident Lead)**
- Confirms whether the SLO is breached per the canonical evaluator.
- Drives mitigation and coordinates cross-functional response.
- Provides internal/external technical summary for comms.

**Infra/Fleet On-Call (Capacity / shape / provisioning owner)**
- Addresses capacity constraints, noisy-neighbor isolation issues, node failures, and shape-level performance anomalies.

**Observability (as-needed)**
- Validates metric correctness (e.g., streaming accounting anomalies), dashboard discrepancies, missing tags.

**Product Dedicated (as-needed)**
- Confirms entitlement interpretation (tier rules, burst policy, failover posture).
- Aligns on customer-facing messaging for edge cases.

**Finance/FP&A + Legal (as-needed for credits/comp)**
- Approves service credits above policy thresholds and any non-standard compensation.

---

## Severity levels (Support/CS view)
Use these severities to standardize response; SRE may map to their incident severities.

**SEV-1 (Critical, likely customer-impacting breach)**
- Premium tier customer reports sustained throughput below floor OR SRE burn-rate alert is firing.
- Clear production impact (e.g., queue buildup, timeouts, user-visible latency spikes).
- Immediate paging required.

**SEV-2 (High, likely degradation / at-risk of breach)**
- Throughput is below expected but not yet confirmed as an SLO breach.
- Customer impact is moderate OR limited to a subset of traffic/routes.
- Paging may be required depending on tier and duration.

**SEV-3 (Moderate, investigation / metrics discrepancy)**
- Conflicting metrics (customer vs Redwood dashboards), short-lived dips, or suspected measurement artifacts.
- No clear production impact.

---

## Paging and escalation triggers (when to page SRE / Infra)
### Page SRE On-Call immediately when any of the following is true
- Premium tier customer: reported throughput below committed floor for **>10 minutes**, AND they confirm they are not client-throttled.
- Any tier: a Dedicated Throughput SLO **burn-rate alert** is firing for the deployment.
- Any tier: repeated or escalating customer timeouts/5xx correlated with throughput drop.
- Any tier: throughput drop correlates with recent Redwood changes (rollout, routing, driver/runtime update).

### Page Infra/Fleet On-Call when any of the following is true (often in parallel)
- GPU/node health signals indicate failures, evictions, ECC errors, or repeated container restarts.
- Saturation: GPU utilization pinned while throughput drops (suspected contention, thermal throttling, network bottleneck).
- Capacity: the deployment appears mapped to the wrong pool/shape or lacks the expected headroom.
- Region-wide capacity constraint impacting Dedicated pools.

### Engage Observability (not a page; async unless SEV-1)
- Throughput dashboards disagree with billing/export numbers.
- Recent metric changes (e.g., streaming token accounting) suspected.
- Missing labels (deployment_id, model, region) prevents accurate attribution.

---

## Triage checklist (Support/CS)
Perform steps 1–8 before (or while) paging; do not block paging for SEV-1.

### 1) Confirm customer context (capture in ticket)
Collect:
- Deployment ID(s)
- Customer name + environment (prod/stage)
- Region
- Tier (Standard / Plus / Premium)
- Model + version (exact identifier)
- Shape (if known)
- Time window of impact (with timezone)
- Symptoms (queueing, latency, timeouts, functional failure)

### 2) Confirm the customer is driving enough load
Ask:
- Current request concurrency
- Any client-side rate limiting (explicit QPS cap, worker pool limit)
- Any recent application deploy or traffic change

If the customer is under-driving load, throughput may be low without being an SLO failure.

### 3) Validate “eligibility” red flags quickly
- Context lengths: are they within contracted max context / recommended ranges?
- Parameters: extreme max_tokens or non-streaming vs streaming changes?
- Model/version pin: did they change models recently?

### 4) Check the Dedicated Throughput dashboard for that deployment
Look for:
- Rolling-window throughput vs committed floor
- Error rate/timeout correlation
- GPU saturation, queue depth, admission control shedding
- Recent routing pool changes or failovers

### 5) Check if this is a metrics discrepancy
If customer numbers differ from Redwood:
- Ask what they are measuring (wall-clock end-to-end, tokens counted, streaming vs non-streaming).
- Request a single representative request ID and a 10–20 minute sample window.

### 6) Determine if the issue is isolated or systemic
- One deployment vs multiple
- One region vs multiple
- One model family vs multiple

### 7) Mitigation options you can offer immediately (tier-dependent)
These are customer-facing actions CS/Support may propose, with SRE confirmation where possible:
- Temporarily reduce max context / max output tokens (if within acceptable product behavior).
- Temporarily shift traffic to the contracted primary route only (avoid experimental routes).
- Enable/disable streaming based on their client constraints (note: streaming affects perceived latency but should not invalidate throughput).
- If the customer has multi-deployment setup: fail over to a secondary deployment (Premium: may be Redwood-assisted).

Do not promise failover across regions unless the tier/contract explicitly includes it.

### 8) Open an incident record (internal) if SEV-1/SEV-2
- Create/associate the internal incident ticket.
- Add the customer support ticket link.
- Add a running timeline (timestamps for all key events).

---

## Warm pool / startup considerations
Some tiers include warm pool guarantees (or faster recovery expectations). During triage, identify if the customer is within a warm-up window:
- Did the deployment recently scale from zero?
- Was there a recent model reload, config change, or node replacement?
- Did traffic resume after prolonged inactivity?

If warm-up is suspected, note it explicitly in the customer thread and ask SRE to confirm whether the event is excluded by policy.

---

## Customer communications guide
### Communication principles
- Use a single owner for customer updates (CS/Support), even if multiple engineering teams are engaged.
- Acknowledge impact quickly; avoid speculating on root cause.
- Provide time-bound next update (“next update in 30 minutes”).
- For Premium accounts, default to more frequent updates and written summaries.

### Target update cadence (recommended)
- SEV-1: initial response within 15 minutes; updates every 30 minutes until stable.
- SEV-2: initial response within 1 hour; updates every 2–4 hours until stable.
- SEV-3: initial response within 1 business day; updates daily or as material changes occur.

---

## Templates (copy/paste)
### Template A — Initial acknowledgement (suspected throughput miss)
Subject: Dedicated deployment throughput investigation (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

Thanks for flagging this. We’re investigating reduced throughput on your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}) in {{region}}.

To help us confirm impact and correlate logs/metrics, could you share:
- The approximate start time and whether the issue is ongoing
- The model + version you’re using
- A representative request ID (or a small set) from the impacted window
- Any recent traffic/concurrency changes on your side

We’ll provide the next update by {{time}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template B — Confirmed incident + engaged on-call
Subject: Update: Dedicated throughput degradation (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

We’ve confirmed a throughput degradation affecting your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}). We’ve engaged our Site Reliability Engineering and Infrastructure on-call teams and are actively mitigating.

Current status:
- Impact: {{impact_summary}}
- Start time (UTC): {{start_time_utc}}
- Mitigation in progress: {{mitigation_summary}}

Next update by {{time}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template C — Request customer confirmation on throttling / load
Subject: Quick check: request load/concurrency for Dedicated deployment {{deployment_id}}

Hi {{customer_name}},

To confirm whether the deployment is being fully utilized during the impacted window, can you confirm:
- Current concurrency / worker count hitting the deployment
- Whether you have any client-side QPS limits or retries/backoff changes
- Whether the workload mix changed (context length, max output tokens, streaming on/off)

This helps us differentiate capacity/perf issues from under-driven traffic.

Thanks,
{{agent_name}}

### Template D — Resolution note + next steps
Subject: Resolved: Dedicated throughput issue (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

The throughput issue affecting your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}) is now resolved as of {{resolved_time_utc}}.

Summary:
- Root cause (preliminary): {{rca_summary}}
- What we changed: {{mitigation_actions}}
- Verification: throughput has been stable above the committed floor over the last {{window}}.

We’ll follow up with a written incident summary and, if applicable under your Dedicated tier terms, guidance on service credits.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template E — Service credit intake (do not pre-approve)
Subject: Dedicated throughput incident — service credit review

Hi {{customer_name}},

We can review this incident for service credits under your Dedicated terms. To proceed, we will document:
- Deployment ID(s)
- Impact window (UTC)
- Confirmed SLO measurement from Redwood’s evaluator

We’ll share the outcome of the credit review by {{date}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

---

## Service credits / compensation (internal guidance)
**Important:** Do not commit to a credit amount in writing until approvals are complete.

### Which tiers are typically credit-eligible?
- **Premium:** written throughput commitment; credit review is standard for confirmed breaches.
- **Plus:** may be eligible depending on contract addenda; treat as “review required.”
- **Standard:** generally best-effort; credit requests require Product + Finance approval.

### When to initiate a credit review
Initiate when:
- SRE confirms an SLO breach per canonical evaluator, AND
- The breach is not excluded (e.g., customer throttling), AND
- The customer requests credits OR the account team recommends proactive review.

### Approval path (default)
1) CS/Support compiles incident details and SRE confirmation.
2) SRE provides breach window + summary.
3) Product Dedicated confirms entitlement interpretation (tier/exclusions).
4) Finance (Laura Bennett / delegate) validates policy compliance and amount.
5) Legal (Sofia Mendes / delegate) reviews if there is contract ambiguity or if a non-standard remedy is proposed.
6) CS communicates outcome to customer.

### What to include in the internal credit request
- Customer + contract/tier
- Deployment ID(s)
- Breach window (start/end in UTC)
- Evidence links: dashboard screenshots/links, evaluator output
- Customer impact summary (what broke, business impact if shared)
- Exclusion checks performed (throttling, workload constraints, warm-up)
- Proposed remedy type (service credit vs other)

---

## Evidence and logging requirements
For any SEV-1/SEV-2 and any credit review, capture:
- Customer ticket link
- Incident link (if created)
- Dashboard links (throughput vs floor, saturation, errors)
- Request IDs / traces used for debugging
- Summary of customer-side traffic confirmation
- Timeline of mitigations and when throughput stabilized

---

## Common failure modes and quick routing hints
- **Customer sees lower throughput than Redwood reports:** often a measurement mismatch (streaming token accounting, end-to-end latency vs throughput, counting input tokens). Engage Observability if persistent.
- **Throughput drops with GPU pegged:** suspect contention, node health, thermal throttling, network bottleneck; page Infra.
- **Throughput drops with low utilization:** suspect admission control limiting, customer throttling, or workload shift to longer contexts; page SRE; ask customer for concurrency/workload details.
- **Regional capacity constraint / failover behavior:** check tier failover posture; page Infra and SRE; do not promise cross-region without tier/contract confirmation.

---

## Post-incident expectations
For confirmed Premium-tier breaches (and significant Plus-tier events), request:
- SRE-written incident summary within 5 business days.
- Follow-up action items tagged to owning teams (SRE/Infra/Observability/Routing).
- If credits were issued: ensure the billing/metering record reflects committed vs burst usage correctly.

---

## Appendix: Minimal internal handoff note (for paging)
When paging SRE/Infra, include:
- “Dedicated Throughput SLO: suspected/confirmed”
- Deployment ID
- Tier + region
- Time window and whether ongoing
- Customer-confirmed load/concurrency (if available)
- Dashboard link
- Any correlated symptoms (timeouts, 5xx, queue depth)

This reduces back-and-forth and speeds mitigation.

Subject: Dedicated throughput incident — service credit review

Hi {{customer_name}},

We can review this incident for service credits under your Dedicated terms. To proceed, we will document:
- Deployment ID(s)
- Impact window (UTC)
- Confirmed SLO measurement from Redwood’s evaluator

We’ll share the outcome of the credit review by {{date}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

---

## Service credits / compensation (internal guidance)
**Important:** Do not commit to a credit amount in writing until approvals are complete.

### Which tiers are typically credit-eligible?
- **Premium:** written throughput commitment; credit review is standard for confirmed breaches.
- **Plus:** may be eligible depending on contract addenda; treat as “review required.”
- **Standard:** generally best-effort; credit requests require Product + Finance approval.

### When to initiate a credit review
Initiate when:
- SRE confirms an SLO breach per canonical evaluator, AND
- The breach is not excluded (e.g., customer throttling), AND
- The customer requests credits OR the account team recommends proactive review.

### Approval path (default)
1) CS/Support compiles incident details and SRE confirmation.
2) SRE provides breach window + summary.
3) Product Dedicated confirms entitlement interpretation (tier/exclusions).
4) Finance (Laura Bennett / delegate) validates policy compliance and amount.
5) Legal (Sofia Mendes / delegate) reviews if there is contract ambiguity or if a non-standard remedy is proposed.
6) CS communicates outcome to customer.

### What to include in the internal credit request
- Customer + contract/tier
- Deployment ID(s)
- Breach window (start/end in UTC)
- Evidence links: dashboard screenshots/links, evaluator output
- Customer impact summary (what broke, business impact if shared)
- Exclusion checks performed (throttling, workload constraints, warm-up)
- Proposed remedy type (service credit vs other)

---

## Evidence and logging requirements
For any SEV-1/SEV-2 and any credit review, capture:
- Customer ticket link
- Incident link (if created)
- Dashboard links (throughput vs floor, saturation, errors)
- Request IDs / traces used for debugging
- Summary of customer-side traffic confirmation
- Timeline of mitigations and when throughput stabilized

---

## Common failure modes and quick routing hints
- **Customer sees lower throughput than Redwood reports:** often a measurement mismatch (streaming token accounting, end-to-end latency vs throughput, counting input tokens). Engage Observability if persistent.
- **Throughput drops with GPU pegged:** suspect contention, node health, thermal throttling, network bottleneck; page Infra.
- **Throughput drops with low utilization:** suspect admission control limiting, customer throttling, or workload shift to longer contexts; page SRE; ask customer for concurrency/workload details.
- **Regional capacity constraint / failover behavior:** check tier failover posture; page Infra and SRE; do not promise cross-region without tier/contract confirmation.

---

## Post-incident expectations
For confirmed Premium-tier breaches (and significant Plus-tier events), request:
- SRE-written incident summary within 5 business days.
- Follow-up action items tagged to owning teams (SRE/Infra/Observability/Routing).
- If credits were issued: ensure the billing/metering record reflects committed vs burst usage correctly.

---

## Appendix: Minimal internal handoff note (for paging)
When paging SRE/Infra, include:
- “Dedicated Throughput SLO: suspected/confirmed”
- Deployment ID
- Tier + region
- Time window and whether ongoing
- Customer-confirmed load/concurrency (if available)
- Dashboard link
- Any correlated symptoms (timeouts, 5xx, queue depth)

This reduces back-and-forth and speeds mitigation.
text:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the end-to-end process for handling Dedicated **Throughput SLO** misses or suspected misses. It is designed for Customer Success (CS) and Support to:
- Triage quickly and consistently.
- Engage the correct on-call teams (SRE / Infra / Observability) at the right time.
- Communicate clearly with customers, including status updates and written summaries.
- Route service-credit / compensation requests through the correct approval path.

This page is **restricted** because it includes internal paging triggers and credit approval guidance.

---

## What is the Dedicated Throughput SLO (customer-facing summary)
Dedicated Throughput SLO guarantees a **minimum sustained throughput floor** for an eligible Dedicated deployment under defined conditions.

**High-level definition (internal summary):**
- Metric: throughput measured as **billable output tokens per second**, computed per deployment, aggregated over a rolling window.
- Scope: Dedicated deployments only (not Hosted API, not eval/sandbox deployments).
- Eligibility: model/version and configuration must be within the customer’s contracted Dedicated configuration (e.g., pinned model family/version, supported max context, supported shape).

**Common non-eligibility / exclusions (not exhaustive):**
- Customer-side throttling or client concurrency limits causing underutilization.
- Customer workload violating sizing assumptions (e.g., contexts above configured limit, unsupported parameter combinations).
- Warm-up periods after scale-from-zero or cold start (tier-dependent; see “Warm pool / startup” below).
- Incidents due to customer networking/VPC misconfig in Private connectivity paths (if applicable).

When in doubt, treat as potentially eligible, start the triage, and engage SRE.

---

## Roles and ownership during an SLO event
**Support/CS (Incident Driver for customer comms)**
- Owns the customer thread, updates, and expectation management.
- Owns the case record: timeline, impact, and customer confirmations.
- Initiates escalation and ensures correct teams are engaged.

**SRE On-Call (Technical Incident Lead)**
- Confirms whether the SLO is breached per the canonical evaluator.
- Drives mitigation and coordinates cross-functional response.
- Provides internal/external technical summary for comms.

**Infra/Fleet On-Call (Capacity / shape / provisioning owner)**
- Addresses capacity constraints, noisy-neighbor isolation issues, node failures, and shape-level performance anomalies.

**Observability (as-needed)**
- Validates metric correctness (e.g., streaming accounting anomalies), dashboard discrepancies, missing tags.

**Product Dedicated (as-needed)**
- Confirms entitlement interpretation (tier rules, burst policy, failover posture).
- Aligns on customer-facing messaging for edge cases.

**Finance/FP&A + Legal (as-needed for credits/comp)**
- Approves service credits above policy thresholds and any non-standard compensation.

---

## Severity levels (Support/CS view)
Use these severities to standardize response; SRE may map to their incident severities.

**SEV-1 (Critical, likely customer-impacting breach)**
- Premium tier customer reports sustained throughput below floor OR SRE burn-rate alert is firing.
- Clear production impact (e.g., queue buildup, timeouts, user-visible latency spikes).
- Immediate paging required.

**SEV-2 (High, likely degradation / at-risk of breach)**
- Throughput is below expected but not yet confirmed as an SLO breach.
- Customer impact is moderate OR limited to a subset of traffic/routes.
- Paging may be required depending on tier and duration.

**SEV-3 (Moderate, investigation / metrics discrepancy)**
- Conflicting metrics (customer vs Redwood dashboards), short-lived dips, or suspected measurement artifacts.
- No clear production impact.

---

## Paging and escalation triggers (when to page SRE / Infra)
### Page SRE On-Call immediately when any of the following is true
- Premium tier customer: reported throughput below committed floor for **>10 minutes**, AND they confirm they are not client-throttled.
- Any tier: a Dedicated Throughput SLO **burn-rate alert** is firing for the deployment.
- Any tier: repeated or escalating customer timeouts/5xx correlated with throughput drop.
- Any tier: throughput drop correlates with recent Redwood changes (rollout, routing, driver/runtime update).

### Page Infra/Fleet On-Call when any of the following is true (often in parallel)
- GPU/node health signals indicate failures, evictions, ECC errors, or repeated container restarts.
- Saturation: GPU utilization pinned while throughput drops (suspected contention, thermal throttling, network bottleneck).
- Capacity: the deployment appears mapped to the wrong pool/shape or lacks the expected headroom.
- Region-wide capacity constraint impacting Dedicated pools.

### Engage Observability (not a page; async unless SEV-1)
- Throughput dashboards disagree with billing/export numbers.
- Recent metric changes (e.g., streaming token accounting) suspected.
- Missing labels (deployment_id, model, region) prevents accurate attribution.

---

## Triage checklist (Support/CS)
Perform steps 1–8 before (or while) paging; do not block paging for SEV-1.

### 1) Confirm customer context (capture in ticket)
Collect:
- Deployment ID(s)
- Customer name + environment (prod/stage)
- Region
- Tier (Standard / Plus / Premium)
- Model + version (exact identifier)
- Shape (if known)
- Time window of impact (with timezone)
- Symptoms (queueing, latency, timeouts, functional failure)

### 2) Confirm the customer is driving enough load
Ask:
- Current request concurrency
- Any client-side rate limiting (explicit QPS cap, worker pool limit)
- Any recent application deploy or traffic change

If the customer is under-driving load, throughput may be low without being an SLO failure.

### 3) Validate “eligibility” red flags quickly
- Context lengths: are they within contracted max context / recommended ranges?
- Parameters: extreme max_tokens or non-streaming vs streaming changes?
- Model/version pin: did they change models recently?

### 4) Check the Dedicated Throughput dashboard for that deployment
Look for:
- Rolling-window throughput vs committed floor
- Error rate/timeout correlation
- GPU saturation, queue depth, admission control shedding
- Recent routing pool changes or failovers

### 5) Check if this is a metrics discrepancy
If customer numbers differ from Redwood:
- Ask what they are measuring (wall-clock end-to-end, tokens counted, streaming vs non-streaming).
- Request a single representative request ID and a 10–20 minute sample window.

### 6) Determine if the issue is isolated or systemic
- One deployment vs multiple
- One region vs multiple
- One model family vs multiple

### 7) Mitigation options you can offer immediately (tier-dependent)
These are customer-facing actions CS/Support may propose, with SRE confirmation where possible:
- Temporarily reduce max context / max output tokens (if within acceptable product behavior).
- Temporarily shift traffic to the contracted primary route only (avoid experimental routes).
- Enable/disable streaming based on their client constraints (note: streaming affects perceived latency but should not invalidate throughput).
- If the customer has multi-deployment setup: fail over to a secondary deployment (Premium: may be Redwood-assisted).

Do not promise failover across regions unless the tier/contract explicitly includes it.

### 8) Open an incident record (internal) if SEV-1/SEV-2
- Create/associate the internal incident ticket.
- Add the customer support ticket link.
- Add a running timeline (timestamps for all key events).

---

## Warm pool / startup considerations
Some tiers include warm pool guarantees (or faster recovery expectations). During triage, identify if the customer is within a warm-up window:
- Did the deployment recently scale from zero?
- Was there a recent model reload, config change, or node replacement?
- Did traffic resume after prolonged inactivity?

If warm-up is suspected, note it explicitly in the customer thread and ask SRE to confirm whether the event is excluded by policy.

---

## Customer communications guide
### Communication principles
- Use a single owner for customer updates (CS/Support), even if multiple engineering teams are engaged.
- Acknowledge impact quickly; avoid speculating on root cause.
- Provide time-bound next update (“next update in 30 minutes”).
- For Premium accounts, default to more frequent updates and written summaries.

### Target update cadence (recommended)
- SEV-1: initial response within 15 minutes; updates every 30 minutes until stable.
- SEV-2: initial response within 1 hour; updates every 2–4 hours until stable.
- SEV-3: initial response within 1 business day; updates daily or as material changes occur.

---

## Templates (copy/paste)
### Template A — Initial acknowledgement (suspected throughput miss)
Subject: Dedicated deployment throughput investigation (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

Thanks for flagging this. We’re investigating reduced throughput on your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}) in {{region}}.

To help us confirm impact and correlate logs/metrics, could you share:
- The approximate start time and whether the issue is ongoing
- The model + version you’re using
- A representative request ID (or a small set) from the impacted window
- Any recent traffic/concurrency changes on your side

We’ll provide the next update by {{time}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template B — Confirmed incident + engaged on-call
Subject: Update: Dedicated throughput degradation (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

We’ve confirmed a throughput degradation affecting your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}). We’ve engaged our Site Reliability Engineering and Infrastructure on-call teams and are actively mitigating.

Current status:
- Impact: {{impact_summary}}
- Start time (UTC): {{start_time_utc}}
- Mitigation in progress: {{mitigation_summary}}

Next update by {{time}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template C — Request customer confirmation on throttling / load
Subject: Quick check: request load/concurrency for Dedicated deployment {{deployment_id}}

Hi {{customer_name}},

To confirm whether the deployment is being fully utilized during the impacted window, can you confirm:
- Current concurrency / worker count hitting the deployment
- Whether you have any client-side QPS limits or retries/backoff changes
- Whether the workload mix changed (context length, max output tokens, streaming on/off)

This helps us differentiate capacity/perf issues from under-driven traffic.

Thanks,
{{agent_name}}

### Template D — Resolution note + next steps
Subject: Resolved: Dedicated throughput issue (Deployment {{deployment_id}})

Hi {{customer_name}},

The throughput issue affecting your Dedicated deployment ({{deployment_id}}) is now resolved as of {{resolved_time_utc}}.

Summary:
- Root cause (preliminary): {{rca_summary}}
- What we changed: {{mitigation_actions}}
- Verification: throughput has been stable above the committed floor over the last {{window}}.

We’ll follow up with a written incident summary and, if applicable under your Dedicated tier terms, guidance on service credits.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

### Template E — Service credit intake (do not pre-approve)
Subject: Dedicated throughput incident — service credit review

Hi {{customer_name}},

We can review this incident for service credits under your Dedicated terms. To proceed, we will document:
- Deployment ID(s)
- Impact window (UTC)
- Confirmed SLO measurement from Redwood’s evaluator

We’ll share the outcome of the credit review by {{date}}.

Best,
{{agent_name}}

---

## Service credits / compensation (internal guidance)
**Important:** Do not commit to a credit amount in writing until approvals are complete.

### Which tiers are typically credit-eligible?
- **Premium:** written throughput commitment; credit review is standard for confirmed breaches.
- **Plus:** may be eligible depending on contract addenda; treat as “review required.”
- **Standard:** generally best-effort; credit requests require Product + Finance approval.

### When to initiate a credit review
Initiate when:
- SRE confirms an SLO breach per canonical evaluator, AND
- The breach is not excluded (e.g., customer throttling), AND
- The customer requests credits OR the account team recommends proactive review.

### Approval path (default)
1) CS/Support compiles incident details and SRE confirmation.
2) SRE provides breach window + summary.
3) Product Dedicated confirms entitlement interpretation (tier/exclusions).
4) Finance (Laura Bennett / delegate) validates policy compliance and amount.
5) Legal (Sofia Mendes / delegate) reviews if there is contract ambiguity or if a non-standard remedy is proposed.
6) CS communicates outcome to customer.

### What to include in the internal credit request
- Customer + contract/tier
- Deployment ID(s)
- Breach window (start/end in UTC)
- Evidence links: dashboard screenshots/links, evaluator output
- Customer impact summary (what broke, business impact if shared)
- Exclusion checks performed (throttling, workload constraints, warm-up)
- Proposed remedy type (service credit vs other)

---

## Evidence and logging requirements
For any SEV-1/SEV-2 and any credit review, capture:
- Customer ticket link
- Incident link (if created)
- Dashboard links (throughput vs floor, saturation, errors)
- Request IDs / traces used for debugging
- Summary of customer-side traffic confirmation
- Timeline of mitigations and when throughput stabilized

---

## Common failure modes and quick routing hints
- **Customer sees lower throughput than Redwood reports:** often a measurement mismatch (streaming token accounting, end-to-end latency vs throughput, counting input tokens). Engage Observability if persistent.
- **Throughput drops with GPU pegged:** suspect contention, node health, thermal throttling, network bottleneck; page Infra.
- **Throughput drops with low utilization:** suspect admission control limiting, customer throttling, or workload shift to longer contexts; page SRE; ask customer for concurrency/workload details.
- **Regional capacity constraint / failover behavior:** check tier failover posture; page Infra and SRE; do not promise cross-region without tier/contract confirmation.

---

## Post-incident expectations
For confirmed Premium-tier breaches (and significant Plus-tier events), request:
- SRE-written incident summary within 5 business days.
- Follow-up action items tagged to owning teams (SRE/Infra/Observability/Routing).
- If credits were issued: ensure the billing/metering record reflects committed vs burst usage correctly.

---

## Appendix: Minimal internal handoff note (for paging)
When paging SRE/Infra, include:
- “Dedicated Throughput SLO: suspected/confirmed”
- Deployment ID
- Tier + region
- Time window and whether ongoing
- Customer-confirmed load/concurrency (if available)
- Dashboard link
- Any correlated symptoms (timeouts, 5xx, queue depth)

This reduces back-and-forth and speeds mitigation.

source.title: Splunk HEC Audit Export (Beta) — Internal CS/SE Integration Guide
source.author: Colin O'Donnell
source.content:
## Purpose
This page is an internal Customer Success / Solutions Engineering guide for configuring **Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC)** as a destination for the **SIEM-ready audit export (beta)** feature in Redwood Inference. It covers:

- What to confirm with the customer before enablement
- How to configure Splunk HEC and Redwood’s destination settings
- How to validate events end-to-end (including dedup expectations)
- Troubleshooting common Splunk-side and Redwood-side errors
- Setting correct beta expectations (delivery semantics, backfill, limitations)

This doc is written for Hosted + Dedicated beta customers. Private/on-prem deployments are **out of scope for beta**.

---

## TL;DR (for fast enablement)
1) Confirm customer has Splunk HEC enabled and can provide: **HEC endpoint URL**, **HEC token**, **index**, and preferred **sourcetype/source**.
2) Ensure the customer’s networking permits inbound traffic to Splunk HEC from Redwood’s egress (customer may need allowlisting; see “Network & security prerequisites”).
3) Confirm Redwood tenant is **allowlisted** for the beta feature flag and the customer admin has the right RBAC permissions.
4) Create the Splunk destination in Redwood Console → **Audit Exports**.
5) Run **Test connection** in Console, then generate a known audit event (e.g., login, API key create, export config update) and verify it appears in Splunk.

---

## Beta expectations to set with customers (copy/paste)
Use this wording in email and calls to avoid misalignment:

- **Delivery semantics:** Audit export is **at-least-once**. During retries, the same event may be delivered more than once. Customers should deduplicate by **event_id**.
- **Ordering:** No strict ordering guarantees across categories/resources. Timestamps are included; use them for timelines.
- **Latency:** Typical delivery is near real-time, but short periods of lag can occur during downstream throttling/outages. Redwood provides export health and lag indicators.
- **Backfill:** Backfill is supported up to the configured window (beta limits apply per PRD). Backfill can increase throughput temporarily and may hit Splunk limits unless tuned.
- **Scope:** Hosted + Dedicated only (beta). Private/on-prem is not supported in beta.
- **PII:** Audit events follow Redwood’s audit schema and **PII minimization/redaction** policy; raw prompt or model output is not included in audit logs.

If the customer asks for exactly-once: “We provide at-least-once delivery; exactly-once is not guaranteed. Deduplication by event_id is the recommended pattern.”

---

## Prerequisites
### Redwood prerequisites
- Tenant must be enabled via the **SIEM audit export beta feature flag** (tenant allowlist).
- Customer user must have the correct RBAC permissions:
  - View export configs
  - Create/update export configs
  - Rotate credentials (if applicable)
  - View export health/status

If the customer cannot see the **Audit Exports** UI section, suspect **feature flag** or RBAC.

### Splunk prerequisites
Customer must:
- Have Splunk HEC enabled on Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud
- Provide:
  - **HEC endpoint** (including scheme/port), e.g. `https://splunk.company.com:8088`
  - **HEC token** with permissions to write to the chosen index
  - Target **index** (e.g., `redwood_audit`)
  - Desired **source** and **sourcetype** (recommended below)
- Confirm HEC is reachable from Redwood’s egress (or an intermediary gateway/ingress they manage)

Recommended Splunk naming conventions:
- index: `redwood_audit` (or customer standard, e.g., `security_audit`)
- sourcetype: `redwood:audit:v2` (stable; do not include tenant name)
- source: `redwood-inference` or `redwood-audit-export`

---

## Network & security prerequisites
Splunk HEC is customer-hosted; most integration failures are network/security related.

Confirm with customer:
- **TLS required:** Redwood requires HTTPS endpoints for HEC.
- **Allowlisting / firewall:** Customer must allow inbound to HEC from Redwood’s egress. If the customer requires IP allowlisting, coordinate via the standard Security/Networking intake process. (Do not guess IP ranges in writing.)
- **Reverse proxy/WAF:** If they front HEC with a WAF or proxy, confirm it supports:
  - POST requests
  - Payload sizes consistent with batching
  - Optional gzip compression (if enabled)
- **Token handling:** HEC token is a secret. It should be stored as a secret reference in Redwood (Console/API will treat it as sensitive; do not paste tokens into tickets or Slack).

---

## Configuration steps
### 1) Configure Splunk HEC (customer-owned)
Ask the customer Splunk admin to:

1. Enable HEC (if not enabled)
   - Splunk Web → Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector
   - Enable, choose a port (commonly 8088)
2. Create a new HEC token for Redwood Audit Export
   - Set a clear name, e.g., `redwood-audit-export-prod`
   - Limit index access to the intended index only
   - (Recommended) Disable “indexer acknowledgement” unless they explicitly require it; Redwood’s exporter uses its own retry + checkpointing model.
3. Confirm endpoint format
   - Standard endpoint: `https://<host>:8088/services/collector/event`
   - Some deployments accept `.../services/collector` as well; Redwood’s adapter targets the event endpoint behavior.

What we need from the customer:
- HEC base URL (host + port)
- HEC token
- Index
- Any required extra headers or proxy routing requirements (rare)

### 2) Configure the Splunk destination in Redwood Console (Redwood-owned)
In Redwood Console:

1. Navigate to: **Settings → Security & Compliance → Audit Exports**
2. Click **Create destination** → Choose **Splunk HEC (Beta)**
3. Fill in destination fields:
   - Destination name: e.g., `splunk-prod`
   - Endpoint URL: e.g., `https://splunk.company.com:8088`
   - Token: (store as secret)
   - Index: `redwood_audit`
   - Source: `redwood-inference` (optional; recommended)
   - Sourcetype: `redwood:audit:v2` (recommended)
   - Compression: leave default (generally ON if supported)
4. Configure filters (beta guidance):
   - Start with a minimal allowlist (e.g., authentication + admin/config changes) and expand after confirming throughput and indexing behavior.
   - Avoid overly broad exports on day 1 for high-volume tenants.
5. Set backfill window (if requested):
   - Keep initial backfill small (e.g., last 1–6 hours) to validate end-to-end.
   - Warn customer about potential burst volume and Splunk rate limits.
6. Click **Test connection**
   - This sends a lightweight test event (non-sensitive) to confirm authentication and connectivity.

If Test connection fails:
- Capture the UI error and export status details (do not capture token)
- See troubleshooting section below

---

## Validation checklist (end-to-end)
### A) Generate a known audit event
Recommended: generate an action that reliably emits audit events and is safe.
Examples:
- Create/update an audit export config (this itself is auditable)
- Create an API key (if policy allows)
- Change an RBAC role assignment (admin action)

### B) Validate in Redwood Console
In **Audit Exports → Destination → Status** confirm:
- Destination is **Enabled**
- Last success timestamp updates
- Lag is within acceptable range
- No sustained adapter errors

### C) Validate in Splunk search
Have customer run:

`index=redwood_audit sourcetype="redwood:audit:v2" earliest=-15m`

Recommended fields to spot-check:
- `event_id` (stable, unique per event; used for dedup)
- `timestamp` and/or `occurred_at`
- `tenant_id` / `workspace_id` (as applicable)
- `actor.*` (type/id; may include hashed/normalized identifiers per policy)
- `action` and `category`
- `resource.*` (type/id)
- `source_ip` and `user_agent` only when applicable and allowed by policy

### D) Deduplication guidance (Splunk)
Because delivery is at-least-once, duplicates may exist.

Customer can:
- Deduplicate in dashboards/searches using `event_id`
- Use Splunk’s search-level dedup, e.g.:
  - `... | dedup event_id`

Important: do not recommend dedup by timestamp alone.

---

## Data format notes (what Splunk receives)
- Payload: JSON events
- Each event includes stable identifiers and SIEM-friendly fields as defined in the Audit Event Schema v2
- Sourcetype/source/index are set per destination configuration

If a customer requests field mapping to Splunk CIM:
- Point them to the internal mapping guidance in: **Audit Event Schema v2 and SIEM mapping** (related page)
- In beta we prioritize stable schema + reliable delivery; custom per-customer field transforms are not included

---

## Common Splunk-side errors and fixes
### Quick reference table
| Symptom | Where seen | Likely cause | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| 401 Unauthorized | Redwood destination status / exporter logs | Wrong HEC token; token disabled; wrong endpoint path behind proxy | Ask Splunk admin to confirm token, re-create token if needed; confirm endpoint and proxy routing; rotate secret in Redwood |
| 403 Forbidden | Redwood status; customer ticket | Token scoped to different index; Splunk Cloud permissions; key restrictions | Confirm token permissions and target index; Splunk admin to adjust token/index settings |
| 404 Not Found | Test connection failure | Wrong URL (missing port, wrong base path), proxy misroute | Verify URL format and port; ensure HEC is enabled and reachable; check proxy configuration |
| 408/timeout / connection refused | Test connection failure / lag spikes | Firewall blocks, allowlist missing, TLS termination issues | Confirm network path; customer to allowlist Redwood egress; verify TLS cert chain; check WAF blocks |
| 429 Too Many Requests | Exporter errors; increased lag | Splunk HEC throttling / ingestion cap | Reduce backfill and filters; ask customer to raise HEC limits; Redwood exporter backs off and retries; duplicates may increase during retry windows |
| Events appear but fields are not extracted | Splunk UI/search | Sourcetype not configured for JSON, or props/transforms not enabled | Set sourcetype to JSON-friendly type or configure props.conf for JSON; confirm Splunk version and parsing rules |
| Large bursts overwhelm indexing | Customer reports missing/late events | Backfill too large; HEC queue/limits | Reduce backfill; stage rollout; consider separate index; monitor lag and throttle |

---

## Redwood-side troubleshooting (what CS/SE should check)
### 1) Is the tenant enabled for beta?
- If Audit Exports UI is absent or API returns “not found/disabled”, open an internal request to confirm the tenant is on the allowlist / feature flag enabled.

### 2) RBAC: does the user have permissions?
- If user can view but not edit, confirm they have export management permissions.

### 3) Destination health signals
Check the destination status panel:
- Last success
- Last error message (redacted)
- Current lag
- Retry counts

If lag grows and errors show 429/timeout:
- Treat as likely Splunk ingestion throttling or network instability
- Recommend reducing export scope temporarily (narrow filter, disable backfill)

### 4) Safe data handling while troubleshooting
- Do not request or paste HEC tokens in Slack.
- Use approved secret-rotation workflow if token must be replaced.
- When collecting evidence, capture:
  - Destination ID/name
  - Timestamp range
  - Error codes and summary
  - Exporter lag metrics screenshot (no sensitive payload)

### 5) Escalation
Escalate to on-call/SRE when:
- Export lag breaches agreed beta thresholds and persists
- Multiple tenants show similar failures (suspect platform regression)
- There is any sign of data leakage / misrouting

Reference: **Runbook — Audit Exporter Delivery Failures** (related page).

---

## Recommended customer enablement flow (SE-led)
1. Kickoff (30 min): confirm desired categories/filters and Splunk constraints (throttling, indexing rules).
2. Configure Splunk HEC token + index (customer admin).
3. Configure Redwood destination and run Test connection.
4. Validation (15–30 min): generate known audit events; confirm in Splunk.
5. Expand scope gradually:
   - Add more categories/actions
   - Add limited backfill if they need historical continuity
6. Set monitoring expectations:
   - Customer monitors Splunk ingestion and dashboards
   - Redwood monitors exporter health; weekly check-ins during beta

---

## FAQ (internal)
### Q: Does Redwood guarantee no duplicates?
No. Delivery is at-least-once. Dedup by `event_id`.

### Q: What if the customer demands exactly-once or Splunk ack?
In beta, we do not promise exactly-once. We also do not require Splunk indexer acknowledgement for correctness; Redwood’s exporter uses checkpointing + retries. If a strategic account requires ack semantics, capture as feedback for Product (Naomi Feldman) and Security (Marcus Lin) and do not commit.

### Q: Can the customer route to multiple Splunk HEC endpoints?
Not in the initial beta UI unless explicitly supported by destination configuration. Workaround is typically customer-side load balancing/ingress in front of HEC.

### Q: Can we send events to a customer-managed Splunk Cloud URL?
Yes if it is reachable and supports HEC; network allowlisting is usually the gating item.

### Q: Are prompts/model outputs included in audit events?
No. Audit events cover security-relevant actions and metadata per policy. If the customer requests payload-level logging, treat as a separate product conversation (and involve Security/Legal).

---

## Internal notes for CS/SE (do’s and don’ts)
- Do emphasize: stable schema, delivery guarantees (at-least-once), operational visibility.
- Do not promise: exactly-once, strict ordering, unlimited backfill, Private/on-prem support in beta.
- Do document in the account record:
  - Splunk endpoint type (enterprise vs cloud)
  - Throttling limits or ingestion caps mentioned
  - Index/sourcetype decisions
  - Any required allowlisting steps and current status

---

- 2025-04-02: Added recommended sourcetype/source conventions and validation checklist.
- 2025-03-12: Initial version published for beta cohort enablement.

title: Splunk HEC Audit Export (Beta) — Internal CS/SE Integration Guide
preview:
## Purpose
This page is an internal Customer Success / Solutions Engineering guide for configuring **Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC)** as a destination for the **SIEM-ready audit export (beta)** feature in Redwood Inference. It covers:

- What to confirm with the customer before enablement
- How to configure Splunk HEC and Redwood’s destination settings
- How to validate events end-to-end (including dedup expectations)
- Troubleshooting common Splunk-side and Redwood-side errors
- Setting correct beta expectations (delivery semantics, backfill, limitations)

This doc is written for Hosted + Dedicated beta customers. Private/on-prem deployments are **out of scope for beta**.

---

## TL;DR (for fast enablement)
1) Confirm customer has Splunk HEC enabled and can provide: **HEC endpoint URL**, **HEC token**, **index**, and preferred **sourcetype/source**.
2) Ensure the customer’s networking permits inbound traffic to Splunk HEC from Redwood’s egress (customer may need allowlisting; see “Network & security prerequisites”).
3) Confirm Redwood tenant is **allowlisted** for the beta feature flag and the customer admin has the right RBAC permissions.
4) Create the Splunk destination in Redwood Console → **Audit Exports**.
5) Run **Test connection** in Console, then generate a known audit event (e.g., login, API key create, export config update) and verify it appears in Splunk.

---

## Beta expectations to set with customers (copy/paste)
Use this wording in email and calls to avoid misalignment:

- **Delivery semantics:** Audit export is **at-least-once**. During retries, the same event may be delivered more than once. Customers should deduplicate by **event_id**.
- **Ordering:** No strict ordering guarantees across categories/resources. Timestamps are included; use them for timelines.
- **Latency:** Typical delivery is near real-time, but short periods of lag can occur during downstream throttling/outages. Redwood provides export health and lag indicators.
- **Backfill:** Backfill is supported up to the configured window (beta limits apply per PRD). Backfill can increase throughput temporarily and may hit Splunk limits unless tuned.
- **Scope:** Hosted + Dedicated only (beta). Private/on-prem is not supported in beta.
- **PII:** Audit events follow Redwood’s audit schema and **PII minimization/redaction** policy; raw prompt or model output is not included in audit logs.

If the customer asks for exactly-once: “We provide at-least-once delivery; exactly-once is not guaranteed. Deduplication by event_id is the recommended pattern.”

---

## Prerequisites
### Redwood prerequisites
- Tenant must be enabled via the **SIEM audit export beta feature flag** (tenant allowlist).
- Customer user must have the correct RBAC permissions:
  - View export configs
  - Create/update export configs
  - Rotate credentials (if applicable)
  - View export health/status

If the customer cannot see the **Audit Exports** UI section, suspect **feature flag** or RBAC.

### Splunk prerequisites
Customer must:
- Have Splunk HEC enabled on Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud
- Provide:
  - **HEC endpoint** (including scheme/port), e.g. `https://splunk.company.com:8088`
  - **HEC token** with permissions to write to the chosen index
  - Target **index** (e.g., `redwood_audit`)
  - Desired **source** and **sourcetype** (recommended below)
- Confirm HEC is reachable from Redwood’s egress (or an intermediary gateway/ingress they manage)

Recommended Splunk naming conventions:
- index: `redwood_audit` (or customer standard, e.g., `security_audit`)
- sourcetype: `redwood:audit:v2` (stable; do not include tenant name)
- source: `redwood-inference` or `redwood-audit-export`

---

## Network & security prerequisites
Splunk HEC is customer-hosted; most integration failures are network/security related.

Confirm with customer:
- **TLS required:** Redwood requires HTTPS endpoints for HEC.
- **Allowlisting / firewall:** Customer must allow inbound to HEC from Redwood’s egress. If the customer requires IP allowlisting, coordinate via the standard Security/Networking intake process. (Do not guess IP ranges in writing.)
- **Reverse proxy/WAF:** If they front HEC with a WAF or proxy, confirm it supports:
  - POST requests
  - Payload sizes consistent with batching
  - Optional gzip compression (if enabled)
- **Token handling:** HEC token is a secret. It should be stored as a secret reference in Redwood (Console/API will treat it as sensitive; do not paste tokens into tickets or Slack).

---

## Configuration steps
### 1) Configure Splunk HEC (customer-owned)
Ask the customer Splunk admin to:

1. Enable HEC (if not enabled)
   - Splunk Web → Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector
   - Enable, choose a port (commonly 8088)
2. Create a new HEC token for Redwood Audit Export
   - Set a clear name, e.g., `redwood-audit-export-prod`
   - Limit index access to the intended index only
   - (Recommended) Disable “indexer acknowledgement” unless they explicitly require it; Redwood’s exporter uses its own retry + checkpointing model.
3. Confirm endpoint format
   - Standard endpoint: `https://<host>:8088/services/collector/event`
   - Some deployments accept `.../services/collector` as well; Redwood’s adapter targets the event endpoint behavior.

What we need from the customer:
- HEC base URL (host + port)
- HEC token
- Index
- Any required extra headers or proxy routing requirements (rare)

### 2) Configure the Splunk destination in Redwood Console (Redwood-owned)
In Redwood Console:

1. Navigate to: **Settings → Security & Compliance → Audit Exports**
2. Click **Create destination** → Choose **Splunk HEC (Beta)**
3. Fill in destination fields:
   - Destination name: e.g., `splunk-prod`
   - Endpoint URL: e.g., `https://splunk.company.com:8088`
   - Token: (store as secret)
   - Index: `redwood_audit`
   - Source: `redwood-inference` (optional; recommended)
   - Sourcetype: `redwood:audit:v2` (recommended)
   - Compression: leave default (generally ON if supported)
4. Configure filters (beta guidance):
   - Start with a minimal allowlist (e.g., authentication + admin/config changes) and expand after confirming throughput and indexing behavior.
   - Avoid overly broad exports on day 1 for high-volume tenants.
5. Set backfill window (if requested):
   - Keep initial backfill small (e.g., last 1–6 hours) to validate end-to-end.
   - Warn customer about potential burst volume and Splunk rate limits.
6. Click **Test connection**
   - This sends a lightweight test event (non-sensitive) to confirm authentication and connectivity.

If Test connection fails:
- Capture the UI error and export status details (do not capture token)
- See troubleshooting section below

---

## Validation checklist (end-to-end)
### A) Generate a known audit event
Recommended: generate an action that reliably emits audit events and is safe.
Examples:
- Create/update an audit export config (this itself is auditable)
- Create an API key (if policy allows)
- Change an RBAC role assignment (admin action)

### B) Validate in Redwood Console
In **Audit Exports → Destination → Status** confirm:
- Destination is **Enabled**
- Last success timestamp updates
- Lag is within acceptable range
- No sustained adapter errors

### C) Validate in Splunk search
Have customer run:

`index=redwood_audit sourcetype="redwood:audit:v2" earliest=-15m`

Recommended fields to spot-check:
- `event_id` (stable, unique per event; used for dedup)
- `timestamp` and/or `occurred_at`
- `tenant_id` / `workspace_id` (as applicable)
- `actor.*` (type/id; may include hashed/normalized identifiers per policy)
- `action` and `category`
- `resource.*` (type/id)
- `source_ip` and `user_agent` only when applicable and allowed by policy

### D) Deduplication guidance (Splunk)
Because delivery is at-least-once, duplicates may exist.

Customer can:
- Deduplicate in dashboards/searches using `event_id`
- Use Splunk’s search-level dedup, e.g.:
  - `... | dedup event_id`

Important: do not recommend dedup by timestamp alone.

---

## Data format notes (what Splunk receives)
- Payload: JSON events
- Each event includes stable identifiers and SIEM-friendly fields as defined in the Audit Event Schema v2
- Sourcetype/source/index are set per destination configuration

If a customer requests field mapping to Splunk CIM:
- Point them to the internal mapping guidance in: **Audit Event Schema v2 and SIEM mapping** (related page)
- In beta we prioritize stable schema + reliable delivery; custom per-customer field transforms are not included

---

## Common Splunk-side errors and fixes
### Quick reference table
| Symptom | Where seen | Likely cause | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| 401 Unauthorized | Redwood destination status / exporter logs | Wrong HEC token; token disabled; wrong endpoint path behind proxy | Ask Splunk admin to confirm token, re-create token if needed; confirm endpoint and proxy routing; rotate secret in Redwood |
| 403 Forbidden | Redwood status; customer ticket | Token scoped to different index; Splunk Cloud permissions; key restrictions | Confirm token permissions and target index; Splunk admin to adjust token/index settings |
| 404 Not Found | Test connection failure | Wrong URL (missing port, wrong base path), proxy misroute | Verify URL format and port; ensure HEC is enabled and reachable; check proxy configuration |
| 408/timeout / connection refused | Test connection failure / lag spikes | Firewall blocks, allowlist missing, TLS termination issues | Confirm network path; customer to allowlist Redwood egress; verify TLS cert chain; check WAF blocks |
| 429 Too Many Requests | Exporter errors; increased lag | Splunk HEC throttling / ingestion cap | Reduce backfill and filters; ask customer to raise HEC limits; Redwood exporter backs off and retries; duplicates may increase during retry windows |
| Events appear but fields are not extracted | Splunk UI/search | Sourcetype not configured for JSON, or props/transforms not enabled | Set sourcetype to JSON-friendly type or configure props.conf for JSON; confirm Splunk version and parsing rules |
| Large bursts overwhelm indexing | Customer reports missing/late events | Backfill too large; HEC queue/limits | Reduce backfill; stage rollout; consider separate index; monitor lag and throttle |

---

## Redwood-side troubleshooting (what CS/SE should check)
### 1) Is the tenant enabled for beta?
- If Audit Exports UI is absent or API returns “not found/disabled”, open an internal request to confirm the tenant is on the allowlist / feature flag enabled.

### 2) RBAC: does the user have permissions?
- If user can view but not edit, confirm they have export management permissions.

### 3) Destination health signals
Check the destination status panel:
- Last success
- Last error message (redacted)
- Current lag
- Retry counts

If lag grows and errors show 429/timeout:
- Treat as likely Splunk ingestion throttling or network instability
- Recommend reducing export scope temporarily (narrow filter, disable backfill)

### 4) Safe data handling while troubleshooting
- Do not request or paste HEC tokens in Slack.
- Use approved secret-rotation workflow if token must be replaced.
- When collecting evidence, capture:
  - Destination ID/name
  - Timestamp range
  - Error codes and summary
  - Exporter lag metrics screenshot (no sensitive payload)

### 5) Escalation
Escalate to on-call/SRE when:
- Export lag breaches agreed beta thresholds and persists
- Multiple tenants show similar failures (suspect platform regression)
- There is any sign of data leakage / misrouting

Reference: **Runbook — Audit Exporter Delivery Failures** (related page).

---

## Recommended customer enablement flow (SE-led)
1. Kickoff (30 min): confirm desired categories/filters and Splunk constraints (throttling, indexing rules).
2. Configure Splunk HEC token + index (customer admin).
3. Configure Redwood destination and run Test connection.
4. Validation (15–30 min): generate known audit events; confirm in Splunk.
5. Expand scope gradually:
   - Add more categories/actions
   - Add limited backfill if they need historical continuity
6. Set monitoring expectations:
   - Customer monitors Splunk ingestion and dashboards
   - Redwood monitors exporter health; weekly check-ins during beta

---

## FAQ (internal)
### Q: Does Redwood guarantee no duplicates?
No. Delivery is at-least-once. Dedup by `event_id`.

### Q: What if the customer demands exactly-once or Splunk ack?
In beta, we do not promise exactly-once. We also do not require Splunk indexer acknowledgement for correctness; Redwood’s exporter uses checkpointing + retries. If a strategic account requires ack semantics, capture as feedback for Product (Naomi Feldman) and Security (Marcus Lin) and do not commit.

### Q: Can the customer route to multiple Splunk HEC endpoints?
Not in the initial beta UI unless explicitly supported by destination configuration. Workaround is typically customer-side load balancing/ingress in front of HEC.

### Q: Can we send events to a customer-managed Splunk Cloud URL?
Yes if it is reachable and supports HEC; network allowlisting is usually the gating item.

### Q: Are prompts/model outputs included in audit events?
No. Audit events cover security-relevant actions and metadata per policy. If the customer requests payload-level logging, treat as a separate product conversation (and involve Security/Legal).

---

## Internal notes for CS/SE (do’s and don’ts)
- Do emphasize: stable schema, delivery guarantees (at-least-once), operational visibility.
- Do not promise: exactly-once, strict ordering, unlimited backfill, Private/on-prem support in beta.
- Do document in the account record:
  - Splunk endpoint type (enterprise vs cloud)
  - Throttling limits or ingestion caps mentioned
  - Index/sourcetype decisions
  - Any required allowlisting steps and current status

---

## Change log
- 2025-04-29: Updated troubleshooting table and added explicit dedup search example; aligned beta expectations language with Security FAQ.
- 2025-04-02: Added recommended sourcetype/source conventions and validation checklist.
- 2025-03-12: Initial version published for beta cohort enablement.
